Possible joins: dispatches -> b2b_orders / shipments to map events to orders, customers, and delivery SLA calculations. device_id may join to a device registry (not listed) or to event streams; scanned_by can map to staff/agent tables for accountability. These joins enable multi-table NL2SQL questions combining customer, order, invoice and dispatch context.

b2b_vendor_partners.purpose: Repository of partner vendor master data for B2B operations — contains identifying, contact, service and commercial terms for vendors that provide services (logistics, quality inspection, cold storage, bulk packaging, transport) used across B2B workflows.
b2b_vendor_partners.key_characteristics: Small table (45 rows). vendor_id, vendor_name, contact_name, phone, email and gst_no are high-cardinality (mostly unique). service_type and credit_terms are low-cardinality (5 and 3 distinct values respectively). city has moderate cardinality (9). created_at is stored as nvarchar and has a single repeated value (likely ingestion timestamp). All columns are nullable and table has no declared primary or foreign keys. Data types are all nvarchar; several columns contain structured values (phone, gst_no, email) that may require normalization or cleaning for reliable joins and filtering.
b2b_vendor_partners.analysis_capabilities: This table alone supports vendor inventory/registry queries (counts by service_type, city distribution, credit term distribution), contact lookups, and data quality checks. It enables categorization and filtering of vendors by service, location, and payment terms. Alone it cannot provide spend/transaction metrics without joining to transactional tables but can be used to aggregate vendor counts and profile vendors.
business_questions: How many active vendors do we have by service type (e.g., Transport, Cold Storage, Quality Inspection)?
Which cities host the most vendors and what is the geographic distribution of vendor services?
What proportion of vendors are on NET 7 vs NET 15 vs NET 30 credit terms?
Who are the primary contacts and contact details for a given vendor_id or vendor_name?
Are there potential duplicate vendors (matching gst_no or phone/email) that need deduplication?
b2b_vendor_partners.relationships: No declared FKs, but vendor_id and gst_no are natural join candidates. Likely joins: vendor_id -> b2b_orders, b2b_invoices, b2b_payments, b2b_dispatches and other B2B tables to analyze spend, service utilization and vendor performance. gst_no is reliable for cross-system identity reconciliation. Joining enables analyses like total spend per vendor, late-payment rates by credit_terms, service_type performance and city-based logistics planning.

delivery_slots.purpose: Represents delivery time slots offered by fulfilment centres (centre_id) for specific dates and times, with capacity and remaining availability. Used to track and query which slots exist, their capacities, and how many order slots remain.
delivery_slots.key_characteristics: Small table (80 rows). slot_id is unique across rows (80 distinct). Many columns are stored as nvarchar rather than native date/time/numeric types. date has 15 distinct values concentrated around a specific window (multiple rows per date). start_time and end_time have very low cardinality (5 distinct each) indicating repeated time windows. centre_id has 3 distinct values. capacity_orders and available_orders contain numeric values stored as strings and have moderate cardinality (56 and 59 distinct). created_at appears to be a timestamp string and is unique per row. No declared primary or foreign keys.
delivery_slots.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports: availability reporting (total/available capacity by date/centre/time window), identifying fully booked or underutilized slots, trend analysis over the small date range, and scheduling queries (earliest/next available slot). Because of the lack of typed columns, analyses require casting/parsing of date/time/numeric fields. Joins to order/dispatch tables would enable booked vs available reconciliation, but even standalone it can answer capacity planning and slot inventory questions.
business_questions: Which delivery slots have available capacity greater than zero for a given date and centre?
What is the total delivery capacity and total available slots per centre per date?
Which time windows (start_time - end_time) are most utilized (lowest available_orders / highest booked) across the date range?
When were the slots created (created_at) and which slots were added most recently for a centre?
List the next N earliest available slots across all centres (by date then start_time) with available_orders > 0
delivery_slots.relationships: No declared foreign keys. centre_id can be joined to any master table that lists centre/warehouse/store metadata if such a table exists (e.g., a centres or pos_stores-like table) to enrich location name/details. slot_id can be joined to booking/order allocation tables (if they reference slot_id) to reconcile actual booked orders vs available_orders. created_at can be used to join or filter against event logs (e.g., b2b_events_stream) for slot creation events. Because no explicit keys are defined, joins are heuristic and rely on matching column values; ensure consistent data types and vocabularies when joining.

delivery_status.purpose: Event-level delivery tracking table that records status updates for deliveries/shipments tied to orders. Serves as the event stream for delivery lifecycle (loaded, in transit, out for delivery, failed attempt, delivered) and stores who updated the event and where (location + GPS).
delivery_status.key_characteristics: Small table (232 rows) with event-granularity records. Several high-cardinality text columns (event_ts, updated_by, gps_lat, gps_long) stored as nvarchar rather than native types. Categorical columns event_type (6 values), location (4 values), status (3 values) are low-cardinality and good for grouping. delivery_id/shipment_id/order_id each have many repeated values (multiple events per delivery/shipment/order). photo_url and notes are 100% NULL. No primary/foreign keys defined. Top values show even distribution among many entities; some columns require casting for temporal or numeric operations.
delivery_status.analysis_capabilities: Can compute delivery lifecycle metrics (time between events, counts per status/event_type), run SLA/latency analysis (time from LOADED to DELIVERED), identify geography-level performance (by location or GPS clustering), operator performance (updated_by), and failure analysis (FAILED/FAILED_ATTEMPT patterns). Standalone can answer time-series and aggregation questions about delivery events but needs joins to order/shipment tables for richer commercial context (customer, revenue).
business_questions: What percentage of delivery events are DELIVERED vs FAILED vs IN_TRANSIT over a time window?
How long (median/avg) does it take from LOADED to DELIVERED per location or per shipment?
Which couriers/operators (updated_by) have the highest failed attempt or failure rates?
Which locations (or geographic clusters using gps_lat/gps_long) have the most failed deliveries?
What is the distribution of event types over the course of a day / hour to identify peak delivery activity?
delivery_status.relationships: Logical joins: order_id -> orders (ecom_orders / b2b_orders / orders) to enrich with customer, order_value, shipping method; shipment_id -> shipments / b2b_dispatches / b2b_shipment_tracking_events to correlate with carrier and tracking details; delivery_id -> dispatches/delivery tables if present to get planned delivery metadata. Those joins enable end-to-end delivery KPIs (on-time delivery vs order revenue) and cross-table filtering (e.g., customer segments, product-level impacts). Currently no FK constraints in DDL; NL2SQL should match keys by name and cast types appropriately.

ecom_ab_test.purpose: Stores metadata and summary metrics for e‑commerce A/B tests (experiments) run on the platform. Each row describes one experiment instance including identifiers, variants, timestamps, status, winner, and summary conversion rates. This table is used for experiment tracking, basic summarization, and downstream analysis of experiment outcomes.
ecom_ab_test.key_characteristics: Small table (~80 rows). test_id is unique per row (80 distinct values). test_name has low cardinality (3). variant_a and variant_b are constant across rows ('Control' and 'Treatment'), indicating naming rather than variant-level detail. Timestamps (start_ts, end_ts, created_at) are stored as strings (nvarchar) but contain ISO-like datetime values. Conversion rates are stored as strings but represent numeric percentages/values with varied formatting (some decimal artifacts). status has three states: RUNNING, CANCELLED, COMPLETED. winner_variant has three values: A, B, NONE. Many analytic operations require casting conversion and timestamp columns to numeric/datetime types. There are no primary/foreign keys or explicit relationships.
ecom_ab_test.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table enables experiment-level analysis: distribution of statuses and winners, comparison of conversion_rate_a vs conversion_rate_b, experiment durations, time-based counts (by start/end/created), and identification of top/bottom performing experiments. It cannot attribute experiment outcomes to users or orders without joining to event/assignment logs in other tables (e.g., event streams, orders, product events).
business_questions: Which experiments are currently RUNNING, and what are their start and expected end dates?
For completed experiments, which variant (A or B) won and how many experiments did each variant win?
What is the distribution and average of conversion_rate_a and conversion_rate_b across experiments?
Which experiments produced a statistically meaningful improvement in conversion (e.g., B > A) and how large was the uplift?
What is the typical experiment duration and are cancelled experiments correlated with short durations or low sample conversion?
ecom_ab_test.relationships: No explicit foreign keys. Potential joins: - ecom_product_events, ecom_activity_log, ecom_orders or ecom_carts to map experiment assignments to users/orders (requires a common experiment id field in those event tables). - ecom_acquisition_agents or ecom_customer_segmentation for cohort analysis of experiment effects. These joins enable per-user or per-order outcome analysis, statistical testing, funnel analysis, and segmented lift calculations. In NL2SQL, joining requires canonical keys (likely test_id) to appear in the event logs; confirm presence of test_id or experiment identifiers in those other tables before joining.

ecom_acquisition_agents.purpose: Holds e-commerce customer-acquisition agent directory and metadata used to track who performed customer acquisition, their contact information, acquisition channel, area, join date and record creation timestamp. Used for reporting on acquisition workforce, channel mix and simple agent-level lookups.
ecom_acquisition_agents.key_characteristics: Small reference table (55 rows). agent_id is distinct for every row (55 distinct) but not declared as a primary key. Most columns are textual (nvarchar) including dates and timestamps stored as strings. Low-cardinality categorical columns: acq_channel (5 values) and status (2 values). area has 9 distinct values. Contact columns (phone/email) appear unique per agent. agent_id has a consistent prefix pattern (e.g., 'ECOMAG3001'). join_date and created_at follow ISO-like formats but are stored as nvarchar which prevents native date arithmetic without casting.
ecom_acquisition_agents.analysis_capabilities: Alone, the table supports agent counts, distribution by acquisition channel/area/status, contact lists, join-date based cohorts after casting join_date, and basic change-audit using created_at after casting. It can supply lookup/metadata for joins to transactional tables (orders, sales) to attribute activity to agents.
business_questions: How many acquisition agents are active vs inactive?
How many agents joined in each month/quarter/year?
Which acquisition channels supplied the most agents and how are they distributed across areas?
List contact details (phone, email) for all ACTIVE agents or agents in a given area.
Which agents were created/added in a given period (by created_at) for auditing/recent-hire reporting?
ecom_acquisition_agents.relationships: No foreign keys defined. Natural join candidates: agent_id (primary candidate), email and phone (fallback keys) to link with order/customer/contact or CRM tables. Joining to orders/payments/sales tables enables attribution of orders, customer acquisitions and revenue to acquisition agents. Because dates are strings, joins or time-window filters that rely on join_date/created_at should cast those columns to proper DATE/DATETIME first.

ecom_activity_log.purpose: Activity / audit log for e-commerce entities (customers, orders, payments, products). Captures who performed what action, on which entity, when, from which IP, and optional notes. Serves auditing, troubleshooting, security monitoring, and change-tracking purposes in the schema.
ecom_activity_log.key_characteristics: Small table (200 rows) in the sample. audit_id is unique per row (200 distinct). entity_type and action are low-cardinality (4 values each). performed_by is low-cardinality (3 values). entity_id has moderate cardinality (126 distinct) and contains prefixed identifiers (examples: CUST1022, EORD2086). action_ts is stored as nvarchar but uses an ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format and is distinct per row. ip_address appears well-formed IPv4 strings. notes are fully null in this sample. No declared PK/FK. Because timestamps are stored as strings, queries that use time ranges should CAST/CONVERT to native datetime for correct ordering and range filtering.
ecom_activity_log.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: (1) count and trend actions over time, (2) see who (performed_by) is performing types of actions and how often, (3) list recent changes to a particular entity or entity type, (4) detect suspicious activity by IP or by action mix (e.g., many DELETEs), (5) compute last action per entity to derive simple state snapshots or audit trails. You cannot (without joins) enrich logs with entity attributes (e.g., order totals, customer emails) or confirm the entity canonical keys if entity_id is a prefixed identifier.
business_questions: How many CREATE / UPDATE / DELETE / STATUS_CHANGE actions happened each day over the last 30 days?
Which users (performed_by) initiated the most DELETE actions in the last month and from which IPs?
What was the last recorded action for a given entity (by entity_id) and when did it happen?
Are there spikes in actions (especially DELETE or STATUS_CHANGE) for a specific entity_type or IP address indicating unusual activity?
How many actions were recorded per entity_type (ORDER, CUSTOMER, PAYMENT, PRODUCT) and what is the distribution of action types within each entity_type?
ecom_activity_log.relationships: entity_type + entity_id together suggest polymorphic relationships to domain tables: CUSTOMER → ecom_customers (or b2b_customers), ORDER → ecom_orders (or b2b_orders), PAYMENT → ecom_payments (or b2b_payments), PRODUCT → ecom_products/products. Joins should be conditional on entity_type (e.g., join to ecom_orders when entity_type='ORDER'). performed_by may map to user/admin/support agents in user or admin tables (pos_users, internal admin tables). No explicit FK; matching may require stripping prefixes on entity_id or using direct string match depending on target table key format. These relationships enable linking audit events to business attributes (order total, customer segment, payment status) for richer analytics and root-cause investigations.

ecom_browsing_history.purpose: Captures raw user browsing events on the e-commerce site (searches, category clicks, product views) and serves as the behavioural event source for analytics, personalization and funnel analyses.
ecom_browsing_history.key_characteristics: Small table (~300 rows). event_ts and created_at are stored as nvarchar (string) but represent timestamps — both are high-cardinality. browse_id and event_ts appear unique per row (browse_id distinct 300). customer_id has moderate cardinality (~95). sku has very low cardinality (7 distinct). event_type and device_type are low-cardinality enumerations. search_query and referrer_source have few repeated values, suitable for aggregation. No primary/foreign keys defined; many columns are nullable.
ecom_browsing_history.analysis_capabilities: User behaviour analytics (top search terms, device breakdown, referrer performance), simple funnels (search -> view product), time-series of events, per-customer activity profiling, conversion attribution when joined to orders/payments tables, and product interest measurement when joined to products/inventory.
business_questions: Which search queries generate the most product views or category clicks?
How many unique customers viewed a specific SKU in a given date range and on which device types?
Which referrer sources drive the highest volume of search events and product views?
What is the hourly/daily distribution of browsing events (event_ts) to detect peak times?
Which customers performed searches but did not convert (requires join to orders) in the same session/window?
ecom_browsing_history.relationships: This table can be joined to ecom_customers on customer_id to enrich user attributes, to products/product_variants (or product tables) on sku to map to product metadata, to ecom_search_keywords for standardized query mapping, and to ecom_orders/ecom_payments to attribute browsing to conversions. Those joins enable funnels, attribution and personalized recommendations.

ecom_cart_items.purpose: Holds individual e‑commerce cart line items captured from the raw data feed. Each row represents a product variant added to a shopping cart, with quantity, price at the time of add, timestamps and a reservation flag. It is a raw, denormalized source used for cart-level and item-level analyses, behavioral analysis and joining to product, variant and cart metadata.
ecom_cart_items.key_characteristics: Small table (150 rows). No declared PKs or FKs. Many columns are stored as nvarchar even though they contain numeric or datetime semantics (qty, price_at_add, added_at, updated_at). cart_item_id is unique per row (150 distinct). cart_id has moderate cardinality (81 distinct) and will be used to group items into carts. sku is highly categorical (10 distinct) and skewed toward a few SKUs. reserved_flag is binary stored as text ('1'/'0'). notes is entirely null. Timestamps appear in ISO-like format but stored as text and require parsing for temporal queries.
ecom_cart_items.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table enables item-level metrics (counts, sum qty, sum revenue using price_at_add), cart composition analyses (items per cart, common SKUs per cart), temporal analyses at item-add and update times after casting timestamps, reservation-rate analysis, and SKU / variant popularity. It can also be joined to carts, products, variants and orders to enrich with customer/context attributes and to compute conversion funnel metrics.
business_questions: Which SKUs and variants are most frequently added to carts (by count and quantity)?
What is the distribution of items per cart and average basket size (item count / quantity)?
How much nominal revenue (price_at_add * qty) is added to carts over time and by SKU/variant?
What percentage of cart items are reserved (reserved_flag) and how does reservation correlate with conversion?
What are peak times / hourly patterns for adding items to carts (based on added_at)?
ecom_cart_items.relationships: Natural join keys: cart_id (links to ecom_carts and ecom_orders to relate item to cart and order lifecycle), sku (links to products, ecom_products, b2b_products for product-level metadata), variant_id (links to product_variants or product_inventory tables). Joining enables conversion analysis (when a cart item later appears in an order), enrichment with product attributes (category, price bands), inventory checks, and customer-level behavior when joined to carts/customers.

ecom_carts.purpose: Captures shopping cart snapshots for e-commerce sessions: who created the cart (customer_id, session_id), timing (created_at, last_updated_at), contents summary (total_items, total_amount), promotion usage (coupon_code), platform (device_type), and abandonment state (abandoned_flag, abandoned_at). It is used as a staging/analytics source for cart behaviour, abandonment and pre-checkout metrics.
ecom_carts.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as nvarchar (strings). Row count is small (100) for this sample; cart_id, customer_id and session_id are unique per row (distinct = 100). total_items has low cardinality (8 distinct values) and looks integer-like but is stored as text. total_amount looks numeric (many distinct values) but stored as text and includes decimal precision. coupon_code has only 2 non-null values and ~37% nulls. currency is a constant (INR). device_type is tri-modal (web, ios, android). abandoned_flag is binary but stored as string ('0'/'1') and abandoned_at is null for most non-abandoned carts (~59% nulls). Timestamps follow 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' text pattern but are not typed as datetime.
ecom_carts.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can provide cart-level summaries: counts, average and distribution of total_amount and total_items, abandonment rates and timing, coupon adoption, device segmentation, and basic time-series trends (after converting created_at to datetime). It cannot compute line-item level or conversion to orders without joining to cart_items, orders or payments tables.
business_questions: What is the overall cart abandonment rate and how has it trended over time?
What is the average cart value (total_amount) and distribution by device_type or coupon_code?
How effective are coupons (NEWUSER vs FRESH10) in changing average cart value or abandonment rate?
Which customers or sessions generate the highest cart values and how many items do they contain?
What is the time between cart creation and abandonment (or last update) and how does that vary by device?
ecom_carts.relationships: No declared foreign keys in this table, but logical joins exist: cart_id -> ecom_cart_items (line-level items), cart_id -> ecom_orders (to measure conversion), customer_id -> ecom_customers / b2b_customers (customer profiles), session_id -> ecom_browsing_history (session context). These joins enable funnel analysis (cart -> order), richer customer segmentation, product-level contribution to cart values, and session behaviour analysis.

ecom_coupon_usage.purpose: Records instances of coupon usage in the e-commerce system — one row per use of a coupon tied to a customer and an order. It is used to measure coupon redemptions, discounts applied, and temporal patterns of coupon usage for marketing and finance analyses.
ecom_coupon_usage.key_characteristics: Small table (140 rows). coupon_usage_id is nearly unique (120 distinct) indicating mostly one row per usage id; coupon_id has moderate cardinality (54 distinct) meaning coupons are reused; customer_id (78 distinct) and order_id (84 distinct) show many single-use relationships but some repeat customers. applied_ts and created_at are stored as strings in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format (based on samples) with full distinctness (140 distinct). discount_given is stored as text but represents numeric monetary values (135 distinct). No declared primary or foreign keys in DDL.
ecom_coupon_usage.analysis_capabilities: By itself the table can be used to count redemptions per coupon, aggregate discounts given, build basic time series of coupon redemptions, identify repeat coupon redeemers, and compute per-customer or per-order discount totals. When joined to orders/customers/coupons tables it enables ROI and uplift analyses, coupon lifecycle analytics, and financial reconciliation.
business_questions: Which coupons were redeemed most often in a given date range?
What is the total discount given per coupon and overall in a period?
How many unique customers used coupons vs total coupon uses (repeat usage)?
What is the time distribution (hour/day/month) of coupon redemptions?
Which customers or orders received the largest total discounts?
ecom_coupon_usage.relationships: Logical joins (not enforced as FKs) exist: coupon_id -> ecom_coupons (coupon metadata and campaign info); customer_id -> ecom_customers (customer demographics/segments); order_id -> ecom_orders (order totals, items, revenue). These joins enable conversion and uplift analysis (e.g., coupon usage -> order revenue), segmentation (which customer segments redeem coupons), and campaign attribution when joined with coupon definitions and campaign start/end.

ecom_coupons.purpose: Holds master/definition records for promotional coupons used in the e-commerce system. It describes coupon identifiers, codes, discount rules, validity windows, eligibility criteria and administrative metadata. Used to determine which coupons exist and their properties for validation, application and reporting.
ecom_coupons.key_characteristics: Small table (65 rows). coupon_id and coupon_code are unique per row (distinct = 65) and can act as natural keys despite no declared primary key. Many attributes are stored as nvarchar although they represent numeric or datetime semantics (discount_value, max_discount_amount, min_order_amount, usage_limit, valid_from/valid_to, created_at/updated_at). Low cardinality columns exist (description and discount_type have 2 distinct values; active_flag is constant '1' across sample). Several columns contain timestamp-like strings in ISO-like format but stored as text — requires casting for temporal queries. Some numeric-like columns have small set of distinct values creating good grouping/aggregation potential. No declared FKs, but logical links exist to coupon usage/order tables (ecom_coupon_usage, ecom_orders, ecom_customers).
ecom_coupons.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table can: list available coupons, examine coupon attributes and validity windows, profile coupon types and distribution of discount values, count coupons by type/description, identify expired/active coupons (after casting valid_to/valid_from), filter coupons by eligibility thresholds (min_order_amount) and usage limits. It cannot compute redemption rates, revenue impact or per-customer usage without joining usage or orders tables.
business_questions: Which coupons are currently valid (now between valid_from and valid_to) and active?
How many coupons of each discount_type (PERCENT vs FLAT) exist and what are their typical discount_value and max_discount_amount?
What coupons require a minimum order amount above a given threshold (e.g., > 200)?
Which coupons will expire in the next X days?
Which coupons have low usage limits (e.g., usage_limit = 1) and might need replenishment or monitoring?
ecom_coupons.relationships: Logical relationship to ecom_coupon_usage (to measure redemptions), ecom_orders/ecom_payments (to measure revenue impact when a coupon was applied), ecom_customers (to analyze coupon usage by customer segments), ecom_carts/ecom_cart_items (to simulate coupon applicability), and ecom_notifications or marketing tables (for targeted campaigns). Those joins enable redemption rates, revenue-per-coupon, customer-level coupon effectiveness and coupon lifecycle analysis.

ecom_customer_addresses.purpose: Stores customer postal and geolocation address records for the e-commerce domain. Used to map customers to delivery locations, support order routing, delivery analytics and customer contact/address history.
ecom_customer_addresses.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows) with high-cardinality identifiers (address_id, customer_id ~100 distinct each). label is low-cardinality (Home/Work). state and country are constant (Gujarat, India). addr_line1 and pincode are moderately high-cardinality. addr_line2 has ~27% nulls and 3 distinct values (common landmark text). latitude/longitude are stored as nvarchar but are full precision and unique per row. created_at stored as nvarchar with 100 distinct timestamps (likely event times). No declared primary/foreign keys in DDL.
ecom_customer_addresses.analysis_capabilities: Can compute addresses per customer, distribution by city/pincode/label, geographic clustering (once lat/lon cast to numeric), recent address additions using created_at (after casting to datetime), join to customers/orders/payments tables to attribute addresses to orders/deliveries. Can support coverage and delivery performance analysis when joined to dispatch/shipment tables.
business_questions: How many addresses does each customer have and what proportion are 'Home' vs 'Work'?
Which cities and pincodes have the highest number of customer addresses?
What is the geographic (lat/lon) coverage of our customer base within India and are there clusters with high address density?
Which recent addresses (by created_at) were added in the last N days and which customers added them?
Are there customers that have addresses in multiple cities or pincodes (potential fraud or multi-location customers)?
ecom_customer_addresses.relationships: Natural join candidate to ecom_customers (customer_id) to enrich with customer profile data and to ecom_orders/ecom_dispatches/ecom_shipments via order->address references. Adding a foreign key to ecom_customers enables linking addresses to order history, payments, returns and delivery events; this enables questions like orders by delivery city, delivery success rate by pincode, and revenue per region.

ecom_customer_segmentation.purpose: Stores per-customer segmentation metrics derived from RFM analysis and related loyalty/order behavior. Intended to support marketing segmentation, targeted campaigns, retention analysis and customer-value reporting within the e‑commerce analytics schema.
ecom_customer_segmentation.key_characteristics: Small reference table (~100 rows). customer_id appears unique across rows (100 distinct). rfm_segment is low cardinality (4 values: Hibernating 33%, Regulars 28%, At Risk 24%, Champions 15%). loyalty_score stored as text but behaves numeric (59 distinct values). avg_order_value and purchase_frequency are stored as text but are numeric measures (both high cardinality ~100 and ~84 distinct respectively). last_purchase_date and created_at are text columns containing ISO-like timestamps (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). All columns are nullable (nvarchar) which requires defensive casting/validation. No declared primary key or foreign keys.
ecom_customer_segmentation.analysis_capabilities: Can be used alone to: profile customer distribution across segments, compute aggregate metrics by segment (mean/median avg_order_value, mean purchase_frequency, loyalty_score distributions), compute recency (relative to now) after casting last_purchase_date, identify high-value or at-risk customers, and generate small sample lists for targeted campaigns. Because columns are stored as strings, cast-to-numeric/date is required for correct sorting/aggregations.
business_questions: How many customers fall into each RFM segment and what proportion of the customer base does each segment represent?
What is the average (and median) avg_order_value and purchase_frequency by rfm_segment?
Which customers are 'Champions' and have a loyalty_score above a given threshold for VIP targeting?
Who are the customers with recent purchases (last_purchase_date within X days) but low loyalty_score (possible conversion opportunities)?
When were the segmentation records created (created_at) and how many new segment records were generated in a given time window?
ecom_customer_segmentation.relationships: customer_id can be joined to canonical customer tables (ecom_customers.customer_id or b2b_customers.customer_id) to enrich segmentation with customer profile fields (email, name, lifecycle stage) and transactional tables (ecom_orders, ecom_payments, product_sales) to validate/derive metrics. These joins enable cross-referencing segmented cohorts with actual order history, lifetime value reconstructions, contact details for campaign execution, and merging segmentation with product or channel-level analysis. Because no FK is declared, joins should match types (cast if necessary) and consider nulls/duplicates on the other side.

ecom_customers.purpose: Stores customer master/profile data for the e-commerce domain. It is the canonical customer dimension used to describe who the customers are, their contact details, demographic attributes, account status, consent and acquisition channel. It supports customer-centric reporting, segmentation, and joining to transactional tables (orders, payments, carts, addresses, etc.).
ecom_customers.key_characteristics: 100 rows (small sample). customer_id and external_customer_code appear unique (distinct = row count). Many columns are stored as nvarchar including dates/timestamps (dob, created_at, last_login). Low-cardinality categorical fields: gender (3 values), preferred_language (3 values), status (2 values), source_channel (4 values), marketing_opt_in (2 values). Names and contact fields are high-cardinality. created_at/last_login strings follow 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' pattern; dob follows 'YYYY-MM-DD'. There are no declared primary or foreign keys. Approximately balanced gender distribution with a notable 'Other' category (40%). Marketing opt-in is majority '1' (63%).
ecom_customers.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can be used for customer counts, demographic distributions (age/gender/language), onboarding trends (created_at), activity recency (last_login), opt-in rates, status segmentation (active vs inactive), and acquisition source breakdowns. It can produce cohorts by signup date, age groups (after converting dob), language segments for localization planning, and lists for marketing targeting (opt-ins).
business_questions: How many customers signed up each month and how has sign-up trend changed over time?
What is the distribution of customers by preferred_language, gender, and source_channel?
What percentage of customers have marketing_opt_in = 1, and how does opt-in vary by acquisition channel?
How many customers are currently ACTIVE vs INACTIVE and how recently did they login?
What are cohorts of customers by signup month and their last_login recency (to measure retention)?
ecom_customers.relationships: Natural join keys for this table are customer_id and email (both appear unique). It connects to transactional tables such as ecom_orders, ecom_payments, ecom_customer_addresses, ecom_cart_items, ecom_coupons, ecom_refunds, ecom_returns, ecom_wishlist, and b2b_* tables that reference customers. Joining enables customer-level aggregations: lifetime value and order counts from orders/payments, address enrichment from customer_addresses, behavior from browsing_history and carts, support/tickets from ecom_ticket. Because there are no declared foreign keys, joins should explicitly use the appropriate column (customer_id preferred if present in other tables; fallback to email for cross-system matching).

ecom_frequently_bought.purpose: Stores pairwise product co-purchase (frequently bought together) signals for e-commerce. Each row represents an association between a base SKU (sku) and another SKU frequently bought with it (freq_with_sku), with a numeric co-purchase strength and a last-updated timestamp. This table is used for product affinity, recommendation, and cross-sell analyses.
ecom_frequently_bought.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). map_id appears unique (100 distinct) but table has no declared primary key. sku and freq_with_sku have very low cardinality (6 distinct each) suggesting many-to-many pairings among a small product subset. co_purchase_rate is stored as text but represents a numeric ratio/score with high cardinality (~96 distinct) and values that look like floating-point numbers. last_updated is stored as text in a consistent 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format and is effectively a timestamp. Many columns are nullable (nvarchar) and there are no declared foreign keys.
ecom_frequently_bought.analysis_capabilities: Alone, the table supports: ranking most strongly co-purchased pairs; producing top-N co-purchase targets for a given SKU; filtering pairs by a minimum co-purchase rate; detecting recent updates (if last_updated is parsed to datetime); basic recommendation signals. It cannot enrich product metadata (name, category, price) without joins to product/product_variants tables. Because of small size, ad-hoc analytics and quick lookups are efficient but productionizing recommendations will require joining to product master tables and storing co_purchase_rate as numeric.
business_questions: For a given SKU, which other SKUs are most frequently bought together (top N by co_purchase_rate)?
Which SKU pairs have a co_purchase_rate above a chosen threshold (e.g., 5) for inclusion in cross-sell campaigns?
When were the affinity signals last updated, and which signals changed recently?
Which products (by sku) are the most common 'freq_with_sku' targets across all base SKUs (best candidates for site-level cross-sell slots)?
Are there any SKU pairs where co_purchase_rate has increased or decreased over time (requires historical snapshots/join to previous tables if available)?
ecom_frequently_bought.relationships: This table is an affinity/link table that should be joined to product master tables (e.g., products, product_variants, ecom_products, b2b_products) on SKU columns to retrieve product names, categories, images, and pricing. Typical joins: zuna_seed_raw.ecom_frequently_bought.sku = products.sku OR product_variants.sku OR ecom_products.sku; similarly for freq_with_sku. map_id appears internal and does not map to other schema tables. Joining to sales tables (ecom_order_lines, order_items, product_sales) can validate or re-compute co-purchase metrics and enable trend analysis. These joins enable building UI recommendations, promotional bundles, and measuring conversion lift.

ecom_notifications.purpose: Stores records of notifications (email, SMS, push) sent to customers related to orders and marketing; serves as the event/log table for outbound customer communications in the e-commerce schema.
ecom_notifications.key_characteristics: 150 rows (small sample). notification_id and timestamps are high-cardinality (150 distinct). customer_id and linked_order_id are medium-cardinality (~78 and ~75 distinct) indicating repeat customers and repeated links to orders. notification_type, title, message, delivery_status are low-cardinality categorical fields (3 distinct values each) with clear dominant values: PUSH/EMAIL/SMS (~34/34/31%), titles: Order Update/Delivery Confirmation/New Offer (~39/31/30%), messages mapping strongly to titles, and delivery_status dominated by FAILED/DELIVERED/SENT (~36/33/31%). sent_ts and created_at are stored as nvarchar but appear in ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format — treat as timestamps for time-based analytics. No primary/foreign key constraints are defined at the DDL level.
ecom_notifications.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table can be used to analyze notification volumes, channel mix, delivery effectiveness (rates of DELIVERED vs FAILED), time patterns (after casting sent_ts/created_at to datetime), per-customer notification frequency, and simple joins to orders/customers if linked by id strings. It supports cohorting by notification type/title/message and temporal slicing (day/hour) once timestamps are cast.
business_questions: What is the volume of notifications sent by channel (PUSH/EMAIL/SMS) over time?
What are delivery outcomes (DELIVERED / FAILED / SENT) by notification type or by customer cohort?
Which customers received the most notifications in a given period?
How many notifications are tied to orders and what delivery outcomes do those tied notifications have?
What are the peak hours/days when notifications are sent and which channels perform better at those times?
ecom_notifications.relationships: Logical joins: customer_id can join to ecom_customers (or b2b_customers) to enrich customer attributes (segment, region, lifecycle). linked_order_id can join to ecom_orders/ecom_order_events to relate notifications to order lifecycle. Notifications can be correlated with ecom_payments, ecom_dispatches or b2b_dispatches via order id for root-cause analysis of failed deliveries or shipping updates. These joins enable cross-analysis of notifications vs order status, payments, deliveries, and customer attributes. Note: no FK constraints exist in DDL — join keys are string identifiers and require careful equality matching and type normalization.
ecom_notifications.nl2sql_optimization_recommendations: Treat sent_ts/created_at as datetime by casting (e.g., CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP) in queries. Use equality or IN filters for low-cardinality columns (notification_type, delivery_status) and GROUP BY them. For common NL2SQL intents (e.g., 'last 7 days'), parse natural-language date ranges into concrete predicates against cast timestamps. Add indexes if used frequently in analytics: (sent_ts), (customer_id), (linked_order_id), and consider unique index or primary key on notification_id. Normalize repeated message/title strings into a reference table if the dataset grows to reduce text comparisons.

ecom_orders.purpose: Orders table recording e-commerce order-level events and monetary amounts. It captures order identifiers, customer linkage, timestamps, channel and campaign attribution, fulfillment and delivery metadata, and financials (total, tax, discount, shipping, net). It acts as a primary fact table for sales/fulfilment/marketing analysis in the schema.
ecom_orders.key_characteristics: Low row count (601) but many high-cardinality text fields where numeric/date semantics are represented as nvarchar. Several categorical columns with low cardinality (channel, order_status, payment_status, coupon_code, fulfillment_centre_id). Timestamps (order_ts, created_at, updated_at) stored as strings with consistent 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern; many unique values. Monetary columns (total_amount, tax_amount, discount_amount, shipping_charge, net_amount) stored as strings and contain floating values. customer_id and delivery_address_id are moderately to highly cardinal and are natural join keys to customer/address dimension tables present in schema. No declared primary or foreign keys.
ecom_orders.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can compute order counts, revenue metrics (total/net revenue, taxes, discounts, shipping), time-based trends (orders per day/hour/week), channel and campaign performance (orders and revenue by channel/source_campaign), fulfillment center and delivery slot utilization, order lifecycle distributions (order_status/payment_status), coupon usage and basic repeat/new segmentation (Unnamed: 19). You can also compute cancellation/refund rates when combined with payment_status and order_status fields.
business_questions: What is total/net revenue and number of orders over time (day/week/month) and by channel?
Which campaigns or coupon codes generate the most revenue, orders, and highest average order value?
What are cancellation and refund rates by channel, fulfillment centre, or delivery slot?
How does order distribution and fulfillment load vary by delivery_slot_id and fulfillment_centre_id?
Which customers are repeat vs new and what is their average order value and frequency?
ecom_orders.relationships: Although there are no declared foreign keys, natural joins exist: customer_id -> ecom_customers / b2b_customers; delivery_address_id -> ecom_customer_addresses; order_number/order_id -> order_lines / order_items / b2b_order_lines / ecom_payments / ecom_refunds / ecom_return_items; fulfillment_centre_id -> fulfillment/warehouse dimension (pos_stores/ecom_vendors or internal centers). Joining enables item-level metrics, per-customer lifetime value, payment and refund reconciliation, and linking orders to shipment/tracking and returns tables.

ecom_payment_status_log.purpose: Event log of payment status transitions and processing attempts for e-commerce payments. Each row records a single logged transition or processing attempt for a payment (identified by payment_id), along with metadata about source, response, retry attempts, and timestamps. This table is used for operational monitoring, retry logic analysis, and debugging payment processing flows.
ecom_payment_status_log.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). Most columns are nvarchar/text; timestamps (created_at, updated_at) are stored as strings (consistent format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). log_id and payment_id are high-cardinality (distinct=100) and appear unique per row (log_id distinct=100 suggests row-level uniqueness). status_before and status_after are low-cardinality enums (3 distinct values each). source and response_code are low-cardinality (2 and 3 distinct values). response_body and notes contain only NULLs in this snapshot (100% null). retry_count has small integer-like set (0-3) stored as nvarchar. processed_flag is a binary flag stored as nvarchar with values '0'/'1'. No declared primary/foreign keys though payment_id can be used to join to payments tables. Because of small size, full-table scans are inexpensive, but proper typing/indexing will improve NL2SQL conversions and predicate pushdown.
ecom_payment_status_log.analysis_capabilities: On its own the table can: analyze status transition distributions and frequencies; compute failure/success rates by source and response_code; measure retry patterns and average retries per payment; time-based analysis of events using created_at/updated_at after casting to timestamps; identify unprocessed or repeatedly retried messages via processed_flag and retry_count. It cannot by itself link to order, customer, or invoice context without joining on payment_id to other tables.
business_questions: What proportion of payment status transitions result in FAILED vs PAID vs PENDING?
How many retries are performed per payment and how many payments exceed a retry threshold (e.g., >2)?
Are failures more common for a particular source (Manual vs PG Webhook) or response_code (200/402/500)?
Which payments had status changed most recently (latest updated_at) and are still unprocessed (processed_flag = 0)?
What is the time lag between created_at and updated_at for status changes and does it differ by source or response_code?
ecom_payment_status_log.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Practical joins: payment_id -> ecom_payments (or b2b_payments/b2b_invoices/ecom_orders) to enrich with order/customer/payment amount/merchant info. Timestamps enable joining with other event tables (e.g., ecom_payment, ecom_orders, ecom_product_events) for sequence analysis. status_before/status_after can be correlated with order/payment lifecycle columns in ecom_payments or ecom_orders to compute end-to-end success rates and reconciliation. Because payment_id is distinct and present in payment tables, it is the primary candidate join key.

ecom_payments.purpose: Payment ledger for e-commerce transactions capturing payment attempts, status, gateway details, settlement and refund eligibility. It serves as the canonical source for payment events and reconciliation in the schema.
ecom_payments.key_characteristics: Small table sample (100 rows). Many identifier columns are high-cardinality (payment_id, order_id, gateway_ref_no). Several columns use enumerations with low cardinality (payment_mode: COD/NETBANKING/UPI/CARD; payment_gateway: Razorpay/Paytm/BankTransfer; status: PENDING/FAILED/SUCCESS; settlement_status: SETTLED/UNSETTLED; refund_eligible_flag: 1/0). Date/time and numeric values are stored as nvarchar strings (payment_ts, settlement_date, created_at, amount) and need casting for accurate time-series and arithmetic. No primary key or FKs declared in DDL, but logical joins exist (order_id -> orders). 'processed_by' is constant ('system'). 'notes' is fully null.
ecom_payments.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can compute payment success/failure rates, gateway and mode performance, revenue by payment attributes (after casting amount), settlement delays (payment_ts -> settlement_date), refund eligibility counts, and basic time-series of payments. It cannot enrich payments with customer, product or order-level attributes without joining orders or customers tables.
business_questions: What is the total collected amount and count of payments by payment_mode or payment_gateway over a period?
What percentage of payments are successful vs failed or pending, and how does that vary by gateway or mode?
What is the average settlement lag (time between payment_ts and settlement_date) and settlement rate by gateway?
How many payments are refund-eligible and what is their total amount?
Which orders have payments with failed or pending status (to drive follow-ups / retries)?
ecom_payments.relationships: Primary logical relationship is to orders via order_id (ecom_orders / b2b_orders). Gateway_ref_no can be used to reconcile with external payment gateway logs. settlement_status and settlement_date enable joins with settlement or reconciliation tables (if present). No declared FK in DDL; NL2SQL should prefer explicit join predicates when linking to ecom_orders/ecom_payment_status_log or other order/payment-related tables in the schema.

ecom_product_events.purpose: Records discrete product-related events on the e‑commerce platform (stock changes and price changes). It is an event log used to understanding when and why product attributes (stock or price) changed and who/what triggered those changes.
ecom_product_events.key_characteristics: Small table (250 rows). event_id and timestamps (event_ts, created_at) are high‑cardinality and appear unique per row; sku is low cardinality (7 distinct values) with a few skus dominating; event_type and triggered_by are low cardinality (3 values each). old_value and new_value are fully NULL in the sample (100% nulls). Timestamps are stored as nvarchar but use an ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format, so they can be cast to proper datetime types without complex parsing. No declared primary or foreign keys in DDL, but event_id appears unique in the sample and sku is a natural join candidate with product tables.
ecom_product_events.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports event-counting, event-type distribution, trend/time-series of events, per-sku event frequency, and attribution of changes to automated agents vs system. It can reveal patterns like which SKUs have the most price drops or stock updates and when those changes clustered in time.
business_questions: Which SKUs experienced the most BACK_IN_STOCK, OUT_OF_STOCK or PRICE_DROP events in a given date range?
How did frequency of PRICE_DROP events vary over time (daily/weekly) and which agent (triggered_by) initiated most price changes?
Which SKUs had frequent inventory fluctuations (OUT_OF_STOCK / BACK_IN_STOCK) and when did those events occur?
Are price drops followed by spikes in orders/sales (requires joining with sales/orders tables)?
Which automated agent (pricing_bot, inventory_bot, system) is responsible for the majority of events and how does that vary by SKU?
ecom_product_events.relationships: This table should be joined to product master tables by sku (e.g., products, product_variants, b2b_products, ecom_products) to enrich events with product attributes (name, category). It should join to sales/ordering tables (ecom_orders, ecom_product_wise_sales, order_items) to measure impact of PRICE_DROP or BACK_IN_STOCK on sales. It can link to inventory_items or product_inventory for stock reconciliation and to product_pricing for price history validation. These relationships enable causal / impact analyses (price change -> sales), root‑cause investigations (inventory events -> shipments/returns), and enriched reporting (category-level event summaries).

ecom_product_wise_sales.purpose: Daily product-level ecommerce sales snapshot. Each row captures sales metrics (orders, customers, quantity, gross/net amounts, fees, discounts, taxes) for a given product and date. Used for reporting, trend analysis, and feeding downstream KPIs.
ecom_product_wise_sales.key_characteristics: Small table (21,300 rows). Date stored as nvarchar but appears in ISO-like YYYY-MM-DD format (max len 10). product_id has very low cardinality (~10 distinct), indicating a small product subset or aggregated product groups. All numeric metrics are stored as nvarchar (high cardinality ~one value per row) which prevents native numeric/date operations and slows aggregations. No primary key defined (date+product_id is a natural candidate). Many rows are unique across combinations causing most numeric columns to be high-cardinality strings. Nullability present across columns.
ecom_product_wise_sales.analysis_capabilities: Time series and trend analysis per product (daily granularity), top-N product ranking by sales or quantity, computation of derived KPIs (average order size, AOV, discount% of gross), simple forecasting inputs, reconciliation against other sales tables. However, current storage as strings requires casting before numeric/date analytics. Joins with product dimension tables (products/ecom_products) can enrich product metadata.
business_questions: Which products drove the most net sales over a given date range?
How did daily gross sales and net sales trend for a particular product last month?
Which products have the highest average quantity per order or highest discount rates?
What is the contribution of delivery_fee and tax_amount to total costs across products?
Which days had anomalous sales (spikes/dips) for top SKUs?
ecom_product_wise_sales.relationships: Natural join key: product_id (links to products, ecom_products, b2b_products, product_variants). date links conceptually to daily/monthly/yearly aggregate tables (ecom_sales_daily, ecom_sales_monthly, ecom_sales_yearly, b2b_sales_daily). Joining to product_pricing, product_inventory or product_sales enables price, stock, and multi-channel reconciliation. Recommended relationships: join on product_id to enrich name/category; aggregate joins on date to align with other time-based sales/returns tables.

ecom_ratings_summary.purpose: This table stores summarized e-commerce product rating metrics (one row per rating summary record) used to report product-level average rating, review counts, and timestamps for last review and last update. It is intended as a lightweight summary view of product ratings for reporting, dashboards, and quick lookups.
ecom_ratings_summary.key_characteristics: 100 rows total; rating_summary_id is unique across the 100 rows (distinct 100) but the table has no declared primary key. sku has very low cardinality (7 distinct values) and is highly skewed (top SKU accounts for 25% of rows). avg_rating and total_reviews are stored as nvarchar (strings) but represent numeric values; avg_rating shows many floating representations (long decimals). last_review_ts and updated_at are strings representing datetimes (format appears to be 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS') and are distinct for each row. All columns are nullable and require explicit casting/validation for numeric/date operations.
ecom_ratings_summary.analysis_capabilities: Using this table alone you can: compute per-SKU aggregates (average rating, total reviews), rank products by rating or review volume, identify most/least recently reviewed products, detect stale or frequently updated summaries, and evaluate correlation between review count and average rating. You can also produce small dashboards (top-N lists, distributions) and filter by recency windows after casting dates.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest average rating and how many reviews do they have?
Which SKUs have the most total reviews and what are their average ratings?
Which products have not received any recent reviews (stale last_review_ts) or have old summary updates (updated_at)?
Is there a relationship between total_reviews (popularity) and avg_rating (quality)?
What are the top N SKUs by weighted rating (e.g., avg_rating weighted by total_reviews) in the last 30 days?
ecom_ratings_summary.relationships: Natural join candidate is sku -> product dimension tables (products, product_variants, ecom_products, b2b_products) to enrich with product name, category, vendor, etc. rating_summary_id appears to be a unique identifier for a rating summary record but is not declared as a primary key; it can be used to track or deduplicate records. Joining on sku enables cross-table analyses (sales vs ratings, inventory vs ratings, product metadata). Because columns are strings, joins on sku are straightforward, but numeric/date columns must be cast before numeric/date comparisons or time-based joins.

ecom_refunds.purpose: Holds refund events for e-commerce orders: one row per refund transaction capturing when the refund was requested/processed, its amount, mode, status and reason. It is used to analyze refund volume, reasons, processing outcomes and to reconcile with orders and payments.
ecom_refunds.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows). refund_id is unique across rows (distinct=50). order_id and payment_id show many-to-one relationships (order_id distinct 39, payment_id distinct 39) indicating some orders/payments have multiple refunds. refund_ts and processed_at are stored as nvarchar but contain timestamp strings (distinct=50). refund_amount is stored as nvarchar but contains decimal numeric values (distinct=50). refund_mode and status are low-cardinality enums (2 and 3 distinct values respectively). processed_by is constant (finance_team) and notes are entirely NULL. gateway_ref_no is unique per row (distinct=50).
ecom_refunds.analysis_capabilities: By itself this table can compute refund counts, total/refunded amount aggregates, refund trends over time, status distribution and reason breakdowns. It supports identifying orders/payments with multiple refunds, average refund size, and mode-based analysis (ACCOUNT_CREDIT vs PG_REFUND). For deeper root-cause or customer-level insights it should be joined to orders, payments and customer tables.
business_questions: What is the total refunded amount and number of refunds over a time window (daily/weekly/monthly)?
Which reasons contribute most to refunds and how do they trend over time (Quality Issue vs Order Cancelled vs Customer Return)?
What proportion of refunds are completed vs initiated vs failed and how much money is in each status?
Which orders or payments have multiple refunds and what is the aggregate refunded amount per order?
How does refund mode (ACCOUNT_CREDIT vs PG_REFUND) impact refund success rates and processing times?
ecom_refunds.relationships: No declared foreign keys in DDL, but natural joins are: order_id -> ecom_orders (or ecom_order_items), payment_id -> ecom_payments / ecom_payment_status_log, gateway_ref_no -> payment gateway logs or ecom_payment_status_log, and processed_by -> pos/users or finance team user table if available. Joining to ecom_customers via ecom_orders enables customer-level refund analysis. These joins enable reconciliation (order amount vs refund amount), customer-level churn or returns analysis, and linking refunds to payment gateway outcomes.

ecom_return_items.purpose: Represents individual items within returned orders for the ecommerce returns pipeline. Each row is one returned item instance capturing identifiers (return_item_id, return_id, order_item_id), product reference (sku), quantity, inspection status/timestamp, restockability, disposition decision and optional notes.
ecom_return_items.key_characteristics: Small table (60 rows). Most identifier columns are stored as nvarchar even when they behave like keys. return_item_id is unique across rows (distinct: 60). return_id has moderate cardinality (29 distinct) indicating many returns have multiple items. order_item_id nearly unique (53 distinct). sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) with a few SKUs dominating (ONI003 = 20%). qty is stored as text (nvarchar) but contains small integer-like values (top values: 7,6,10,2,4). inspected_flag and restockable_flag are textual boolean flags with values '1'/'0' (inspected_flag: 76.7% '1'; restockable_flag: 53.3% '1'). inspected_at is textual but appears to be ISO-like timestamps (distinct for each row). disposition is categorical with three values: DISCARD, RESTOCK, RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER. notes column is completely null.
ecom_return_items.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports item-level return analytics: counts of returned items, distribution of dispositions, restockable rate and inspected rate by SKU or return, item quantities distribution, and identification/listing of return items with inspection timestamps. Temporal analyses relying on true dates require casting inspected_at to datetime. Cross-entity analyses (e.g., monetary impact, customer segmentation, original order dates/products metadata) require joins to orders, order_lines, products, returns tables using order_item_id, sku and return_id.
business_questions: Which SKUs generate the most returned items and what are their dispositions (RESTOCK/DISCARD/RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER)?
What proportion of returned items are restockable, and does that vary by SKU or disposition?
How many items per return (return_id) on average and which returns contain the largest quantities?
What percentage of return items are inspected and what is the distribution of inspection timestamps (to measure inspection throughput)?
Which order items (order_item_id) are most frequently returned (repeat returns) and how many times?
ecom_return_items.relationships: Primary join keys: return_id joins to higher-level returns table (e.g., ecom_returns / returns) to get customer, return creation time, and monetary context; order_item_id joins to order line tables (e.g., ecom_order_lines, order_items, b2b_order_lines) to get product details, price and order metadata; sku joins to product catalog tables (products, product_variants, ecom_products) for product attributes. These relationships enable root-cause analysis (product defect vs customer misuse), financial impact calculation (refund amounts), time-to-inspection SLAs (compare return.created_at to inspected_at), and inventory reconciliation (use restockable_flag + disposition to update stock).

ecom_reviews.purpose: Stores customer product reviews captured from the e-commerce channel. Each row represents one review instance with text, rating, timestamps and metadata; used to analyze product sentiment, quality, and customer feedback trends.
ecom_reviews.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows). Many attributes are stored as strings (nvarchar) although several represent numeric or temporal data. High cardinality on review_id and customer_id (100 distinct each). sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) and is skewed (top SKUs cover large share). rating has only 3 distinct values (3/4/5) with skew toward 4. verified_purchase_flag is constant ('1') across all rows. responded_by and response_text are completely NULL. review_ts and created_at are stored as text in ISO-like format (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS) and are parseable as datetimes.
ecom_reviews.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can: compute rating distributions and averages by sku; track review counts and trends over time; surface most upvoted/helpful reviews; calculate proportion of reviews that are verified purchases (currently constant); identify reviews requiring merchant response (none populated presently); perform basic text analysis or sentiment classification on title/review_text (suitable for full-text or NLP).
business_questions: Which products (sku) have the highest and lowest average rating?
How have review volumes and average ratings changed over time (daily/weekly/monthly)?
Which reviews received the most helpful votes and who wrote them?
What proportion of reviews are verified purchases and is there a rating difference between verified and non-verified reviews?
Which reviews are negative (low rating / negative text) and have not been responded to by support?
ecom_reviews.relationships: Natural joins: customer_id -> ecom_customers (customer profile, segments, lifetime value), sku -> products / ecom_products / product_variants (product metadata, category, vendor), review_ts/created_at -> ecom_orders or ecom_payments (to link review timing to purchase/fulfillment). These joins enable cross-analysis (e.g., link review sentiment to customer cohorts, order recency, product attributes, or fulfillment events).

ecom_sales_daily.purpose: Daily aggregated e-commerce sales metrics by order channel. It appears to be a lightly processed daily rollup that captures counts (orders, customers), quantities (kg), monetary metrics (gross, discount, delivery, tax, net) and ingestion metadata. Its role in the schema is as a daily summary table used for reporting, dashboards and quick trend queries across order channels.
ecom_sales_daily.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as nvarchar; many numeric columns are string-encoded with high cardinality. 'date' is a string in ISO-like format (yyyy-mm-dd) with ~710 distinct values across 2,130 rows (suggests multiple channels per date). 'order_channel' has low cardinality (3). Monetary measures have near-row-unique values (high cardinality), indicating per-day unique aggregates. created_at looks like ingestion timestamps with only two distinct values. Several Unnamed columns are entirely or mostly NULL; two of them contain sparse values (Unnamed: 13 has 5 short codes present in ~0.2% of rows; Unnamed: 14 has 5 large numeric-like strings present in ~0.2%). No declared keys or foreign keys.
ecom_sales_daily.analysis_capabilities: This table alone supports time series analysis of sales and operational KPIs by channel (daily trends, moving averages, channel comparisons), simple aggregation (totals, averages), period-over-period growth, and basic unit metrics (avg order size, avg kg per order). It is not suitable for order-level drilling without joining to detailed order or product tables.
business_questions: How do gross, discount, and net sales trend daily and by channel over a given period?
Which order channel (Website, Mobile App, WhatsApp) generates the highest average order value or highest net sales per day/week?
What is the relationship between total_qty_kg shipped and net sales (kg per unit revenue) over time?
How do discounts and delivery fees impact net sales and margin estimates over time?
Are there anomalous days with unusually high delivery fees, tax amounts, or discount rates that require investigation?
ecom_sales_daily.relationships: No explicit FKs. Typical joins are: join to ecom_orders/ecom_payments/ecom_product_wise_sales using date and/or order_channel when those columns exist in detail tables; join to ecom_sales_monthly / ecom_sales_yearly to validate rollups; join to products or product_sales for per-product decomposition when aggregating source-level data. Useful join keys: date (after casting to date), order_channel (normalize values to match other tables' channel identifiers). The Unnamed columns may represent source metadata or aggregated organizational totals that should be validated before joining.

ecom_sales_monthly.purpose: Monthly e-commerce sales rollup by order channel. This table stores pre-aggregated monthly metrics (orders, customers, quantities and monetary amounts) per order channel and month for the year 2024. It is intended for reporting, trend analysis and quick KPI lookups without joining raw transaction tables.
ecom_sales_monthly.key_characteristics: 36 rows (small table) — appears to be 12 months × 3 order channels. All numeric metrics and date parts are stored as nvarchar (strings). year and created_at have only a single distinct value each (year = '2024', created_at is a single ingestion timestamp). month has 12 distinct values representing months 1..12. order_channel has low cardinality (3 values). Monetary and quantity columns have full precision decimals encoded as strings. There are no primary or foreign keys defined.
ecom_sales_monthly.analysis_capabilities: Standalone: monthly trend analysis, channel comparison, month-over-month growth, simple KPI computation (ARPU = gross_sales / total_customers), contribution analysis (channel share of net_sales), seasonality checks in 2024. Because values are pre-aggregated, the table supports fast rollups and comparisons but cannot be used to drill down to order-level detail without joining to orders or order_lines tables.
business_questions: How did net sales trend month-over-month across the year 2024 for each order channel?
Which order channel contributed the most to gross_sales and net_sales each month and overall in 2024?
What is the month with highest total_qty_kg and how does that relate to total_orders across channels?
What are month-over-month percentage changes in total_orders and total_customers by channel?
What is the average order value (gross_sales / total_orders) and average customer spend (gross_sales / total_customers) by channel per month?
ecom_sales_monthly.relationships: No enforced foreign keys. Logical join keys for temporal consolidation: (year, month) can be joined to other monthly or yearly summary tables (ecom_sales_yearly, ecom_sales_daily aggregated to month) and to channel-level tables where order_channel exists. Joining to order-level tables (ecom_orders, ecom_order_lines) requires careful mapping since this table is aggregated: use year/month derived from order date and order_channel. Joins enable reconciliation (compare aggregated totals to raw transactions), enrich with product-level or location-level metrics, and cross-channel comparisons with other sales sources (b2b_, pos_ tables).

ecom_sales_yearly.purpose: Aggregated yearly e-commerce sales metrics broken down by order channel. This table appears to store pre-aggregated KPIs (orders, customers, quantities, and financial metrics) for each year × order_channel combination and is intended for reporting and trend analysis at a high level.
ecom_sales_yearly.key_characteristics: Very small table (3 rows). All columns stored as nvarchar despite representing numeric/date semantics. Low cardinality: year has 1 distinct value (2024), order_channel has 3 values. Values appear to be yearly totals per channel (three rows likely for three channels). created_at is identical for all rows. Numeric-looking strings contain many decimal places. No primary key or foreign keys. Because values are already aggregated, the table is optimized for read/report queries but not for row-level joins.
ecom_sales_yearly.analysis_capabilities: Direct reporting of yearly totals and channel comparisons (e.g., gross/net sales by channel, orders/customers by channel, average order size approximations). Calculation of derived KPIs (ARPU, average order value, discount rate, tax share, delivery fee share). Time-based analysis is limited here (only one year present) but can be joined to monthly/daily tables for drill-down. Useful for dashboards showing channel mix and high-level financial summaries.
business_questions: How do gross sales, net sales and total orders compare across order channels for the year?
What is the average order value (gross and net) per channel for the year?
What percent of gross sales is taken up by discounts, tax and delivery fees per channel?
Which channel delivered the highest quantity (kg) sold and how does that compare to revenue share?
Has customer count or orders concentration shifted between channels for this year (channel mix)?
ecom_sales_yearly.relationships: No declared keys. Natural join keys for analytical joins are year and order_channel (to join with ecom_sales_monthly, ecom_sales_daily, ecom_product_wise_sales, b2b/ecom product sales tables). Because year and numeric columns are strings, joins and aggregations must cast to appropriate types. This table complements lower-granularity tables (daily/monthly/product) for roll-up/validation and enables fast channel-level KPIs in dashboards.

ecom_search_keywords.purpose: This table captures search keyword records for the e-commerce site: each row links a search keyword to measured metrics (search_volume, conversion_rate), the top-selling SKU for that keyword, and a created_at timestamp. It is primarily an analytics/marketing table used to evaluate keyword performance, discover product demand signals from search, and prioritize SEO/merchandising.
ecom_search_keywords.key_characteristics: 100 rows, no declared primary key or foreign keys. keyword_id appears unique (distinct=100) and is a candidate surrogate key. keyword has low cardinality (9 distinct values) with heavy repetition (top values: potato 17%, apple 14%, tomato 12%, organic veg 12%, cheap veg 11%). search_volume is stored as nvarchar but semantics are integer counts (100 distinct values). conversion_rate is stored as nvarchar but represents numeric percentages/ratios (97 distinct values). top_sku is low-cardinality (7 distinct values) and likely corresponds to product SKUs in product tables. created_at stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' format and appears to be unique per row (distinct=100). Nullability: all columns are nullable in DDL — queries must defensively handle NULLs.
ecom_search_keywords.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: aggregate search_volume and conversion_rate by keyword or top_sku; rank keywords by search volume or conversion; compute averages/medians of conversion_rate for keywords; identify top SKUs associated with particular search terms; perform simple time-based analyses if created_at is cast to a datetime (trends over time). You cannot compute revenue or order-level attribution without joining order/sales tables.
business_questions: Which keywords generate the highest total search volume and which of those have the highest conversion rates?
Which SKUs are most frequently the top result for search terms and how do their associated conversion rates compare?
How has search volume and conversion rate for a given keyword changed over time (trend) based on created_at timestamps?
Which low-volume keywords have unusually high conversion rates (opportunity keywords)?
What are the top-performing keywords to prioritize for SEO or paid bids (high search volume and high conversion)?
ecom_search_keywords.relationships: No declared FKs, but useful joins: top_sku -> products.product_sku or product_variants.sku to pull product metadata (name, category, price), enabling SKU-level revenue, inventory, and category analyses. created_at (after casting to datetime) can be joined to order/event timestamps (ecom_orders.created_at, ecom_product_events, ecom_browsing_history) to correlate search events with orders or browsing. keyword can be used (with fuzzy matching) to join or correlate with browsing_history.search_term or product search logs if available. These joins enable attribution (search -> purchases), SKU conversion uplift analyses, and enrichment with product categories and price to explain conversion_rate patterns.
ecom_search_keywords.recommended_improvements_for_nl2sql_opti: Convert semantic columns to proper types (search_volume INT, conversion_rate FLOAT/DECIMAL, created_at DATETIME). Declare keyword_id as primary key if it is unique and immutable, or add a surrogate id. Add an index on keyword and on top_sku for frequent GROUP BY/WHERE. Normalize top_sku into a foreign key to products. Standardize keyword normalization (trim, lower) for reliable filtering and fuzzy matches. These changes reduce casting overhead in NL2SQL queries and improve planner choice for aggregations and joins.

ecom_ticket.purpose: Stores customer support tickets raised via the e-commerce platform; tracks ticket metadata (who, when, what, status, priority) and links to customers and orders. Used for support operations, reporting and root-cause analysis.
ecom_ticket.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). All columns are nvarchar (strings). ticket_id appears unique across rows but no PK enforced. created_at/updated_at stored as text in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format (distinct = 100). subject, description, status and priority are low-cardinality (4,4,4,3 distinct respectively) suitable for GROUP BY and fast aggregation. customer_id and order_id have moderate cardinality (~60 and ~53 distinct). assigned_to has very low cardinality (3). No declared foreign keys; other schema tables (ecom_customers, ecom_orders, ecom_ticket_messages) are natural join targets.
ecom_ticket.analysis_capabilities: Alone it can answer operational/support KPIs: ticket counts by status/priority/subject/agent, ticket volumes over time, per-customer ticket frequency, basic SLA/resolution time (if using created_at and updated_at), and identify common complaint types. Cannot enrich with customer attributes or order details without joins to other tables.
business_questions: How many tickets are open, in-progress, resolved or closed over a given period?
What is the distribution of ticket priority (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW) and which agents handle the most high-priority tickets?
What is the average time to resolution for resolved tickets (updated_at - created_at) and how does it vary by assigned_to or priority?
Which customers or orders generate the most tickets in a time window?
What are the top ticket subjects/reasons (e.g., Delivery Delay, Refund Query) and their proportions?
ecom_ticket.relationships: Logical joins: customer_id -> ecom_customers.customer_id for customer attributes; order_id -> ecom_orders.order_id for order details and order timestamps; ticket_id -> ecom_ticket_messages.ticket_id to get message-level threads. No FK constraints in DDL, so joins should use equality on the string fields and may require trimming/casing or type alignment. Joining enables customer segmentation, linking ticket outcomes to order data, and detailed message-level analytics.

ecom_ticket_messages.purpose: Holds individual messages (conversation rows) for e-commerce support tickets. Each row represents one message in a ticket thread, capturing who sent it, when it was sent, the message text, and any attachment reference. It is used to analyze ticket conversation patterns, response times, and message content.
ecom_ticket_messages.key_characteristics: Small table (293 rows). High cardinality on ticket_message_id and timestamps. ticket_id has moderate cardinality (~100 distinct), indicating multiple messages per ticket. sender is binary/categorical (support, customer). message_text has very low cardinality (4 common templates). attachment_url is always null in current snapshot. message_ts and created_at are stored as nvarchar strings in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern and should be cast to datetime for temporal operations.
ecom_ticket_messages.analysis_capabilities: Can compute counts of messages by ticket/sender, measure response and resolution timelines (when combined with ticket lifecycle data), extract most common message texts, identify tickets with attachments (none present here yet), and reconstruct conversation order per ticket. Alone it cannot tell ticket status, owner, or customer identity without joining ecom_ticket or related tables.
business_questions: How many messages are exchanged per ticket (average, median, distribution)?
What is the distribution of senders (support vs customer) across messages and per ticket?
What are the most frequent message texts/templates and their proportions?
