What is the average time between consecutive messages on a ticket (response time)?
Which tickets have the most messages (longest conversations)?
ecom_ticket_messages.relationships: ticket_id is the natural foreign key to join to ecom_ticket (exists in schema). Joining to ecom_ticket enables linking messages to ticket status, customer_id, assigned agent, and resolution timestamps. From ecom_ticket you can join to ecom_customers, ecom_orders, and other tables to combine conversation data with customer profiles, order context, and operational KPIs.

ecom_vendors.purpose: Master vendor reference for the e-commerce system: stores vendor identifiers, contact and tax details, categorization, location and lifecycle status. Used as a lookup for payments, orders, contracts and reporting about vendors.
ecom_vendors.key_characteristics: Small reference table (50 rows). vendor_id appears unique (distinct=50). Low cardinality in vendor_category (5 categories) and status (2 values). city has moderate cardinality (9). created_at is constant across all rows (single distinct value) — likely an ingestion timestamp. Several columns contain PII (phone, email, gst_no, pan_no). Many textual columns are stored as nvarchar and may require normalization/casting for effective filtering and joins.
ecom_vendors.analysis_capabilities: Can be used alone for descriptive and categorical analyses: vendor counts, distribution by category/city/status, contact lists, PII audits, and identifying vendor metadata completeness. Enables enrichment of transactional tables (orders, invoices, payments, shipments) when joined on vendor identifier to compute vendor-level KPIs (sales, returns, invoices, payments).
business_questions: How many vendors do we have overall and by status (ACTIVE vs INACTIVE)?
What is the distribution of vendors by vendor_category and by city?
Which vendors lack valid tax identifiers (missing gst_no or pan_no)?
Provide contact details for all ACTIVE vendors in a given city or category.
Which vendor partners should be excluded from procurement due to INACTIVE status?
ecom_vendors.relationships: No declared foreign keys, but logical joins exist: vendor_id can join to transactional tables (ecom_orders, ecom_payments, ecom_returns, b2b_invoices, pos_vendors, b2b_vendor_partners) to attribute transactions to vendors. Joins enable vendor-level revenue/performance analysis, reconciliation of payments/invoices, and operational lookups (shipments, returns). Because vendor_id is the de-facto key, it should be used as the primary join key.

ecom_wishlist.purpose: Customer wishlist items captured by the e-commerce system. Each row represents a single item that a customer has added to their wishlist, along with when it was added and whether it was flagged as a priority. The table supports personalization, product interest analytics, and cross-referencing with orders and product catalogs.
ecom_wishlist.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows). wishlist_id appears unique per row (100 distinct). customer_id has moderate cardinality (65 distinct), meaning many customers have 0–few wishlist items in this sample. sku has very low cardinality (7 distinct) with skewed distribution (top SKUs: TOM001 20%, POT002 16%, CAR004 15%, ONI003 13%, APP009 12%). added_at is stored as text (nvarchar) and is distinct for all rows (100 distinct timestamps) — indicates per-row timestamps but requires parsing to datetime for time-based queries. priority_flag stored as nvarchar with two values '0' and '1' (54% '0', 46% '1'). notes is fully NULL (100%). No primary or foreign keys defined in DDL.
ecom_wishlist.analysis_capabilities: Alone, the table can be used to: count wishlist items, identify most wished SKUs, measure number of unique customers with wishlist entries, compute distribution of priority vs non-priority items, and perform recency analyses after casting added_at to datetime. When joined to customers and product tables it enables conversion-rate, retention, product interest vs purchase analysis, and personalized outreach.
business_questions: Which products (SKUs) are most commonly added to wishlists?
How many unique customers have added items to their wishlist in a given time range?
What proportion of wishlist items are marked as priority?
Which customers have the most high-priority wishlist items (potential targets for campaigns)?
How long after adding an item to wishlist do customers typically purchase it? (requires join to orders/order_items)
ecom_wishlist.relationships: Potential joins: customer_id -> ecom_customers (customer metadata: name, email, segment). sku -> products/product_variants/product_sales (product metadata, availability, price, inventory). Joining to ecom_orders or order_items allows measuring wishlist -> purchase conversion and time-to-purchase. These relationships enable cohort analyses (customers who wished then purchased), product demand vs supply analysis (wishlists vs inventory), and targeted marketing (priority items with no purchase). Because this table currently lacks declared foreign key constraints, joins should be done on matching string keys and validated for formatting differences.

inventory_items.purpose: Raw inventory items ledger capturing received batches of items with stock, batch, supplier and timestamp/cost metadata. Likely used as ingestion/raw source for inventory, expiry/ageing and costing analyses within the wider retail/fulfillment schema.
inventory_items.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as NVARCHAR (text). Row count small (100) but values show real-world patterns: item_id is unique per row (100 distinct), sku and name have low cardinality (~10), supplier is very low-cardinality (3). Many numeric and datetime semantic columns are stored as text (total_stock_units, cost_per_unit, received_at, created_at, updated_at, expiry_date, mfg_date). Batch-level data present (batch_id distinct ~99). Dates use ISO-like formats (yyyy-mm-dd or yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss) — consistent but stored as strings. Cost has high cardinality and decimal precision encoded as text. There are no declared keys or foreign keys.
inventory_items.analysis_capabilities: Using this table alone you can compute current stock by item/batch/supplier, identify near-expiry or expired batches, compute basic cost summaries (avg/min/max cost per unit) after converting types, inspect stock receipts over time, and detect supplier concentration. You can also trace batch-level inventory movements if batches are unique.
business_questions: Which SKUs or item names have the largest total_stock_units in inventory?
What batches are expiring within the next N days (near-expiry) and what is their total stock?
Which supplier supplies the most stock (units) and what is their average cost_per_unit?
What was the average cost_per_unit over time for a given SKU or supplier?
When were specific batches received and how old is inventory by batch (ageing since received_at or mfg_date)?
inventory_items.relationships: No explicit foreign keys. Practical joins: sku or name can join to products, product_variants, product_inventory, or order_items to enrich with product master data (category, brand, product_id). batch_id can be linked to product_inventory or QA/inspection tables if they track batches. supplier can join vendor/partners or b2b_vendor_partners/ecom_vendors/pos_vendors to get supplier metadata. created_at/updated_at can be used to join/change-detect with event or audit tables. Because keys are not enforced, NL2SQL should assume joins on sku or batch_id are best-effort and may require normalization.

pos_daily_sales_summary.purpose: Daily aggregated point-of-sale summary records per store/date. It captures precomputed daily metrics (transactions, sales, tax, refunds, avg transaction value) and metadata for each summary row. Intended for reporting, trend analysis, and high-level KPI checks rather than transaction-level queries.
pos_daily_sales_summary.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows sample) with summary granularity. No primary key or foreign keys declared. Many numeric measures are stored as nvarchar (strings) and need casting for arithmetic. summary_id is unique per row (distinct=50) but not declared PK. store_id has low cardinality (4 distinct) and date has moderate cardinality (24 distinct). created_at contains timestamps (string). notes is constant ('Daily summary').
pos_daily_sales_summary.analysis_capabilities: Can produce per-store and per-date reports, time-series trends (sales, refunds, tax), aggregated KPIs (sum, average, growth rates), refund and tax ratios, per-transaction averages and anomaly detection of summaries (by recomputing metrics from base tables if available). Good for quick dashboards and joins to store metadata for enrichment.
business_questions: What were daily total sales, tax and refunds by store for the last N days?
Which store had the highest average transaction value or highest total transactions on a given day?
How have total sales and refunds trended week-over-week or month-over-month per store?
What is the refund rate (refunds / total_sales) or tax rate (total_tax / total_sales) per store or overall?
Are there outlier dates (very high/low sales or avg_txn_value) that need investigation?
pos_daily_sales_summary.relationships: Logical joins: store_id can join to pos_stores (store metadata), pos_sales_daily/pos_sales_monthly for comparison/rollups, pos_transactions/pos_transaction_lines for drilldown to transactions, and ecom/b2b daily/monthly tables for cross-channel comparisons. date can be joined to calendar or snapshot tables for time dimension. summary_id is unique but not used as a foreign key. Because types are string, joins on date may require consistent formatting or casting when other tables use DATE/TIMESTAMP types.

pos_inventory_adjustments.purpose: Records individual point-of-sale inventory adjustments (manual corrections, shrinkage, damage) at store + SKU level. Serves as an audit trail for inventory changes and a source for analysis of inventory accuracy, reasons for adjustments, and operator behavior.
pos_inventory_adjustments.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows). Many columns stored as text (nvarchar) although several represent numeric or timestamp data. adjustment_id and adjustment_ts/created_at are high-cardinality (distinct ~50). pos_sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) with a few SKUs dominating; store_id has 4 distinct values. reason, adjusted_by, approval_by are low-cardinality categorical fields. inventory_before and inventory_after are almost unique per row (48/50, 50/50 distinct) stored as strings. qty_change has ~19 distinct values, includes negative and positive integers. No declared primary or foreign keys.
pos_inventory_adjustments.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can: compute aggregated qty changes by SKU/store/reason/time period; detect and quantify shrinkage and damage; profile which operators adjust or approve the most adjustments; validate that inventory_after == inventory_before + qty_change; timeline analyses of adjustments; identify anomalous adjustments (large qty_change, frequent adjustments for same SKU/store). It cannot enrich with product metadata (names, categories) or store metadata (region) without joins to other tables.
business_questions: Which SKUs and stores contribute most to inventory shrinkage (sum of negative qty_change grouped by SKU/store)?
Which users make the most adjustments and which users approve the most adjustments (counts and avg qty_change)?
Are there adjustments where inventory_after does not equal inventory_before + qty_change (potential data quality or process issues)?
What are the top reasons for adjustments and how much quantity is attributed to each reason over a period?
How do adjustment volumes and magnitudes evolve over time (daily/weekly trends) for specific stores or SKUs?
pos_inventory_adjustments.relationships: No declared foreign keys but natural joins are possible: pos_sku can join to pos_products, products, product_variants or product_inventory on SKU fields to get product metadata and category. store_id can join to pos_stores for store name/region. adjusted_by and approval_by likely map to pos_users (or similar) for operator metadata. Joining enables enriched reports (e.g., shrinkage by product category, adjustments per region), root-cause investigations, and cross-checks with sales/returns/inventory snapshots tables (product_inventory, pos_transactions) to reconcile stock changes.

pos_loyalty_redemptions.purpose: Records individual point-redemption events at point-of-sale: which customer redeemed loyalty points, which POS transaction they applied it to, how many points were used, the monetary discount granted, when and where the redemption occurred, who created the record and the approval reference. Serves as the ledger of loyalty redemptions for POS analytics, audits and reconciliation with POS transactions and customer profiles.
pos_loyalty_redemptions.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows). redemption_id appears unique (50 distinct) but table has no declared PK. Several columns are stored as nvarchar though representing numeric or datetime values (loyalty_points_used, discount_amount, redeemed_at) — this will impact filtering, sorting and aggregation unless cast. store_id and created_by are low-cardinality (store_id: 4 values, created_by: 3 values). customer_id and pos_txn_id show moderate cardinality (39 and 40 distinct). approval_id nearly unique (49 distinct). notes is constant ('Loyalty redemption'). All columns nullable in DDL. Date strings follow ISO-like pattern 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' in sample rows.
pos_loyalty_redemptions.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports: total and average discount granted by redemptions, aggregate loyalty points consumed, redemptions per customer, per-store and per-creator, time-based trends if redeemed_at is converted to datetime, detection of anomalous redemptions (e.g., unusually high discounts or points). It can also be joined to POS transactions to reconcile transaction amounts and to customer tables to enrich with demographics or segmentation. Limited for product-level analysis unless joined to POS transaction lines.
business_questions: How much discount (total and average) has been given through loyalty redemptions over a period?
Which customers redeem loyalty points most frequently and which generate the largest total discount?
How many redemptions and what value of discounts occur per store or per creator (staff member)?
What is the time distribution of redemptions (hourly/daily/monthly trends)?
Are there anomalous redemptions (very high discount_amount or loyalty_points_used) that require investigation?
pos_loyalty_redemptions.relationships: Logical joins: pos_txn_id -> pos_transactions (reconcile with transaction totals, payment methods); customer_id -> pos_customers / ecom_customers / b2b_customers (enrich with customer attributes and segmentation); store_id -> pos_stores (store metadata and region); created_by -> pos_users or pos_terminals (staff or terminal analytics); approval_id -> approvals/authorization table if present (audit trails). These joins enable end-to-end analyses: impact of redemptions on sales, customer lifetime value adjusted for loyalty discounts, store-level cost of loyalty programs, and audit of approvals.

pos_product_wise_sales.purpose: Pos-level product-wise daily sales snapshot. Each row records sales metrics (quantity, gross, discount, tax, net) for a product on a date. It appears to be an ingest/raw staging table holding point-of-sale aggregated metrics before normalization or joining to masters.
pos_product_wise_sales.key_characteristics: Small table (~21.9k rows). No primary key. Date is stored as nvarchar (max len 10 actual values) with high cardinality (731 distinct). product_id is low-cardinality (10 distinct values dominate). All numeric measures (quantity and monetary fields) are stored as nvarchar strings (high cardinality ~equal to row count), so values need cleaning/casting for numeric operations. No explicit foreign keys, but product_id maps to product master tables elsewhere in schema. Values likely in ISO date format (YYYY-MM-DD) but stored as text.
pos_product_wise_sales.analysis_capabilities: Time-series and product-level sales analysis at daily grain: trend analysis (daily/weekly/monthly), top/bottom products by volume or revenue, discount and tax impact analysis, compute derived KPIs (average price per kg, discount rate, tax rate), and reconciliation checks (e.g., gross - discount + tax ≈ net). Because fields are strings, pre-processing or casting is required before numeric aggregations.
business_questions: What is the daily net sales and quantity sold per product over a given date range?
Which products contribute most to gross sales, net sales, or volume in a period?
How much discount and tax are being applied per product and how do they change over time?
What are weekly/monthly trends in quantity (kg) and revenue for each product?
Are there discrepancies between gross, discounts, tax and net (data quality check) for given dates/products?
pos_product_wise_sales.relationships: product_id can join to product master tables (products, pos_products, b2b_products, ecom_vendors, product_variants) enabling enrichment with product name, category, cost, and vendor. Joining date to order/transaction tables (pos_transactions, pos_transaction_lines, pos_sales_daily) and order-level tables (b2b_order_lines, ecom_orders) enables attribution to stores, customers, orders, and terminals. These joins enable drill-down (order-level), store-level aggregation, and cross-channel comparisons. Because the table stores string measures, joining before casting is fine but numeric aggregations require casting/cleaning either in the query or via a materialized/cleaned view.

pos_products.purpose: Catalog of point-of-sale products for a specific store ingestion (raw POS product snapshot). It contains SKU identifiers, PLU codes, display name, unit-of-measure, POS price, tax and stock metrics and a last-updated timestamp. Likely used as the canonical POS product feed for POS reporting, inventory checks and price lookups.
pos_products.key_characteristics: Small snapshot table (13 rows). Many fields stored as nvarchar (text) although they represent numeric and datetime values. Several columns contain a single repeated value (store_id, tax_rate, active_flag), indicating a single-store snapshot and homogeneous tax/active status for these rows. Several columns contain nulls (~23% for most meaningful columns). Distinct counts: ~10 distinct product SKUs (pos_sku and sku). PLU values appear as floats represented as text (e.g., '4011.0'). UoM distribution is small (primarily 'kg'). There are three empty/unused columns (Unnamed: 12, Unnamed: 13 fully null; Unnamed: 14 almost entirely null/space).
pos_products.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: produce price lists, inventory on-hand summaries, identify items below reorder point, get last updated timestamps for POS product metadata, group/catalog products by UoM or PLU, and generate simple stock alerts. You cannot compute sales, margins or turnover without joining to transaction or cost tables. Because values are strings, numeric/date conversions are required prior to numeric aggregation.
business_questions: What items are currently below their reorder point and need restocking?
What is the current POS price list for the store and how many items are in each UoM?
When was each product last updated in the POS feed?
How many active products are listed in this POS snapshot and which ones have null or missing fields?
What is the on-hand stock quantity per SKU (for inventory check) and which SKUs are low?
pos_products.relationships: Natural join keys for integration: sku and pos_sku (product-level joins), plucode (PLU-based joins), store_id (store-level joins). Typical joins: join to pos_transaction_lines / pos_transactions or pos_product_wise_sales using pos_sku or sku for sales aggregation; join to pos_stores on store_id for store metadata; join to product_pricing or products / product_variants / product_inventory using sku/plucode to enrich with cost, master product metadata and historical pricing. Because this table is raw and textual, join predicates should use cleaned/cast columns (trimmed and normalized sku/plucode).

pos_products_sales.purpose: Raw point-of-sale (POS) product-level sales snapshot. Each row records sales-related dollar amounts for a product SKU. This table appears to be an ingestion or staging table (zuna_seed_raw) holding per-SKU financial metrics (gross, discounts, tax, net) likely exported from POS or ETL pipeline before normalization.
pos_products_sales.key_characteristics: Very small sample (10 rows) in the snapshot provided. All numeric monetary columns are stored as nvarchar strings with up to 18 characters visible and many decimal digits — indicating numeric data was not typed correctly. 'sku' is nvarchar(22) with 10 distinct values in the sample (high cardinality relative to row count). Column names include spaces and mixed casing. There are no keys or timestamps on the table; no foreign keys defined. Values show high precision decimals, likely currency values. Because values are strings, numeric operations require casting/cleaning. Nullability: all columns are nullable.
pos_products_sales.analysis_capabilities: Can perform SKU-level financial summaries (sum, avg, min, max) after type conversion and basic data cleaning. Useful for calculating gross and net totals, discount totals, and tax totals per SKU or across the table. Cannot perform temporal analysis, store/terminal-level analysis, or customer-level segmentation because there are no date, store, or customer columns. Limited ability to join unless SKU maps reliably to product tables elsewhere in schema.
business_questions: What is the total gross sales, total discounts, total tax, and total net sales for each SKU?
Which SKUs have the highest gross sales and which have the highest discount amount?
What is the effective discount or tax rate by SKU (discount/gross, tax/gross) in the provided snapshot?
Are there any SKUs where net sales exceed gross sales (indicating data quality issues)?
How do per-SKU gross, discount, tax, and net sales rank (top-N) across the dataset?
pos_products_sales.relationships: The natural join key is sku which can link this table to product master tables (pos_products, products, product_variants) and to other sales or aggregation tables (pos_product_wise_sales, pos_transactions, product_sales). Joining by sku enables enrichment with product name, category, cost, vendor, and inventory attributes. Because this is a raw staging table, joins are commonly used after cleaning/casting to associate financial values with product metadata and with other sales entities for temporal or organizational context.

pos_returns.purpose: Records of point-of-sale return events: one row per returned SKU line capturing which POS transaction it came from, when the return occurred and was recorded, quantity, reason, refund amount, who processed it and simple flags (approval required, restock). Used to analyze return volume, refund cost and operational handling of returns at POS.
pos_returns.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows). All columns are stored as nvarchar (strings) even when they represent numeric or datetime values. pos_return_id appears unique across rows (50 distinct). pos_txn_id has lower cardinality (34 distinct) indicating multiple returns can map to the same transaction. sku has low cardinality (10) and skew (top SKUs account for large share). reason is highly categorical (3 values). processed_by is very low cardinality (3). approval_required_flag and restock_flag are binary string flags ('0'/'1') roughly balanced. return_ts and created_at values appear ISO-like (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS) but stored as strings and must be cast for time-based queries. refund_amount contains numeric values but stored as strings with float formatting.
pos_returns.analysis_capabilities: By itself this table can: count returns, sum refund amounts and returned quantities, compute aggregates by sku/reason/processor/time window, measure approval and restock rates, compute average refund per return, distribution of reasons, and process-time metrics (after casting timestamps). It cannot on its own compute return rates relative to sales or link returns to customer records without joins.
business_questions: What is the total number of returns and total refund amount over a date range?
Which SKUs generate the most return refunds and quantities?
What are the most common return reasons and their share of total refunds?
Which POS users/processors handle the most returns and what are their average refund amounts?
What percentage of returns required approval or were restocked, and how has that changed over time?
pos_returns.relationships: Natural join keys: pos_txn_id -> pos_transactions (or ecom_orders/pos_transactions depending on schema), sku -> product tables (pos_products, products, product_variants), processed_by -> pos_users (POS operators) or employee/user tables. Joining to transactions/orders lets you compute return rate vs sales and link refunds to customers/invoices. Joining to inventory/product tables enables SKU-level return rates and cost-of-goods analyses. There is also a 'returns' table in schema that may be a canonical returns store; map pos_return_id or pos_txn_id to that table where appropriate.

pos_sales_daily.purpose: Daily point-of-sale (POS) sales metrics at the store level. Each row appears to represent aggregated daily metrics for a specific store (bills, quantity in kg, sales, discounts, tax, net sales) as ingested from a raw source.
pos_sales_daily.key_characteristics: Small table (~2.2k rows). The date column is stored as text with ~731 distinct dates (high-cardinality time dimension). store_id has very low cardinality (3 stores). Most financial/quantity columns are stored as nvarchar but are numeric-looking and high-cardinality (almost unique per row) -> indicates raw ingestion without typing. created_at is constant for all rows (single ingestion timestamp). Three Unnamed columns are fully or mostly null; two Unnamed columns have a tiny number of non-null values (likely metadata or mis-parsed totals). No primary/foreign keys defined.
pos_sales_daily.analysis_capabilities: Time-series and store-level aggregations: daily/weekly/monthly sales, discounts, tax and quantities; store comparisons and trend analysis; KPI calculations such as average bill value (gross or net / total_bills), discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), tax rate, and sales per kg. Can also be used to validate or reconcile POS data with other sales tables if joined on store_id and date after type normalization.
business_questions: How have net sales trended day-over-day or month-over-month for each store?
Which store has the highest average ticket (gross or net) and how does that change over time?
What is the total gross, discount and tax per period (day/week/month) and discount as a percentage of gross?
How many bills and total quantity (kg) are sold per store per period (capacity / throughput metrics)?
Are there anomalies (days with unusually high discounts, tax, or sales per kg) that require investigation?
pos_sales_daily.relationships: No explicit foreign keys. Logical joins: join to pos_stores (store metadata) on store_id; join to other sales/transactions tables (pos_transactions, pos_transaction_lines, pos_product_wise_sales, pos_daily_sales_summary) on date and store_id to reconcile or drill down into transactions and product-level sales. Joining to calendar/date dimension on parsed date enables richer time bucketing and fiscal period calculations. Because there is no enforced PK, use (date, store_id) as a natural composite key after normalization; consider adding indexes on date and store_id for NL2SQL performance.

pos_sales_monthly.purpose: Monthly Point-of-Sale (POS) sales aggregates per store. This table stores one row per store per month summarizing bills, quantities, gross/discount/tax/net amounts and an ingestion timestamp. It is intended as an analytical summary layer for monthly reporting and comparisons.
pos_sales_monthly.key_characteristics: Very small table (36 rows). All numeric measures are stored as nvarchar (strings). year and created_at are non-varying in this snapshot (year = '2024', created_at constant). month has 12 distinct string values representing months. store_id has low cardinality (3 stores). Measures (total_bills, total_qty_kg, gross_sales, discount_amount, tax_amount, net_sales) have full distinctness across rows (36 distinct each) and contain decimal-like strings. No primary key or foreign keys defined.
pos_sales_monthly.analysis_capabilities: Can compute month-over-month trends, store-by-store comparisons, contribution of discounts and taxes to gross/net sales, average bill size (net_sales/total_bills), and quantity-per-bill metrics. Because measures are textual, casting to numeric is required before aggregations. Can be joined to canonical store metadata (pos_stores) using store_id for richer analysis.
business_questions: How do gross, discount, tax and net sales trend across months for each store in 2024?
Which store has the highest average bill (net_sales / total_bills) in a given month or across the year?
What is the monthly total quantity sold (kg) and how does it correlate with net sales per store?
How much of gross sales is given away as discounts and how does discount percent vary month-to-month?
Which months show the largest month-over-month net sales growth or decline for each store?
pos_sales_monthly.relationships: No explicit foreign keys, but logical joins: store_id can join to pos_stores (to get store name, region, manager), to other POS/product sales tables (pos_sales_monthly/daily/pos_product_wise_sales) or ecom/ b2b sales tables to compare channels. Joining enables cross-channel aggregation, enrichment with store attributes, and drill-down from monthly summaries to daily or transaction-level data.

pos_sales_yearly.purpose: Annual aggregated POS sales metrics per store. This table holds yearly rollups (billed transactions, quantities, gross/discount/tax/net values) for physical stores and is intended for reporting and high-level trend analysis.
pos_sales_yearly.key_characteristics: Very small cardinality in the sample (3 rows). All columns are stored as nvarchar despite representing numeric or date data. 'year' has low cardinality (single value '2024' in sample). store_id has a few distinct values and follows a STORE_<LOC>_<NN> naming pattern. Monetary and quantity fields contain decimal-like strings with varying max lengths. created_at appears to be an ingestion timestamp and is constant in the sample. No declared primary/foreign keys. Nullability present for all columns.
pos_sales_yearly.analysis_capabilities: Can produce store-level annual summaries (counts, sums, averages) and compare stores within the year. Allows calculation of derived KPIs like average bill value (gross_sales/total_bills), discount rate (discount_amount/gross_sales), tax rate, net margin, and sales per kg. Because fields are strings, cast-to-numeric is required before numeric aggregations. Can be joined to store master and lower-granularity sales tables to validate rollups.
business_questions: Which stores had the highest net sales for the year?
What is the average bill value per store for the year?
Which store had the largest total quantity sold (kg) this year?
What is the overall discount rate and tax rate across stores for the year?
Are these yearly rollups consistent with monthly/daily POS rollups (reconciliation)?
pos_sales_yearly.relationships: Logical relationship: store_id should join to pos_stores(store_id) or other store/master tables to pull store attributes (region, city). This table can be compared/joined to pos_sales_monthly/pos_sales_daily to validate rollups and drill down. It can also join to pos_product_wise_sales or product_sales for product-level attribution if a mapping of store->product sales exists. No declared foreign keys exist in DDL; joins should be performed by matching store_id strings and casting year to numeric or matching to date-derived fields in other tables.

pos_shift_closures.purpose: POS shift closure records: one row per closed shift capturing terminal, user (cashier), start/end timestamps, monetary totals (sales, cash/card), refunds and cash reconciliation. Used for end-of-shift reconciliation, cashier performance and terminal utilization reporting.
pos_shift_closures.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows). No declared primary key or foreign keys. Many columns stored as nvarchar (timestamps and numeric monetary values stored as strings). Distinct counts: shift_id distinct=50, terminal_id distinct=4 (skewed: TERM-001-4 ~38%), user_id distinct=3 (skewed across three users). shift_start_ts and shift_end_ts look like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' strings. Monetary columns contain decimal values as strings with identical distinct counts as rows. discrepancies_note is 100% NULL. Potential for duplicate or missing uniqueness guarantees since no PK.
pos_shift_closures.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: compute shift counts by terminal or user, aggregate sales/cash/card/refund totals by user/terminal/date range, compute reconciliation metrics (expected_cash vs counted_cash) and discrepancy amounts, compute shift durations if timestamps are parsed, identify refund patterns per shift, and detect cash-counting issues. Time-based analyses require casting timestamp strings to datetime.
business_questions: Which cashier (user_id) generated the highest total sales over a period of time?
Which terminal has the most frequent cash-count discrepancies (expected_cash != counted_cash)?
What is the average shift duration per terminal and per user?
How much cash and card were collected across all shifts in a given date range?
Which shifts had refunds above a set threshold and who operated them?
pos_shift_closures.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Logical joins: terminal_id can join to pos_terminals or pos_terminals metadata; user_id can join to pos_users or employee/cashier tables; shift_id can be used to join with shift-specific logs if other tables (pos_transactions or pos_transaction_lines) store shift_id. Joining enables drilldown from shift totals to individual transactions, reconciliation with bank/card settlement tables, and linking to store/terminal metadata for operational reporting.

pos_stores.purpose: A master/reference table of point-of-sale (POS) store locations and their last / first connectivity timestamps. It is used to identify stores by store_id and provide basic geographic (city/region) and operational (first_active_date, last_online) metadata for POS-related analyses.
pos_stores.key_characteristics: Very small table (4 rows) with no declared primary key or foreign keys. All columns are nullable nvarchar with short max lengths. store_id has 4 distinct values (each unique here). city and region are low-cardinality and overlap one-to-one in this dataset. Date/time columns are stored as nvarchar (text) using a consistent 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern; they should be converted to native datetime for most analyses. Because of the small size, query performance is not a current concern, but schema design (types, keys, indexes) should be improved for integration with larger datasets.
pos_stores.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: enumerate stores, group by city/region, compute simple recency metrics (last_online recency if cast to datetime), compute tenure since first_active_date, and detect missing or outlier metadata. It cannot provide sales or transaction-level insights without joining to transaction/sales tables by store_id.
business_questions: Which stores are currently online or were last online within a given interval (e.g., last 7 days)?
How many stores exist per city or region and which stores belong to each city/region?
Which stores were activated within a given date range (first_active_date) and what's their tenure?
Which stores have not been online recently (stale offline stores) to target support or check hardware?)
Are there inconsistent or missing geographic entries (city vs region mismatches) that need cleaning?
pos_stores.relationships: This table is intended to join to POS and analytics tables on store_id (e.g., pos_transactions, pos_sales_daily, pos_shift_closures, pos_terminals, pos_inventory_adjustments). Those joins enable per-store sales aggregation, shift analysis, inventory checks, and operational monitoring. Currently no foreign-key constraints are declared, so joins must rely on convention (matching store_id strings).

pos_sync_log.purpose: Log of Point-of-Sale (POS) synchronization attempts from terminals to a central system. Each row records a sync event (attempt) including timestamps, counts of records exchanged, status, errors and retry attempts. Used to monitor reliability and throughput of POS syncs and to troubleshoot failed syncs.
pos_sync_log.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as nvarchar (strings). Row count is small in sample (50) but table is a log and likely to grow. sync_id and timestamp columns have high cardinality (distinct per row). terminal_id is low-cardinality and skewed (one terminal accounts for ~56% of rows). sync_status is binary-ish: SUCCESS (58%), FAILED (42%). records_sent/received appear numeric but are stored as strings and have many distinct values. error_message is largely NULL with a single non-null value 'Network error'. retry_count has very low cardinality (0-3) but stored as string. Timestamps (sync_ts, last_success_ts, created_at) are ISO-like strings but stored as nvarchar, so type conversions will be required for time-based analysis.
pos_sync_log.analysis_capabilities: Can compute success/failure rates, retry distributions, record transfer volumes per sync, per-terminal aggregates (throughput, failure counts), time-series trends (daily/weekly sync counts, failure spikes), identify top error messages and correlate retries with success, measure latency between created_at and sync_ts or between sync_ts and last_success_ts if meaningful. Can flag terminals with frequent failures or high retry counts. Limited for deeper joins unless matched to terminals or transactions tables using terminal_id or sync_id.
business_questions: What is the overall sync success rate and how does it change over time?
Which terminals have the highest failure rate or highest average retry_count?
What are the common error messages and how many syncs are impacted by each?
How many records are being sent/received per terminal per day and are there trends or anomalies?
When was the last successful sync per terminal and which terminals are overdue for a successful sync?
pos_sync_log.relationships: No explicit foreign keys. Practical joins: terminal_id can join to pos_terminals or pos_stores to enrich with terminal/store metadata; sync_id could potentially map to other tables that track batch or API syncs if those tables store the same id; created_at/sync_ts can join to event or audit tables to correlate with other system events. These joins enable root-cause analysis (e.g., map failures to store locations or terminal hardware) and cross-system correlation (e.g., match a failed POS sync to backend ingestion logs).

pos_terminals.purpose: Inventory of point-of-sale (POS) terminals: identity, location, assignment, software/version and recent connectivity metadata. It is a device registry used to manage terminals across stores and to relate terminals to transactions, shifts and operational users.
pos_terminals.key_characteristics: All columns stored as nvarchar (strings). Low distinct counts in sample for pos_software_version and status (single value each), small sample cardinality (8 rows) but likely larger in production. terminal_id and store_id are not declared keys but behave like identifiers; terminal_code and device_serial appear unique-per-terminal. Timestamps (last_online_ts, created_at) and coordinates (location_lat, location_long) are text and should be cast to datetime/decimal for accurate time and spatial queries. Many NULL-able columns; no declared PK/FK even though logical joins exist (store_id -> pos_stores, assigned_user_id -> pos_users).
pos_terminals.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports device inventory counts, distribution of terminals per store, monitoring recency (last online), software roll-out coverage, mapping terminals geographically, identifying assigned users, and computing onboarding timelines (created_at). It enables operational queries (which terminals are offline/active, who is responsible) and preparation for joins to sales/transactions tables for terminal-level sales analysis.
business_questions: How many terminals are deployed per store and what are their identifiers?
Which terminals have not checked in within a given time window (potentially offline)?
Which software versions are running on terminals and which stores still need upgrades?
What are the geolocations of our terminals (for mapping/region coverage)?
Which user (operator) is assigned to each terminal and when was each terminal provisioned?
pos_terminals.relationships: Although no foreign keys are declared, common joins are: store_id -> pos_stores(store_id) to get store metadata (name, region); assigned_user_id -> pos_users(user_id) to get operator details; terminal_id or terminal_code -> pos_transactions/pos_transaction_lines/pos_shift_closures to attribute transactions or shifts to a terminal; device_serial may be used to link to hardware inventory or vendor tables. These relationships enable linking device operational state to sales performance, shift activity, returns and support workflows.

pos_transaction_lines.purpose: Represents individual POS transaction lines (items) sold in point-of-sale transactions. Each row is one line item on a POS receipt containing SKU, quantity, pricing, tax, batch and creation timestamp. It is a detailed sales activity table used to compute revenue, tax, SKU-level sales and inventory/batch traceability.
pos_transaction_lines.key_characteristics: Small table (125 rows). Many numeric fields are stored as nvarchar (strings) and require casting for numeric/date operations. pos_line_id is unique per row (125 distinct), pos_txn_id has ~50 distinct values (multiple lines per transaction). sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) with clear top SKUs (BRN007, ONI003, TOM001, LIM008, BTR006). batch_id is almost unique (124 distinct) indicating tight batch tracking. discount_amt is constant '0' for all rows. variant_info and expiry_date are 100% null. created_at appears as a datetime string (50 distinct values). tax_amt and line_total contain decimal-like strings with floating representations (e.g., 4.7999999999999998).
pos_transaction_lines.analysis_capabilities: On its own this table can support SKU-level sales analytics (units sold, revenue, avg price), per-transaction breakdowns, tax aggregation, batch-level item traceability, and time-based trends using created_at after casting. It cannot provide customer, store or full transaction metadata without joins (e.g., to pos_transactions, pos_stores, products). Because several columns are strings, correct type casting is necessary for accurate numeric/date aggregations.
business_questions: Which SKUs generated the most revenue and units sold in this dataset?
What is total revenue and tax collected for the recorded POS lines over a given time range?
Which batches were sold and how many units per batch (batch traceability)?
What is the average unit price and average line_total per SKU?
How many line items are associated with each POS transaction (lines per receipt)?
pos_transaction_lines.relationships: Natural joins: pos_txn_id -> pos_transactions (to get store, terminal, cashier, transaction-level status); sku -> pos_products / products / product_variants (to enrich with product name, category); batch_id -> inventory_items or product_inventory (to link batch manufacture/expiry info). These relationships enable full sales analytics by store/customer, product dimension enrichment, inventory/batch expiry traceability and mapping sales to orders/payments.

pos_transactions.purpose: Point-of-sale transactions raw table capturing individual POS sales events and their basic financial and operational attributes. It serves as the canonical ingest of POS transaction headers for downstream reporting, reconciliation, and joins to line-level sales, terminals, users and shift metadata.
pos_transactions.key_characteristics: Small table sample (50 rows). Many columns stored as nvarchar though semantically numeric or datetime. pos_txn_id appears to be unique (distinct = row count) but table has no declared primary key. txn_type and status are single-valued (SALE, COMPLETED). discount_total is constant '0' in sample. payment_mode has low cardinality (CASH, UPI, CARD). terminal_id and pos_user_id are low cardinality and suitable for grouping. txn_ts and created_at have datetime-like strings in ISO format but stored as nvarchar, requiring casting. txn_total and tax_total are high-cardinality numeric values stored as strings; precision artifacts visible (floating point string formatting). external_txn_ref is unique per transaction.
pos_transactions.analysis_capabilities: This table alone can provide transaction-level financial metrics (counts, sums, averages of txn_total and tax_total), temporal analyses (by txn_ts or created_at after casting), operational metrics (sales by terminal, user, shift, payment_mode), simple reconciliation with external systems via external_txn_ref and synced_flag, and quality checks (missing notes, constant discount). It can also identify suspicious patterns (duplicate external refs, unexpected payment modes) but cannot break down items sold without joining to pos_transaction_lines or product tables.
business_questions: What is the total and average transaction value by day/week/month?
How much sales (sum txn_total) did each terminal or POS user generate in a time range?
What is the distribution of payment modes (CASH/UPI/CARD) and associated totals?
How much tax was collected by day and by payment_mode?
How many transactions were completed/failed and what is the sync status to downstream systems?
pos_transactions.relationships: No formal foreign keys declared, but logical joins exist: pos_txn_id -> pos_transaction_lines for line-level details; terminal_id -> pos_terminals for terminal metadata; pos_user_id -> pos_users for cashier metadata; shift_id -> pos_shift_closures for shift timing and manager; external_txn_ref and synced_flag can be used to join/reconcile with ecom_payments/ecom_payments or b2b_payments/external payment tables. These joins enable item-level sales, product attribution, inventory impact, cashier performance, and shift-based reconciliations.

pos_users.purpose: This table stores point-of-sale (POS) user / staff records (cashiers, supervisors) including identifiers, contact details, assigned store, status and basic activity timestamps. It is a reference/master table for POS user identities used by transaction and shift systems across the POS-related schema.
pos_users.key_characteristics: Very small sample (3 rows). All columns are stored as nvarchar (string) including timestamps and flags. pos_user_id appears unique in sample (distinct = 3). role has low cardinality (Cashier, Supervisor). active_flag in sample is always '1' (single value). created_at and last_login are stored as strings in YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS format and should be cast to datetime for temporal queries. pin_hash contains hashed PINs and is sensitive. No declared primary or foreign keys. store_id values look like surrogate codes and likely link to pos_stores; phone/email unique per user in sample.
pos_users.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports identity and profile-level analyses: counts of users by role/store/active status, user creation trends (after casting created_at), last-login recency and inactive user identification, contact lists. It cannot provide transactional metrics (sales per user) without joining to transaction or shift tables.
business_questions: How many POS users exist by role and by store?
Which POS users have not logged in in the last N days (identify inactive staff)?
When were POS users added (new hires) over a chosen date range?
Which users are active vs inactive and what is the headcount per store?
What are contact details (phone/email) for POS users in a specific store or role?
pos_users.relationships: No foreign keys declared but typical joins: store_id -> pos_stores (store metadata), pos_user_id -> pos_transactions or pos_shift_closures or pos_transaction_lines (to attribute sales/transactions to users). These joins enable linking user profiles to transactional activity, shift performance, and store-level analyses (e.g., sales per cashier, last activity per user).

pos_vendors.purpose: Master/reference list of point-of-sale (POS) vendors used by the application. Stores vendor identifiers, contact details, location, tax identifier and business category. Intended to support lookups, reporting and joins against transactional tables (purchases, returns, vendor invoices) to attribute activity to vendors.
pos_vendors.key_characteristics: Small table (40 rows). vendor_id and gst_no appear unique per row (vendor_id distinct=40). created_at is stored as text and has a single value across all rows (all rows share same timestamp). city has low cardinality (9 distinct values) and business_type is categorical (5 distinct values). All columns nullable and defined as nvarchar; declared lengths exceed observed max lengths. No primary key or foreign keys declared.
pos_vendors.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table enables vendor-level reporting (counts, grouping by city or business_type), contact lists and validation of master data quality (duplicates, missing contacts, malformed GST). It cannot provide financial metrics without joins to transactional tables (orders, invoices, payments). Useful for enrichment and filters in NL2SQL queries (e.g., 'vendors in Gandhinagar', 'equipment repair vendors').
business_questions: How many vendors are registered in each city?
What is the distribution of vendors by business_type (e.g., Equipment Repair vs Hardware)?
Which vendors do we have contact information for and which have missing phone/email?
Which vendors are located in a given city (e.g., Gandhinagar) and what are their GST numbers?
Are there potential duplicate vendors by name or GST number in the master list?
pos_vendors.relationships: Although no formal foreign keys are declared, vendor_id is the natural join key to transactional tables that refer to vendors (likely candidates: pos_transactions, pos_transaction_lines, pos_products, ecom_vendors, b2b_vendor_partners, b2b_orders, b2b_invoices). Joins enable attribution of spend, orders, returns and product-supplier relationships. vendor_name or gst_no can be used for fuzzy joins or external system reconciliation; phone/email can be used for identity matching where vendor_id is missing in other sources.

product_inventory.purpose: Product-level inventory snapshot per centre. Contains inventory identifiers, product SKU, storage location (centre), quantities (units and kg), reserved and available balances, replenishment parameters (reorder point, lead time, safety stock), expiry timestamp and storage temperature. Supports inventory management, replenishment decisioning and expiry/temperature compliance checks.
product_inventory.key_characteristics: Small table (40 rows). No declared PKs or FKs, but inventory_id appears unique (40 distinct). Many numeric-semantics columns are stored as nvarchar (qty_units, qty_kg, reserved_stock, available_stock, reorder_point, lead_time_days, safety_stock_kg, storage_temp_c) and need casting for numeric operations. expiry_ts is an nvarchar but contains full timestamps (40 distinct). sku cardinality is 10 and centre_id cardinality is 4 — ideal for grouping by product and site. lead_time_days has low cardinality (3 values), storage_temp_c has 2 values indicating a small set of storage classes. Few repeating top values but overall moderate variety in quantities.
product_inventory.analysis_capabilities: Can compute current stock by sku and centre, available vs reserved balances, identify items below reorder point, compute days-to-expiry and list expired/near-expiry inventory, check storage temperature compliance by sku/centre, compute safety stock coverage (available_stock vs safety_stock_kg), and create simple replenishment lead-time analyses. Because columns are strings, numeric/date casting is required before aggregation or filtering.
business_questions: Which SKUs at which centres are below their reorder point and need replenishment?
What is the total available stock (kg and units) per SKU and per centre?
Which inventory items will expire within X days (or are already expired)?
How much of available stock is reserved vs free per SKU/centre?
Are any items stored at incorrect temperatures or do certain centres have only cold-storage SKUs?
product_inventory.relationships: Logical joins: sku can join to product tables (products, product_variants, b2b_products, ecom_products, product_pricing, product_sales) to enrich with product name, category, or cost. centre_id can join to location/centre tables (pos_stores, pos_stores metadata, inventory_items, b2b_vendor_partners or a centres table if present) to retrieve centre attributes (type, region). inventory_id could link to inventory_items or inventory adjustment/audit tables if present. Joining to sales/order tables (order_items, b2b_order_lines, ecom_order_items) enables demand vs stock analyses (days of cover). These relationships enable replenishment optimization, stock-to-sales matching, expiry-driven promotions, and allocation planning.

product_pricing.purpose: Holds time-ranged pricing records for products (by SKU). Each row defines a price_per_unit, minimum order quantity and validity window (start_ts - end_ts) for a SKU. It appears to be a raw / staging table capturing price versions rather than a normalized canonical price table.
product_pricing.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). price_id is effectively unique (100 distinct). sku cardinality is low (10 distinct SKUs) with multiple time-versioned rows per SKU. start_ts, end_ts and created_at are stored as strings (nvarchar) but contain timestamp-like values. price_per_unit is stored as string but contains numeric decimals. currency and created_by are constant across rows (INR and pricing_admin). min_order_qty has very small domain (1, 5, 10). There are no declared primary/foreign keys.
product_pricing.analysis_capabilities: Can answer price history and time-window queries (active price at a given timestamp, price changes over time), compute average/min/max prices per SKU, detect overlapping/duplicate validity windows, count pricing records per SKU, and derive change frequency. Because columns are strings, accurate temporal and numeric analyses require casting to proper types.
business_questions: What is the active price for a given SKU at a specific timestamp (e.g., now)?
How has the unit price for SKU X changed over the last 6 months?
Which SKUs have overlapping price validity windows or gaps in pricing?
What is the distribution of minimum order quantities by SKU and how does min_order_qty correlate with price tiers?
How many price updates were made by the pricing team in a given period (using created_at)?
product_pricing.relationships: Natural join keys: sku links this table to product/master tables (e.g., products, b2b_products, product_variants, product_sales). Joining by sku enables enrichment with product metadata (name, category, cost), sales, and inventory to evaluate pricing impact. No explicit foreign key constraints are declared; price_id can serve as a unique identifier when joining to audit or change-log tables if present.

product_sales.purpose: This table appears to be a raw, product-level sales snapshot keyed by SKU. It contains monetary metrics (gross sales, discounts, delivery fees, tax and net sales) for products. It is likely a staging or seed/raw import used for downstream reporting, validation, or ETL into canonical product-sales tables.
product_sales.key_characteristics: Very small sample (10 rows). All monetary columns are stored as nvarchar (text) rather than numeric types. Each column shows distinct values equal to row count (high cardinality for this sample). Column names include spaces and mixed casing (e.g., 'Gross Sales', 'Delivery Fee') which requires quoting in SQL. 'sku' has max length 6 and 10 distinct values. Numeric text values have up to 18 characters and include decimal points. No primary key or foreign keys are declared.
product_sales.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: compute per-SKU aggregates (sum/avg) after casting, compute simple ratios (discount % of gross, tax %), validate Net Sales against a computed formula, identify anomalies or parsing errors in numeric fields, and produce small verification reports. You cannot reliably join to other tables without establishing that 'sku' matches canonical product key formats in product tables.
business_questions: Which SKUs generated the highest gross sales in the sample?
What is the discount amount and discount percentage by SKU (Discount / Gross Sales)?
How much of total charges come from delivery fees and tax per SKU?
Does Net Sales match the expected computed value (e.g., Gross - Discount +/- Delivery Fee +/- Tax)?
Which SKUs have inconsistent or unparsable numeric fields that need data cleaning?
product_sales.relationships: The natural join key is 'sku' — it can connect to canonical product tables (e.g., products, product_variants, b2b_products, ecom_products) to enrich with product name, category, cost, etc. Joining enables SKU-level rollups to categories, reconciliation with order-level sales tables (order_items, b2b_order_lines), and populating analytics tables (pos_product_wise_sales, ecom_product_wise_sales). Because there are no declared foreign keys, NL2SQL systems should treat 'sku' as a candidate join key but validate format/lookup before automatic joins.

product_variants.purpose: Holds product variant level master data for SKUs (different pack sizes / loose vs packed) including identifiers, unit/weight metadata, barcodes and pricing. Serves as the canonical lookup for variant attributes used by inventory, pricing and sales tables in the schema.
product_variants.key_characteristics: Small table (20 rows in sample). Many columns are stored as nvarchar (strings) although several represent numeric (prices, weights, units_per_pack) or datetime values (created_at). variant_id and variant_sku are unique per row (distinct counts = row count). variant_name has very low cardinality (2 values). uom dominated by 'kg'. active_flag has single value '1' in the sample (no inactive examples). price_retail and price_wholesale contain many distinct numeric-like strings, some with floating point representation artifacts.
product_variants.analysis_capabilities: Can be used alone to profile variant catalog (counts by variant type, uom, pack sizes, price distributions), compute simple variant-level metrics (e.g., retail vs wholesale spread), and validate master data quality (missing barcodes, inconsistent weights). When joined to sales, inventory or pricing tables it enables variant-level revenue, margin and stock analyses.
business_questions: What variants exist for a given SKU and what are their pack sizes and weights?
What is the retail vs wholesale price for each product variant and what is the price spread?
How many active variants use each unit-of-measure (uom) and what pack sizes are most common?
Which variants are missing barcodes or have inconsistent weight/pricing data?
Which variants were added in a given time period (based on created_at)?
product_variants.relationships: No declared foreign keys in DDL, but typical joins: sku (or variant_sku) -> products/products.sku (or product master tables) to enrich variant metadata; barcode -> inventory_items or shipments for scan-level joins; variant_sku or variant_id -> order_lines/order_items for sales/invoice joins; sku -> product_pricing/product_sales/product_inventory for pricing and inventory lookups. These relationships enable linking variant attributes to transactions, inventory and pricing histories for analytics and reporting.

products.purpose: Product master / catalog feed containing SKU-level product attributes used to describe items sold across channels. It appears to be a raw ingestion table (zuna_seed_raw) that provides descriptive metadata (names, weights, categorization, storage and shelf-life) which downstream systems (pricing, inventory, sales) can join to.
products.key_characteristics: Small sample (row_count: 10). No primary key declared. Many columns are stored as nvarchar though several represent numeric or datetime semantics (gross/net weight, shelf_life_days, storage_temp_c, created_at). Several low-cardinality columns (product_type: 2 values; category: 1 value; brand: 1 value; is_perishable_flag single value). sku and product_name are unique per row in this sample (distinct:10). hs_code and default_image_url are 100% NULL in this snapshot. pack_size constant '1'.
products.analysis_capabilities: Can be used as a product dimension for joins to sales, inventory, pricing and returns tables. Enables grouping and filtering by product_type, category, subcategory, brand and uom. Supports logistics/operations analyses using shelf life and storage temperature once cast to numeric. Supports catalog audits (missing HS codes/images), perishable-item segmentation, and enrichment of transactions with product attributes.
business_questions: Which SKUs are perishable and what are their shelf-life distributions?
How many unique products exist by product_type, category and subcategory?
Which products require cold storage (based on storage_temp_c) and should be handled differently in logistics?
Which SKUs are missing HS codes or default images (data quality gaps)?
What is the mapping of SKU to pack sizes and weights for packaging and freight planning?
products.relationships: No declared foreign keys in DDL, but SKU is the natural linking key to many tables in the schema (b2b_products, b2b_product_wise_sales, product_pricing, product_inventory, product_variants, product_sales, pos_products, ecom_product_wise_sales, order_items, product_sales). Joining on sku enables enrichment of transactional data (orders, sales, returns, inventory) with descriptive attributes (category, shelf life, storage temp) for analytics such as channel-level sales by subcategory, perishable-item shrinkage, and temperature-controlled logistics planning.

returns.purpose: Records of product returns initiated against orders. Each row captures a return request lifecycle (request, approval, pickup scheduling, centre receipt, refund and credit note linkage). The table is a raw ingest (all columns are nvarchar) intended to be joined with orders, credit notes, return items and invoices for business analytics and operational reporting.
returns.key_characteristics: Small table (40 rows). All columns stored as nvarchar (strings) even where numeric or datetime semantics exist. return_id is effectively unique (40 distinct). order_id repeats (35 distinct) — some orders have multiple returns. Timestamps (request_ts, pickup_scheduled_ts, received_at_centre_ts, created_at) are ISO-like strings but not typed; consistent formatting observed. refund_amount is a string but contains decimal numeric values. reason_code has low cardinality (4 values). status has 4 states with skew towards COMPLETED and APPROVED. requested_items_json is always '[]' in this sample and resolution_notes are fully null. approved_by has two distinct non-null values and ~52% nulls.
returns.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can support analyses of return volumes, reason distributions, status distributions, refund totals, per-approver counts and timing metrics between request/ pickup/ centre receipt (after casting timestamps). It cannot attribute returns to customers or product-level metrics without joins to orders or return-items tables. It can link refunds to credit notes via credit_note_id for reconciliation.
business_questions: How many returns were created in a given date range and how are they distributed by reason_code and status?
What is the total and average refund_amount for returns (and by reason_code or status)?
Which approvers processed the most returns and what are their approval rates or average refund amounts?
What are the average times from request_ts -> pickup_scheduled_ts -> received_at_centre_ts (operational latency) for completed returns?
Which orders have multiple returns (and how many returns per order)?
returns.relationships: Natural join keys: order_id can join to orders tables (b2b_orders, ecom_orders, pos_transactions/order_items depending on domain) to attribute returns to customers, order totals and product lines. credit_note_id can join to b2b_credit_notes (or ecom_refunds/ecom_return_items depending on naming) to reconcile refund documents. Detailed product-level analysis requires joining to a return-items table (not present here) such as ecom_return_items or order_lines. Joins enable computing return rate per SKU, customer-level return behavior, revenue impact and reconciliation with invoices/payments.

shipments.purpose: This table stores shipment-level records for orders: one row per physical shipment describing carrier, tracking, dates, status, weight, charge and timestamps. It is used for logistics and delivery performance analysis and for reconciling shipment costs with orders.
shipments.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). No primary key defined. All columns are nvarchar (strings) in the DDL though many contain structured data (timestamps, numeric values). shipment_date/expected_delivery_date/actual_delivery_date/created_at/last_update use a consistent 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern but are stored as text. package_weight_kg and shipping_charge are textual but numeric-like. shipment_status and carrier_id are low-cardinality categorical fields (status distinct=4, carrier_id distinct=3). actual_delivery_date has substantial nulls (~41%). package_dimensions_json contains only '{}' for every row (no useful data).
shipments.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports shipment counts, carrier and pickup-centre performance, on-time delivery and delay calculations (where actual_delivery_date present after casting), shipping cost summaries and weight-based aggregations after parsing numeric strings. It can also be used to identify shipments without final delivery dates, track status distributions, and link to orders or tracking events via order_id/tracking_no for richer analysis.
business_questions: What is the on-time delivery rate by carrier and pickup centre?
Which carriers or pickup centres have the highest average shipping charge and shipping charge per kg?
How many shipments are currently OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, IN_TRANSIT, PICKED or DELIVERED?
What is the distribution of delivery delays (actual - expected) and how many shipments are overdue?
Which orders have shipments missing an actual_delivery_date (possible delivery exceptions)?
shipments.relationships: Primary join keys are order_id (to orders tables such as b2b_orders or ecom_orders) and tracking_no (to tracking/events tables such as b2b_shipment_tracking_events). carrier_id can be joined to carrier or partner reference tables (e.g., b2b_vendor_partners or a carriers master table) if present. These joins enable combining shipment-level operational metrics with order, customer, product, or event-level data for end-to-end analytics (cost per order, delayed deliveries by customer segment, shipment history per order).

contract_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for selecting and grouping contract-level records, de-duplication, and joining back to contract detail tables or logs (if present). Useful for contract-level aggregations, lookups and auditing.
business_questions: Show full contract details for contract_id = 'CON3001'.
How many orders/invoices are tied to a specific contract (after joining orders by customer and SKU)?
contract_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string in WHERE/JOIN predicates. There is no enforced PK — consider enforcing uniqueness or using it as primary key. Use equality or IN lists for identification. Watch for trailing spaces; trim values when matching external keys.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer the contract applies to.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group or filter contracts by customer, join to customer master (b2b_customers) to enrich with account attributes, and join to orders/invoices for compliance and revenue attribution.
business_questions: List all active contracts for customer CUST1001.
Which customers have more than one open contract or overlapping contract periods?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Acts as the natural FK to b2b_customers.customer_id (no declared constraint). Ensure consistent formatting/casing when joining. Use as a primary join key when reconciling contracts to orders/invoices by customer.

start_date.description: Contract effective start timestamp (stored as nvarchar).
start_date.analysis_use: Used to compute contract duration, determine whether a contract is in force at a given date, cohort analysis (contracts started in a time window), and filtering by period. Must be CAST/CONVERT to datetime for accurate comparisons and date-based grouping.
business_questions: How many contracts started in Q1 2025?
Which contracts are already in effect as of today (start_date <= today and end_date >= today)?
start_date.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — always CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type before date arithmetic. Guard against malformed or null strings; specify format in conversion if needed (e.g., 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss'). Prefer storing as native datetime in future schema changes.

end_date.analysis_use: Used to determine expiry, compute remaining duration, filter expiring contracts, and classify ACTIVE vs EXPIRED when combined with start_date and current date. Required for renewal and expiration analyses.
business_questions: Which contracts will expire within the next 30/90 days?
How many contracts have already expired as of today?
end_date.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string — CAST/CONVERT before comparisons. Use combined logic (start_date <= date AND end_date >= date) to identify active contracts instead of relying solely on status. Handle open-ended/null end_date as ongoing contracts.

sku_list_json.description: Serialized list of SKUs covered by the contract. In the current data it looks like array text with single quotes (e.g., ['SKU1','SKU2']).
sku_list_json.analysis_use: Explode to rows to answer SKU-level coverage queries, join to product or order line tables to compute contracted vs actual sales, and filter contracts that include a given SKU. Also used to compute SKU penetration and portfolio overlap across contracts.
business_questions: Which contracts cover SKU 'LIM008'?
What percentage of SKUs sold in the last month are under an active contract?
sku_list_json.nl2sql_hints: Current format may not be valid JSON (single quotes). Preprocess: replace single quotes with double quotes or use string-splitting functions to create a normalized (contract_id, sku) table. If DB supports JSON functions, ensure proper JSON array format (double quotes) before using JSON_QUERY/OPENJSON. Indexing will be much more effective after normalizing SKUs into a separate child table.

pricing_model.description: Categorical field indicating the contract's pricing strategy (e.g., Dynamic, Fixed, Tiered).
pricing_model.analysis_use: Group and filter contracts by pricing model, compare performance (revenue, margins, compliance) across models, and segment contract analytics by pricing type.
business_questions: How many contracts use the Dynamic pricing model vs Fixed or Tiered?
Which pricing model corresponds to the highest average monthly invoice amount (when joined with invoice data)?
pricing_model.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Watch for inconsistent casing/typos — normalize values in queries (UPPER/TRIM). Consider refactoring to a code table for cleaner joins and to support additional model metadata.

min_monthly_volume.description: Minimum monthly purchase volume required by the contract (stored as nvarchar but represents a numeric quantity).
min_monthly_volume.analysis_use: Numeric threshold used to check customer compliance (actual monthly sales vs min requirement), to flag high-volume contracts, and to aggregate total committed volume across contracts. Requires casting to integer/decimal for aggregation and comparison.
business_questions: Which contracts have a min_monthly_volume > 1000 and are currently undersupplied?
What is the total committed minimum monthly volume across all active contracts?
min_monthly_volume.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — CAST/CONVERT to INT or DECIMAL before numeric operations. Handle non-numeric or null values defensively (TRY_CAST or CASE). Normalize units if multiple unit conventions might exist.

discount_schema_json.description: Structured discount rules for the contract (JSON). Current sample shows a consistent JSON object {"vol_discount":"5%"}.
discount_schema_json.analysis_use: Extract discount rates/tiers for pricing calculations, apply discounts when computing expected revenue, and compare discount generosity across contracts. If more complex (multi-tier) it can drive pricing logic.
business_questions: What is the volume discount for contract CON3001?
Which contracts provide a volume discount of 5% or greater?
discount_schema_json.nl2sql_hints: This appears to be valid JSON — use JSON parsing functions (JSON_VALUE/OPENJSON) to extract fields. Percent values are strings (e.g., '5%') — strip '%' and cast to numeric for arithmetic. Given low variance in current data, consider normalizing if additional discount schemas are introduced.

delivery_windows_json.description: Structured delivery window information for the contract (JSON). Current value example: {"mon-fri":"08:00-12:00"}.
delivery_windows_json.analysis_use: Filter or match deliveries to allowed windows, compute delivery compliance, and plan logistics. Useful for operational scheduling and SLA checks when joined with delivery or dispatch events.
business_questions: What delivery window applies to contract CON3001?
How many contracts restrict deliveries to '08:00-12:00' on weekdays?
delivery_windows_json.nl2sql_hints: Valid JSON — extract keys/values using JSON functions. If the format is simple and constant, consider storing normalized delivery window identifiers for efficient filtering. If windows vary, ensure parsing covers multiple keys/days.

penalty_terms.description: Human-readable penalty clause text describing contract penalties (string). Example: 'Late delivery penalty applies'.
penalty_terms.analysis_use: Used for reference and reporting, to flag contracts that include penalty conditions, and as a join filter when computing penalty-triggering events from delivery/dispatch logs.
business_questions: Which contracts include a late delivery penalty?
List contracts and their penalty terms for commercial review.
penalty_terms.nl2sql_hints: Free text field — not ideal for predicate logic. If penalty types must be queried frequently, normalize into structured columns (e.g., penalty_type, penalty_rate). For text filtering, use LIKE/ILIKE or full-text search depending on DB capabilities.

status.description: Lifecycle status of the contract (PENDING, ACTIVE, EXPIRED).
status.analysis_use: Primary filter for lifecycle analytics (counts by status), segmentation of active vs non-active agreements, and candidate selection for renewal/activation processes.
business_questions: How many contracts are currently PENDING and require activation?
How does contract status distribution change month-over-month?
status.nl2sql_hints: Use in WHERE and GROUP BY; standardize casing with UPPER/TRIM to avoid mismatches. Do not rely solely on status to determine 'active' — cross-check start_date/end_date to protect against stale status values.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the contract record was created (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and lineage (when contract was added), cohort and trend analysis (contracts created per period), and for ordering results by creation time. Use for SLA or onboarding metrics.
business_questions: How many contracts were created each month in 2025?
What is the average time from created_at to start_date for contracts?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — CAST/CONVERT to datetime before use in time-based GROUP BY or date arithmetic. Be mindful of inconsistent formats/timezones. Use created_at for creation-time filters but verify with start_date for effective contract timelines.

credit_note_id.description: Unique identifier for the credit or debit note (e.g., CN17001).
credit_note_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, point lookups, grouping by note, and counting unique notes. Useful for joins when referencing specific notes.
business_questions: How many credit notes were issued in a given period (distinct credit_note_id)?
Show details for credit note CN17001.
credit_note_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. It appears unique—use it as the primary key in queries (WHERE credit_note_id = 'CN17001'). Indexing this column improves point lookups. No casting required.

invoice_id.description: Identifier of the invoice against which this credit/debit note was issued (e.g., INV13001).
invoice_id.analysis_use: Join key to invoices to attribute credits to customers/orders; grouping by invoice to compute net adjustments; filtering to examine notes for a particular invoice or order cycle.
business_questions: List all credit notes issued against invoice INV13001.
What is the total credit value applied to a specific invoice or customer (after joining invoices to customers)?
invoice_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; use string equality for joins (e.g., JOIN b2b_invoices i ON cn.invoice_id = i.invoice_id). Consider indexing invoice_id for faster joins/filters. Watch for whitespace or case differences; trim values if necessary.

note_type.description: Indicates whether the record is a CREDIT or a DEBIT note.
note_type.analysis_use: Dimension for grouping and aggregation (sum/avg/ count by type). Filter to separate credits and debits and compute net adjustments.
business_questions: What is the total amount issued as CREDIT vs DEBIT in the last month?
How many debit notes were raised compared to credit notes?
note_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field with values like 'CREDIT' and 'DEBIT'. Use simple WHERE note_type = 'CREDIT' or GROUP BY note_type. Normalize case if inputs are inconsistent.

amount.description: Monetary value of the credit/debit note stored as a string (examples: '104.13', '1120.3699999999999').
amount.analysis_use: Primary numeric measure for aggregation (SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX). Required for financial totals, impact analysis, and rank by amount.
business_questions: What is the total value of credit notes in the last quarter?
Which credit notes are the largest (top N by amount)?
amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — always CAST/TRY_CAST to a numeric type (DECIMAL(18,2) or FLOAT) before aggregation: e.g., TRY_CAST(amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)).
