remarks.description: Short categorical remark about packaging/handling (e.g., 'Bulk crate', 'Standard pack', 'Prewashed').
remarks.analysis_use: Categorical grouping to analyze packaging preferences and correlation with quantity, weight or revenue. Useful for operational filtering and reporting.
business_questions: Which packaging type generates the most revenue or is most common?
Do certain remarks correlate with higher average unit_price or quantity?
remarks.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — good for GROUP BY. Use TRIM and case-normalization. Use exact matches or IN lists in NL2SQL (e.g., WHERE remarks = 'Bulk crate').

order_id.description: Internal unique identifier for the order (string).
order_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for counts, deduplication, joining to order_lines/invoices/dispatches/payments. Grouping by order_id is typically used only when joining line-level data.
business_questions: Which orders correspond to a given customer or external code?
Retrieve full order header when given an order_id for auditing or reconciliation.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a key string. Use exact equality for lookups (WHERE order_id = '...'). When joining, prefer this column to link to order_lines/invoices. Note: no declared PK so deduplicate if duplicates expected.

external_order_code.description: Order identifier from an external system or partner (string).
external_order_code.analysis_use: Map/lookup to external systems, deduplicate cross-system records, group by external source codes when reconciling with partner reports.
business_questions: Which external orders failed to be ingested or reconciled?
Find internal orders that map to a specific external_order_code or partner batch.
external_order_code.nl2sql_hints: Often used for JOINs to external ETL/partner tables. Treat as string; use equality matching and be mindful of trimming/case differences.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer placing the order (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group/aggregate by customer (order counts, revenue), join to customer profile tables for segmentation, cohort or lifetime value calculations.
business_questions: Which customers contribute the highest net revenue?
How many orders did a given customer place in a window?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Join key to b2b_customers. Cardinality moderate (~50 distinct). Use equality joins; handle NULLs. Consider casting to consistent type if customer id formats vary across tables.

order_ts.description: Timestamp when the order was placed (stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
order_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time series (orders per day/week/month), sessionization, time-to-fulfillment calculations when paired with dispatch/delivery timestamps.
business_questions: How many orders were placed per day/week and what are revenue trends?
How long between order placement and dispatch/delivery on average?
order_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — always CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type (e.g., CAST(order_ts AS datetime) or parse YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS) before applying date functions or range filters. Watch for NULLs and inconsistent formats.

requested_delivery_ts.description: Customer-requested delivery timestamp (text, same datetime format).
requested_delivery_ts.analysis_use: Measure SLA adherence (requested vs actual delivery), plan fulfillment schedules, and analyze requested vs actual delivery patterns.
business_questions: What proportion of orders are delivered on the requested date?
Are urgent orders requesting earlier delivery times concentrated in specific pickup centres?
requested_delivery_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string — CAST to datetime for comparisons with dispatch/delivery timestamps. May be NULL for open or unspecified requests. Use safe parsing and timezone awareness if applicable.

order_channel.description: Channel through which the order was placed (categorical: WhatsApp, Email, Phone, Sales Rep).
order_channel.analysis_use: Group and filter to analyze channel performance (volume, revenue, cancellation rates), measure channel-specific KPIs and conversion funnels.
business_questions: Which channels drive the most revenue and highest average order value?
Do cancellation or confirmation rates vary by order_channel?
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical column — use GROUP BY order_channel. Use normalized string matching (trim/case) for robust NL2SQL generation and consider mapping synonyms (e.g., 'WA' -> 'WhatsApp').

order_status.description: Current lifecycle status of the order (PLACED, CANCELLED, CONFIRMED, DISPATCHED, DELIVERED).
order_status.analysis_use: Filter for active/completed orders, compute counts by status, calculate cancellation rates, funnel analysis from placed -> confirmed -> dispatched -> delivered.
business_questions: What percentage of orders are cancelled vs delivered over a period?
How long do orders spend in each status (when status history available)?
order_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with known small set of values. Use explicit value lists in NL2SQL (e.g., WHERE order_status = 'CANCELLED'). For status-change timelines, join to order_events or order_events_stream tables.

currency.description: Currency for the amounts (string) — in this dataset constant 'INR'.
currency.analysis_use: Validation and ensuring consistent currency before aggregating monetary metrics. Filter if multi-currency in fuller datasets.
business_questions: Are all orders billed in the expected currency (INR)?
When multi-currency exists, convert to base currency for consolidated revenue.
currency.nl2sql_hints: Often constant — but still include in WHERE or CAST checks when building NL2SQL that aggregates finance. Treat as string; if multi-currency expected, plan for currency conversion joins.

gross_amount.description: Gross order amount before discounts and tax, stored as nvarchar with decimal values.
gross_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate SUM(gross_amount) for revenue, AVG for AOV calculations, used in P&L and margin analyses after casting to numeric/decimal.
business_questions: What is total gross revenue in a date range?
How does average gross order value differ across channels or pickup centres?
gross_amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — must CAST/CONVERT to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., CAST(gross_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Handle empty strings/NULLs and non-numeric noise. Use COALESCE(TRY_CAST(..., DECIMAL),0) where supported.

tax_amount.description: Tax component of the order amount (stored as nvarchar). Currently all values 0 in sample.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Tax reporting and reconciliation; often added to gross/net calculations. If constant zero, may be ignored in short-term analyses but include for completeness.
business_questions: What is total tax collected over a period?
Are there orders with non-zero tax amounts (validate tax capture)?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric for aggregation. Note the column appears constant in sample; still include null/zero handling. If always zero, can be excluded from heavy calculations to optimize performance.

discount_amount.description: Total discount applied to the order (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate discounts to measure promotion cost, compute net revenue = gross - discount - tax, analyze discount impact by channel or customer segment.
business_questions: How much have discounts reduced revenue in a period?
Which channels or customers receive the most discount value?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before SUM/AVG. Watch for negative discounts or inconsistent formatting. Use COALESCE/TRY_CAST to avoid query errors on malformed text.

net_amount.description: Net payable amount after discounts and taxes (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
net_amount.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for financial reporting, margin calculations when joined with cost data, and ranking customers by spend.
business_questions: What is total net revenue and average net order value?
Which orders/customers contributed the top net amounts?
net_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL for SUM/AVG/RANK. Prefer net_amount as the main revenue column but validate that net_amount == gross_amount - discount_amount - tax_amount when reconciling.

pickup_centre_id.description: Identifier for the pickup/fulfillment centre servicing the order (categorical string, low cardinality).
pickup_centre_id.analysis_use: Group by pickup centre to measure volume, revenue, fulfillment performance, capacity planning and centre-specific SLA adherence.
business_questions: Which pickup centres handle the most volume and revenue?
Do cancellation rates or average order values vary by pickup centre?
pickup_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — good candidate for GROUP BY or WHERE filters. Joinable to centre metadata tables if present; unify ID format for joins.

fulfillment_priority.description: Priority assigned to fulfillment (URGENT, NORMAL, HIGH).
fulfillment_priority.analysis_use: Segment orders by priority for SLA measurement, resource allocation analytics, and correlation with revenue or order value.
business_questions: What percentage of revenue/request volume is marked URGENT?
Are URGENT orders fulfilled faster or do they have higher cancellation?
fulfillment_priority.nl2sql_hints: Categorical; treat as low-cardinality dimension. Map synonyms or casing to canonical values in NL2SQL. Use when filtering for urgent workflows.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp for the order row (text, same format as order_ts).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/time-of-ingestion analysis, compare with order_ts to detect delays in ingestion, time-window filtering for incremental loads.
business_questions: When were orders ingested into the system and are there ingestion delays?
Filter records by created_at for incremental ETL or recent orders.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — CAST to datetime for date filters and ordering. For ETL/near-real-time queries, prefer created_at to find newly inserted rows. Be careful: created_at can differ from order_ts.

payment_id.description: Unique identifier for the payment event (string).
payment_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication; grouping by payment_id rarely required but useful for lookups and uniqueness checks; candidate join key if other systems reference payment_id.
business_questions: Is a given payment_id present and what are its details?
Are there duplicate payment_id entries indicating ingestion issues?
payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text key. Expect uniqueness (distinct=50 == row count) but DDL lacks PK. Use equality filters (WHERE payment_id = 'PAY14001'). Avoid casting. Consider creating a primary key or unique index for performance in large datasets.

invoice_id.description: Identifier of the invoice associated with the payment (string).
invoice_id.analysis_use: Join key to invoice tables for joining payments to invoice totals, aging, and order metadata; grouping by invoice_id to compute payments per invoice and outstanding balances.
business_questions: Which invoices have received full/partial/no payments?
How many payments map to a single invoice (split payments)?
invoice_id.nl2sql_hints: Use equality joins (AND invoice_id = invoices.invoice_id). Strings should match exact invoice ids. If invoices table uses different casing/format, apply normalization (TRIM/UPPER).

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who made the payment (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate payments per customer, compute top customers by amount or count, and join to customer master for segmentation.
business_questions: What is total payments per customer in a period?
Which customers have the highest number of failed or pending payments?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text join key to b2b_customers or ecom_customers. When writing NL2SQL translate 'customer' references to customer_id and prefer direct equality or IN lists. Consider casting/trimming if inconsistent.

payment_ts.description: Timestamp of when the payment occurred, stored as a string in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
payment_ts.analysis_use: Primary temporal column for time-series analysis: grouping by day/month/hour, computing time windows, filtering by date ranges, and latency calculations against reconciled_at.
business_questions: How many payments per day/hour were received?
How has payment volume trended over the last N days?
payment_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to a proper datetime in queries (e.g., CAST(payment_ts AS DATETIME) or to_timestamp). The string format is ISO-like (safe to parse). Use date_trunc/grouping after cast. Beware NULLs and malformed values; use TRY_CAST where available.

amount.description: Monetary amount of the payment stored as a string (contains decimal numeric values).
amount.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM, AVG), sort, and filter (e.g., payments > X). Needed for revenue calculations, top payments, refunds netting, and reconciled totals.
business_questions: What is the total amount received in a given period?
What is the average payment amount by payment_mode or customer?
amount.nl2sql_hints: Always CAST/CONVERT to a decimal/numeric type before aggregation (e.g., CAST(amount AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Strings include many decimal digits—define appropriate precision/scale. Handle empty or malformed strings with TRY_CAST or NULLIF.

payment_mode.description: How payment was made (CHEQUE, RTGS, UPI, NEFT) stored as text with low cardinality.
payment_mode.analysis_use: Group and filter to compare volumes and amounts by payment mode, analyze failure/reconciliation rates per mode, and evaluate preferred channels.
business_questions: Which payment mode contributes the highest volume/value of receipts?
Are certain payment modes more likely to fail or remain unreconciled?
payment_mode.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field — use GROUP BY payment_mode. Expect small fixed set of values; consider mapping to normalized enum if building NL2SQL domain models. Use exact match strings; consider UPPER/TRIM normalization.

bank_ref_no.description: Bank reference number associated with the payment (string), useful for bank reconciliation.
bank_ref_no.analysis_use: Join or match against bank statement imports, group to detect duplicates or mapping issues, and filter suspicious or missing bank references.
business_questions: Which payments correspond to a specific bank reference (for reconciliation)?
Are there duplicate bank_ref_no values indicating same bank transfer recorded multiple times?
bank_ref_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; exact matches are typical. Be cautious of missing/NULL values. Use equality or pattern LIKE for partial matches; preserve leading zeros if present (strings).

status.description: Payment processing outcome (SUCCESS, PENDING, FAILED) stored as text.
status.analysis_use: Primary categorical outcome for filtering and grouping to compute success/failure rates, trend of failed payments, and identifying payments requiring action.
business_questions: What percentage of payments are successful vs failed vs pending?
How many failed payments occurred in the last 30 days and who are the customers?
status.nl2sql_hints: Use exact matches or IN lists (e.g., WHERE status = 'FAILED'). Low cardinality; consider mapping synonyms (e.g., 'SUCCEEDED' vs 'SUCCESS') if present in other tables. Use status together with payment_mode and reconciled_flag for richer queries.

reconciled_flag.description: Indicator whether payment has been reconciled ('1' or '0' stored as text).
reconciled_flag.analysis_use: Filter payments by reconciliation status, compute reconciliation rates, and find unreconciled payments for follow-up.
business_questions: How many payments remain unreconciled?
Which customers/invoices have unreconciled payments older than X days?
reconciled_flag.nl2sql_hints: Map '1'/'0' to boolean in NL2SQL (e.g., reconciled_flag = '1' OR CAST(reconciled_flag AS BIT)). Normalize NULL or other values. Prefer boolean semantics in generated SQL to simplify predicates.

reconciled_at.description: Timestamp when the payment was reconciled, stored as string in the same format as payment_ts.
reconciled_at.analysis_use: Compute reconciliation latency (reconciled_at - payment_ts), filter by reconciliation date ranges, and time-to-reconcile SLAs.
business_questions: What is the average time to reconcile payments?
How many payments were reconciled within SLA (e.g., 48 hours)?
reconciled_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for comparisons and arithmetic. Use TRY_CAST/COALESCE to handle NULLs (unreconciled rows). When computing latency, ensure both payment_ts and reconciled_at are parsed safely.

created_by.description: User or system who recorded the payment row (string).
created_by.analysis_use: Audit/filter writes by user, detect manual entries vs automated imports, and group counts by creator for operational metrics.
business_questions: Which users entered or modified the most payment records?
Are manual entries associated with higher failure or unreconciled rates?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text. Use exact or partial matches for names. If mapping to user ids is required, join to user directory (pos_users or internal user table). Watch for inconsistent name formatting; normalize (TRIM/UPPER) in NL2SQL or ETL.

notes.description: Free-text notes related to the payment (currently all NULL in sample).
notes.analysis_use: Limited in current dataset (100% NULL). If populated, used for manual exception comments, audit trails, and text-based searches for remarks.
business_questions: Are there notes explaining failed or manual payments?
Which payments have exception notes indicating follow-up is required?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Expect sparse values. Use LIKE for text search (WHERE notes LIKE '%refund%'). Consider full-text search if notes become populated and large. Handle NULLs explicitly in predicates.

picking_batch_id.description: Unique identifier for the picking batch (string).
picking_batch_id.analysis_use: Row identifier for grouping, deduplication and joining to any picking-batch-level detail tables (lines, allocations). Ideal primary key for batch-level aggregation.
business_questions: How many unique picking batches were processed in a date range?
Show all batches linked to a specific route or picker.
Which picking_batch_ids correspond to failed or incomplete batches?
picking_batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key; frequently used in equality filters and joins. Although stored as nvarchar, expect uniqueness — consider casting to a stable key type or index. Use exact matches (WHERE picking_batch_id = 'PB...') rather than LIKE unless searching prefixes.

centre_id.description: Identifier of the fulfilment centre where the batch was created (string).
centre_id.analysis_use: Common grouping/filter dimension for performance by location; low cardinality => good candidate for GROUP BY and bitmap/indexing. Useful for partitioning large tables.
business_questions: Which centres produce the most picking batches?
What are average durations and completion rates by centre?
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar but behaves like a categorical dimension. Use equality and IN filters (WHERE centre_id = 'CTR_VDR_001' or IN (...)). Consider join to centre metadata tables on centre_id for human-readable names.

picker_id.description: Identifier of the picker (employee) who performed the batch (string).
picker_id.analysis_use: Key for productivity and performance analysis: group by picker to compute avg duration, lines per hour, variance between expected/actual. Useful to correlate individual performance.
business_questions: Which pickers have the highest average lines picked per hour?
Which pickers have the highest mismatch between expected_lines and actual_lines?
picker_id.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN for filtering and GROUP BY for ranking. Join to employee/user tables for names and attributes. Stored as nvarchar — treat as categorical ID; watch for inconsistent formats or leading/trailing spaces when matching other tables.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the picking batch was created (string in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
created_at.analysis_use: Filter and time-series grouping (day/week/month/hour). Used for ingestion timelines, backlog and SLA measurement from creation to start/finish.
business_questions: How many batches were created per day/week/month?
What is average time from creation to start?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Column is stored as nvarchar; cast/parse to proper datetime in queries (e.g., CAST(created_at AS datetime) or TO_TIMESTAMP). Use normalized timezone assumptions and consistent parsing. For performance, convert to datetime at ingestion or create computed column for filtering on ranges.

started_at.description: Timestamp when picking for this batch actually started (string).
started_at.analysis_use: Calculate active picking duration (started_at to finished_at), measure delays from creation to start, and compute picker idle/wait metrics.
business_questions: What is the average delay between batch creation and start by centre?
Which batches had unusually long wait times before starting?
started_at.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar; must be parsed to datetime for arithmetic. Some rows may be NULL for batches not yet started — use safe null-aware calculations. Prefer indexing on parsed datetime for range queries if table grows.

finished_at.description: Timestamp when picking for this batch finished (string).
finished_at.analysis_use: Primary for throughput and duration calculations; used to determine completion status and SLA adherence. Combine with started_at/created_at for lifecycle metrics.
business_questions: What is the distribution of picking durations (finished_at - started_at)?
What percentage of batches finish within SLA thresholds per centre?
finished_at.nl2sql_hints: Contains nvarchar timestamps — cast to datetime before computing durations. Null values indicate incomplete batches; filter status or NULL finished_at accordingly. For NL2SQL, handle expressions like DATEDIFF or TIMESTAMPDIFF after casting.

status.description: Lifecycle state of a picking batch (CREATED, IN_PROGRESS, COMPLETED).
status.analysis_use: Categorical filter for operational views and KPIs (counts by status, aging of CREATED/IN_PROGRESS batches). Low-cardinality dimension good for group-by and dashboard filters.
business_questions: How many batches are currently IN_PROGRESS or CREATED (work backlog)?
What is the completion rate over time (COMPLETED vs total batches)?
status.nl2sql_hints: Use exact matches (WHERE status = 'COMPLETED') and IN for multi-status filters. Ensure case sensitivity matches DB collation. Consider mapping statuses to ordered states for SLA logic.

expected_lines.description: Number of lines expected to be picked in the batch (stored as string but numeric semantically).
expected_lines.analysis_use: Measure planned workload; used to compute expected vs actual performance, average lines per batch and capacity planning.
business_questions: What is the average expected_lines per batch by centre or route?
How often do actual_lines fall short of expected_lines?
expected_lines.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; cast to integer for aggregation and arithmetic (e.g., CAST(expected_lines AS INT)). Beware of non-numeric or NULL values; use safe casts or TRY_CAST where supported. Grouping by this as a numeric bucket is useful (e.g., expected_lines <= 5).

actual_lines.description: Number of lines actually picked in the batch (stored as string but numeric semantically).
actual_lines.analysis_use: Primary measure of throughput and picking accuracy; used in variance calculations, productivity (lines/hour) and reconciliation against expected_lines.
business_questions: Which batches or pickers consistently pick more or fewer lines than expected?
What is the average actual_lines per hour or per batch across centres?
actual_lines.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for aggregations and comparisons. Consider creating derived columns like lines_variance = CAST(actual_lines AS INT) - CAST(expected_lines AS INT). Handle NULLs and bad values with COALESCE/TRY_CAST.

total_kg.description: Total weight of the batch in kilograms (stored as string, decimal values present).
total_kg.analysis_use: Used for weight-based capacity planning, transport/load balancing, and to compute kg per line. Useful for reconciling shipments and load planning per route/vehicle.
business_questions: What is average total_kg per batch by route or centre?
Is transport_priority correlated with heavier batches?
total_kg.nl2sql_hints: Contains decimal numeric text — cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for sums/averages (e.g., CAST(total_kg AS DECIMAL(12,3))). Watch for localization (commas vs dots) and potential trailing precision artifacts. For NL2SQL, always cast before numeric aggregation.

route_id.description: Identifier of the delivery route associated with the batch (string).
route_id.analysis_use: Dimension for route-level batching and load analysis; group to see route utilisation, average weight/lines per route, and to identify bottleneck routes.
business_questions: Which routes have the highest number of batches and total weight?
Which routes have the highest average picking durations?
route_id.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN for join/filter operations. May need normalization (join to route master table) to get attributes like vehicle, driver, or destination area. Might contain NULLs when route not yet assigned.

transport_priority.description: Priority level for transporting the batch (HIGH or NORMAL).
transport_priority.analysis_use: Binary categorical flag for SLA and prioritization analysis — compare completion rates and durations between HIGH and NORMAL priority batches.
business_questions: Do HIGH priority batches complete faster than NORMAL ones?
What proportion of HIGH priority batches are still IN_PROGRESS or CREATED?
transport_priority.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string — use equality filtering. For NL2SQL, consider mapping to boolean or numeric priority scores for ordering. Ensure consistent values (e.g., trim spaces and consistent case).

created_by.description: Person or system user who created the picking batch (string, human-readable name).
created_by.analysis_use: Audit dimension and for user-level operational monitoring (who creates most batches), reconciling system vs manual batch creation and identifying creator-related patterns.
business_questions: Which users create the most batches and are their batches more/less likely to complete?
Is there a relationship between creator and batch size or priority?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: High-cardinality human-readable string — joins to user/employee tables should use normalized user id if available; beware of variations in name spelling. Use exact matches or full-text search when natural-language names are used in queries.

api_client_id.description: Unique identifier for the API client (human-friendly code like 'API6001').
api_client_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering key for client-level reports; used to identify rows in lists and to join to any client-specific logs if available. Useful for counts, existence checks, and deduplication.
business_questions: List all API clients for a given customer.
Show details for a specific api_client_id (rate limit, scopes, last_used).
api_client_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as the natural primary key though not enforced. It's nvarchar; compare with exact string matches. When users reference 'client id' map to this column. Consider indexing api_client_id for point lookups.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer that owns the API client (e.g., 'CUST1001').
customer_id.analysis_use: Used to group clients by customer, join to customers table to enrich with names/segments, and filter clients for a given customer. Useful in aggregations (clients per customer).
business_questions: How many API clients does a given customer have?
Which customers have API clients that are inactive?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Although not declared as FK, treat as join key to b2b_customers or ecom_customers. Use exact string equality. Consider indexing for frequent join/filter operations.

api_key_hash.description: Hashed API key value (opaque; used for verification but not reversible).
api_key_hash.analysis_use: High-cardinality identifier for credential audits; used in security/audit reports to show unique keys per client and to detect duplicate hashes (unlikely). Not usable for joins to other tables.
business_questions: Are there any duplicate api_key_hash values (possible leak or reuse)?
When was an api_key_hash last used (by mapping to last_used)?
api_key_hash.nl2sql_hints: Opaque hashed strings; do not attempt to parse. Avoid displaying full hashes in UI; mask if necessary. For NL2SQL, treat as string equality only and do not expect joins. Use COUNT(DISTINCT) for uniqueness checks.

secret_hash.description: Hashed secret associated with the API client (opaque credential material).
secret_hash.analysis_use: Security/audit column to detect reuse/duplicates and to list clients with secrets. Can be counted or checked for uniqueness; not for joining.
business_questions: Are any clients sharing the same secret_hash?
Which clients have secrets created before a given date (via created_at)?
secret_hash.nl2sql_hints: Opaque nvarchar; treat as string. Do not include in outputs that require readable secrets. Use only equality or aggregation functions (COUNT, DISTINCT).

allowed_ips_json.description: JSON array of allowed IP addresses for the client (currently all rows contain []).
allowed_ips_json.analysis_use: Filter clients that have IP restrictions vs none. Since column is constant in this snapshot, limited analytical value but important for policy enforcement checks.
business_questions: Which clients have IP allowlists configured?
How many clients have non-empty allowed_ips_json?
allowed_ips_json.nl2sql_hints: Value is a JSON string stored as nvarchar. For NL2SQL: if user asks for 'clients with IP restrictions' translate to WHERE allowed_ips_json NOT IN ('[]', '') or JSON length > 0 if DB supports JSON. Expect most rows to be '[]' here.

rate_limit.description: Rate limit quota for the client (stored as nvarchar but represents numeric limits like 100/200/500 requests).
rate_limit.analysis_use: Key aggregatable metric: distribution of quotas, grouping by rate_limit for counts, filtering high/low quota clients, correlating quota with active usage.
business_questions: How many clients have rate_limit 500?
What is the distribution of clients by rate_limit?
rate_limit.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; cast to integer for numeric comparisons and ordering (e.g., CAST(rate_limit AS INT)). Filter values are exact (100, 200, 500). Ensure to handle NULLs and unexpected values; use safe cast or TRY_CAST where supported.

scopes_json.description: JSON array describing authorized scopes for the client (in this dataset always ["orders","inventory"]).
scopes_json.analysis_use: Filter/group clients by scopes (e.g., clients allowed to access 'orders'). Useful for access audits and scope-based usage analyses.
business_questions: Which clients have 'orders' scope?
How many clients have both 'orders' and 'inventory' scopes?
scopes_json.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar JSON. For NL2SQL: when users ask about scope membership, translate to JSON_CONTAINS/scalar LIKE '%"orders"%' or JSON functions where available. Note that in this snapshot scopes are identical for all rows, so scope filters will usually return all rows.

created_at.description: Timestamp (stored as nvarchar) when the client was created (ISO-like format).
created_at.analysis_use: Used for onboarding/time-series analyses (clients created over time), cohorting, and retention analysis (compare created_at to last_used). Requires casting to datetime for range filters and ordering.
business_questions: How many clients were created in the last 30 days?
What is the trend of new API client creation by week/month?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; convert to DATETIME (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE) for date filters, grouping by date parts, and computing age. Parse errors possible; guard with TRY_CAST or validate format in NL2SQL translations.

last_used.description: Timestamp (stored as nvarchar) of the last observed use of the API client.
last_used.analysis_use: Primary signal for activity/staleness. Use to find unused clients, compute days since last use, and to filter active/inactive by recent usage. Requires casting to datetime to do date arithmetic, ordering and range filtering.
business_questions: Which clients have not been used in the last 90 days?
Show the top 10 most recently used API clients.
last_used.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar; CAST/TRY_CAST to DATETIME for comparisons and date math. When users ask 'never used', check for NULL/empty last_used or compare created_at==last_used patterns. Consider timezone expectations if present.

active_flag.description: Flag indicating if the client is active ('1') or inactive/disabled ('0') stored as nvarchar.
active_flag.analysis_use: Primary filter for enabled clients. Used in counts, filtering for operational clients in audits, and to join with usage data to correlate active_flag with last_used.
business_questions: How many clients are active vs inactive?
List inactive clients that were last used within the past 30 days (possible accidental disable).
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat '1'/'0' as booleans. For NL2SQL translate phrases like 'active clients' to WHERE active_flag = '1'. Consider normalizing to boolean or tinyint; when comparing use string literal comparisons or cast to INT. Watch for NULLs or other flag encodings.

price_list_id.description: Identifier for the price list entry (string). Appears to be unique per row in this snapshot and likely the canonical id for a price rule.
price_list_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for lookups, grouping by price_list_id for audits, detecting duplicates/versions. Can be used to join to any external audit/versioning table if present.
business_questions: Which price_list entries were created/modified within a date range?
Show details for a specific price_list_id (sku, customer, date range, price).
price_list_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string identifier. Prefer indexing for fast lookup if queries target specific price_list_id. Expect uniqueness but validate (use COUNT(*) GROUP BY price_list_id to detect duplicates).

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit — product identifier the price applies to.
sku.analysis_use: Filter and group by sku to compute per-product price statistics, join to product tables to get product names/attributes, and to order_lines to map prices to actual sales.
business_questions: What are historical prices for SKU LIM008 over the past 6 months?
Which SKUs have special customer-specific prices vs group prices?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Join key to products/product_variants/product_pricing; be careful about SKU normalization (case/suffix) — perform exact-match joins. Consider indexing on sku for frequent product lookups. For time-based price resolution, combine sku with start_ts/end_ts filters.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the specific customer the price is targeted at. Null likely indicates the absence of customer-specific price (could be group-level).
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter for customer-specific pricing, count how many custom prices per customer, and join to customers table to get customer attributes. Useful to determine overrides versus default/group pricing.
business_questions: Which customers have custom price lists and how many entries each has?
What price did customer CUST1047 receive for SKU TOM001 on 2024-11-20?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Use LEFT JOIN to customers table when enriching. When resolving applicable price for an order, prefer customer_id match first, then fall back to customer_group. Be aware of NULLs meaning 'not customer-specific'.

customer_group.description: The customer group the price applies to (e.g., RETAIL, WHOLESALE).
customer_group.analysis_use: Group-level aggregation and comparisons between groups, used as fallback when customer_id is NULL, and for segmentation analysis of pricing strategies.
business_questions: How do average prices differ between RETAIL and WHOLESALE across SKUs?
Which SKUs have different price_list entries for RETAIL vs WHOLESALE?
customer_group.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality (two values); queries should prefer predicate pushdown on customer_group when selecting group prices. Clarify precedence with customer_id: customer-specific prices usually override group prices.

start_ts.description: Start timestamp of the price validity window stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
start_ts.analysis_use: Filter price lists by effective date (e.g., active price at a given time), sort by validity, detect overlapping windows. Key for time-range queries and temporal joins to orders.
business_questions: What price lists become effective next week for SKU ONI003?
Which price entries were active on 2024-11-19 for customer CUST1005?
start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — convert to DATE/TIMESTAMP in NL2SQL conversions (e.g., CAST(start_ts AS DATETIME) or use TO_TIMESTAMP). Use inclusive/exclusive rules consistently (e.g., start <= order_ts < end). Index computed datetime column if many time-based queries exist.

end_ts.description: End timestamp of the price validity window stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
end_ts.analysis_use: Combined with start_ts to determine active prices and detect overlapping/conflicting entries. Use for scheduling future price changes.
business_questions: Which price entries will expire in the next 30 days?
Are there overlapping price windows for the same SKU and customer that must be resolved?
end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string — convert to proper TIMESTAMP/DATE. Be explicit about whether end_ts is inclusive/exclusive in queries. For range checks use start_ts <= X AND end_ts > X (or other agreed semantics).

price_per_unit.description: Unit price for the SKU under this price_list entry. Stored as nvarchar but represents a numeric monetary value.
price_per_unit.analysis_use: Aggregate (avg/min/max), compute revenue estimates when joined with quantities, compare against cost/pricing tables to compute margin, and detect price anomalies/outliers.
business_questions: What is the average price_per_unit for SKU POT002 across all price lists?
Which price_list entries have price_per_unit outside normal bounds for a SKU?
price_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric/decimal before arithmetic (e.g., CAST(price_per_unit AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Watch for inconsistent formatting (commas, trailing zeros). Consider normalization to a consistent precision and currency before aggregation.

currency.description: Currency code for the price (ISO code), stored as nvarchar.
currency.analysis_use: Filter/validate that pricing is in expected currency; required when converting prices or aggregating across currencies. In this snapshot it's constant (INR).
business_questions: Are all price_list entries in INR or are there multi-currency prices?
Flag price entries not in the default currency for review.
currency.nl2sql_hints: Expect short code (e.g., 'INR'). If multi-currency exists, normalize conversions in queries or join to FX rates. If constant, can be defaulted in application logic.

min_order_qty.description: Minimum order quantity required for the price to apply. Stored as nvarchar but represents integer quantity.
min_order_qty.analysis_use: Filter price applications based on ordered quantity, aggregate to understand typical MOQ levels, and detect high minimums that might block small orders.
business_questions: What is the typical min_order_qty for wholesale price lists vs retail?
Which price_list entries require a min_order_qty greater than 50?
min_order_qty.nl2sql_hints: CAST to integer for comparisons and numeric aggregates. Beware of null/empty strings. Consider default semantics (NULL vs 0) when applying price rules.

created_by.description: User that created the price_list entry. In this snapshot always 'admin'.
created_by.analysis_use: Audit who created entries, filter changes by user or system process; useful in governance and traceability.
business_questions: Which admin created price lists in July 2024?
How many price entries were created by a given user in a period?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: String column. If only system values exist, may not be useful; still include in audits. Consider joining to users table if available for richer metadata.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the price_list entry was created, stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and trend analysis of price list changes, detect recently created price rules, and order by creation time for history.
business_questions: Show price_list entries created between 2024-07-20 and 2024-08-01.
Which SKUs had price lists created most recently?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Convert to TIMESTAMP/DATE for comparisons and ordering. Use consistent timezone assumptions. If performing temporal analysis, create proper datetime typed column or cast at query time.

notes.description: Free-text notes for the price list entry. Currently entirely NULL in this snapshot.
notes.analysis_use: May hold human explanations for special pricing; useful for audits or manual review when present. Currently unusable (all null).
business_questions: Retrieve notes for a given price_list_id to understand reasoning for special price.
Which price_list entries include explanatory notes?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Free-text column — may require LIKE/ILIKE searches. Because it's empty in this data, queries should handle NULLs. If used for NL2SQL, advise that natural language queries referencing notes will only return results when not null.

sku.description: Product SKU identifier (string). Represents the stock-keeping unit for the product that the sales metrics apply to.
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for aggregations, ranking, filtering, and joins to product master and transactional tables. Use to join to product metadata (name, category, vendor) and order/order_line tables.
business_questions: Which SKUs are the top contributors to gross and net sales?
For a given SKU, what are the gross, discount, tax and net sales values?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string. Use exact match or IN-list filters for SKU. When joining, ensure both sides use the same collation and trimmed values (TRIM) if there may be padding. Use canonicalization (upper/lower) if SKUs have inconsistent casing. Consider adding an index on sku and normalizing to a single SKU key in production. Use backticks/brackets or quoting in SQL if the naming context requires it (e.g., [sku]).

Gross Sales.description: Total gross sales amount for the SKU before discounts and tax. Stored as nvarchar containing decimal numeric text.
Gross Sales.analysis_use: Must be CAST/CONVERT to a numeric type to SUM, AVG, ORDER BY, or compute ratios. Use for total revenue, contribution percentage, and base for computing discount/tax percentages.
business_questions: What is the total gross sales per SKU and across all SKUs?
Which SKUs have the fastest-growing gross sales (requires historical data or joins)?
Gross Sales.nl2sql_hints: Column name contains a space -- quote or bracket it (e.g., [Gross Sales]). Always CAST/TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT or use safe numeric conversion (e.g., TRY_CAST(REPLACE([Gross Sales], ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,2))) before numeric ops. Beware of NULLs and malformed strings. Consider creating a persisted computed numeric column or materialized numeric view to avoid repeated casting in queries.

Discount Amount.description: Total discount amount applied to the SKU (string representation of decimal). Represents absolute discount currency value.
Discount Amount.analysis_use: Convert to numeric to aggregate, compute discount rate = Discount Amount / Gross Sales, or to identify top discount recipients. Useful for margin analysis and promotion impact analysis.
business_questions: What is the discount amount and discount percentage for each SKU?
Which SKUs receive the largest discounts in absolute terms and relative to gross sales?
Discount Amount.nl2sql_hints: Quoted column name [Discount Amount]. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to decimal for SUM/AVG. Defensive coding: handle division by zero when computing percentages. Watch for negative discounts or returns encoded here; validate sign semantics. Consider trimming whitespace and removing thousand separators if present.

Tax Amount.description: Total tax amount charged on the SKU (stored as nvarchar decimal text).
Tax Amount.analysis_use: Cast to numeric to compute total tax by SKU, tax rate relative to gross (Tax Amount / Gross Sales), and to reconcile tax collected to tax rules or tax tables.
business_questions: How much tax is collected per SKU and what is the effective tax rate?
Are there SKUs with unexpected tax amounts (e.g., zero tax when taxable)?
Tax Amount.nl2sql_hints: Quoted column name [Tax Amount]. Use safe numeric conversion (TRY_CAST) before aggregation. Be cautious if tax logic depends on jurisdiction—store/join jurisdiction metadata when needed. Validate that Gross Sales minus Discount Amount plus Tax Amount equals documented Net Sales if that is the expected formula.

Net Sales.description: Net sales amount for the SKU after discounts and including tax where appropriate. Stored as nvarchar decimal text.
Net Sales.analysis_use: Important for final revenue figures and margin calculations. Cast to numeric to sum net revenue, compute net-to-gross ratios, and rank SKUs by realized revenue.
business_questions: What is the net sales per SKU and how does it compare to gross sales?
Which SKUs contribute most to net revenue after discounts and taxes?
Net Sales.nl2sql_hints: Quoted column name [Net Sales]. Convert to numeric with TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to avoid runtime errors. Verify arithmetic consistency (Gross Sales - Discount Amount + Tax Amount ?= Net Sales) and handle rounding differences. Consider creating derived numeric columns and normalizing column names to remove spaces for easier NL2SQL parsing.

date.description: The reporting date for the metrics in the row (stored as nvarchar, observed format YYYY-MM-DD; max length 10).
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for filtering and grouping (daily, weekly, monthly, yearly aggregations). Often cast to DATE/DATE/TIMESTAMP. Used for time window filters and joins to calendar or snapshot tables.
business_questions: Show net sales by date for product X between two dates.
Aggregate gross sales by month for reporting and trend analysis.
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a date field even though it's nvarchar. Convert using CAST(date AS DATE) or TRY_CAST depending on DB. Support common patterns: 'between YYYY-MM-DD and YYYY-MM-DD', 'group by month(date)' (use DATE_TRUNC/FORMAT after casting). Handle invalid/null strings with TRY_CAST and default behavior.

product_id.description: SKU or product identifier (nvarchar up to length 6 observed) representing the product associated with the metrics.
product_id.analysis_use: Dimension used for grouping, filtering, and joining to product master tables (b2b_products/products). Low cardinality makes it efficient for GROUP BY and pivot operations.
business_questions: Which products contributed most to net sales in Q1?
Filter the time-series to show only product ONI003.
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Used as the natural join key to product master tables; be aware of possible mismatches in casing or whitespace. Use TRIM/UPPER when matching. Because cardinality is low, queries filtering by product_id are selective but not highly selective; consider adding an index on (product_id, date) for frequent time series queries.

total_orders.description: Number of orders for the product on the given date (stored as nvarchar). Could be integer or decimal formatted as text.
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) and average (AVG) after casting to integer/decimal. Useful for order frequency metrics, computing orders per day, and deriving per-order averages.
business_questions: How many orders did product GUR010 receive last month?
What is the average orders per day for product BRN007 over the last quarter?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Convert to integer with CAST/TRY_CAST. Handle thousand separators or non-numeric characters if present (use REPLACE or REGEXP to strip). NULLs should be treated as 0 for sums when appropriate. Use SUM(CAST(total_orders AS INT)) and guard with TRY_CAST in NL2SQL templates.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the product on the date (stored as nvarchar).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Numeric aggregations (SUM/AVG) for volume-based KPIs, compute unit price per kg (net_sales / total_qty_kg), and compute inventory/fulfillment planning metrics.
business_questions: What total kg of POT002 was sold last week?
What is the average kg per order for product CAR004?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC after removing unit suffixes if present. Watch out for decimal separators (comma vs period). Use NULL-safe division when computing derived metrics (e.g., CASE WHEN total_qty_kg=0 THEN NULL ELSE net_sales/total_qty_kg END).

gross_sales.description: Gross monetary sales for the product before discounts and taxes on that date (stored as nvarchar).
gross_sales.analysis_use: SUM/AVG for revenue reporting, compute discount percent = discount_amount / gross_sales, and compare gross vs net. Often converted to DECIMAL(18,2).
business_questions: Which day had the highest gross sales for product ONI003?
What is the monthly gross sales for all products combined?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Strip currency symbols and thousand separators before CAST to DECIMAL. Use TRY_CAST or SAFE_CAST to avoid failures on malformed strings. Consider storing currency and decimals in a consistent numeric type during ETL for reliable NL2SQL parsing.

discount_amount.description: Total discount applied to the product's sales on that date (stored as nvarchar).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate discounts, compute discount percentage vs gross_sales, analyze promotional impact, and compute net sales reconciliation (gross - discount + tax = net ideally).
business_questions: How much total discount was applied to product BRN007 this month?
What percentage of gross sales did discounts represent for POT002 last quarter?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Same cleaning as other monetary fields: remove symbols/commas and CAST to DECIMAL. For percent calculations, ensure gross_sales is non-zero and use NULL-safe expressions. Watch for negative discounts (surcharges) encoded as parentheses or leading '-' characters.

tax_amount.description: Tax collected on the product's sales for the date (stored as nvarchar).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liability, compute tax as a percentage of net/gross, and reconcile tax across channels. Useful for compliance and tax reporting.
business_questions: Total tax collected for product GUR010 in the previous fiscal year?
What is the average tax rate applied to product CAR004 (tax_amount / gross_sales)?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Parse and cast to DECIMAL. Use consistent scale for monetary calculations. When generating NL2SQL, include logic to handle zeros and NULLs and to compute rates defensively (e.g., CASE WHEN gross_sales IS NULL OR gross_sales=0 THEN NULL ELSE tax_amount/gross_sales END).

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for the product on the date after discounts and taxes (stored as nvarchar).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for performance (SUM, trend analysis, top-N product by net sales). Used in profitability, commissions, and KPI calculations (e.g., revenue per kg, revenue per order).
business_questions: Which product had the highest net sales yesterday?
Trend of net sales by product over the last 12 months.
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Store and treat as DECIMAL for accurate aggregations. Validate that net_sales reconciles with gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount; use numeric casts and tolerances for reconciliation checks. In NL2SQL generation, prefer explicit casting: CAST(net_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2)) or TRY_CAST to avoid query failures.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit — internal product identifier (nvarchar).
sku.analysis_use: Primary natural key for product-level grouping and joins to transactional tables; used for filtering, counting distinct products and mapping inventory to sales/orders.
business_questions: How many distinct SKUs do we currently stock?
Show inventory, price and last update for SKU = 'APP009'.
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as an identifier string. Join candidate to order_lines, product_sales, product_variants on sku. Watch for case/whitespace differences; apply TRIM/UPPER normalization if dataset mixing occurs. Do not cast to numeric.

plucode.description: PLU (Price Look-Up) code — alternate product identifier often used at POS (nvarchar).
plucode.analysis_use: Useful for joining with POS tables and legacy systems, aggregating by PLU, detecting duplicate SKUs sharing PLU, and mapping POS sales to inventory.
business_questions: Which SKUs map to the same PLU?
Show POS sales joined to inventory by plucode for reconciliation.
plucode.nl2sql_hints: Also an identifier string — join key to pos_transactions/pos_product_wise_sales. Normalize (TRIM) before join. Some PLU systems are numeric-looking; keep as string for joins but cast when needed for numeric filters.

name.analysis_use: Used for display, text-based filtering (LIKE), grouping by product family or keywords, and deriving category by name parsing. Avoid using as reliable join key.
business_questions: List all products whose name contains 'Apple' with stock < reorder_point.
What are the top product name patterns by inventory value?
name.nl2sql_hints: Text column — commonly filtered with LIKE / ILIKE. Consider stemming or tokenization for natural language queries. Do not assume uniqueness. Use name for presentation and pattern matching only.

uom.description: Unit of measure for the product (e.g., kg, 250g, 500g) stored as nvarchar.
uom.analysis_use: Group and aggregate inventory/price by unit measure, normalize stock quantities to a base unit if comparing across UOMs, and filter by packaging size.
business_questions: How much inventory do we hold in kg vs package sizes?
List all products sold in '250g' packaging that are low on stock.
uom.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality; good candidate for GROUP BY. For quantitative comparisons across UOMs, NL2SQL should trigger unit conversions — require UOM conversion table or rules. Use equality or IN filters for fast predicates.

pos_price.description: POS price as stored text (nvarchar) — numeric semantic representing selling price.
pos_price.analysis_use: Numeric operations (AVG, SUM, price * stock_qty for inventory value) after casting to DECIMAL/NUMERIC. Used for price checks, price variance analysis and revenue estimation when joined with sales volume.
business_questions: What is the average pos_price across all active products?
Compute total inventory value (sum(pos_price * stock_qty)).
pos_price.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to numeric (e.g., CAST(pos_price AS DECIMAL(12,2)) or SAFE_CAST) before arithmetic. Handle non-numeric anomalies and empty strings; provide fallback or filter invalid rows. Consider currency normalization if multiple currencies exist elsewhere.

tax_rate.description: Applicable tax rate for the product, stored as nvarchar but represents a numeric percent/decimal.
tax_rate.analysis_use: Used to compute tax amounts on sales and validate tax policy compliance. If constant, can be used as a filter or to join to tax rules.
business_questions: Which products have tax_rate not equal to the expected 5%?
Calculate expected tax liability for inventory if sold (pos_price * tax_rate * stock_qty).
tax_rate.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before math. The sample shows a single value (‘5.0000000000000003’); NL2SQL should normalize to a human-friendly representation (e.g., 0.05 or 5%). Be cautious of trailing zeros/precision; use ROUND when presenting.

stock_qty.description: Current stock quantity on hand, stored as nvarchar but semantically integer/decimal.
stock_qty.analysis_use: Primary inventory measure — filter for low stock, compute reorder needs, aggregate total stock, and multiply with price for inventory valuation. Use in joins for availability checks.
business_questions: List products with stock_qty <= reorder_point.
What is the total stock quantity of all active products in kg?
stock_qty.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INTEGER/DECIMAL before comparisons and arithmetic. Handle negative or non-numeric entries defensively. If UOM varies, convert quantities to a common base unit for aggregate metrics.

reorder_point.description: Threshold quantity at which product should be reordered (nvarchar representing numeric).
reorder_point.analysis_use: Used in comparison with stock_qty to generate replenishment alerts and prioritize procurement. Also used in historical analysis if tracked over time.
business_questions: Which products should be reordered now (stock_qty <= reorder_point)?
How many SKUs have reorder_point > X indicating higher safety stock?
reorder_point.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INTEGER/DECIMAL for comparisons. Missing or zero values may indicate no reorder policy; treat accordingly. Use in WHERE clauses for boolean low-stock filters.

active_flag.description: Activity indicator stored as nvarchar (sample value '1' for active).
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter active vs inactive products in queries; control visibility in product lists and joins to sales or pricing logic.
business_questions: How many active products are in the catalog?
Show inactive products that nonetheless have positive stock_qty.
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Convert to BOOLEAN/INT (e.g., CASE WHEN active_flag IN ('1','true','t') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END). Watch for other encodings (Y/N, 0/1). Use as a fast filter in WHERE clauses.

updated_at.description: Last update timestamp stored as nvarchar (ISO-like datetime strings).
updated_at.analysis_use: Recency/freshness checks; partitioning/filtering by update date; temporal analyses to detect stale product data or recent price/stock changes.
business_questions: Which products were updated in the last 7 days?
Find products with no updates in the last 90 days (possibly stale).
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Parse into DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., TRY_CAST(updated_at AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP). Ensure consistent timezone assumptions if present. For NL2SQL date-range requests, prefer converting column once and using indexed datetime predicates where possible.

inspection_id.description: Unique identifier assigned to each inspection record (string). Appears nearly unique across rows.
inspection_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for de-duplication, counting inspections, and grouping by inspection sessions. Use for selecting a single inspection row.
business_questions: How many unique inspections were performed in a period?
Can we retrieve the full inspection record given an inspection_id?
inspection_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string in queries. Use equality or IN for direct lookups (WHERE inspection_id = 'INS16001'). Consider using it in DISTINCT/COUNT or as target of deduplication filters. No casting needed unless you normalize format.

batch_item_id.description: Identifier for the batch item or inventory batch being inspected (string).
batch_item_id.analysis_use: Join/filter key to map inspection outcomes to specific batches; group by to find batch-level failure rates or repeated inspections.
business_questions: Which batches have the highest rejection rates?
Are particular batch_item_id values repeatedly inspected or reworked?
batch_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Often used in equality joins (WHERE batch_item_id = 'B2BOL5022'). May require joining to product_inventory, picking_batches or order allocations tables. Indexing or filtering on this column yields efficient batch-level analysis.

order_line_id.description: Identifier of the order line associated with the inspected item (string).
order_line_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to orders/order_lines to attribute inspection results to specific orders, SKUs, customers or vendors; grouping for order-line level aggregations.
business_questions: Which order lines resulted in rejected items?
How many inspections are associated with a given order_line_id?
order_line_id.nl2sql_hints: Use for joins to b2b_order_lines or order_items. Because it's a string, use equality matching. When generating temporal aggregates by order, convert inspection_ts/created_at to datetime (see inspection_ts hints).

inspector_id.description: Identifier of the inspector who performed the inspection (string).
inspector_id.analysis_use: Group/filter to compute inspector-level metrics: counts, pass rates, average visual_score/temp, outlier detection.
business_questions: Which inspectors have the highest number of rejections?
What is each inspector's average visual_score and temperature readings?
inspector_id.nl2sql_hints: Joinable to a users/employees table if present for names/roles. Treat as categorical string; use GROUP BY inspector_id. Beware of minor variations in ID format; consider trimming whitespace.

inspection_ts.description: Timestamp when the inspection took place, stored as nvarchar but representing a datetime (e.g., '2025-11-04 12:31:26').
inspection_ts.analysis_use: Critical for time series analysis, windowing (first/last inspection per item), and aligning inspections to dispatch/shipment events.
business_questions: How does acceptance rate vary by day/week/month?
Were failures clustered around specific time windows (shifts/days)?
inspection_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to native datetime for correct ordering and grouping (e.g., CAST(inspection_ts AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP). Handle invalid/null strings defensively (use TRY_CAST/TRY_PARSE). When filtering by date range, ensure you convert the string once (or create persisted computed column) for performance.

temp_c.description: Measured temperature in Celsius at inspection time, stored as string (values like '8.6199999999').
temp_c.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation: average/min/max temperatures, detect out-of-spec readings, correlate temperature with rejection or microbial tests.
business_questions: What is the average temperature of inspected items and how does it differ between accepted and rejected items?
Are temperature excursions predictive of rejections or specific corrective actions?
temp_c.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/decimal for aggregations (e.g., CAST(temp_c AS DECIMAL(10,4)) or TRY_CAST). Normalize precision. Consider truncation/rounding for reporting. Handle nulls and non-numeric values with TRY_CAST or CASE.

visual_score.description: Numeric visual inspection score stored as string (integers like '89', '99').
visual_score.analysis_use: Aggregate (AVG, MIN, MAX), compute distributions and thresholds for accept/reject logic, and compare by inspector or product.
business_questions: What is the distribution of visual scores for accepted vs rejected items?
Which inspectors or batches have unusually low visual scores?
visual_score.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer (e.g., CAST(visual_score AS INT) or TRY_CAST). Treat malformed or NULLs cautiously. Use GROUP BY on binned score ranges for histograms.

acceptance_flag.description: Binary-like flag indicating acceptance (values '1' or '0' stored as string).
acceptance_flag.analysis_use: Primary outcome metric — compute pass/fail rates, filter to accept/reject subsets, and use as dependent variable in correlations.
business_questions: What percentage of inspections are accepted vs rejected?
Which corrective actions are most common for rejected items?
acceptance_flag.nl2sql_hints: Normalize to boolean or integer (CASE WHEN acceptance_flag IN ('1','true','t') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END). When writing NL2SQL, interpret '1' as accepted. Use explicit casting to avoid lexicographic pitfalls.

rejection_reason.description: Text reason for rejection. In current data largely null; non-null values only show 'Spoilage'.
rejection_reason.analysis_use: Categorical analysis of failure causes, grouping counts by reason. Identify recurring root causes if populated.
business_questions: What are the top reasons for rejection?
How often is 'Spoilage' cited relative to other reasons?
rejection_reason.nl2sql_hints: Expect sparse values and many NULLs. Use COALESCE or IS NOT NULL filters. When searching text, use exact match or ILIKE for case-insensitive matches. Consider normalizing free-text reasons to a code table for robust joins.

photo_url.description: Link to inspection photo/evidence (string). Currently entirely NULL in the sample.
photo_url.analysis_use: When populated, used to retrieve/preview inspection images and verify visual inspection notes; used as enrichment for audits.
business_questions: How many inspections have photographic evidence attached?
Can we sample photos for audits of rejected items?
photo_url.nl2sql_hints: May contain URLs; treat as string. Check for NULL or empty string. If empty in dataset, exclude from joins. Use LENGTH(photo_url) > 0 or IS NOT NULL to filter populated rows.

microbial_test.description: Indicator if a microbial test was performed / result flag (values '0' or '1' stored as string).
microbial_test.analysis_use: Filter/group by microbial test presence or positive result, correlate with acceptance_flag and corrective_action, compute frequencies.
business_questions: What proportion of inspected items require microbial testing?
Do positive microbial tests correlate with higher rejection rates?
microbial_test.nl2sql_hints: Normalize to integer/boolean for aggregates. Clarify semantics: whether '1' means positive, test performed, or failed — confirm upstream. Use explicit conversion and document interpretation in queries.

corrective_action.description: Action taken after inspection for problematic items (categorical: 'Discount', 'Dispose', 'Repack').
corrective_action.analysis_use: Group and count corrective actions to understand remediation patterns and cost/impact; join to financial tables to estimate losses if needed.
business_questions: What corrective actions are most common for rejected items?
How often is disposal used vs discounting or repacking?
corrective_action.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; normalize values (trim, case). Use GROUP BY corrective_action and filter by acceptance_flag = '0' to analyze rejections. Map to a canonical code table if available.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp (string), likely when inspection row was created in the system.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time analyses: latency between inspection_ts and created_at, detect delayed entries, or use as ingestion watermark.
business_questions: What is the latency between inspection occurrence and record creation?
Are there periods with delayed ingestion of inspection records?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for comparisons (TRY_CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP). When computing latency, use TIMESTAMPDIFF or equivalent between CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) and CAST(inspection_ts AS DATETIME). Handle nulls and invalid formats.

remarks.description: Free text remarks for the inspection — currently all NULL in the sample.
remarks.analysis_use: Qualitative notes for investigations; can be used for text search or manual review when non-null.
business_questions: What remarks are provided for rejected inspections?
Can remarks be used to identify patterns not captured in structured fields?
remarks.nl2sql_hints: Expect sparse text. For NL2SQL, use ILIKE/LIKE or full-text search if supported. Consider normalizing or extracting keywords with preprocessing before relying on remarks in automated queries.

return_id.description: Unique identifier for the return event.
return_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, grouping returns by id, and linking return-level data to other systems; candidate PK or unique index.
business_questions: How many unique returns were processed in a period?
Can we identify duplicate or retried return requests by return_id?
return_id.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, treat as a unique identifier string. Use equality filters and GROUP BY on return_id. Recommend creating an index / PK on this column. Do not cast to numeric or date.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the return.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders/order_lines for product-level and order-level analytics, grouping returns by order, calculating returns per order, and filtering returns for a specific order.
business_questions: Which orders have returns and how many returns per order?
What is the financial impact (refunds) of returns per order?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; use as join key to b2b_orders (exact match). Expect many-to-one relationship (multiple returns per order possible). Indexing order_id improves join performance.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who initiated the return.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group and filter returns by customer for lifetime return metrics, customer-level refund totals and return frequency, and to join to customer metadata.
business_questions: Which customers have the highest total returns by weight or value?
Are specific customer segments experiencing more returns?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to b2b_customers. Treat as string; possible many returns per customer. Consider joining to customer segmentation tables to enrich analysis.

requested_at.description: Timestamp when the return was requested (stored as nvarchar).
requested_at.analysis_use: Time-based filtering and grouping (by day/week/month), compute request volumes, seasonality, and latency metrics when paired with received_at.
business_questions: How many return requests are received per day/week/month?
Do return request volumes spike on certain dates or after campaigns?
requested_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format; cast to datetime (e.g., CAST(requested_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE) for temporal functions. NL2SQL should infer date parsing and use BETWEEN / date_trunc semantics. Ensure null-safe conversions.

received_at.description: Timestamp when the returned goods were received (stored as nvarchar).
received_at.analysis_use: Compute turnaround time (received_at - requested_at), filter completed receipts, and time-series analysis of received returns.
business_questions: What is the median/average time between return request and receipt?
Are certain dispositions or reasons associated with longer receipt times?
received_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string; cast to datetime for diffs and aggregations. When computing durations, handle missing or equal timestamps. Use safe casts (TRY_CAST) to avoid errors on malformed values.

return_status.description: Lifecycle status of the return (REQUESTED, APPROVED, REJECTED, COMPLETED).
return_status.analysis_use: Filter and group by state to track progress and funnel conversion (requested -> approved -> completed), compute counts and rates by status.
business_questions: What proportion of return requests are approved vs rejected?
How many returns are pending (requested) vs completed?
return_status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality enum-like string. Use exact matching and consider normalizing to a searchable enum in the NL2SQL layer. Map synonyms (e.g., 'complete' vs 'completed') and support case-insensitive comparisons.

reason_code.description: Categorized reason for return (EXCESS_QUANTITY, QUALITY, DAMAGED, WRONG_ITEM).
reason_code.analysis_use: Group to find most common causes, attribute weight/value to reasons, and prioritize process improvements or vendor issues.
business_questions: Which reason codes account for the highest returned weight and refund amounts?
Are certain reasons more likely to result in rejection or discard disposition?
reason_code.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; consider mapping to normalized reason table. Support GROUP BY reason_code and filtering. NL2SQL should accept synonyms and phrase mappings (e.g., 'wrong item' -> WRONG_ITEM).

qty_kg.description: Returned quantity in kilograms, stored as nvarchar containing numeric values.
qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate returned mass (SUM, AVG), detect outliers, compute refund per kg, and weigh operational capacity implications.
business_questions: What is the total returned weight over a period?
What is the average weight per return and are there outliers?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for aggregation (e.g., CAST(qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,4)) or TRY_CAST). Strings may have floating formatting; use locale-agnostic parsing. NL2SQL templates should automatically cast numeric-like nvarchars when aggregate functions are requested.

refund_amount.description: Monetary refund issued for the return, stored as nvarchar containing numeric values (floats).
refund_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate refund totals, compute average refund per return/ per kg, reconcile with credit notes, and measure financial impact.
business_questions: What is the total refund issued in a period and average refund per return?
Which returns produce the highest refunds (by order, customer, reason)?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL or MONEY before SUM/AVG (e.g., CAST(refund_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Watch for floating precision strings (many rows show long float representations). Use TRY_CAST to handle malformed values and enable currency formatting in presentation layer.

credit_note_id.description: Identifier of the credit note issued for the refund (nullable; references credit notes).
credit_note_id.analysis_use: Join to credit note table for reconciliation, filter returns that have been credited, and count unmatched refunds (returns without credit notes).
business_questions: How many returns have associated credit notes vs pending credit issuance?
What is the total refunded amount reconciled through credit notes?
credit_note_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as nullable join key to b2b_credit_notes. Strings may be repeated; do exact matches. Consider LEFT JOINs to find returns lacking credit_note_id. Indexing credit_note_id can speed reconciliation queries.

disposition.description: Post-inspection handling instruction for returned goods (RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER, RESTOCK, DISCARD).
disposition.analysis_use: Group returns by disposition to understand downstream operations (restocking vs disposal) and cost/handling implications; relate disposition to reasons/status.
business_questions: What percentage of returns are restocked vs discarded vs returned to supplier?
Which reasons lead to disposal (discard) rather than restock?
disposition.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality; treat as enum. Use GROUP BY disposition and joins to inventory/product tables if analyzing stock adjustments. Normalize values to support synonyms and case-insensitive matching.

inspected_by.description: Name of the inspector who processed the return inspection.
inspected_by.analysis_use: Attribute returns to inspectors to analyze inspector-level throughput, error rates, or discrepancies in disposition decisions.
business_questions: Which inspectors process the most returns by count and weight?
Do certain inspectors approve/reject more returns than peers?
inspected_by.nl2sql_hints: High-cardinality string field; consider joining to an employee table if available for consistent IDs. NL2SQL should support exact and fuzzy name matching (e.g., last name only) and grouping by inspector. Watch out for inconsistent name spellings.

notes.description: Short textual notes about the return (packaging torn, spoiled, customer complaint); low-cardinality in sample.
notes.analysis_use: Filter or group for common textual reasons, quick textual categorization, and support manual review. Useful for free-text search and tag extraction.
business_questions: What are the most common textual notes associated with returns?
Are certain notes (e.g., 'Packaging torn') correlated with specific reason_codes or dispositions?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Free text but low-cardinality in sample; use LIKE/ILIKE or full-text search for pattern matching. NL2SQL should map natural-language phrases (e.g., 'spoiled' -> Spoiled) to notes filter and consider normalizing common phrases into categories.

agent_id.description: Unique identifier for the sales agent (string).
agent_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to link agent metadata to transactional tables; exact-match filters; grouping by agent for per-agent aggregates; can be used as primary key after validation.
business_questions: Which transactions/orders are assigned to a given agent_id?
How many agents exist and is agent_id unique across the table?
agent_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical exact-match key. Trim whitespace and ensure consistent case before joining. Although nvarchar, agent_id appears unique — recommend casting/declaring as primary key in downstream schema for faster joins and indexing.

name.analysis_use: Display/lookup and grouping by agent. Useful for reporting and contact lists. Use with agent_id to present readable outputs.
business_questions: What is the contact name for agent_id X?
List agent names in region Y for outreach.
name.nl2sql_hints: Typically used in SELECT or ORDER BY. Support fuzzy or partial matches using LIKE for name-based searches. Be aware of potential duplicates or naming variations; join operations should rely on agent_id not name.

phone.description: Agent phone number (stored as string, includes country code/formatting).
phone.analysis_use: Contact lists, filtering for missing/invalid phone numbers, deduplication; not suitable for numeric aggregation. Can be normalized for consistent formatting.
business_questions: Which agents do not have a phone number on file?
Get phone numbers for all ACTIVE agents in region Z.
phone.nl2sql_hints: Use string operations for normalization (remove spaces, parentheses, dashes). Beware of international formats. Use IS NOT NULL and TRIM checks to filter missing values. Not appropriate as join key unless normalized and guaranteed unique.

email.analysis_use: Contact lists, filtering for missing/invalid emails, deduplication and email-targeted campaigns. Validate pattern with LIKE '%@%'.
business_questions: List email addresses for ACTIVE agents in specialization S.
Which agents have corporate emails vs missing/invalid emails?
email.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text; use LOWER() and TRIM() when matching. Validate with simple pattern matching (LIKE '%@%'). Do not rely on email for joins unless uniqueness and normalization are confirmed.

region.description: Geographic region or city the agent operates in (low cardinality).
region.analysis_use: Grouping, aggregation and segmentation (counts, averages) by geography; useful for regional performance or capacity planning. Candidate for indexing due to frequent GROUP BY/WHERE usage.
business_questions: How many ACTIVE agents are in each region?
What is the average commission rate for agents in Region R?
region.nl2sql_hints: Because it's low-cardinality, use region in GROUP BY and WHERE clauses. Expect a small, enumerated set — consider mapping typo variants. Normalize casing and trim before comparisons. Indexing or a dimension table could improve join performance for larger queries.

specialization.description: Agent business specialization (e.g., Caterer, HORECA, Retail Chain, Wholesale).
