Are there common searches with no matching products (zero-results) that need catalog updates?
search_query.nl2sql_hints: Text field — use exact grouping for cleaned terms or full-text / LIKE for partial matches. Normalize (lowercase, trim) and possibly map synonyms/stemmed forms. Join to normalized search keyword table if available. Handle NULLs for non-SEARCH events. Use COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE search_query IS NOT NULL) or WHERE event_type='SEARCH'.

device_type.analysis_use: Segment events by device for UX/marketing insights, device-specific funnels, and A/B testing analysis.
business_questions: Which devices generate the most browsing events and views?
Do conversion rates differ by device type (requires join to orders)?
device_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical column. Use in GROUP BY and WHERE filters. Normalize strings (lowercase) and coalesce NULLs into 'unknown'. Good candidate for pivoting. Be careful of typos or variants (e.g., 'iOS' vs 'ios').

referrer_source.description: Traffic source/referrer (FB Ads, WhatsApp, Google, Direct).
referrer_source.analysis_use: Attribution and channel performance: group by referrer to understand which channels drive searches, views, and later conversions when joined with orders.
business_questions: Which referrer sources drive the highest volume of product views and searches?
Which referrers bring higher-intent users (more VIEW_PRODUCT events per search)?
referrer_source.nl2sql_hints: Categorical column for GROUP BY and attribution analysis. Normalize names (e.g., 'FB Ads' vs 'Facebook Ads') and coalesce nulls. When combining with marketing campaign data, ensure consistent naming or map to canonical channel ids.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the record was created or ingested into the raw table (string). May differ from event_ts.
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for ingestion latency analysis, debugging reprocessing, and incremental ETL (e.g., selecting rows WHERE created_at > last_run). Also can serve as a fallback for event ordering if event_ts missing or malformed.
business_questions: What is the ingestion latency between event_ts and created_at?
Which events were ingested in the last ETL window?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar — CAST/TRY_CAST to DATETIME for time comparisons. Use created_at for incremental pulls: WHERE CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) > @last_checkpoint. Normalize timezone and handle malformed values. Prefer indexing or storing a parsed datetime column for frequent incremental queries.

cart_item_id.description: Surrogate identifier for the cart line item (unique id per item row).
cart_item_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de‑duplicating rows, tracking lifecycle of a specific cart entry, and joining back to raw logs. Useful in dedup and change-tracking queries.
business_questions: How many unique cart item records were created in a period?
Are there duplicate or repeated cart_item_id values indicating ingestion issues?
cart_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. Use equality joins and distinct counts. Since it appears unique, it can be used as a row identifier. No casting required, but trim whitespace; beware NULLs if present.

cart_id.description: Identifier for the shopping cart that contains this item (links the item to a multi‑item cart).
cart_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate items to produce cart-level metrics (items per cart, total cart value). Join key to ecom_carts and ecom_orders for customer, session and order enrichment.
business_questions: What is average number of items per cart (group by cart_id)?
Which carts have the highest nominal cart value at add time?
cart_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; commonly used in GROUP BY and joins. Expect many-to-one (multiple items per cart). When joining to ecom_carts use exact string match; consider normalizing case and trimming if mismatches occur.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit code identifying the product model at a SKU level (product-level identifier).
sku.analysis_use: Categorical grouping for popularity, frequency, and share-of-cart analyses; join to products/ecom_products for attributes (category, name). Useful for top-N queries.
business_questions: Which SKUs are added to carts most frequently?
Which SKUs contribute most to cart-added revenue (price_at_add * qty)?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text and highly categorical; use equality or IN filters. Join to product tables on sku (string equality). Consider mapping synonyms or trimming. Use GROUP BY sku for top-N queries.

qty.description: Quantity of the SKU added to the cart for this cart_item. Stored as nvarchar but represents an integer.
qty.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to compute total units added, average qty per item, and compute revenue when multiplied by price_at_add. Filter for extreme quantities or outliers.
business_questions: What is the total quantity added per SKU or per cart?
What is the distribution of quantities chosen at add time (e.g., proportion of single-item adds)?
qty.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to integer for numeric operations (e.g., CAST(qty AS INT) or SAFE_CAST). Handle non-numeric or NULL values defensively (use TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC patterns). Use in GROUP BY after casting when necessary.

added_at.description: Timestamp (string) when the item was added to the cart.
added_at.analysis_use: Time-series and funnel analyses: sessions of add events, hourly/daily patterns, lag from add to order, retention of cart items. Filter by date range for period analyses.
business_questions: What times of day / days of week see the most cart-add activity?
How long on average do items remain in a cart before being updated or converted?
added_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; parse to a datetime type (e.g., CAST(added_at AS DATETIME) or PARSE/TO_TIMESTAMP depending on DB). Ensure consistent format and handle malformed/missing strings. Use timezone awareness if required.

updated_at.description: Timestamp (string) of the last update to this cart item (quantity change, reservation change, etc.).
updated_at.analysis_use: Track modifications to cart items, compute time-to-update, detect churn or abandonment patterns, and deduplicate by latest update per cart_item_id.
business_questions: How often are cart items updated after initial add (qty changes, price changes)?
What is the typical time between item add and item update?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Same as added_at — parse to datetime before temporal comparisons. Use to identify latest state: e.g., SELECT ... WHERE updated_at = (SELECT MAX(updated_at) ...). Handle nulls and invalid formats.

reserved_flag.description: Binary flag stored as text indicating whether the item is reserved (values '1' or '0').
reserved_flag.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate by reservation state (counts and conversion comparisons), segment carts/items by reservation status, and compute reservation rates.
business_questions: What proportion of cart items are reserved versus not reserved?
Do reserved items convert to orders at a higher rate than non-reserved items?
reserved_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical/text but often castable to boolean/integer (CASE WHEN reserved_flag = '1' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END). Normalize values (trim, check for other representations). Use explicit comparisons to '1'/'0' to avoid truthiness issues.

price_at_add.description: The price recorded for the item at the time it was added to the cart (stored as nvarchar; integer-like values observed).
price_at_add.analysis_use: Compute nominal cart value (SUM(price_at_add * qty)), price bucket analyses, and monitor price changes by comparing to product price records. Useful for lost revenue and price sensitivity analyses.
business_questions: What is the total value added to carts by SKU/variant or over time?
How often do prices in carts differ from current product pricing (indicating price changes or promotions)?
price_at_add.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to numeric (INT or DECIMAL) before arithmetic. Beware of currency symbols or commas—clean strings first. Use COALESCE to handle NULLs. When joining to product_pricing, prefer casting both sides consistently.

variant_id.description: Identifier for the specific product variant (size/color/pack) added to the cart.
variant_id.analysis_use: Granular product-level analysis: variant popularity, inventory joins, SKU-to-variant mappings. Grouping by variant for detailed sales funnel metrics.
business_questions: Which variants are most frequently added and which variants have the highest cart conversion?
Are there variants with high add rates but no conversions (potential inventory or UX issues)?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to variant or inventory tables (product_variants, product_inventory). Stored as text — match exactly. Check for variant_id nulls and trim. When both sku and variant_id exist, prefer variant_id for variant-level joins.

notes.description: Free text notes for the cart item (user or system notes). Currently fully NULL in this dataset.
notes.analysis_use: When populated, can be used for qualitative analysis, text mining for reasons for notes (e.g., gift messages, special instructions) and anomaly detection. Currently no analytic value due to 100% nulls.
business_questions: Are customers leaving notes that correlate with conversion (e.g., gift messages leading to higher purchases)?
Do particular note patterns indicate friction or helpdesk issues?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nullable text; use LIKE/full-text search if populated. Expect many nulls—filter nulls out when scanning. May require trimming/normalization for text analysis.

cart_id.description: Unique identifier for a cart snapshot. Appears unique per row (distinct 100).
cart_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for cart-level aggregations, join key to cart_items and order tables for conversion/funnel analysis, used in de-duplication and event correlation.
business_questions: How many unique carts were created in a time window?
Which carts converted to orders (by joining to ecom_orders)?
What is the distribution of cart sizes and values per cart_id?
cart_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key in SQL. Use equality or IN predicates for lookups. Recommend adding an index or primary key on cart_id in production. When generating joins, explicitly join on zuna_seed_raw.ecom_carts.cart_id = ecom_cart_items.cart_id.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who owns or created the cart; distinct per row in sample.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group/filter to compute per-customer metrics (average cart value, abandonment rate), join to customer profile tables for segmentation and LTV analysis, and to measure repeat cart behaviour.
business_questions: Which customers have the highest average cart value?
How often do customers create carts but not convert (abandonment by customer)?
Which customer segments use coupons most frequently?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; join to ecom_customers on customer_id. Watch for guest/anonymous carts (customer_id could be empty in other datasets). Consider deduplicating multiple carts per customer when computing per-customer metrics (e.g., COUNT(DISTINCT cart_id)).

session_id.description: Identifier for browser/app session associated with the cart; useful to tie cart activity to session-level behaviours.
session_id.analysis_use: Join to browsing/session tables to correlate pageviews or product impressions with cart creation; group by session to find multi-cart sessions; filter to examine session-level abandonment.
business_questions: How many carts are created per session on average?
Do specific session behaviours (pageviews/time on site) correlate with abandonment?
session_id.nl2sql_hints: String type; join to ecom_browsing_history or session tables using exact equality. Sessions may span multiple carts—use COUNT(DISTINCT cart_id) when aggregating by session. Avoid implicitly casting session_id.

created_at.description: Timestamp (as text) when the cart was created. Pattern appears to be 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss'.
created_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trends, grouping (daily/weekly/monthly), time-based filters, cohort definitions and funnel time calculations (time-to-abandon). Requires casting to datetime/timestamp for date functions.
business_questions: How many carts were created per day/hour?
Are there day-of-week or hour-of-day patterns in cart creation?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; always CAST/CONVERT to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP in NL2SQL queries (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or use TO_TIMESTAMP with format). If filtering by date ranges, convert literals to datetime to avoid string-comparison pitfalls. Recommend migrating to a proper datetime type for production and indexing on created_at for time-range queries.

last_updated_at.description: Text timestamp for the latest update to the cart (item added/removed or other changes).
last_updated_at.analysis_use: Useful to calculate activity windows, session activity recency, and time between updates and abandonment/checkout. Use for ordering by recent behavior and for identifying stale carts.
business_questions: What is the average time between cart creation and last update?
How many carts were updated in the last X hours/days?
last_updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar; CAST to datetime for date arithmetic and window functions. When calculating durations, compute DATEDIFF between CAST(last_updated_at AS datetime) and CAST(created_at AS datetime).

total_items.description: Text field indicating the total count of items in the cart; low cardinality (8 distinct values) and small max length.
total_items.analysis_use: Aggregation and distribution analysis (SUM, AVG, histograms), segmentation (small vs large carts), and correlation with abandonment and total_amount. Convert to INTEGER for numeric operations.
business_questions: What is the distribution of cart sizes (item counts)?
Do larger carts have higher or lower abandonment rates?
total_items.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; CAST(total_items AS INT) before aggregations. Watch for malformed values or nulls. For grouping, consider bucketing (e.g., CASE WHEN CAST(total_items AS INT) >= X THEN 'large' ...).

total_amount.description: Monetary total for the cart stored as text with decimals (many distinct values).
total_amount.analysis_use: Key metric for revenue analysis at cart level: AVG, SUM, median, top-N carts. Used to compute ARPU, AOV, and to compare coupon impacts. Must be cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for safe aggregation.
business_questions: What is the average order value (AOV) at the cart stage?
How much potential revenue is held in abandoned carts?
total_amount.nl2sql_hints: Text type—CAST(total_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)) or appropriate precision before SUM/AVG. Be careful with locale/formatting (decimal point vs comma); dataset appears to use period. Null or empty strings should be handled (NULLIF/TRY_CAST) to avoid query errors. Consider storing currency-normalized amounts if multi-currency support is added.

coupon_code.description: Applied coupon identifier (e.g., NEWUSER, FRESH10). Low cardinality (2 values) with ~37% nulls (no coupon).
coupon_code.analysis_use: Segment carts by coupon usage to measure lift in cart value or reduction in abandonment. Filter to calculate counts/percentages of coupon usage and revenue impact.
business_questions: How does coupon usage affect average cart value and abandonment?
Which coupon codes drive the most incremental cart additions?
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string. Use COALESCE or explicit IS NULL checks for 'no coupon' segments. When comparing, watch for case-sensitivity depending on DB collation. JOINs are rare; treat as categorical in GROUP BY. For NL2SQL, allow synonyms like 'no coupon', 'null coupon', or specific codes.

currency.description: Currency code for the cart amounts (INR for all rows in this sample).
currency.analysis_use: Used to validate/normalize monetary aggregations and for cross-region comparisons. If single currency, can be ignored for numeric aggregations; otherwise requires normalization to a base currency.
business_questions: Are carts across currencies present and how should amounts be normalized?
Is there any regional pattern in currency usage tied to device or coupon?
currency.nl2sql_hints: Constant in this sample (INR). Still include in GROUP BY if mixing currencies. When currency is multi-valued, ensure total_amount is normalized before SUM across currencies.

device_type.description: Platform where cart was created: web, ios, android. Small number of categories.
device_type.analysis_use: Segment metrics (abandonment, AOV, conversion) by device to detect platform-specific issues or opportunities.
business_questions: Which device has the highest abandonment rate?
Does average cart value differ significantly between web and mobile?
device_type.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string—use GROUP BY device_type. Normalize variants (e.g., 'iOS' vs 'ios') in queries or preprocessing. When users ask 'mobile' vs 'desktop', map device_type values accordingly in NL2SQL translation.

abandoned_flag.description: Binary indicator (stored as string) denoting whether the cart was abandoned: '0' = not abandoned, '1' = abandoned in this sample.
abandoned_flag.analysis_use: Primary filter for abandonment analyses, computing abandonment rate, and joining to abandoned_at for timing. Use as boolean by mapping '1'/'0' or casting when appropriate.
business_questions: What proportion of carts are abandoned vs completed?
What is the average value of abandoned carts vs non-abandoned carts?
abandoned_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—treat '1' as abandoned. When writing NL2SQL, explicitly compare to string literal ('1') or convert to BIT/INTEGER using CAST. Be careful of other values (e.g., 'true'/'false') in different datasets. Recommend normalizing to a boolean type in ETL and indexing if frequently filtered.

abandoned_at.description: Timestamp (text) when the cart was marked abandoned. Many nulls (~59%) for carts not abandoned.
abandoned_at.analysis_use: Compute time-to-abandon (abandoned_at - created_at), abandonment timing distributions, and window-based abandonment counts. Requires casting to datetime and careful NULL handling.
business_questions: What is the distribution of time between cart creation and abandonment?
When (hour/day) do abandonments peak?
abandoned_at.nl2sql_hints: Text timestamp—CAST to datetime for DATEDIFF and time-based grouping. Handle NULLs: use WHERE abandoned_flag = '1' AND abandoned_at IS NOT NULL for abandonment calculations. Normalize timestamp formats and consider timezone implications if present.

coupon_usage_id.description: Unique identifier for this coupon usage event (string).
coupon_usage_id.analysis_use: Primary deduplication key to identify distinct usage rows; useful for counting distinct redemptions, de-duplicating import data, and joining to audit logs if available.
business_questions: How many distinct coupon usage records exist in the dataset?
Are there duplicate records for the same coupon usage id that need cleansing?
coupon_usage_id.nl2sql_hints: Treated as a string; may be nearly unique but not declared as PK. Use DISTINCT or COUNT(DISTINCT coupon_usage_id) to deduplicate. For joins, prefer coupon_usage_id only if other tables reference it (none declared). Consider enforcing uniqueness or creating a surrogate id if needed.

coupon_id.description: Identifier of the coupon that was applied (links to coupon metadata/campaign).
coupon_id.analysis_use: Common grouping and filtering dimension for redemption counts, sums of discounts, and per-coupon performance metrics. Join key to coupon master table for campaign details, eligibility and discount rules.
business_questions: Which coupons have the highest total discount given?
How many times was coupon COUP01007 redeemed last month?
coupon_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; join to ecom_coupons using equality on coupon_id. Use GROUP BY coupon_id for top-N queries. Be mindful of null coupon_id values; cast is unnecessary for joins but convert when joining to numeric coupon attributes in other tables.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who used the coupon (links to customer profile).
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter and group dimension to compute unique redeemers, repeat redemption behavior, churn/engagement segments, and to join with customer demographic or segmentation tables.
business_questions: How many unique customers used coupons in Q4 2024?
Which customers used coupons most frequently (top redeemers)?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — join to ecom_customers on customer_id. Use COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) to get unique redeemers. Watch for anonymized or truncated ids; handle nulls and leading/trailing whitespace. Consider joining on trimmed values if source data inconsistencies exist.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the coupon usage (links to order and revenue data).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to derive order-level revenue, items, and to compute discount as share of order total. Use for revenue attribution and verification (e.g., ensure coupon usage matches an actual order).
business_questions: What was the average order value for orders where a coupon was used?
Which orders had the largest discount amounts applied?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; join to ecom_orders on order_id. Validate that order_id is not null before joining. If multiple coupon_usage rows reference the same order (multi-coupon orders), take care when aggregating order totals (use DISTINCT order_id or join and allocate discounts appropriately).

applied_ts.description: Timestamp when the coupon was applied (stored as text, samples show 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
applied_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-series analysis (redemptions by hour/day/week/month), cohorting, and calculating latency between coupon issuance and redemption.
business_questions: What is the daily trend of coupon redemptions over the past 6 months?
How many coupons were redeemed during a specific promotion window?
applied_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; for temporal queries CAST/CONVERT to appropriate DATE/TIMESTAMP type (e.g., CAST(applied_ts AS DATETIME)) before grouping/filtering. Ensure format consistency; handle timezone assumptions (samples appear UTC-like but check). Use date_trunc or equivalent after casting for aggregation. Filter ranges should convert literals similarly.

discount_given.description: Monetary value of discount applied for this usage (stored as text representing numeric values).
discount_given.analysis_use: Numeric measure for aggregations: SUM(discount_given), AVG(discount_given), max/min per coupon/order/customer. Used to measure cost of promotions and per-order discount share.
business_questions: What is the total discount cost per coupon campaign?
What is the average discount given per redemption?
discount_given.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — cast to numeric/decimal before aggregation (e.g., CAST(discount_given AS DECIMAL(12,2))). Watch out for non-numeric values or localized decimal separators. Nulls or empty strings should be treated as zero or excluded depending on business rules. When joining to revenue, compute discount/total_order_value ratios carefully and deduplicate by order if needed.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the coupon usage record was created in the system (stored as text, sample format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/time-of-ingest metadata used to detect late-arriving records, ingestion lag (created_at vs applied_ts), and for ETL troubleshooting. Also used for temporal filtering if applied_ts is missing or unreliable.
business_questions: What is the lag between coupon application and record creation (to detect processing delays)?
Were there any coupon usage records created outside expected campaign windows?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; CAST/CONVERT to DATETIME for comparisons and computing time differences (e.g., DATEDIFF between applied_ts and created_at after casting). Use created_at when applied_ts is null or for ingestion-time logic. Ensure format consistency and handle timezone considerations.

coupon_id.description: Unique identifier for the coupon record (string).
coupon_id.analysis_use: Primary natural key for deduplication and joins to coupon usage or administrative systems; grouping and counting distinct coupons.
business_questions: How many unique coupons exist in the system?
Show details for coupon_id = 'COUP01001'.
Has this coupon_id been updated recently (compare created_at/updated_at)?
coupon_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as the primary join key to ecom_coupon_usage and marketing tables. Although nvarchar, values are stable and unique — consider using equality joins. No casting required for joins. Recommend declaring PK or index on coupon_id for performance.

coupon_code.description: Human-facing coupon code customers enter at checkout (string).
coupon_code.analysis_use: Filtering and grouping by code (report by coupon_code), linking to usage records that store coupon_code, and aggregating performance per code.
business_questions: Which coupon_code generated the most redemptions?
List coupon_codes that are active and valid today.
Are there duplicate coupon_codes in the table (should be unique)?
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Often used in user-facing queries; treat as candidate key. When joining to event/usage tables verify which side stores the code versus coupon_id. Use equality comparisons and consider an index on coupon_code. Watch out for case-sensitivity and whitespace; trim and normalize before comparing.

description.description: Short human-readable classification of the coupon (e.g., 'Percentage Discount' or 'Flat Discount').
description.analysis_use: Grouping and summarization to show distribution of discount styles; useful for labels in reports.
business_questions: What proportion of coupons are percentage vs flat discounts?
How do average discount_value and max_discount_amount differ by description?
description.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality text field. Use simple equality/group-by. Might be redundant with discount_type; prefer discount_type for standardized logic. Normalize values if inconsistent before grouping.

discount_type.description: Indicates calculation method for discount (e.g., 'PERCENT' or 'FLAT').
discount_type.analysis_use: Filter or group queries by type to compute type-specific aggregates (mean discount value, max cap behavior). Important when calculating applied discount logic in joins with orders.
business_questions: How many PERCENT coupons vs FLAT coupons are active?
What is the average discount_value for PERCENT coupons?
discount_type.nl2sql_hints: Use in conditional logic (CASE) when computing expected discount amounts. Low cardinality — safe to index if frequently filtered. Values are text; ensure exact matches and consistent casing.

discount_value.description: Numeric value representing discount magnitude (percentage or flat amount) stored as text.
discount_value.analysis_use: Must be cast to numeric for aggregations (AVG, SUM, MAX). Used in formulas to compute expected discount amounts when joined to order totals.
business_questions: What is the distribution of discount_value across coupons?
Which coupons offer discount_value >= 30?
discount_value.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; in NL2SQL cast to integer/decimal (e.g., CAST(discount_value AS DECIMAL(10,2))). Validate non-numeric anomalies or NULLs. When discount_type = 'PERCENT', interpret as percentage; for FLAT treat as currency. Consider creating a numeric column to avoid repeated casting.

max_discount_amount.description: Maximum cap on discount amount (numeric stored as text) applied to percent discounts or absolute cap for promotions.
max_discount_amount.analysis_use: Cast to numeric for aggregations and final discount computations; used to compute effective discount given order totals and discount rules.
business_questions: Which coupons have a max_discount_amount >= 100?
For PERCENT coupons, what is the typical cap and how many exceed a certain cap?
max_discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Text field containing numeric values — CAST to numeric before arithmetic. Nulls or missing caps may indicate unlimited; define business rule. Consider data-cleaning and type migration to numeric for performance.

valid_from.description: Start of coupon validity window stored as a text timestamp.
valid_from.analysis_use: Filtering by date ranges to determine active coupons, grouping by validity period, time-based lifecycle queries. Requires casting to datetime.
business_questions: Which coupons became active in the last 30 days?
Show coupons with valid_from before a given date.
valid_from.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar with ISO-like timestamps — use CAST/CONVERT to proper datetime (e.g., CAST(valid_from AS DATETIME) or parse format). Watch for timezone/format inconsistencies. Indexing on converted form or materialized datetime column improves performance for temporal filters.

valid_to.description: End of coupon validity window stored as a text timestamp.
valid_to.analysis_use: Identify expired or expiring coupons, filter active coupons by now between valid_from and valid_to, and schedule deactivation/notifications.
business_questions: Which coupons expire within the next 7 days?
Which coupons are expired as of today?
valid_to.nl2sql_hints: Same casting requirement as valid_from. Use CAST/CONVERT to compare with current timestamp. Consider creating an indexed computed/datetime column for frequent temporal queries. Handle NULLs (no expiry) explicitly in NL2SQL logic.

min_order_amount.description: Minimum order total required for coupon to be applicable, stored as text numeric.
min_order_amount.analysis_use: Filter coupons by eligibility thresholds, join with order totals to test applicability, bucket coupons by min_order_amount for marketing segmentation.
business_questions: List coupons that require min_order_amount = 0 (no minimum).
How many coupons require min_order_amount >= 300?
min_order_amount.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric before comparisons. Be explicit about currency units. Watch for empty strings or non-numeric values. Consider normalizing to integer/decimal type for faster numeric filtering.

usage_limit.description: Maximum allowed uses for the coupon (per-coupon or global depending on business rules) stored as text numeric.
usage_limit.analysis_use: Filter by scarcity (e.g., single-use coupons), join with usage table to compute remaining capacity, aggregate counts by usage_limit buckets.
business_questions: Which coupons are single-use (usage_limit = 1)?
Which coupons have usage_limit >= 10 and are still active?
usage_limit.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — cast to INT for numeric comparisons. Clarify whether usage_limit is global or per-customer; NL2SQL must join to usage records to compute consumed uses. Handle NULLs as unlimited if that is business convention.

active_flag.description: Administrative indicator whether a coupon is active (stored as string '1' sample).
active_flag.analysis_use: Primary filter to exclude inactive coupons. Combine with validity window to determine operational availability.
business_questions: How many coupons are marked active?
Show coupons that are active but already expired.
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Values stored as text; often '1'/'0' or 'Y'/'N'. Normalize to boolean in queries (e.g., active_flag = '1'). Note in sample all rows are '1' — check for stale/inconsistent values. Consider converting to a boolean column and indexing if used frequently.

created_at.description: Timestamp when coupon was created, stored as text.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and trend analysis (coupon creation cadence), bucket creations by date, measure time-to-first-use when joined with usage records.
business_questions: How many coupons were created by month?
Which coupons were created in the last quarter?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for time-based grouping and filtering. Ensure consistent timestamp format. Consider index on created_at (or derived date) if used in time-window queries.

updated_at.description: Timestamp of last update to coupon metadata, stored as text.
updated_at.analysis_use: Detect recently changed coupons, audit changes, find coupons with stale metadata; useful for incremental syncs.
business_questions: Which coupons were updated in the last 7 days?
Show coupons that have never been updated since creation (created_at = updated_at).
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar — cast to datetime for comparisons. Use updated_at for incremental ETL (e.g., WHERE updated_at > last_sync). Ensure time format and timezone are handled consistently.

address_id.description: Unique identifier for the address record (string ID like 'ADDR2001').
address_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication and updates; use for direct lookups, join keys when referencing a specific address. Good for counting distinct addresses.
business_questions: Which address records were created in the last month (by address_id)?
Are there duplicate address_id values indicating data quality issues?
address_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as TEXT/STRING. Although distinct=100 suggests uniqueness, DDL doesn't enforce PK; consider declaring PRIMARY KEY. Use equality filters (WHERE address_id = '...') or IN lists. Avoid numeric casts.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who owns the address (string ID like 'CUST1001').
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to customer tables (ecom_customers). Useful for grouping (addresses per customer), filtering (addresses for a given customer) and joins to orders/payments/returns to associate deliveries with customers.
business_questions: How many addresses does CUST1001 have?
Which customers have more than one address?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as TEXT/STRING and use as join predicate to ecom_customers.customer_id. Consider indexing for common filter/joins. Watch NULLs if present (none reported).

label.analysis_use: Low-cardinality dimension for grouping and segmentation (counts and proportions by label). Useful for filtering (only Home addresses) and pivoting.
business_questions: What proportion of addresses are labeled 'Home' vs 'Work'?
Do delivery success rates differ between 'Home' and 'Work' addresses (when joined to dispatch data)?
label.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical STRING. Use exact-match filters or GROUP BY. Expect only a few distinct values—use equality rather than LIKE. Handle case-insensitivity if input may vary.

addr_line1.description: Primary street address line containing house/number/street.
addr_line1.analysis_use: Text field for display, grouping by common street names, fuzzy de-duplication, and geocoding matches. Useful for address normalization and full-text search when locating addresses.
business_questions: Which streets (addr_line1) host the most customers?
Are there frequent duplicate or similar addr_line1 entries that could indicate data normalization opportunities?
addr_line1.nl2sql_hints: Stored as STRING. For NL2SQL, generate queries with exact matches or use LIKE/% for partial matches. Consider creating full-text index for search queries. Beware of inconsistent formatting—normalization (trim, upper/lower) recommended.

addr_line2.description: Secondary address line often containing landmarks or descriptors (e.g., 'Near Bus Stop').
addr_line2.analysis_use: Used for filtering by landmark, improving geocoding precision and identifying commonly used descriptors. Note significant null rate (~27%).
business_questions: How many addresses include 'Near Bus Stop' as a landmark?
Which landmarks are most common and do they correlate with failed deliveries?
addr_line2.nl2sql_hints: STRING with many NULLs. Use IS NULL/IS NOT NULL to handle missing values. Use exact-match or LIKE for the few distinct landmarks. Avoid assuming presence for joins.

city.description: City name where the address is located (e.g., 'Surat', 'Vadodara').
city.analysis_use: Key geographic dimension for grouping, aggregations, region-based reporting, and joins to city-level demographic or logistics tables. Useful in GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and filters for regional reports.
business_questions: Which cities have the most registered customer addresses?
How does order volume per city compare when addresses are joined to orders?
city.nl2sql_hints: STRING categorical. Use exact-match filters and GROUP BY city. Consider normalizing capitalization/spelling. Good candidate for an index if frequently filtered.

state.description: State/province of the address (all rows show 'Gujarat').
state.analysis_use: High-level geographic segmentation. Currently constant—useful as a filter but adds little variance. Keep for future multi-state expansion.
business_questions: Are all customer addresses in Gujarat, or do we have multi-state presence?
If multi-state, how does address distribution vary by state?
state.nl2sql_hints: STRING; currently constant. No need for heavy indexing. Expect simple equality checks. Consider validating values if data expands.

pincode.description: Postal code for the address (string form; numeric-like content).
pincode.analysis_use: Important for local/regional grouping, logistics routing, and joining to postal-region tables. Good for aggregation and density analysis at postal-code level. High cardinality (~99 distinct).
business_questions: Which pincodes have the highest number of customer addresses?
Are there pincodes with poor delivery performance when joined to dispatch events?
pincode.nl2sql_hints: Stored as STRING—treat as text for equality/grouping. If numeric operations required (range), consider casting to integer if format is consistent. Index on pincode recommended for frequent filtering.

country.description: Country of the address (all rows show 'India').
country.analysis_use: Top-level geographic filter. Currently constant, useful for multi-country expansion in the future.
business_questions: Are all addresses in India or do we support multi-country addresses?
If multi-country, what is the address distribution by country?
country.nl2sql_hints: STRING; constant in current dataset. Simple equality checks suffice. No heavy indexing needed unless multi-country queries are common.

latitude.description: Latitude coordinate of the address stored as nvarchar (e.g., '22.007482').
latitude.analysis_use: Essential for geospatial analysis (distance calculations, clustering, map visualizations) after casting to numeric. Useful for proximity searches and delivery zone calculations.
business_questions: What is the density of customer addresses within a 5 km radius of a warehouse?
Which addresses are within a specific delivery zone or within X km of a store?
latitude.nl2sql_hints: Stored as STRING—cast to FLOAT/DECIMAL for arithmetic or GIS functions (e.g., CAST(latitude AS FLOAT)). Ensure consistent decimal formats and handle NULLs. Consider creating computed numeric columns or proper geography/geometry types for spatial indexes.

longitude.description: Longitude coordinate of the address stored as nvarchar (e.g., '72.034121').
longitude.analysis_use: Paired with latitude for spatial queries (distance, clustering) after converting to numeric or geography type. Useful for mapping, routing and delivery optimization.
business_questions: Which addresses are furthest from the nearest fulfillment center?
Can we visualize customer address clusters on a map for route planning?
longitude.nl2sql_hints: STRING—cast to FLOAT/DECIMAL or convert to geography/geometry for spatial operations. Use together with latitude. Consider validating coordinate ranges and populating proper spatial indexes.

created_at.description: Timestamp string indicating when the address record was created (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal analysis of address creation trends, filtering recent additions, cohort analysis for new customers, and auditing. Needs casting to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for time functions.
business_questions: How many addresses were created each week/month?
Which customers added new addresses in the last 30 days?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as STRING—always CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)) before using temporal functions or range filters. Be mindful of timezone/format inconsistencies and nulls. Indexing on converted datetime column could improve time-based queries.

customer_id.description: Unique identifier for the customer in the segmentation dataset. Stored as nvarchar(26).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to link segmentation to customer master and transactional tables; used for selecting target lists; grouping/COUNT DISTINCT to validate uniqueness; used as identifier in exports for campaigns.
business_questions: Which customer_ids are in the 'At Risk' segment?
How many unique customer_ids exist in the segmentation table (should match downstream customer tables)?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a join-key string. Use exact equality joins with ecom_customers.customer_id (ensure both sides have same casing/trim). Guard against leading/trailing whitespace and NULLs. If other tables store IDs as different types, apply explicit casting or string conversion on the join. Avoid casting to numeric unless other sources require it.

rfm_segment.description: Categorical RFM segmentation label (e.g., Hibernating, Regulars, At Risk, Champions). nvarchar(32).
rfm_segment.analysis_use: Categorical dimension for GROUP BY, filtering and cohort selection. Useful for aggregations (counts, average metrics per segment), percentage distributions, and trend analyses when combined with created_at or last_purchase_date.
business_questions: What share of customers are 'Hibernating' vs 'Champions'?
Which segments have the highest avg_order_value and purchase_frequency?
rfm_segment.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical text. Use equality or IN filters (e.g., WHERE rfm_segment = 'Champions'). When using ORDER BY for segment priority, use explicit mapping (CASE) since lexicographic order may not reflect business priority. Be mindful of NULLs; include IS NULL checks if needed.

loyalty_score.description: A numeric-like loyalty score stored as nvarchar(16) representing customer loyalty (integer-like values stored as text).
loyalty_score.analysis_use: Numeric measure for ranking/filtering customers, bucketization, computing averages/percentiles, and threshold-based targeting. Must be cast to numeric type for correct aggregations and comparisons.
business_questions: Who are customers with loyalty_score >= 75 for VIP outreach?
What is the average loyalty_score by rfm_segment?
loyalty_score.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before aggregation or comparisons (e.g., TRY_CAST/CAST to INT or DECIMAL). Use safe parsing (TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC) where available to handle malformed values or NULLs; treat non-numeric or empty strings as NULL. Avoid string comparisons (e.g., '9' vs '10' lexicographic issues).

avg_order_value.description: Average order value per customer stored as nvarchar(24) (decimal-like values in string form).
avg_order_value.analysis_use: Monetary metric for aggregation (mean/median/sum), segmentation (high AOV customers), lifetime-value proxies, and correlation with loyalty_score/rfm_segment. Must be converted to a numeric/decimal type for meaningful operations.
business_questions: What is the average AOV of 'Champions' vs 'Hibernating' customers?
List top N customers by avg_order_value for high-value outreach.
avg_order_value.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric (DECIMAL/NUMERIC) before SUM/AVG/MEDIAN. Use locale-insensitive parsing; remove stray characters (currency symbols, commas) if present. Use TRY_CAST/NULLIF to avoid query failures on bad data. When ordering, cast first to avoid lexicographic ordering (e.g., '1000' < '200' as strings).

purchase_frequency.description: Frequency of purchases for the customer (numeric-like) stored as nvarchar(18).
purchase_frequency.analysis_use: Measure of activity used for cohort analysis, frequency-based segmentation, computing average orders per period, and identifying dormant vs frequent buyers. Requires numeric casting for aggregations and thresholds.
business_questions: Which customers have purchase_frequency > 1.0 (more than one purchase per period)?
How does purchase_frequency correlate with loyalty_score across segments?
purchase_frequency.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for numeric operations. Watch precision (store adequate scale). Handle unusual string formats and NULLs with TRY_CAST/COALESCE. When bucketing frequencies (e.g., 0-0.5, 0.5-1), compute numeric bins after casting.

last_purchase_date.description: Timestamp of the customer's last purchase stored as text (nvarchar(48)) in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
last_purchase_date.analysis_use: Recency calculations (days since last purchase), cohort and retention analyses, filters for recent activity, and identifying churn risk. Must be parsed to DATE/DATETIME for date arithmetic and proper sorting.
business_questions: Which customers had a last_purchase_date within the past 30 days?
Which customers are at risk (no purchase in X days) based on last_purchase_date and rfm_segment?
last_purchase_date.nl2sql_hints: Parse using CAST/TRY_CAST or date parsing functions to DATETIME/DATE. Validate format; use safe parsing (TRY_PARSE/TRY_CAST) to avoid exceptions. Use datediff/date_diff functions to compute recency. Beware of time zone assumptions and ensure consistent reference date (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP vs a reporting cut-off).

created_at.description: Timestamp when the segmentation record was created in the system, stored as nvarchar(48).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/timeline field for measuring when segments were computed, for incremental refresh logic, and for filtering segmentation snapshots by creation window. Useful for time-series of segmentation changes when combined with historical snapshots.
business_questions: How many segmentation records were created in the last 7 days?
When was the most recent segmentation refresh performed?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Store/convert to DATETIME when used in WHERE clauses for time windows. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to safely parse values. If comparing against other timestamp fields, ensure same timezone and datatype. For incremental NL2SQL queries, filter on created_at after casting to avoid scanning full table.

customer_id.description: Internal customer identifier used by the platform; appears unique per row (nvarchar).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to transactions and dimension lookups; grouping and counting distinct customers; de-duplication checks.
business_questions: How many unique customers do we have?
What are the top acquisition channels for customers by customer_id counts?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key; prefer exact equality joins. Consider enforcing/assuming uniqueness. Use customer_id for joins to ecom_orders, ecom_payments, ecom_customer_addresses. Indexing this column drastically speeds joins/filters.

external_customer_code.description: External/legacy identifier for the customer used by upstream/downstream systems.
external_customer_code.analysis_use: Mapping/crosswalk between systems when customer_id differs; join key for integrations; useful for ETL lineage and reconciliation.
business_questions: Which external customer codes are missing a matching internal customer_id?
How many customers originate from a particular external integration partner?
external_customer_code.nl2sql_hints: Also a string key; may require exact matches or trimmed comparisons. When matching with external systems, be careful of prefixes/case differences. Use COALESCE or LEFT JOIN logic for reconciliation queries.

first_name.analysis_use: Lightweight grouping for personalization analysis; name-based deduplication or fuzzy matching for identity resolution; extracting initials for UI. Not useful for uniqueness.
business_questions: Which first names are most common among our customers?
Can we detect potential duplicate accounts by matching first_name + last_name + dob?
first_name.nl2sql_hints: Strings can contain mixed case; for matching use LOWER(TRIM(first_name)). Avoid using name fields as primary join keys. For partial matches use LIKE or full-text / fuzzy matching functions.

last_name.analysis_use: Name-based grouping, deduplication and identity resolution with other fields; segmentation for personalization.
business_questions: What are the most frequent last names in the customer base?
How many customers share the same first+last name (potential duplicates)?
last_name.nl2sql_hints: Same considerations as first_name: normalize case/whitespace for comparisons. Be cautious of cultural naming conventions and null values when deduplicating.

email.description: Customer email address; appears unique in sample and is a common identifier for communications.
email.analysis_use: Primary contact channel for marketing and transactional communications; join key for external marketing systems; filter for deliverability checks and grouping by domain.
business_questions: How many customers have an email address and what domains are most common?
What percent of ACTIVE customers have a valid email on file?
email.nl2sql_hints: Normalize with LOWER(TRIM(email)) before joins and deduplication. Beware of multiple accounts per email in some datasets; prefer customer_id for joins if both available. Validate format using LIKE '%@%.__' or regex where supported.

phone.description: Customer phone number, stored as a string with country code included in samples.
phone.analysis_use: Contact channel for SMS and phone outreach; deduplication and identity resolution; grouping by country code for regional analysis.
business_questions: How many customers have a phone number on file?
What proportion of customers provide a phone with a specific country code (e.g., +91)?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Normalize by removing spaces, punctuation and standardizing country codes (e.g., replace ' ' with ''). Use string operations or regex to extract country code. Avoid numeric casts that drop leading zeros or plus sign.

dob.description: Date of birth stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format in sample.
dob.analysis_use: Derive age and age buckets for demographic analysis, lifetime value modeling by age cohort, eligibility checks. Can be used to segment customers by generation.
business_questions: What is the age distribution of our customers?
Are certain age groups more likely to opt-in to marketing?
dob.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE (e.g., CAST(dob AS DATE) or TRY_CAST) for comparisons and age calculation. Handle invalid/null values; use SAFE parsing functions. Prefer storing as DATE in upstream schema to simplify queries.

gender.description: Self-declared gender with low cardinality values (Other, Male, Female).
gender.analysis_use: Grouping and segmentation for marketing, personalization and demographic analysis; cross-tab with purchase/engagement metrics.
business_questions: What is the distribution of customers by gender?
Does average order value or retention differ by gender?
gender.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; standardize common values (e.g., case normalization). Consider mapping synonyms or blanks to 'Unknown'. Use ENUM-like filters for fast filtering and GROUP BY.

created_at.description: Account creation timestamp stored as nvarchar; samples suggest format 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss'.
created_at.analysis_use: Cohorting by signup date, funnel/time-to-first-order analysis, trend analysis of new customers over time, lifetime calculations relative to account age.
business_questions: How many customers signed up each week/month?
What is the conversion rate to first purchase within X days of created_at?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for time-based grouping and range filters (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Use date_trunc/DATEPART functions in NL2SQL translations. Be careful with timezone assumptions if any; validate format consistency before casting.

last_login.description: Timestamp of the customer's last login, stored as nvarchar (format similar to created_at).
last_login.analysis_use: Recency analysis (R in RFM), active user segmentation, churn risk modeling, filtering active users for campaigns.
business_questions: Which customers have not logged in for more than 90 days?
How many customers logged in during the last 7/30/90 days?
last_login.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for recency calculations. Use COALESCE to handle nulls. For NL2SQL, encourage explicit date arithmetic (e.g., DATEDIFF or timestampdiff) and ensure string-to-date conversion errors are handled (TRY_CAST).

marketing_opt_in.description: Consent flag stored as nvarchar with '1' or '0' samples indicating opt-in/out.
marketing_opt_in.analysis_use: Filter for permissioned marketing audiences; calculate opt-in rates and segment response rates. Critical for privacy/compliance filters.
business_questions: What percent of customers have opted in to marketing?
How does opt-in rate vary by acquisition channel or language?
marketing_opt_in.nl2sql_hints: Treat as boolean-like value: CAST to BIT/BOOLEAN or compare to '1'/'0'. Normalize variants like 'true', 'yes', '1'. Use as a WHERE filter for marketing recipient lists and ensure GDPR/consent checks are enforced in query logic.

preferred_language.description: Customer's preferred language for communications (Hindi, Gujarati, English observed).
preferred_language.analysis_use: Localization and personalization (email/SMS language selection), segmentation for content tests, A/B testing across languages.
business_questions: How many customers prefer Hindi vs English vs Gujarati?
Should we localize marketing campaigns based on the distribution of preferred_language per region?
preferred_language.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field: use exact matches. Normalize case and whitespace. When generating NL2SQL, map user requests like 'customers who prefer Hindi' to WHERE preferred_language = 'Hindi'. Consider filling unknowns with a default.

status.analysis_use: Filter active customers for engagement, measure churn (change from ACTIVE to INACTIVE over time if history tracked elsewhere), and compute active user metrics.
business_questions: How many ACTIVE customers do we have vs INACTIVE?
What is the reactivation rate from INACTIVE to ACTIVE over X period?
status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string categorical; normalize values and consider mapping to boolean is_active for easier NL2SQL translation. If status transitions need tracking, join to audit/event tables (ecom_activity_log, ecom_events_stream).

source_channel.description: Acquisition or signup channel (Organic, Referral, Website, Mobile App).
source_channel.analysis_use: Analyze ROI and performance by acquisition channel, cohort analysis by source, channel-level opt-in or retention comparisons.
business_questions: Which acquisition channel brings the most customers?
Which source_channel has the highest marketing_opt_in or retention?
source_channel.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field: use GROUP BY for channel breakdowns. When mapping natural language questions, treat synonyms (e.g., 'app' -> 'Mobile App') and be careful about historical channel attribution; use consistent naming and consider normalization dictionary for NL2SQL canonicalization.

map_id.description: Internal mapping identifier for each affinity row. Likely a surrogate or source-system id for the map/record.
map_id.analysis_use: Used for row-level identity, de-duplication, and accounting which specific mapping record was updated. Not typically used for analytics aggregations or joins to product masters.
business_questions: Which affinity mapping records were updated most recently (map_id -> last_updated)?
Are there duplicate or inconsistent map_id values that need cleanup?
map_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as opaque string. Although distinct values equal row count, map_id is not a foreign key. When writing NL2SQL, do not assume map_id implies joinability to other tables. Use map_id only for filtering by identifier or selecting a single row. No numeric casting required.

sku.description: The base product SKU for which co-purchase relationships are recorded (the item a customer viewed or purchased that has associated frequently-bought items).
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering key for queries like 'top co-purchased items for this sku'. Use to join to product/product_variant tables to retrieve descriptive metadata. Good for aggregation (count of associated targets, average co_purchase_rate) and ranking.
business_questions: What are the top 3 products co-purchased with sku = 'ONI003'?
How many distinct freq_with_sku targets exist per sku and what is the average co_purchase_rate per sku?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, treat as categorical join key. When generating SQL, it is common to filter by exact match (sku = 'XYZ') or IN lists. Joins: map sku to products.sku, ecom_products.sku or product_variants.sku. Because cardinality is low, expect small result sets; avoid unnecessary full scans by adding equality predicates. Consider case-sensitivity of string matching depending on DB collation.

freq_with_sku.description: The SKU that is frequently bought together with the base sku — the paired product in the affinity relationship.
freq_with_sku.analysis_use: Used as the target product for recommendations. Useful for counting how often a SKU appears as a recommended target across many base SKUs, for grouping, and for joining to product metadata to present recommendations.
business_questions: Which SKUs appear most frequently as recommended targets (most common freq_with_sku)?
For freq_with_sku = 'CAR004', which base SKUs have the highest co_purchase_rate towards it?
freq_with_sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical join key similar to sku. When writing NL2SQL, join freq_with_sku to product master tables to retrieve name/category. Use equality and IN filters. Be mindful that freq_with_sku and sku can reference the same small SKU set; queries might need to exclude self-pairs (sku != freq_with_sku) if relevant.

co_purchase_rate.description: Numeric score representing the strength or rate of co-purchase between sku and freq_with_sku. Stored as text (nvarchar) but values are floating-point-like (e.g., '11.24', '6.61').
co_purchase_rate.analysis_use: Primary metric for ranking and threshold filters (e.g., top N pairs, pairs with co_purchase_rate > X). Used in aggregations like MAX, AVG, and to order results. For time-series/diff checks, compare values across snapshots or historical tables (if available).
business_questions: List the top 10 sku/freq_with_sku pairs by co_purchase_rate.
Which pairs have co_purchase_rate >= 5 to qualify for a cross-sell placement?
co_purchase_rate.nl2sql_hints: Must be CAST/CONVERTed to a numeric type (e.g., DECIMAL or FLOAT) before numeric comparisons, ordering, or aggregation. Example: CAST(co_purchase_rate AS FLOAT) or TRY_CAST in engines that support it to handle malformed values. Handle NULLs and non-numeric strings defensively (use safe casts or WHERE co_purchase_rate IS NOT NULL AND TRY_CAST(... ) IS NOT NULL). When generating NL2SQL, include explicit casting if the user requests numeric filters or sorts.

last_updated.description: Timestamp string indicating when this affinity record was last updated. Stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
last_updated.analysis_use: Filter or sort by recency (e.g., latest affinities), detect stale records, and support incremental refresh logic. Can be parsed to a datetime/timestamp for time-window filters and trend calculations.
business_questions: Which affinity records have been updated in the last 7 days?
What is the most recent update timestamp for SKU 'COW005'?
last_updated.nl2sql_hints: Convert to a native datetime type before date arithmetic or range filters: e.g., CAST(last_updated AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP(last_updated, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS') depending on RDBMS. Use safe/try-cast if input may be malformed. When writing NL2SQL, expose natural language time filters (e.g., 'last 30 days') by translating them into parsed datetime comparisons. Consider timezone assumptions — strings likely in UTC or source system timezone; clarify when needed.

notification_id.description: Unique identifier for each notification event (string). Currently appears unique per row (150 distinct).
notification_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, counting unique notifications, and grouping by notification event. Use in joins when de-duplicating logs or linking to a message-tracking system.
business_questions: How many unique notifications were generated in a period?
Are there duplicate notification_ids appearing (indicating resend or logging duplication)?
notification_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. Use equality or DISTINCT/COUNT(notification_id) for uniqueness checks. If present as 'id' in other tables, join with exact string equality. Consider creating a PK or unique index for query performance.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who was targeted/received the notification (string, medium cardinality ~78 distinct).
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter and group by customer to get per-customer notification counts, frequency, and delivery success rates. Join key to customer master tables for segmentation and demographic attributes.
business_questions: Which customers received more than X notifications in the last month?
What is the delivery success rate for a given customer or customer segment?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as join key to ecom_customers or b2b_customers using string equality. Watch for nulls; use COALESCE or IS NOT NULL when filtering for known customers. For NL2SQL, map user phrases like 'per customer' to GROUP BY customer_id and optionally join to customer.name to return readable labels.

notification_type.description: Channel/type of notification sent (PUSH/EMAIL/SMS). Low cardinality (3 distinct values).
notification_type.analysis_use: Categorical grouping and filtering; computing channel volumes, channel-specific delivery success/failure, and channel performance over time.
business_questions: Which channel has the highest delivery failure rate?
How has the volume mix across PUSH, EMAIL, and SMS changed over time?
notification_type.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN filters and GROUP BY. Normalize case (upper/lower) when matching. Map natural language 'email vs sms' queries to WHERE notification_type IN ('EMAIL','SMS').

title.description: Short title of the notification (e.g., 'Order Update', 'Delivery Confirmation', 'New Offer'). Low cardinality and closely tied to message text.
title.analysis_use: Group by message intent to analyze which types of notifications drive different outcomes (delivery, clicks if tracked elsewhere). Useful for A/B tests and content performance.
business_questions: Which notification titles have the highest delivery or engagement success?
How many 'New Offer' messages were sent in the last campaign?
title.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical. Use exact matches or IN for known titles. If NL contains synonyms (e.g., 'promotional' vs 'new offer'), map synonyms to title values or use LIKE for partial matching. Titles may be normalized into a lookup table for robust joins.

message.description: Full message body or template short text sent to the customer. Low cardinality here (3 distinct messages).
message.analysis_use: Analyze message performance, group identical templates, detect which content correlates with delivery outcomes or downstream conversions when joined to orders. Use for template A/B comparisons.
business_questions: Which message templates produce the highest delivery (or engagement) rates?
How often was the 'special discount' message sent and to which customer segments?
message.nl2sql_hints: Message is free text stored as nvarchar; use exact string matching for known templates or LIKE for partial matches. Because catalog is small, consider mapping to template_id for efficient queries. Beware of text length when selecting message in large result sets.

sent_ts.description: Timestamp when the notification was sent, stored as nvarchar but formatted like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' (high cardinality).
sent_ts.analysis_use: Primary column for temporal analysis: time series, hourly/daily trends, latency calculations (when combined with created_at or order timestamps). Must be cast to datetime for range filters, date_trunc/grouping.
business_questions: How many notifications were sent per hour/day by channel?
What is the time distribution (hour of day) of notifications and peak send times?
sent_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast or convert nvarchar to datetime (e.g., CAST(sent_ts AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP) before applying date filters or GROUP BY on date parts. Validate format and handle malformed/null values. For NL2SQL, translate relative date phrases ('last 7 days') to concrete predicates on cast(sent_ts).

delivery_status.description: Result of send attempt: FAILED, DELIVERED, SENT (low cardinality 3 values).
delivery_status.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate to compute delivery success/failure rates by channel, message, customer, or time. Useful for alerting and monitoring communication health.
business_questions: What percentage of push notifications failed in the last week?
Which customers experienced repeated FAILED delivery_status and might need contact info update?
delivery_status.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN and GROUP BY. For failure-focused queries, map natural language 'failed notifications' to WHERE delivery_status = 'FAILED'. Consider coalescing different non-delivered statuses if the domain includes more variants in the future.

linked_order_id.description: Identifier of an order related to the notification (string, medium cardinality ~75 distinct). May be null if notification is promotional.
linked_order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders for assessing notification context (order update, delivery confirmations). Enables analysis of notifications triggered by order events and their downstream impact on order lifecycle.
business_questions: How many notifications are associated with orders versus promotional messages?
For order-linked notifications, what is the delivery success rate and subsequent order status changes?
linked_order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as a join key to ecom_orders or order-related tables using string equality. Handle nulls for promotional messages by using WHERE linked_order_id IS NOT NULL when querying order-linked notifications. For NL2SQL intents like 'notifications for order EORD123', match exact string equality.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the notification record was created in the system, stored as nvarchar in ISO-like format. May differ from sent_ts (e.g., queued vs actually sent).
created_at.analysis_use: Can be used to measure queueing latency (sent_ts - created_at), audit when notifications were enqueued, and filter by creation time. Requires casting to datetime for comparisons and time-based grouping.
business_questions: What is the average time between created_at and sent_ts (queue/delivery latency)?
How many notification records were created per day (ingestion/audit volume)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime before aggregations or date-range filters. When calculating latency, ensure both created_at and sent_ts are present and properly parsed; handle missing or malformed values. For NL2SQL, map phrases like 'created today' to appropriate cast(created_at) date filters.

order_id.description: Internal unique order identifier (string). High-cardinality: 601 distinct values across 601 rows.
order_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, linking to other order-level tables (order_lines, order_events) and for filters when inspecting a single order.
business_questions: Show order details for a given order_id.
Count orders and detect duplicates or inconsistent updates using order_id.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique identifier string. Use exact string equality for filters. When joining, prefer order_id if target tables use same key. No numeric/date casting needed. Be cautious of trailing spaces or case sensitivity; apply TRIM()/LOWER() if ingestion may vary.

order_number.description: Human-facing order reference (string). High-cardinality and often shown in reports/UX.
order_number.analysis_use: Used for reporting, user lookups, and joins to external systems that store human-facing order numbers. Useful for grouping when order_id and order_number both exist.
business_questions: Retrieve order by order_number for customer service inquiries.
Count orders per order_number pattern (e.g., by year prefix).
order_number.nl2sql_hints: Also a string key. May contain prefixes (ORDR2025...). For pattern filters use LIKE or substring. Avoid numeric casts; treat as identifier. If users query by order number, allow partial matches and exact matches.

customer_id.description: Identifier linking the order to a customer (string). Distinct: 98 across 601 rows.
customer_id.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation by customer (orders per customer, CLTV proxies, repeat purchase analysis). Join key to ecom_customers and other customer-level tables.
business_questions: Who are the top customers by revenue or order count?
What is each customer's repeat purchase rate and average order value?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Natural join key to customer dimension. Watch for NULLs; treat empty strings and NULL similarly. For NL2SQL, prefer equality joins; when users request 'orders for customer X' match both customer_id and customer name by joining customers table.

order_ts.description: Order timestamp as string in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format. Distinct: 463.
order_ts.analysis_use: Primary event time for time-series analysis (orders by time bucket, funneling, lag calculations). Often used for date-range filters and extracting date/hour/day-of-week.
business_questions: How many orders were placed each day/hour?
What is the peak ordering hour and busiest day of the week?
order_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to proper DATETIME/TIMESTAMP in queries (e.g., CAST or TO_TIMESTAMP) before filtering or grouping by date parts. Avoid lexicographic grouping on the raw string. Support common patterns like DATE(order_ts) and BETWEEN date ranges; handle NULLs and inconsistent formats by TRY_CAST where available.

channel.description: Sales channel where order originated (categorical: 'Web' or 'Mobile App').
channel.analysis_use: Group/filter to compare performance across channels (orders, revenue, conversion proxies). Low cardinality makes it ideal for GROUP BY and pivoting.
business_questions: Which channel drives higher average order value and revenue?
What are cancellation/refund rates by channel?
channel.nl2sql_hints: Simple categorical filter; expect exact matches. When mapping natural language 'mobile' vs 'Mobile App', provide synonym handling in NL2SQL. Consider channel normalization if additional channel variants appear.

order_status.analysis_use: Filter and group to compute funnel metrics (counts by status), cancellation rates, and state transitions. Useful for SLA and operational dashboards.
business_questions: How many orders are currently PACKED vs SHIPPED?
What percent of orders end up CANCELLED by channel or campaign?
order_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with moderate cardinality. Use equality/IN lists for filtering. For timeline queries, combine with updated_at to get status changes if order events tracked elsewhere; avoid interpreting status timestamps from this column alone.

payment_status.analysis_use: Used to calculate realized revenue, outstanding payments, refund rates, and to reconcile with payments table.
business_questions: What is the value and count of PAID vs PENDING orders?
What proportion of orders are REFUNDED and how much revenue is affected?
payment_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical. When computing revenue, restrict to payment_status = 'PAID' unless assessing gross vs net. Consider join with payment logs for partial refunds or multi-payment scenarios.

delivery_slot_id.description: Identifier for the scheduled delivery slot (moderate cardinality: 50 distinct).
delivery_slot_id.analysis_use: Analyze delivery slot demand, capacity planning, and fulfillment scheduling. Group orders by slot to measure volume and average revenue per slot.
business_questions: Which delivery slots have highest order volume and revenue?
Do cancellation or refund rates differ by delivery slot?
delivery_slot_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; may have structure (SLOT9). For time-based mapping, join to delivery_slots table if available to get start/end times. Use GROUP BY and ORDER BY count to find top slots.

delivery_address_id.description: Identifier of delivery address for the order (high-cardinality: ~500 distinct).
delivery_address_id.analysis_use: Join to address table to geocode, cluster deliveries, compute delivery density, or identify frequently used addresses. Useful for repeat delivery detection and fraud checks.
business_questions: Which neighborhoods or postal codes receive highest-order volume?
Which addresses have multiple cancellations or returns?
delivery_address_id.nl2sql_hints: Natural join key to ecom_customer_addresses. High cardinality — avoid GROUP BY over full set unless needed. Normalize NULL/empty values. When users ask by location, join and expose address fields (city, pincode) rather than this ID.

total_amount.description: Order gross total as string representing a numeric (float) value.
total_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG) for gross revenue and AOV. Combined with discount/tax/shipping to reconcile net_amount and compute metrics.
business_questions: What is total gross revenue over a period?
What is the average order value (AOV) by channel/campaign?
total_amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — always CAST/TO_NUMBER to numeric type before aggregating. Handle inconsistent decimal representations and NULLs. Use TRY_CAST/SAFE_CAST to avoid query failures on bad values.

tax_amount.description: Tax component of the order as string representing numeric value.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liabilities, compute effective tax rates (tax/total), and validate tax computations per order.
business_questions: How much tax was collected by period and by fulfillment centre?
What is the average tax rate applied across orders?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric for SUM/AVG calculations. Watch for cases where tax is zero or missing; use COALESCE(0) for aggregates. When comparing tax ratios, avoid division by zero.

discount_amount.description: Discount applied to the order as a string representing numeric value.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure promotion/coupon cost, compute net revenue adjustments, and analyze discount impact on conversion and AOV.
business_questions: How much discount value was redeemed by coupon and campaign?
Does offering discounts increase average order value or order count?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. When connecting discount to coupon_code or source_campaign, aggregate discount_amount grouped by those keys. Ensure negative/positive sign conventions are consistent.

shipping_charge.description: Shipping/fulfillment charge applied to the order as string numeric.
shipping_charge.analysis_use: Aggregate shipping revenue/cost contribution, compute shipping subsidy (if known costs), and analyze shipping charge distribution across regions or fulfillment centers.
business_questions: What shipping revenue is collected by fulfillment centre?
Are certain delivery slots or regions associated with higher shipping charges?
shipping_charge.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Check for zeros and NULLs. Combine with delivery_address or fulfillment centre joins for regional analysis.

net_amount.description: Net payable amount for the order as string numeric (likely total - discount + shipping + tax).
net_amount.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for realized revenue analyses; use for SUM/AVG over time, channel, campaign, and customer segmentation.
business_questions: What is net revenue by day/channel/campaign?
What is the average net order value for repeat vs new customers?
net_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric — treat as the authoritative revenue measure unless payment_status indicates not PAID. For revenue reports filter payment_status='PAID' or join payment tables for realized receipts. Validate net_amount = total - discount + tax + shipping where required.

coupon_code.description: Coupon or promotion code applied (2 distinct values plus NULLs ~33%).
coupon_code.analysis_use: Attribute discount usage and promotion effectiveness, segment orders by coupon usage to compare AOV and conversion.
business_questions: Which coupon code drives the highest revenue and which has highest redemption rate?
Do coupon users have higher or lower return/cancellation rates?
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with many NULLs. Use COALESCE('NONE', coupon_code) to include non-couponed orders in GROUP BY. When users query 'orders with coupon', map natural language coupon names to exact codes or synonyms.

source_campaign.description: Marketing attribution/source of the order (Google, Organic, Referral, FB Ads). Some NULLs (~19.8%).
source_campaign.analysis_use: Marketing performance analytics (orders, revenue, CAC proxies when combined with spend), channel attribution, campaign ROI.
business_questions: Which source_campaign yields the highest net revenue or AOV?
How do refund and cancellation rates vary across source campaigns?
source_campaign.nl2sql_hints: Categorical; when users ask about 'paid ads' or 'organic', map to source_campaign values. Handle NULL/unknown attribution as its own bucket. For multi-touch attribution, join with marketing/event tables if available.

fulfillment_centre_id.analysis_use: Operational analysis: compare order volumes, average processing times (when joined to timestamps), cancellations, and returns by fulfillment centre.
business_questions: Which fulfillment centre handles more volume and higher revenue?
Does order fulfillment speed differ between centres (requires order_events/updated_at)?
fulfillment_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical — ideal for grouping. Use as a filter for supply chain or logistics-related NL2SQL queries. Join to store/centre dimension for human-readable names (if stored separately).

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp as string. Likely indicates when order record was created in system (format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time analysis, detection of late-arriving records, time-based filtering for ETL or incremental processing.
business_questions: When were orders ingested into the system and are there delays between order_ts and created_at?
How many orders were created in a given ingestion window?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for comparisons. When constructing NL2SQL, clarify whether users mean order_ts (placement time) or created_at (ingestion/record creation). Use TIMESTAMPDIFF between order_ts and created_at to find latency.

updated_at.description: Last update timestamp for the order record as string.
updated_at.analysis_use: Track latest status changes, compute event latencies, and identify recent modifications. Useful for incremental syncs and concurrency checks.
business_questions: Which orders changed status in the last 24 hours?
What is the time between created_at and last update for different order_status values?
