gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL with adequate precision (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)) before arithmetic. When calculating derived metrics, prefer using gross_sales rather than net_sales for discount analysis. Use TRY_CAST to protect against malformed strings.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts given in the month/channel (monetary), stored as nvarchar.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure discount impact on revenue, compute discount as percentage of gross_sales, and analyze promotion effectiveness across channels/months.
business_questions: What is the discount_amount as a percentage of gross_sales by channel in December?
Which month saw the largest increase in discounts compared to previous month?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for SUM and percentage calculations. When computing ratios (discount_amount / gross_sales), protect against division-by-zero and NULLs. Consider negative/positive conventions in data (discounts typically positive values representing reduction).

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees collected in the month/channel, stored as nvarchar.
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Used to analyze contribution of delivery fees to revenue, evaluate whether fees offset delivery costs, and compare across channels.
business_questions: How much revenue came from delivery_fee by channel in July 2024?
Are delivery_fee trends correlated with order volume changes?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for aggregation. Some analyses may subtract delivery_fee from net revenue depending on accounting treatment; clarify definitions before computing net metrics.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the month/channel, stored as nvarchar.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Reconciliation with tax reports, compute tax as percentage of gross_sales, and separate taxed vs non-taxed revenue if needed.
business_questions: What percentage of gross_sales was paid as tax in June 2024?
Compare tax_amount across channels — are some channels showing consistently higher tax shares?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for sums and ratios. Check for currency/rounding conventions. When joining to tax reports, year/month alignment and currency must match.

net_sales.description: Net sales for the month/channel after discounts, taxes and fees as defined by upstream calculation, stored as nvarchar.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for performance comparisons and trend analysis. Use for share calculations and for computing margins when combined with cost metrics from other tables.
business_questions: What is the overall net_sales per channel for 2024 and channel share of total?
Which month had the largest net_sales increase month-over-month?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for numeric operations. Validate how net_sales is computed upstream (gross - discounts + delivery - tax?) before deriving additional metrics to avoid double counting. Use TRY_CAST to handle malformed strings.

created_at.description: Ingestion or snapshot timestamp for when the row was created in the raw schema, stored as nvarchar (single distinct value in sample).
created_at.analysis_use: Metadata for data freshness and lineage; used to filter latest snapshots or to audit when rollups were generated. Not for time series analysis of business data.
business_questions: When was this monthly rollup generated or last refreshed?
Show rows created before/after a specific pipeline run for debugging.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string but CAST to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., TRY_CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)) when filtering by timestamp. Because it’s metadata and not a business date, avoid grouping by created_at in business reports. In NL2SQL phrase patterns, map 'latest snapshot' requests to MAX(created_at) or WHERE created_at = (SELECT MAX(created_at)).

year.description: Calendar year for the aggregated metrics (stored as nvarchar).
year.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering key for year-level reports. Use to join/compare with monthly/daily tables by year. Often used in WHERE clauses (e.g., year = '2024').
business_questions: What are the sales totals for a specific year and channel?
How do annual totals change year-over-year (when multiple years exist)?
year.nl2sql_hints: Currently a string; treat as categorical. Prefer casting to INT if numeric year comparisons or range filters are required (CAST(year AS INT)). Expect exact-match filters (e.g., year = '2024'). Be careful of whitespace/padding; trim before casting.

order_channel.analysis_use: Group-by and filter dimension for channel performance comparisons and channel mix visualizations. Good join key to channel-level schemas or to filter dashboards.
business_questions: Which channel generated the most net sales this year?
How does average order value differ between channels?
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string used in GROUP BY and WHERE. Be mindful of inconsistent casing/whitespace in source (use TRIM/UPPER/LOWER normalization). Join with other channel-dimension tables on normalized channel name.

total_orders.description: Total number of orders in the year for the channel (stored as nvarchar).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) across years/channels, or direct display. Used with gross/net sales to compute average order value, orders per customer, growth rates. Filterable as numeric thresholds after casting.
business_questions: How many orders were placed through each channel this year?
What is the orders-per-customer ratio by channel?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Numeric semantic but stored as string — cast to INTEGER (CAST(total_orders AS BIGINT) or INT) before numeric comparisons or arithmetic. Handle NULLs and non-numeric values robustly (TRY_CAST where supported).

total_customers.description: Count of distinct customers who transacted in the year for the channel (stored as nvarchar).
total_customers.analysis_use: Used to compute per-customer metrics (ARPU, transactions per customer). Grouping/filtering for customer-focused KPIs. Combine with total_orders to compute repeat rates.
business_questions: How many unique customers purchased via each channel this year?
What is revenue per customer by channel?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to integer for calculations. If the value represents unique customers in an aggregated source, avoid joining to user tables assuming exact counts; prefer drill-down to base tables for exact deduplication.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Used for volume-based KPIs, unit economics, and yield analyses. Useful to compute revenue per kg and to compare volume vs value across channels.
business_questions: What is the total kg sold per channel this year?
What is gross/net sales per kg by channel?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal numeric stored as string — cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., CAST(total_qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Watch for localization issues (commas) and trailing precision. Use NULL-safe casts and aggregates (SUM) on numeric type.

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount (pre-discount, pre-tax) for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for top-line reporting, share-of-sales calculations, and comparisons against net_sales and discounts. Aggregated or used to compute percentages and growth rates.
business_questions: How much gross sales did each channel generate this year?
What percentage of total gross sales does each channel contribute?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary value stored as string — cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before arithmetic (e.g., CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Beware of rounding/precision; prefer NUMERIC for financial accuracy. Use COALESCE to handle NULLs.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied (monetary) for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to calculate discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), analyze promotional cost, and net impact on revenue. Useful for channel-level promotion efficiency analysis.
business_questions: What is the total discount given per channel this year?
What is the discount as a percent of gross sales by channel?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before computing rates. When computing ratios, protect against division by zero. Consider rounding and formatting for presentation. Ensure negative/positive sign conventions are known (discounts likely positive amounts reducing gross).

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees collected or charged for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar).
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Used to compute net revenue contributions, delivery fee share of gross/net sales, and to offset delivery costs. Useful for channel cost analysis when combined with delivery costs dataset.
business_questions: How much did channels collect in delivery fees this year?
What share of net sales is attributed to delivery fees by channel?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for numeric operations. Clarify whether delivery_fee is revenue or expense in downstream joins. Normalize sign conventions before summation.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected/recorded for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used to calculate tax rate versus gross sales and for compliance/reporting. Useful in computing net-of-tax revenues if needed.
business_questions: What is the total tax collected per channel this year?
What is the effective tax rate (tax / gross_sales) by channel?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to DECIMAL for arithmetic. For percent calculations use safe division. Ensure tax treatment consistency when joining to invoice-level tax data.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for the year and channel (likely gross minus discounts plus/minus fees/taxes) stored as nvarchar.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for reporting. Used to compute channel profitability proxies, revenue per order/customer, and for trend comparisons. Often used in dashboards as the key KPI.
business_questions: What are the net sales by channel for the year?
Which channel has the highest net sales per customer?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Treat as DECIMAL for calculations (CAST(net_sales AS DECIMAL(...))). Validate that net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount + delivery_fee +/- tax_amount if reconciliation is required. Use consistent precision/rounding.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the aggregated row was created or last updated (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit metadata: used to determine freshness of the aggregate and to filter for the latest snapshot. Not typically used in business aggregations but important for ETL monitoring.
business_questions: When was this yearly snapshot generated?
Is this data up-to-date compared to the reporting cut-off?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for comparisons (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Because the value is identical across rows in this sample, queries that fetch the latest snapshot should use MAX(created_at) after casting. Watch for timezone formats and parsing errors; use TRY_PARSE/TRY_CAST if available.

keyword_id.description: A unique identifier for the keyword record (string). Acts as a row identifier for this table's records.
keyword_id.analysis_use: Used for row-level identification, de-duplication and record retrieval. Useful when you need to reference or update a specific record. Not typically used in aggregations except to count distinct rows.
business_questions: How many unique keyword records exist in the table?
Retrieve details for a specific keyword record by id (e.g., show metrics for keyword_id = 'KEYW1001').
keyword_id.nl2sql_hints: Although nvarchar in the DDL, keyword_id appears unique and can be treated as an identifier. Use exact equality filters (keyword_id = '...'). Avoid casting keyword_id. Consider treating it as the primary key in queries (e.g., WHERE keyword_id IN (...)) and in query generation templates prefer equality rather than LIKE. If used for joins, ensure the target table uses the same type/format.

keyword.description: The search term or keyword string people used (e.g., 'potato', 'organic veg').
keyword.analysis_use: Commonly used for grouping and filtering. Aggregate search_volume and conversion_rate by keyword to measure keyword-level performance. Also used for text-based matching and fuzzy matching against user search logs.
business_questions: Which keywords have the highest average conversion_rate?
Which keywords should we target for SEO/paid campaigns based on search_volume and conversion_rate?
keyword.nl2sql_hints: Column is nvarchar and contains repeated values with low cardinality — GROUP BY keyword is very selective and efficient. Normalize case and whitespace in NL2SQL (e.g., LOWER(TRIM(keyword))) for reliable matching. For fuzzy matching use LIKE '%term%' or full-text indexes if supported. Because of low cardinality, queries that aggregate by keyword will produce small result sets and are good candidates for ORDER BY and LIMIT.

search_volume.description: The number of searches (numeric) for the keyword during the captured period, stored as nvarchar.
search_volume.analysis_use: Primary metric for demand; typically cast to integer to SUM, AVG, compute percentiles, or rank keywords by volume. Used in conjunction with conversion_rate to prioritize keywords.
business_questions: What is the total and average search volume per keyword over the period?
Which keywords have rising or falling search volumes when grouped by created_at (time series)?
search_volume.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — always CAST/CONVERT to INT in predicates and aggregations (e.g., CAST(search_volume AS INT)). Handle non-numeric or NULL values defensively (e.g., TRY_CAST or CASE WHEN ISNUMERIC(...) THEN CAST(...) END). Avoid string comparisons for numeric ranking (do not ORDER BY search_volume as text). For performance, prefer pre-casting to numeric in ETL or virtual columns if supported.

conversion_rate.description: Conversion rate for the keyword (likely percent or ratio), stored as nvarchar with decimal values.
conversion_rate.analysis_use: Used to assess effectiveness of a keyword (conversions/searches). Typical aggregations: AVG(conversion_rate), weighted conversion rate (weighted by search_volume), or ranking keywords by conversion. Useful to spot high-conversion low-volume opportunities.
business_questions: Which keywords have the highest conversion_rate and how stable is this over time?
Are there keywords with low search_volume but outstanding conversion_rate that we should promote?
conversion_rate.nl2sql_hints: CAST to FLOAT/DECIMAL before numeric operations (e.g., TRY_CAST(conversion_rate AS FLOAT)). Watch for strings representing percentages vs decimals (confirm scale — e.g., 2.48 likely means 2.48% not 0.0248). When computing weighted averages, join or use CAST(search_volume AS INT) to compute weights. Handle NULLs and invalid strings with TRY_CAST or CASE logic.

top_sku.description: The SKU identifier for the product most associated with this keyword (string).
top_sku.analysis_use: Group and join by top_sku to analyze which products are surfaced by search terms and to cross-reference with product metadata, inventory, and sales tables. Useful for SKU-level performance and attribution analyses.
business_questions: Which SKUs are most frequently the top result across keywords?
For a given SKU, what keywords drive the most search volume and conversions?
top_sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat top_sku as a categorical join key. Joins to product tables should match casing and formatting exactly; canonicalization (TRIM/UPPER) may be necessary. Because top_sku is stored as nvarchar and not declared as a foreign key, verify SKU uniqueness in the product table before joining. Use inner joins when you require product metadata and left joins when you want to keep keyword rows without SKU matches.

created_at.description: The timestamp when the keyword record was captured, stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' format.
created_at.analysis_use: Used for time-series and trend analysis: group by day/week/month, compute rolling metrics, filter to windows (last 7/30/90 days). Also used to join with event/order timestamps for attribution windows.
business_questions: How have search_volume and conversion_rate for a specific keyword changed over time?
What were the top keywords in the last 30 days compared to the previous 30 days?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Always CAST/CONVERT created_at to a datetime (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_CONVERT) before time-based grouping or filtering. The string appears ISO-like so direct cast should work in most SQL engines, but use TRY_CAST to handle malformed values. For NL2SQL generation, allow natural language time windows (e.g., 'last 30 days') and translate to WHERE CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) >= DATEADD(day, -30, GETDATE()). Consider timezone normalization if event times span zones.

ticket_id.description: Unique identifier for the ticket (string). Appears distinct per row but not declared as a primary key.
ticket_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for counting distinct tickets, joining to ticket-message/thread tables, deduplication and event sequencing.
business_questions: How many unique tickets were created in the last month?
Which tickets have multiple messages (join to ecom_ticket_messages) and what is their lifecycle?
ticket_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string when matching. Although distinct in sample, PK is not enforced — consider deduplication in queries. Use exact matching (WHERE ticket_id = 'TICK001001') or IN lists. For joins to message tables, ensure consistent casing/trimming.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who raised the ticket (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group/filter by customer to get ticket counts per customer, join to ecom_customers for attributes, detect repeat complainants.
business_questions: Which customers created the most tickets in the last quarter?
How many unique customers raised tickets vs. total tickets?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Join key to ecom_customers.customer_id — ensure both sides are same string format. Use GROUP BY customer_id for frequency. Watch for NULLs and inconsistent prefixes; consider TRIM() and upper/lower normalization in WHERE/JOIN clauses.

subject.description: Short categorical reason for the ticket (e.g., Delivery Delay, Order Issue). Low cardinality (4 distinct).
subject.analysis_use: Categorical grouping for root-cause analysis, pie charts, top-N reasons, filter for specific complaint types.
business_questions: What percentage of tickets are 'Delivery Delay' vs other subjects?
Which subjects correspond to the longest average resolution times?
subject.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Use equality or IN for filters. Because cardinality is low, GROUP BY subject is efficient. Consider normalizing case/whitespace if data quality issues exist.

description.description: Longer text explaining the customer's complaint (string) — often repeated consistent phrases in this dataset.
description.analysis_use: Text analysis (keyword search, grouping by common phrases), manual review, and deriving issue categories if more subjects exist. Useful for matching common complaint templates.
business_questions: How many tickets contain the phrase 'Item received damaged'?
Which descriptions map to refunds or quality complaints most frequently?
description.nl2sql_hints: Full-text or LIKE searches (e.g., WHERE description LIKE '%damaged%') are common. Stored as nvarchar — watch for case sensitivity. For NL2SQL, map natural language keywords to LIKE patterns or full-text predicates; consider stemming/stopword handling outside SQL if needed.

status.description: Ticket lifecycle state (OPEN, IN_PROGRESS, RESOLVED, CLOSED). Low cardinality (4).
status.analysis_use: Primary field for operational KPIs (open backlog, resolved volumes), filtering for active tickets, and SLA calculations.
business_questions: How many tickets are currently OPEN or IN_PROGRESS?
What is the weekly resolved ticket rate (count of RESOLVED per week)?
status.nl2sql_hints: Exact categorical values — use equality/IN. Consider normalizing case. For time-based queries, combine status filter with created_at/updated_at (cast to datetime). When calculating resolution metrics, use status = 'RESOLVED' and validate updated_at is later than created_at.

priority.description: Priority assigned to the ticket (LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH) — very low cardinality.
priority.analysis_use: Segmentation by urgency, SLA adherence, prioritization analysis and grouping for agent workload.
business_questions: How many HIGH priority tickets were resolved in the last week?
Which agents handle the most HIGH priority tickets?
priority.nl2sql_hints: Categorical equality/IN filtering. Use with status and created_at/updated_at for SLA analysis. Ensure NULL handling; consider mapping synonyms or alternate spellings before matching.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the ticket was created stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
created_at.analysis_use: Time-based filtering and grouping (day/week/month), calculating age (now - created_at) and resolution time (updated_at - created_at) after casting to datetime.
business_questions: How many tickets were created per day/week/month?
What is the average ticket age for OPEN tickets?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type in SQL (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or STR_TO_DATE depending on DB) before date math or date_trunc operations. When writing NL2SQL mappings, convert natural language time ranges to appropriate WHERE clauses on the casted datetime. Watch for invalid/malformed strings; include TRY_CAST where available.

updated_at.description: Timestamp for the last update to the ticket stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
updated_at.analysis_use: Used to compute resolution time (updated_at - created_at), trend analysis of updates, and SLA breaches when combined with priority/status.
business_questions: What is the median time from created_at to updated_at for RESOLVED tickets?
How many tickets were updated in the last 24 hours and by which agents?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Also requires casting to datetime for arithmetic. For resolution time only consider rows where updated_at >= created_at and status indicates resolution. Use TRY_CAST/ISDATE checks to avoid errors. NL2SQL should map phrases like 'average resolution time' to TIMESTAMPDIFF/datediff equivalents after casting.

order_id.description: Order identifier associated with the ticket (string). Nullable; moderate cardinality.
order_id.analysis_use: Join to ecom_orders to link tickets to order details, filter tickets related to particular orders, or compute per-order complaint rates.
business_questions: How many tickets are associated with orders in the last month?
Which orders/customers had repeat tickets or refunds related to the same order?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Join key to ecom_orders.order_id — ensure exact string match and same formatting. Many tickets may have NULL order_id; handle with IS NOT NULL in filters. Use LEFT JOIN when enriching to retain tickets without orders.

assigned_to.description: Username/role of the agent handling the ticket (ops_manager, support_agent_1, support_agent_2). Very low cardinality.
assigned_to.analysis_use: Agent workload, performance tracking (counts, average resolution time per agent), and routing analysis.
business_questions: How many tickets is each agent currently assigned and what is their average resolution time?
Which agent handles the majority of HIGH priority tickets?
assigned_to.nl2sql_hints: Categorical equality/IN. Use GROUP BY assigned_to for agent metrics. Consider mapping aliases or role names (ops_manager vs support_agent_*) consistently. When combining with time-based metrics use casted timestamps. Handle NULL/unassigned cases explicitly.

ticket_message_id.description: Unique identifier for each message row (string).
ticket_message_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for deduplication, ensuring row-level uniqueness and tracing individual messages. Useful for joins when correlating message-level events or logs.
business_questions: Are there any duplicate message records by ticket_message_id?
How many unique messages exist in a given date range?
ticket_message_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string (nvarchar). High cardinality — use for exact equality or DISTINCT. Not suitable for ordering by time; pair with message_ts/created_at for temporal queries. May benefit from an index; consider surrogate integer PK if ingest system allows.

ticket_id.description: Identifier for the support ticket to which the message belongs (string).
ticket_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping key to aggregate messages per ticket, count messages per ticket, find conversations, and join to ecom_ticket to get ticket-level metadata (status, customer, assignee).
business_questions: Which tickets have the most customer interactions?
What is the message volume per ticket category or assignee (after joining ticket metadata)?
ticket_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string. Common join key to ecom_ticket: use WHERE ecom_ticket.ticket_id = zuna_seed_raw.ecom_ticket_messages.ticket_id. Grouping and window functions partitioned by ticket_id are typical. Consider indexing ticket_id for faster grouping/joins.

message_ts.description: Timestamp when the message was sent (stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
message_ts.analysis_use: Ordering messages within tickets, computing response times, filtering by date/time ranges, and generating timelines. Must CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type before using temporal functions.
business_questions: What is the time sequence of messages for a ticket (conversation timeline)?
What is the average response time from support after a customer message?
message_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — always CAST(message_ts AS DATETIME) or equivalent before comparisons, ordering, and date arithmetic. Expect format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' — use safe parsing and handle malformed/null values. For recent/recency queries use ORDER BY CAST(message_ts AS DATETIME) DESC.

sender.description: Role of the sender (categorical): typically 'support' or 'customer'.
sender.analysis_use: Filter and group to compare message volumes and behavior by role, compute response metrics (e.g., first support reply), and identify tickets dominated by one side.
business_questions: What proportion of messages are from support vs customer?
For which tickets does support send the majority of messages?
sender.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical column — suitable for equality filters (sender = 'support') and GROUP BY. Watch for casing/typos; normalize values (LOWER/TRIM) in NL2SQL translations. Use in partitioning/window functions to compute first/last sender events per ticket.

message_text.description: Text content of the message. In this snapshot it contains a few common templates.
message_text.analysis_use: Text analytics (template detection, frequency counts), intent classification, sentiment analysis, and identifying common responses or requests. Useful for grouping identical messages and detecting canned responses.
business_questions: What are the most common message templates customers send?
How often do support agents use standard response templates ('We're checking the issue', 'Your issue has been resolved')?
message_text.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality here but can be free-form in general. Use exact equality for template counts (message_text = '...') or LIKE for pattern matching. For more advanced NLP, extract text to a downstream pipeline; NL2SQL should avoid complex regex unless supported. Consider normalizing whitespace/case when matching templates.

attachment_url.description: URL or reference to any attachment included with the message (string). Currently all values are null in the sample.
attachment_url.analysis_use: Identify messages with attachments, count attachments per ticket, and link to stored media. In current data it will not contribute but schema supports future attachment tracking.
business_questions: Which tickets include attachments and how many attachments are sent per ticket?
Do messages with attachments correlate with longer resolution times?
attachment_url.nl2sql_hints: Can be NULL — filter using WHERE attachment_url IS NOT NULL to find messages with attachments. Expect variable-length strings; may include full URLs. If always null in production snapshot, avoid relying on it until populated. Use LENGTH/CHAR_LENGTH to validate non-empty values.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the message row was created in the system (stored as nvarchar). May differ from message_ts if events are delayed.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time analysis (latency between message_ts and created_at), detecting late arrivals, and ordering when message_ts is missing or unreliable. CAST to datetime for comparisons.
business_questions: What is the ingestion latency between message creation and recorded message_ts (created_at vs message_ts)?
Are there periods with elevated ingestion delays that could affect SLA calculations?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string — CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) before date arithmetic. Use (CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) - CAST(message_ts AS DATETIME)) to compute ingestion latency; guard against NULL/malformed values. Prefer created_at to determine when rows became available in the database if message_ts is event time.

vendor_id.description: Unique identifier for the vendor (string code like 'ECOMV3001').
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup/join key to link vendor metadata to transactional tables; filter or group by vendor; exact-match selection and IN-lists for small sets.
business_questions: Which transactions belong to vendor ECOMV3001?
List all vendors that have been active/inactive in a given period (when joined to transactions).
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical key (varchar). Use equality or IN (avoid LIKE). Prefer exact-match joins; consider adding index/PK. Normalize whitespace/casing if ingestion inconsistent.

vendor_name.analysis_use: Displayed in reports, grouped for counts; used for fuzzy or substring search when users query by business name; not ideal as join key.
business_questions: Show vendor details for 'Tara Ltd'.
Which vendors have similar names (potential duplicates)?
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: Names are free text — NL2SQL should support case-insensitive matching and wildcard/ILIKE searches. Consider fuzzy matching for misspellings and trimming whitespace. Avoid using vendor_name for joins.

vendor_category.description: Categorical classification of vendor (Ad Agency, Courier, Influencer, Marketing, Tech).
vendor_category.analysis_use: Excellent for grouping, aggregation and filters due to low cardinality; computing counts and proportions by category; segment-based joins and filters.
business_questions: How many vendors are in the 'Courier' category?
What percentage of vendors are 'Influencer' vs 'Tech'?
vendor_category.nl2sql_hints: Treat as an ENUM-like filter. Use equality or IN for multiple categories. Map synonyms where natural language may use alternate terms (e.g., 'ad agency' vs 'advertising').

phone.description: Vendor phone number (string, includes country code and formatting).
phone.analysis_use: Contact list generation and validation checks; deduplication (when combined with email); PII audits and masking before export.
business_questions: Provide phone numbers for all ACTIVE vendors.
Which vendors have missing or malformed phone numbers?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone is a free-text field—normalize by removing non-digit characters for comparisons. NL2SQL should support pattern matching (LIKE or regex) to filter by country code or area code. Treat as sensitive data (mask in outputs).

email.description: Vendor email address for contact and billing.
email.analysis_use: Contact list, uniqueness and completeness checks, join target for vendor communication systems. Good identifier for deduplication/checks alongside vendor_name and phone.
business_questions: List emails for vendors with status = 'ACTIVE'.
Which vendors are missing an email or have non-corporate email patterns?
email.nl2sql_hints: Use case-insensitive matching. Support LIKE and regex for domain-based queries (e.g., @ecomvendor.in). Treat as PII and mask when returning results. Validate email format when filtering.

billing_address.description: Free-form billing address (street, area, postal code).
billing_address.analysis_use: Address-level reports, geocoding/enrichment, grouping by locality when normalized. Useful for tax/regulatory checks and audit sampling.
business_questions: Which vendors have billing addresses in a given postal code or city?
How many vendors have missing or unusually short billing addresses?
billing_address.nl2sql_hints: Unstructured text: use LIKE/ILIKE for substring filters. Consider pre-processing to parse city/postal code into separate columns for efficient queries. Avoid exact equality for addresses.

gst_no.analysis_use: Tax compliance checks, join/enrich with finance systems, match invoices/payments to vendor tax identity, completeness and uniqueness checks.
business_questions: Which vendors do not have a GST number on record?
Find vendor by GST number to reconcile invoices.
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as exact-match key (string). Remove non-alphanumeric characters when normalizing. Sensitive PII — mask when returning. Use equality for joins/filters; consider uniqueness constraints.

pan_no.description: Vendor PAN (permanent account number) for tax/identity.
pan_no.analysis_use: Identity verification, tax reporting, deduplication and compliance checks. Useful in finance reconciliations.
business_questions: Which vendors have missing or duplicate PAN numbers?
List vendors with a given PAN to audit payments.
pan_no.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match field; normalize casing and whitespace. Treat as sensitive; mask in outputs. Use equality for filters. Consider indexing if used in lookups.

city.description: City portion of vendor address (normalized to a short token).
city.analysis_use: Geographic aggregation (counts by city), regional reporting, filtering for localized vendor programs, grouping for dashboards.
business_questions: How many vendors are in Anand vs Ahmedabad?
List ACTIVE vendors in a specific city for local procurement.
city.nl2sql_hints: Use equality or IN for filtering. May require standardization of city names (aliases). Good candidate for an index or partitioning key for regional queries.

status.description: Lifecycle state of vendor (ACTIVE or INACTIVE).
status.analysis_use: Primary filter for operational/analytics queries (only ACTIVE vendors for current operations), segmentation in reports, join filters to exclude inactive partners.
business_questions: How many vendors are currently ACTIVE vs INACTIVE?
Provide contact info for all ACTIVE vendors only.
status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field — use equality filters. Ensure queries default to excluding INACTIVE where applicable. Normalize values and consider mapping synonyms ('active','Active').

created_at.description: Ingestion or creation timestamp stored as nvarchar (currently identical for all rows).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit when the reference dataset was loaded; not useful for vendor-created-at analysis unless converted to a proper timestamp and populated per row.
business_questions: When was this vendor reference snapshot loaded?
Are vendor records being updated over time (requires storing per-row timestamp)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Currently stored as string; cast to datetime in queries for time-based filters. If intended as row-level creation time, convert column to a proper TIMESTAMP/DATE during ETL and populate per-row values. NL2SQL should cast string to datetime when parsing temporal predicates.

wishlist_id.description: Surrogate identifier for each wishlist record (nvarchar(28)). In this dataset it appears unique per row (100 distinct values). Example top values include WISH04001..WISH04005.
wishlist_id.analysis_use: Used to deduplicate or reference individual wishlist entries. Useful for row-level analytics, auditing, and joins to activity logs that might reference wishlist events. Not typically aggregated except for counts or existence checks.
business_questions: How many wishlist entries exist in the system (total and by time window)?
Can we retrieve the specific wishlist entry details for audit or customer support?
wishlist_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique/text identifier. Use equality or IN filters for single-record lookups. Because there's no declared PK, consider using SELECT DISTINCT wishlist_id to confirm uniqueness. When generating WHERE wishlist_id = 'WISH04001' ensure string literal matches exact case/whitespace; trim if necessary.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who added the wishlist item (nvarchar(26)). 65 distinct values in 100 rows - indicates some customers add multiple items. Top customers: CUST1058, CUST1066, CUST1083, CUST1081, CUST1018.
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary field for grouping and joining to customer metadata and behavior. Use for counts per customer, frequency of wishlist activity, segmentation, and joining to order history to measure conversion. Useful in GROUP BY, HAVING, and join predicates.
business_questions: Which customers have the most wishlist items?
Which customer segments (from customers table) generate the most wishlist activity?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to ecom_customers on customer_id (string equality). Consider trimming and normalizing case because both sides are nvarchar. For counts use GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC. Use WHERE customer_id IN (...) for targeted queries. Beware of nulls (column allows NULL) — use WHERE customer_id IS NOT NULL when joining.

sku.description: Product SKU code added to the wishlist (nvarchar(22)). Very low cardinality: 7 distinct SKUs; skewed toward a few popular SKUs (TOM001 20%, POT002 16%, CAR004 15%, ONI003 13%, APP009 12%).
sku.analysis_use: Key for product-level analytics: most wished SKUs, SKU popularity over time, SKU-level priority counts. Join key to product tables (products, product_variants, product_sales) to enrich with name, category, price, and inventory. Ideal for GROUP BY sku and aggregations.
business_questions: Which SKUs are most frequently added to wishlists?
Are certain SKUs more often flagged as priority?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Join using sku = products.sku or product_variants.sku; ensure SKU formatting matches (trim/case). Because cardinality is low, GROUP BY sku will be efficient. For time-series SKU trends, combine with parsed added_at. Use WHERE sku = 'TOM001' or WHERE sku IN (...) for targeted reports. Consider joins to product_pricing/product_inventory for stock/price impact on wishlist counts.

added_at.description: Timestamp (nvarchar(48)) when the item was added to the wishlist. Stored as text in format like '2025-10-05 02:46:01'. Distinct for all rows in sample (100 distinct).
added_at.analysis_use: Critical for time-based analysis (activity over time, recency, funneling to purchase). Must be cast/parsed to a datetime datatype for range filters, grouping by date, week, month, and computing intervals to purchases.
business_questions: How has wishlist activity trended over time (daily/weekly/monthly)?
What is the average time between adding to wishlist and purchasing (after joining orders)?
added_at.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT from nvarchar to datetime in SQL (e.g., CONVERT(datetime, added_at, 120) or CAST(added_at AS datetime) depending on SQL dialect). Use safe parsing with TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT if dialect supports it because free-text can contain invalid values. Indexing on a parsed date column (persisted computed column) is recommended for range queries. When writing NL2SQL, detect phrases like 'last 30 days' and convert to date filters on the parsed datetime.

priority_flag.description: String flag (nvarchar(20)) indicating whether the wishlist item is a priority. Values observed are '0' (54%) and '1' (46%).
priority_flag.analysis_use: Binary categorical variable used for segmentation and prioritization. Useful in counts and group-by queries (e.g., high-priority items vs non-priority). Can be used to target customers for campaigns or to prioritize inventory checks.
business_questions: What share of wishlist items are marked as priority?
Which customers have the most priority wishlist items?
priority_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; consider casting to integer or boolean in queries (e.g., CASE WHEN priority_flag='1' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END). Because values are '0'/'1', handle non-standard values and NULLs (use COALESCE). Use WHERE priority_flag = '1' for filters. Normalize stored values when possible to boolean/int in a cleaned table or view to simplify NL2SQL generation.

notes.description: Free-text field for any note associated with the wishlist item (nvarchar(16)). In this dataset the column is 100% NULL, indicating not used.
notes.analysis_use: When populated, could be used for qualitative analysis, keyword extraction, or support context. Given current data is entirely NULL, it has no analytical value in this sample.
business_questions: Are customers leaving notes explaining why they added an item to their wishlist?
Do notes contain time-sensitive requests (e.g., reminders) that affect outreach?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Because notes are all NULL in current sample, most NL2SQL mappings should ignore this column. If present, use LIKE or full-text search (if supported) for keyword queries. Handle NULLs with WHERE notes IS NOT NULL when searching, and be aware of length/truncation given nvarchar(16).

item_id.description: Unique identifier for this inventory row / inventory item instance (string). Appears unique per row in this dataset.
item_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplicating rows, tracing a specific received record or batch item. Useful for exact lookups and point-time inspections.
business_questions: Show details for inventory item with item_id = 'INV5001'.
How many distinct item_id values were received on a given date?
item_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text key. Use equality filters for precise lookups. If expecting many lookups, index this column or introduce a surrogate primary key. No type conversion required. Confirm uniqueness before depending on it as a PK.

sku.description: Product SKU code (text) identifying the product model/variant. Low cardinality (~10 distinct).
sku.analysis_use: Primary join key to product master tables (products, product_variants, product_inventory) and grouping field for aggregated inventory/stock/cost calculations.
business_questions: What is the total stock_units by SKU?
What is the average cost_per_unit for SKU 'ONI003'?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use SKU to join to products/product_variants. Ensure normalization (trim, uppercase) — text may contain whitespace/casing differences. Consider indexing sku. Use GROUP BY sku for aggregations.

name.description: Human-readable product name/description corresponding to the sku.
name.analysis_use: Presentation attribute for reporting, grouping or search. Can be used to de-duplicate or validate SKU mapping and to display friendly names in results.
business_questions: List all inventory items with name containing 'Onion' and their stock.
Which product names correspond to SKU 'APP009'?
name.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text. Use LIKE or full-text search for free-text queries. Prefer JOIN on sku for authoritative product metadata rather than relying on name.

total_stock_units.description: Quantity of stock units available in this inventory row stored as text (numeric semantic).
total_stock_units.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to compute total stock by sku/supplier/batch and filter by stock thresholds. Convert to integer/decimal before numeric operations.
business_questions: What is the total available stock (sum of total_stock_units) across all SKUs?
Which batches have stock below reorder threshold (e.g., stock < 50)?
total_stock_units.nl2sql_hints: Contains numeric values stored as NVARCHAR. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT/CAST to integer (e.g., TRY_CAST(total_stock_units AS INT)). Guard against non-numeric or NULL values; coalesce to 0 where appropriate. Consider adding a computed persisted integer column for NL2SQL performance.

batch_id.description: Batch identifier for the received goods (string). Mostly unique per row (99 distinct).
batch_id.analysis_use: Batch-level grouping and tracing for expiry, QA, and recalls. Identify stock grouped by batch and join with other batch-related tables if present.
business_questions: Show all inventory records for batch_id = 'BATCH6982'.
How much stock is there by batch and what are their expiry dates?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text. Good join candidate when other tables store batch_id. Because batch_id is nearly unique, use equality filters or IN-list for batch-based inspections. Consider indexing batch_id for fast lookup.

received_at.description: Timestamp when the items were received into inventory; stored as text in yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss format.
received_at.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate receipts over time (daily/weekly/monthly), compute receipt age, and order by receipt recency. Convert to datetime for temporal operations.
business_questions: How many units were received per day in the last 30 days?
Which items were received after a specific date/time?
received_at.nl2sql_hints: String-encoded timestamp. Use TRY_CONVERT/TRY_CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP (DB-specific) to parse — format appears ISO so standard conversion should work. For NL2SQL, map temporal qualifiers (today, last 7 days) to conversions on this column. Be mindful of timezone assumptions if any.

expiry_date.description: Expiry date of the batch/item in yyyy-mm-dd format but stored as text. Lower cardinality (~29 distinct values).
expiry_date.analysis_use: Identify near-expiry and expired stock, compute shelf-life remaining, and prioritize disposition or promotions. Group by expiry buckets.
business_questions: Which SKUs expire within the next 7 days and how many units are affected?
List expired batches as of today with their stock counts.
expiry_date.nl2sql_hints: Parse to DATE type before comparisons (TRY_CAST(expiry_date AS DATE)). Watch out for NULL/empty expiry_date for non-perishables. NL2SQL should translate relative date language (e.g., 'next week') to date arithmetic against this column.

supplier.description: Name of the supplier/vendor providing the batch. Very low cardinality (3 suppliers).
supplier.analysis_use: Supplier-level grouping for spend/volume analysis, supplier performance, concentration risk. Joinable to vendor master tables for enrichment.
business_questions: Which supplier provides the most units and what proportion of stock do they represent?
What is the average cost_per_unit by supplier?
supplier.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality text field — ideal grouping column. Normalize names (trim/case) if joining to vendor master. Consider mapping to supplier_id in vendor tables; NL2SQL should prefer joins on vendor id if available.

cost_per_unit.description: Unit cost for the inventory row stored as text with decimal precision (e.g., '111.63').
cost_per_unit.analysis_use: Monetary aggregations: compute inventory value (cost_per_unit * total_stock_units), average/min/max cost trend analysis. Convert to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for calculations.
business_questions: What is the total inventory value by SKU (sum(cost_per_unit * total_stock_units))?
How has the average cost_per_unit changed for a SKU over time?
cost_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: Stored as NVARCHAR — convert using TRY_CAST(cost_per_unit AS DECIMAL(18,4)) or DB-specific functions. Handle non-numeric or null values. Use explicit precision/scale to avoid rounding surprises. Consider a computed numeric column or pre-processed ETL to store cost as numeric for NL2SQL stability.

mfg_date.description: Manufacture date for the batch in yyyy-mm-dd format stored as text.
mfg_date.analysis_use: Compute shelf life (expiry_date - mfg_date), age since manufacture, and quality checks. Useful for batch traceability.
business_questions: What is the average shelf life (days between mfg_date and expiry_date) for perishable SKUs?
Show batches manufactured before a certain date.
mfg_date.nl2sql_hints: Parse to DATE (TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT). Account for missing mfg_date values. Use date arithmetic for age/shelf-life calculations. NL2SQL must map relative terms ('older than 30 days') to comparisons against this column.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this inventory row was created in the source system, stored as text (yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion timing, detect late-arriving records, incremental extraction using max(created_at) for ETL/replication, and temporal queries on when records were added.
business_questions: Show inventory records created in the last 24 hours.
What is the daily count of inventory records created over the last month?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text timestamp. Use TRY_CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP to convert. For incremental NL2SQL queries use max(created_at) as watermark after conversion. Beware of timezone and format consistency.

updated_at.description: Timestamp when the inventory row was last updated, stored as text (yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss).
updated_at.analysis_use: Detect recent updates, backfills, reconciliation between created_at and updated_at, and support CDC-like queries (rows changed since X).
business_questions: Which inventory rows were updated in the last 7 days?
Find items where updated_at differs significantly from created_at (indicating adjustments).
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Parse to timestamp for date-range filters and change detection. Use updated_at > <timestamp> patterns for NL2SQL change queries. Consider indexing or maintaining a boolean 'is_changed_recently' in ETL for faster frequent queries.

summary_id.description: Unique identifier for each daily summary row (e.g., 'SUM1000').
summary_id.analysis_use: Row-level identity for de-duplication and tracing; can be used to reference or filter specific summary rows. Not required for aggregations but useful for audit and joins to metadata tables if such exist.
business_questions: Which summary record corresponds to an anomalous entry for a given date and store?
When was a particular summary row created or updated (trace by summary_id)?
summary_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string. Often unique in this dataset — can be used in WHERE or IN clauses. Not a reliable join key to other transactional tables. If used in NL2SQL generation prefer equality filters (summary_id = 'SUM1000') rather than range filters.

store_id.description: Identifier of the physical POS store (e.g., 'STORE_AHD_001').
store_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering dimension for reporting and comparisons across locations. Low cardinality makes it good for GROUP BY and indexed filtering.
business_questions: What are total daily sales for STORE_AHD_001 over the last 30 days?
Which store has the highest refunds as a percentage of sales?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: String typed; commonly used in WHERE/GROUP BY. Prefer exact matches or IN lists (e.g., store_id IN ('STORE_AHD_001','STORE_RJT_001')). Join to pos_stores on store_id; ensure target table has same string format. Consider normalizing/canonicalizing store_id if other tables use different casing or formats.

date.description: Date of the summary (stored as nvarchar, format appears 'YYYY-MM-DD').
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trend analysis, time-window filters (day/week/month), grouping and joins to calendar tables or other daily snapshots. Used for ORDER BY in time-series queries.
business_questions: What were total sales on 2025-11-16 across all stores?
How did sales change week-over-week for each store?
date.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as string, values appear in ISO 'YYYY-MM-DD' format—safe for lexical ordering but cast to DATE for date arithmetic. NL2SQL should generate CAST(date AS DATE) or TO_DATE(date, 'YYYY-MM-DD') when performing range filters or date functions. Ensure timezone considerations if joining to timestamp-based tables.

total_txns.description: Total number of transactions recorded for the store on the date (stored as nvarchar).
total_txns.analysis_use: Key activity metric for counting operations, used in aggregations (SUM, AVG), denominators for per-transaction metrics, or to compute throughput and conversion metrics. Useful for anomaly detection (sudden drops/spikes).
business_questions: How many transactions did each store process yesterday?
Which days had unusually low transaction counts compared to historical average?
total_txns.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; must CAST/CONVERT to integer (e.g., CAST(total_txns AS INT)) before numeric aggregation or arithmetic. Watch for NULLs or non-numeric characters. When generating SQL, prefer safe casts or TRY_CAST semantics if available to avoid errors.

total_sales.description: Sum of sales (currency) for the store on the date; stored as nvarchar and contains decimal values.
total_sales.analysis_use: Primary financial metric for revenue aggregation, trend analysis, ranking stores by sales, computing growth rates, and calculating ratios (e.g., tax/sales, refunds/sales).
business_questions: What were the daily revenue totals by store for the past month?
Which store had the highest total sales last week?
total_sales.nl2sql_hints: Strings representing decimals—must be CAST/CONVERT to numeric/decimal (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2) or FLOAT) prior to SUM()/AVG()/ratios. Numeric precision is important; choose DECIMAL for monetary calculations. Watch out for scientific or malformed values; prefer TRY_CAST where supported.

total_tax.description: Total tax collected on the day's sales for the store (stored as nvarchar decimals).
total_tax.analysis_use: Used to compute effective tax rate (total_tax / total_sales), to verify tax reporting, and to aggregate tax liabilities by period or store.
business_questions: What is the effective tax rate per store for the given period?
How much tax was collected across all stores last month?
total_tax.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—CAST to DECIMAL for arithmetic. When computing rates, guard against division by zero (total_sales = 0). Use consistent numeric scale to avoid rounding errors (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)).

total_refunds.description: Total monetary value of refunds issued that day for the store (stored as nvarchar decimals).
total_refunds.analysis_use: Used to compute refund rates, impact on net sales, trending returns, and identifying stores/dates with high refunds for quality checks.
business_questions: What percentage of sales were refunded last quarter per store?
Which dates had spikes in refund amounts that require investigation?
total_refunds.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—convert to DECIMAL for aggregations and ratio calculations. When calculating refund rate use safe divisions and consider net_sales = total_sales - total_refunds as a derived metric. Check for negative values or anomalies in source formatting.

avg_txn_value.description: Average transaction value for the day (stored as nvarchar decimal). May be precomputed as total_sales / total_txns or calculated externally.
avg_txn_value.analysis_use: Used as a performance KPI to assess basket size and sales quality. Can be recomputed for validation by dividing total_sales by total_txns after casting.
business_questions: What is the average basket value for each store over the month?
Are there days where avg_txn_value deviates significantly from the historical average?
avg_txn_value.nl2sql_hints: String decimal—CAST to DECIMAL. Validate precomputed avg_txn_value by recomputing: CAST(total_sales AS DECIMAL)/NULLIF(CAST(total_txns AS DECIMAL),0). NL2SQL should prefer recomputing if source reliability is unknown. Handle division-by-zero and null safely.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the summary row was created (stored as nvarchar, appears 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for auditing, determining freshness of data, incremental loads, and ordering by ingestion time. Can be used to filter recent updates.
business_questions: When was this summary row generated or last updated?
How many summaries were created in the last 24 hours?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: String timestamp—CAST/CONVERT to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME for range filtering or time arithmetic. Use consistent timezone assumptions. For incremental NL2SQL queries, filter on CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) >= ... . Expect varying precisions; use TRY_CAST if available.

notes.description: Free-text note about the row; currently constant 'Daily summary'.
notes.analysis_use: Primarily metadata; can be used to filter special rows if notes diverge in future (e.g., 'recalculated', 'estimated'). Currently low analytical value.
business_questions: Are there any summary rows marked as 'recalculated' or with special notes?
Which summaries were created with special annotations?
notes.nl2sql_hints: String; low cardinality and often constant. Use equality filters where applicable (notes = 'Daily summary'). Not used as a join key. Watch for trailing spaces or casing differences when matching.

adjustment_id.description: Unique identifier for the inventory adjustment record (text).
adjustment_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplication, record-level audits, filtering a specific adjustment. Useful when joining or explaining a specific event.
business_questions: Show the details for adjustment PAD3004 (or another specific adjustment_id).
How many distinct adjustment_id values exist in a date range (detect duplicates or re-inserts)?
adjustment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique string. Use exact equality when filtering (WHERE adjustment_id = 'PAD3000'). For joins to audit logs or external sources use exact string match. Consider that there is no declared PK—queries that assume uniqueness should guard for duplicates.

pos_sku.description: SKU identifier used by POS for the product affected by the adjustment.
pos_sku.analysis_use: Grouping (GROUP BY pos_sku) and filtering to compute per-SKU adjustment summaries, join key to product/product_variant tables to enrich with product attributes.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest total negative qty_change (shrinkage)?
For a given SKU (e.g., APP009_001), what is the time series of inventory adjustments?
pos_sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; use exact match or prefix/LIKE for pattern filters. Normalize case if SKU case-sensitivity is inconsistent. When joining to product tables, match on the appropriate SKU column (pos_products.sku or product_variants.sku). Consider indexing this column for frequent SKU-level queries.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store where the adjustment occurred.
store_id.analysis_use: Filter and group adjustments by store, compute per-store metrics, join to pos_stores for store metadata (region, name).
business_questions: Which stores have the highest frequency or value of adjustments?
Is shrinkage concentrated in specific stores or regions?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; join to pos_stores using store_id. Use exact match for single-store filters (WHERE store_id = 'STORE_RJT_001') and IN lists for multi-store operations. Consider adding indexes for store-level analytics.

adjustment_ts.description: Timestamp when the adjustment was performed (stored as nvarchar).
adjustment_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for timeline analyses (grouping by day/week/month), temporal filters, detecting real-time vs backdated adjustments, ordering events.
business_questions: How many adjustments occurred per day/week and what is the trend?
Are there unusual times when many adjustments happen (after-hours spikes)?
adjustment_ts.nl2sql_hints: Values are strings in format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MI:SS'—cast/parse to proper timestamp type in SQL (e.g., CAST(adjustment_ts AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP). Use parsed timestamps for date truncation (DATE_TRUNC, CONVERT, or CAST) and timezone-aware functions if needed. Watch for malformed values or nulls.

qty_change.description: Quantity change applied by the adjustment (signed integer stored as text). Negative values indicate decreases (e.g., shrinkage), positive values indicate additions.
qty_change.analysis_use: Key metric for aggregations (SUM qty_change per SKU/store/reason), anomaly detection (large magnitude changes), and validation against inventory_before/after.
business_questions: What is the net qty_change by reason or SKU over a period?
List adjustments where absolute qty_change exceeds threshold X (e.g., >10).
qty_change.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—always cast to integer for arithmetic (CAST(qty_change AS INT)). Expect negative signs; guard against non-numeric strings and nulls (use TRY_CAST or CASE). Use SUM(CAST(qty_change AS INT)) for aggregations. For WHERE clauses comparing magnitude, use ABS(CAST(qty_change AS INT)).

reason.description: Categorical reason for the adjustment (e.g., Shrinkage, Stock Count Correction, Damage).
reason.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering to quantify reasons, measuring frequency and volume of adjustments by reason, and prioritizing process improvement.
business_questions: What proportion of total quantity adjusted is due to Shrinkage vs Damage vs Stock Count Correction?
Which stores have the most 'Damage' adjustments?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality text field—use exact matches or IN lists. Normalize casing and whitespace in queries if data is inconsistent (e.g., UPPER(reason)). Consider mapping free-form reasons to a canonical set before aggregation if values vary.

adjusted_by.description: Identifier of the user/operator who made the adjustment (text user id).
adjusted_by.analysis_use: Attribute-level audit: counting adjustments per operator, avg qty_change per operator, linking to pos_users for HR/behavioral analysis and permissions checks.
business_questions: Which operator performed the most adjustments and what is their average adjustment size?
Are specific operators associated with more corrections (potential training or abuse)?
adjusted_by.nl2sql_hints: Likely joinable to pos_users.user_id. Use exact matches; join after verifying id formats. For NL2SQL, when the user supplies a name instead of id, plan to join pos_users to translate name -> user_id. Protect queries from nulls and consider grouping by both adjusted_by and approval_by to detect self-approval patterns.

approval_by.description: Identifier of the approver of the adjustment (text user id).
approval_by.analysis_use: Verify approval workflows, identify approvers approving high-volume or large adjustments, detect approvals by same person who adjusted, and compute SLA metrics for approval lag (approval time not in this table unless deduced from other timestamps).
business_questions: Which approvers have approved the most adjustments, and what amounts did they approve?
How often is the adjuster the same as the approver (self-approval)?
approval_by.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; join to pos_users to get approver names/roles. To detect self-approval, compare adjusted_by = approval_by. If names or display values are used in NL queries, translate to IDs via a join to pos_users.

inventory_before.description: Inventory count recorded before the adjustment (stored as text, integer-like).
inventory_before.analysis_use: Validation (inventory_before + qty_change should equal inventory_after), trend analysis of stock levels, detecting negative or implausible inventory counts.
business_questions: List adjustments where inventory_before is negative or implausible.
For a SKU-store, how often did inventory_before fall below reorder threshold prior to adjustments?
inventory_before.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for all numeric operations (CAST(inventory_before AS INT)). Use TRY_CAST or safe parsing to avoid breaking queries. Beware of leading/trailing spaces. Use inventory_before in WHERE clauses after casting for numeric comparisons.

inventory_after.description: Inventory count after the adjustment (stored as text, integer-like).
inventory_after.analysis_use: Used together with inventory_before and qty_change to validate adjustments, compute post-adjustment stock distribution, and detect mismatches.
business_questions: Which adjustments resulted in inventory_after below zero or below threshold?
Find adjustments where inventory_after != inventory_before + qty_change.
inventory_after.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for comparisons and arithmetic (CAST(inventory_after AS INT)). Frequently used in data quality checks: WHERE CAST(inventory_after AS INT) <> CAST(inventory_before AS INT) + CAST(qty_change AS INT). Consider indexing numeric-converted values for large datasets, and handle null/non-numeric carefully with TRY_CAST.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp (when this row was inserted into the system), stored as text.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit when the record was saved compared to adjustment_ts (detect delayed entry), temporal filtering, and debugging ingestion issues.
business_questions: Are adjustments recorded promptly or is there a lag between adjustment_ts and created_at?
Show adjustments created within a specific ingestion window (e.g., during a migration event).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MI:SS'—cast to datetime for time-based filters and for computing lag: DATEDIFF between parsed adjustment_ts and parsed created_at. Use TRY_CAST/ISDATE checks to avoid parse failures. When both adjustment_ts and created_at exist, clarify which to use for reporting (event time vs ingestion time).

redemption_id.description: Unique identifier for a loyalty redemption event (string).
redemption_id.analysis_use: Primary event identifier for de-duplication, counting distinct redemptions, and joining to any external audit logs that reference the redemption id.
business_questions: How many unique redemption events occurred in a period?
Are there duplicate redemption_id values indicating data issues?
redemption_id.nl2sql_hints: Although values look unique, there is no PK constraint. Use redemption_id for exact equality joins (WHERE redemption_id = 'LR4000'). Be mindful of whitespace/case; use TRIM() if ingestion might include padding. For performance, suggest indexing this column if queries frequently filter by redemption_id.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who redeemed loyalty points (string, references customer master).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group and filter to compute per-customer redemption counts, total points used, and total discount_amount. Key column for joins to customer tables to get demographic or segmentation attributes.
business_questions: Which customers redeem the most points or receive the largest discounts?
What is the redemption frequency distribution across customers?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; when joining, match exactly to customer table's id field (e.g., ecom_customers.customer_id or b2b_customers.customer_id). Consider normalizing casing and trimming. Create an index for frequent customer-level reporting. Expect some nulls — guard joins with IS NOT NULL filters.

pos_txn_id.description: POS transaction identifier associated with the redemption (string).
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Join to pos_transactions or pos_transaction_lines to validate transaction totals, compute redemption share of transaction, tie redemption to products sold, and analyze revenue after discount.
business_questions: Which transactions had loyalty redemptions and what was the discount versus transaction total?
Are redemptions associated with returns or refunds (via transaction linkage)?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Use for inner or left joins to pos_transactions.pos_txn_id. Cast carefully if other tables store txn id with different format. When filtering, use equality; if multiple redemptions can map to a single transaction, aggregate by pos_txn_id. Consider index on pos_txn_id if joining frequently.

loyalty_points_used.description: Number of loyalty points consumed in the redemption (stored as nvarchar, numeric content).
loyalty_points_used.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (SUM, AVG), grouping (distribution of points used), and calculating points-to-discount ratios. Useful for program economics and points liability reconciliation.
business_questions: What is the total number of loyalty points redeemed in a period?
What is the average points-per-redemption and distribution (e.g., median, percentiles)?
loyalty_points_used.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — must be cast to integer (e.g., CAST(loyalty_points_used AS INT) or TRY_CAST in SQL Server) before numeric ops. Handle non-numeric or NULL values using TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC checks. When writing NL2SQL, prefer explicit casts and provide fallback for invalid data (COALESCE(TRY_CAST(...),0)).

discount_amount.description: Monetary value of discount applied for the redemption (stored as nvarchar containing decimal values).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to measure cost of loyalty program, compute average discount, percentage of transaction value (when joined to transactions), and per-store/per-customer discount totals.
business_questions: What is the total monetary cost of loyalty redemptions in a timeframe?
How does average discount per redemption vary by store or customer segment?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Because it's stored as string with decimal values, cast to numeric/decimal (e.g., CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)) or USE TRY_CAST). Watch for localization issues (commas vs dots) though sample shows decimal point. For monetary joins and sums, ensure consistent currency and precision. Use COALESCE to handle NULLs.

redeemed_at.description: Timestamp when the redemption occurred (stored as nvarchar, sample format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
redeemed_at.analysis_use: Time-series analyses (hour/day/week/month), sessionization with transactions, time-based filters, peak-hour/store staffing analysis and trend detection.
business_questions: How do redemptions trend over time (daily/weekly/monthly)?
What hours of day have the highest redemption volume?
redeemed_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — convert to native datetime (e.g., CAST(redeemed_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_CAST depending on DB). Validate consistent format; use parsing functions if timezone info present. When writing NL2SQL, always cast before date arithmetic or grouping by date parts. Consider indexing a computed datetime column for performance.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store where redemption happened (string, low cardinality).
store_id.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering to get store-level counts, totals and KPIs (cost of loyalty per store), and joining to store master (pos_stores) for region or category metrics.
business_questions: Which stores generate the most redemptions or highest total discount amounts?
Is redemption behavior (points used or discount size) different across stores or regions?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — good candidate for GROUP BY. Join to pos_stores or store dimension on exact match. Ensure consistent naming (e.g., 'STORE_RJT_001') and trim whitespace. If used in filters often, a small lookup table or mapping can improve readability in NL2SQL responses.

created_by.description: Identifier of the user/terminal that created the redemption record (string, low cardinality).
created_by.analysis_use: Audit and operational analytics: who performed redemptions, staff-level performance, potential misuse or training needs, and reconciliation of manual redemptions.
business_questions: Which staff members create the most redemptions and what is the average discount they apply?
Are certain creators associated with anomalous redemption patterns?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Join to pos_users or terminals table by exact id if available. Because of low cardinality, use for grouping and pivoting. Watch for multiple naming conventions; normalize if necessary.

approval_id.description: Reference identifier for approval/authorization tied to the redemption (string, near-unique).
approval_id.analysis_use: Audit trails and approval workflow analysis: verify that redemptions had proper approvals, count redemptions per approval type/person, and flag missing approvals.
business_questions: What proportion of redemptions have an associated approval_id?
Which approval ids (or approvers) correspond to the largest discounts or highest frequency of approvals?
approval_id.nl2sql_hints: Use equality joins to approvals/audit tables if they exist. Because many values are distinct, treat as categorical but not low-cardinality. Check for NULLs; if showing many nulls, include LEFT JOIN semantics in NL2SQL. No direct foreign key — confirm naming conventions before joining.

notes.description: Free-text notes for the redemption; in sample data this appears constant ('Loyalty redemption').
notes.analysis_use: Limited analytical value in current dataset but can hold operational comments for audits. If populated variably, can be searched for keywords or used in text analysis to detect non-standard reasons.
business_questions: Are there any redemptions with special notes that require follow-up?
Do notes contain reasons or flags that correlate with anomalous redemptions?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Because notes is text and currently constant, avoid heavy use in aggregations. When filtering by content, use LIKE or full-text search (if supported). Trim whitespace and account for NULLs. NL2SQL should avoid assuming structural content in notes.

date.description: The calendar date for which the product-level sales metrics are reported. Stored as nvarchar but values appear in YYYY-MM-DD format.
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for filtering, grouping (day, week, month, year), time-series and trend analysis, window functions for moving averages, and joins to order/transaction date fields.
business_questions: What were the net sales for product X on 2025-01-24?
Which dates had peak gross sales for a given product or overall?
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text that should be CAST/TRY_CAST to DATE (e.g., TRY_CAST(date AS DATE) or CAST(date AS DATE) depending on platform). Accepts YYYY-MM-DD; guard against NULLs and malformed strings. Prefer pushing date filters after a safe cast or use a precomputed date column to allow index seek on real date types. Support grouping by date parts (YEAR(date), MONTH(date)) — implement those via CAST(date AS DATE) then DATEPART/FORMAT.

product_id.description: Identifier for the product sold (text). Appears to be a product SKU/code (e.g., ONI003).
product_id.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation key for product-level KPIs, filtering for product-specific queries, joining to product master tables for attributes (name, category, unit). Useful for top-N product queries and product comparisons.
business_questions: Which products are the top 5 by net sales in the last 30 days?
How does product ONI003's average price per kg change month-over-month?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to product master tables (pos_products, products). Trim and normalize case if inconsistencies exist (e.g., UPPER(TRIM(product_id))). Because product_id is low cardinality here, ensure joins are selective by combining with date filters when possible. Be mindful of NULLs and inconsistent formatting (leading/trailing spaces).

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the product on the date. Stored as nvarchar; values represent weight in kg.
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (SUM, AVG), ratios (qty per transaction, qty per day), unit-price computations (gross_sales / total_qty_kg), share of volume by product, trend analysis of volume sold.
business_questions: What is the total kg sold for product GUR010 in January 2025?
Which products have the highest average daily kg sold over the last quarter?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Must be converted to numeric before aggregation: use safe conversions and cleaning (e.g., TRY_CAST(REPLACE(total_qty_kg, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,3))). Account for possible non-numeric characters or localization (commas/periods). Handle zeros and NULLs. Consider creating a persisted/virtual numeric column to speed up repeated numeric queries.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales amount (before discounts/taxes) for the product on the date. Stored as nvarchar string representing monetary value.
gross_sales.analysis_use: Aggregate for revenue analysis, compute gross margin metrics when joined to cost, derive discount/tax shares, and rank products by gross revenue. Used in time-series and trend analytics.
business_questions: What is the gross sales trend for product POT002 across the last six months?
Which product had the highest single-day gross sales in 2024?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Clean and cast to numeric for sums and comparisons: e.g., TRY_CAST(REPLACE(gross_sales, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,2)). Remove currency symbols, parentheses for negatives, and thousands separators. Use COALESCE to treat NULLs as 0 where appropriate. Precompute numeric gross_sales in a view or column for performance.

discount_amount.description: Total discount given on gross sales for the product on the date. Stored as text but represents monetary value.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to understand discount impact, compute discount rate (discount / gross_sales), trend analysis of discounting behavior by product or time period, and reconciliation with net sales.
business_questions: What is the total discount given on BRN007 during Black Friday week?
Which product has the highest average discount rate (discount/gross) month-to-date?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric after cleaning: e.g., TRY_CAST(REPLACE(discount_amount, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,2)). Watch for negative formatting or parentheses, and ensure division by zero is guarded when computing rates. Consider null-to-zero substitution (COALESCE) for aggregation.

tax_amount.description: Total tax applied to sales for the product on the date. Stored as nvarchar string representing tax amount.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax revenue, compute effective tax rates, separate tax from net/gross analysis, and reconcile with accounting/tax reports when joined to fiscal tables.
business_questions: How much tax was collected for CAR004 in Q4 2024?
What is the effective tax rate (tax/gross) per product over the last year?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric with cleaning: TRY_CAST(REPLACE(tax_amount, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,2)). Ensure consistent decimal separators and remove currency symbols. Guard rate computations with NULL/zero checks and prefer precomputed numeric column or view for frequent queries.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount after discounts and taxes for the product on the date. Stored as nvarchar string representing monetary value.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for business reporting and KPI calculations, aggregation for total revenue, per-product profitability analysis (when joined to cost), and trend analysis. Use for ranking and financial dashboards.
business_questions: What are total net sales by product for the previous month?
Has net sales per kg improved for ONI003 compared to the prior year?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Must be cleaned and cast to numeric (e.g., TRY_CAST(REPLACE(net_sales, ',', '') AS DECIMAL(18,2))).
