Validate net_sales against gross_discount_tax arithmetic (gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount ≈ net_sales) as a data quality check. Prefer materializing casts into numeric persisted columns or build a cleaning view to improve NL2SQL reliability and performance.

pos_sku.description: POS-specific SKU identifier (store-level product key used in the point-of-sale).
pos_sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping/joining key for POS-level sales and inventory analyses; filter to target specific POS items; grouping for counts and lookups.
business_questions: Which pos_sku have stock below reorder_point?
What is the current POS price for a given pos_sku?
pos_sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text; trim whitespace; may contain nulls. Use pos_sku to join to pos_transaction_lines or sales tables. Watch for duplicates and missing values — not guaranteed unique in this table. When comparing, use exact string match after normalizing case/whitespace.

sku.description: Canonical SKU (likely product-level identifier without store suffix).
sku.analysis_use: Join key to master product tables (products, product_variants, product_pricing). Useful for grouping inventory and aggregating at product level across stores.
business_questions: How many distinct SKUs are present in the POS feed?
Which SKUs map to multiple pos_sku (store-level variants)?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar and can be null; often matches product tables. Normalize before join (trim, uppercase). Use sku when aggregating across stores or joining to product catalog.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store this POS snapshot belongs to.
store_id.analysis_use: Filter or group by store; join to pos_stores for store metadata. In this dataset store_id is constant (single store).
business_questions: Which stores have this product in their POS catalog?
For a given store_id, list POS products and stock quantities.
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Value is textual; expect single value in this snapshot. Use to filter (WHERE store_id = 'STORE_AHD_001'). Ensure consistent casing and trim. Join to pos_stores using exact match.

plucode.description: Price Look-Up code (PLU) used for certain product types (often produce), represented here as text (appears with .0 suffix).
plucode.analysis_use: Group or lookup by PLU for cross-referencing PLU-based pricing/pricing lists and integration with other PLU-based systems. Useful for linking to external PLU catalogs.
business_questions: Which products share the same PLU code?
List products with PLU codes and their POS prices.
plucode.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar with decimal-like values (e.g., '4011.0'); when comparing to numeric PLU sources cast to integer or trim the '.0'. Normalize by CAST(REPLACE(plucode, '.0', '') AS INT) or use string pattern. Handle nulls.

name.description: Display name of the product as shown on POS (human-readable product title).
name.analysis_use: Used in reporting, labeling, and grouping in user-facing outputs. Useful for fuzzy matching and verification of SKUs.
business_questions: Show product names with the lowest stock levels.
Which product names were updated most recently?
name.nl2sql_hints: Text field that may contain parentheses or weight descriptors (e.g., '1kg'). When searching by name prefer LIKE or full-text search if available. Beware of duplicates or small variations; consider normalizing for joins (trim/case).

uom.description: Unit of measure for the SKU (e.g., 'kg', '250g', '500g').
uom.analysis_use: Group products by UoM, convert quantities where needed, and normalize weight-based inventory calculations.
business_questions: How many products are sold by 'kg' versus prepackaged units?
What is the average stock level for products measured in kg?
uom.nl2sql_hints: Small set of categorical values. Normalize textual variants (e.g., 'KG' vs 'kg'). When aggregating quantities conversion may be required; UoM conversions are not present here — handle externally.

pos_price.description: Price of the product in POS (as captured in the feed) stored as text (e.g., '185.0').
pos_price.analysis_use: Numeric aggregations (avg, min, max), price lists, price comparisons and revenue estimation when multiplied by quantity. Also for price-change detection when historical snapshots exist.
business_questions: What is the current price list for all POS products?
Which products are the most expensive / least expensive in the POS catalog?
pos_price.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; must be CAST or CONVERT to numeric (DECIMAL/NUMERIC) before arithmetic. Strip currency symbols and whitespace. Handle nulls and non-numeric values. Consider rounding to appropriate decimal precision.

tax_rate.description: Tax rate applicable to the product as text (appears constant as '5.0000000000000003').
tax_rate.analysis_use: Calculate tax-inclusive price or tax amounts on transactions when joined with sales lines; used to verify tax policy applied at POS.
business_questions: What tax rate is applied to products in this POS snapshot?
Which products have non-standard tax rates?
tax_rate.nl2sql_hints: Value stored as text representation of float. CAST to DECIMAL or FLOAT for numeric operations. Watch for consistent representation; in this snapshot it's constant. Use caution with floating precision when comparing.

stock_qty.description: On-hand stock quantity for the SKU in POS (stored as text, e.g., '186.0').
stock_qty.analysis_use: Core metric for inventory analysis: summation, min/max, low-stock alerts, and inventory valuation (if cost data is available).
business_questions: Which SKUs have stock lower than the reorder_point?
Total on-hand units across all SKUs in this snapshot.
stock_qty.nl2sql_hints: Text field that must be cast to numeric for comparisons and aggregation. Remove '.0' if present and CAST to INT or DECIMAL. Handle nulls and non-numeric strings. Beware of differing UoMs — units may represent different measures (kg vs pieces).

reorder_point.description: Configured threshold quantity at which the product should be reordered (text).
reorder_point.analysis_use: Compare against stock_qty to flag restocking needs; group by product types to prioritize replenishment.
business_questions: Which products require reorder (stock_qty <= reorder_point)?
How many items are at or below their reorder point?
reorder_point.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; CAST to numeric for comparisons. Nulls may indicate no reorder policy. Use inclusive comparisons (<=) and ensure both sides are same numeric type and unit.

active_flag.description: Flag indicating whether the product is active in POS (stored as text, here present as '1.0').
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter active products for displays, exclude inactive items from active lists and reporting.
business_questions: How many active products are present in the POS catalog?
List inactive products (if any) to reconcile catalog cleanup.
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Textual boolean-like field; values seen as '1.0'. Normalize to boolean or integer (CAST to INT or parse '1' as active). Treat nulls as unknown/inactive depending on business rule.

updated_at.description: Last updated timestamp for the POS product row stored as text (e.g., '2025-11-05 11:45:22').
updated_at.analysis_use: Temporal analysis of catalog updates, freshness checks, identifying recently changed prices/stock and incremental ingestion windows.
business_questions: When was the POS product list last updated for each SKU?
Which products have not been updated recently (stale data)?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar, requires parsing to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT in SQL Server or equivalent). Handle timezone assumptions if present. Use in WHERE clauses for incremental sync (e.g., WHERE TRY_CAST(updated_at AS DATETIME) > @last_sync). Beware of NULLs or malformed strings.

Unnamed: 12.description: Empty/unused column from the raw ingestion (all values NULL).
Unnamed: 12.analysis_use: No analytic use; safe to drop during cleaning/ETL.
business_questions: Is this column populated? (No — it's empty.)
Should this column be removed from the cleaned schema?
Unnamed: 12.nl2sql_hints: Ignore or drop. If present in queries, filter out NULL values; do not use as join key.

Unnamed: 13.description: Empty/unused column from the raw ingestion (all values NULL).
Unnamed: 13.analysis_use: No analytic use; safe to drop during cleaning/ETL.
business_questions: Is this column populated? (No — it's empty.)
Can this be removed to simplify the schema?
Unnamed: 13.nl2sql_hints: Ignore or drop. No joins or filters should rely on this column.

Unnamed: 14.description: Mostly empty column; contains only whitespace or a single-space value in the small sample (one distinct value that appears to be blank).
Unnamed: 14.analysis_use: No meaningful analytic use; likely artefact of CSV/Excel import.
business_questions: Does this column hold any meaningful values? (No.)
Should this column be removed from downstream reports?
Unnamed: 14.nl2sql_hints: Trim and inspect; if empty for all rows drop it. Do not treat as key or attribute.

sku.description: Product identifier (SKU) from POS/system. nvarchar(22) in raw snapshot; values like 'APP009', 'BRN007'.
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering key for product-level aggregations (SUM, AVG, RANK). Used to join to product master and other sales tables to enrich records with product name, category, vendor, etc. Also used to identify distinct products and compute SKU-level KPIs.
business_questions: Which SKUs generate the most gross sales?
Which SKUs have the largest discounts or highest discount rate?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. Use exact matching for joins (e.g., ON raw.sku = products.sku). Be aware of whitespace/casing — TRIM() and UPPER()/LOWER() normalization may be necessary if other tables store SKU differently.

Gross Sales.description: Total gross sales amount for the SKU (raw nvarchar). Represents pre-discount, pre-tax sales value in currency but stored as text.
Gross Sales.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue analysis: SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, ORDER BY to rank products. Use in calculations for margins and rates (discount/gross, tax/gross). Needs conversion to numeric type (DECIMAL/NUMERIC) before aggregation.
business_questions: What is the total gross sales across all SKUs or for a selected SKU?
What are the top N SKUs by gross sales?
Gross Sales.nl2sql_hints: Column name contains a space; reference with quoting or brackets (e.g., [Gross Sales]). Always TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT or use safe parsing (TRY_PARSE) to DECIMAL (e.g., TRY_CAST([Gross Sales] AS DECIMAL(18,2))) to avoid failures on bad rows. Consider trimming and removing thousands separators/currency symbols prior to casting.

Discount Amount.description: Total discount amount applied to the SKU (raw nvarchar). Monetary value stored as text.
Discount Amount.analysis_use: Used to calculate total discounts, average discount per unit (if quantity available elsewhere), and discount rate relative to gross sales. Aggregation (SUM, AVG) after casting to numeric. Useful in identifying promotions or pricing issues per SKU.
business_questions: What is the total discount amount applied per SKU?
Which SKUs have the highest discount as a percentage of gross sales?
Discount Amount.nl2sql_hints: Reference as [Discount Amount]. CAST to DECIMAL/NUMERIC with TRY_ functions. Compute discount rate as TRY_CAST([Discount Amount] AS DECIMAL)/NULLIF(TRY_CAST([Gross Sales] AS DECIMAL),0). Watch for negative values and malformed strings.

Tax Amount.description: Tax charged on the SKU (raw nvarchar). Monetary tax value stored as text.
Tax Amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax collected by SKU or in total. Useful for calculating effective tax rate per SKU (Tax/Gross) and for auditing tax calculations. Requires casting to numeric before math operations.
business_questions: How much tax was collected per SKU?
What is the effective tax rate (tax/gross) by SKU and does it vary materially across products?
Tax Amount.nl2sql_hints: Reference as [Tax Amount]. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to DECIMAL before aggregation. When calculating rates, protect against division by zero and missing values (NULLIF). Ensure locale decimal separators are handled if present.

Net Sales.description: Net sales amount after discounts and tax adjustments for the SKU (raw nvarchar). Expected to represent Gross - Discount +/− Tax depending on reporting convention; stored as text.
Net Sales.analysis_use: Primary outcome metric to validate against computed net (Gross - Discount + Tax) for data quality checks. Used for aggregation, ranking, and profitability analysis when joined with cost data. Requires casting to numeric.
business_questions: What are the total net sales per SKU?
Does calculated net (Gross - Discount + Tax) match the provided Net Sales values (data consistency check)?
Net Sales.nl2sql_hints: Reference as [Net Sales]. Cast to DECIMAL with TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT for numeric operations. For validation queries compute SUM(TRY_CAST([Gross Sales] AS DECIMAL) - TRY_CAST([Discount Amount] AS DECIMAL) + TRY_CAST([Tax Amount] AS DECIMAL)) and compare to SUM(TRY_CAST([Net Sales] AS DECIMAL)). Beware rounding differences; choose appropriate precision (DECIMAL(18,6) or DECIMAL(18,2) depending on currency rounding).

pos_return_id.description: Unique identifier for the return line (POS return event id).
pos_return_id.analysis_use: Primary row-level identifier: use for deduplication, selecting individual return records, counting returns, grouping unique returns. Candidate primary key for this dataset.
business_questions: Show details for a specific return by pos_return_id.
How many distinct returns occurred in the last week?
pos_return_id.nl2sql_hints: Treated as string. Use equality filters (pos_return_id = 'PR2001') or IN lists. No implicit casts required. When joining, ensure the target key uses same format/leading zeros. Consider indexing when table grows.

pos_txn_id.description: Identifier of the POS transaction the return belongs to (original sale transaction id).
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Join key to transaction/order tables; group by transaction to find transactions with multiple returns; aggregate refunds per transaction.
business_questions: Which transactions had multiple returned items?
Total refunds associated with a given transaction (pos_txn_id)?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; joins must compare string-to-string. Use exact matches; watch for naming differences between POS and ecom transaction ids. When filtering by multiple transactions use IN. No datetime casting here.

return_ts.description: Timestamp when the return occurred (string formatted timestamp).
return_ts.analysis_use: Time-based filtering, grouping (day/week/month/hour), trend analysis, latency calculations (when compared to created_at). Must be cast to datetime/timestamp to use temporal SQL functions.
business_questions: How many returns occurred per day/hour?
What is the trend of returns (count/refund amount) over the last N days?
return_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar in ISO-like format (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS) — use safe conversion (CAST/TRY_CAST or TO_TIMESTAMP depending on dialect) to datetime. Use explicit casts in WHERE and GROUP BY to avoid slow implicit conversions. Example: CAST(return_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE(return_ts AS DATETIME).

sku.description: Product SKU for the returned item.
sku.analysis_use: Group and filter to compute returns by product, join to product metadata (name, category, cost), compute SKU-level return rates when joined to sales data.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest return counts and refund totals?
What is the distribution of return reasons for a specific SKU?
sku.nl2sql_hints: String key—joins to product tables must use same SKU format/case. Normalize casing/trim spaces before joining (e.g., UPPER(TRIM(sku))). When computing SKU return rates, join to sales tables on sku and matching time ranges.

qty.description: Quantity of the SKU returned on this return line (stored as string).
qty.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (SUM of qty) to report total items returned; average returned quantity per return; weight return volume vs refund amount. Cast to integer for arithmetic.
business_questions: Total quantity returned for a given SKU or period?
Average quantity per return transaction line?
qty.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; use CAST(qty AS INT) or TRY_CAST for safety. Avoid implicit conversion in WHERE/GROUP BY; convert once in a derived/subquery or use TRY_CAST to handle malformed values.

reason.description: Categorical reason for the return (e.g., Wrong Item, Customer Return, Damaged).
reason.analysis_use: Group and count returns by reason, filter for specific issues, compute reason-specific refund totals and quantities. Useful for quality and process improvement metrics.
business_questions: What share of returns is due to 'Wrong Item' vs 'Damaged'?
Which reasons account for the highest refund cost?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string; watch for trailing spaces and case. Use exact matches or normalized comparisons (UPPER(TRIM(reason))). Create a small mapping table for normalized reasons if NLP input may vary.

refund_amount.description: Monetary refund issued for the return line (stored as string/float-like text).
refund_amount.analysis_use: Monetary aggregation: SUM(refund_amount) for total cost of returns, AVG for average refund, group by sku/processor/reason/time. Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for accurate sums and rounding.
business_questions: Total refund amount over a period or by SKU?
What is the average refund per return and per processor?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text and sometimes in floating formatting (e.g., 129.90000000000001). Use CAST(refund_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)) or ROUND(TRY_CAST(...),2) to avoid float artifacts. Use TRY_CAST or NULLIF to handle non-numeric strings. Prefer explicit casting in SELECT and WHERE clauses.

processed_by.description: Identifier of the POS user/operator who processed the return.
processed_by.analysis_use: Group by processor to monitor workload, error rates, average refund amounts handled, and operational performance. Join to users/employee tables for human-readable names.
business_questions: Which processors handle the most returns and refunds?
Is there a processor with unusually high refunds or a high share of 'Damaged' reasons?
processed_by.nl2sql_hints: String id (e.g., PU1003). Join to pos_users or staff tables on the same id; normalize spaces/casing if necessary. Low cardinality—use GROUP BY processed_by for summary metrics.

approval_required_flag.description: Binary flag (string '0'/'1') indicating whether this return required managerial/approval workflow.
approval_required_flag.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate to compute percent of returns requiring approval, effect on processing time and refunds, and to audit escalations.
business_questions: What % of returns required approval in the last month?
Do returns requiring approval have higher average refund amounts?
approval_required_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; treat as boolean: WHERE approval_required_flag = '1' or CAST(approval_required_flag AS INT) = 1. Use explicit casting for numeric comparisons to avoid string ordering issues.

restock_flag.description: Binary flag (string '0'/'1') indicating whether the returned item was restocked into inventory.
restock_flag.analysis_use: Calculate restock rate, link to inventory adjustments, and analyze cost recovery vs disposal. Useful to filter for returns that impact inventory vs those that do not.
business_questions: What proportion of returns are restocked vs not restocked?
Are restocked returns associated with different reasons or refund levels?
restock_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; use explicit comparisons (restock_flag = '1') or CAST to INT/BOOLEAN. When joining to inventory tables, ensure the restock logic aligns with inventory transactions and adjust for timing (created_at).

created_at.description: Timestamp when the return row was created in the system (string formatted timestamp).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and latency metrics: compute time between return_ts and created_at, order insertion trends, filter for ingestion windows. Use for operational monitoring of data latency.
business_questions: What is the average time between a return occurring and the record being created (processing latency)?
How many returns were recorded/created on a given day or shift?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for time functions. Use TRY_CAST or explicit format parsing if dialect differs. When computing latency, use CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) - CAST(return_ts AS DATETIME) or equivalent, and handle NULL/malformed strings.

date.description: Reported calendar date for the aggregated daily metrics. Stored as nvarchar(30) but contents follow YYYY-MM-DD (max length 10).
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for filtering, grouping (day/week/month/year), time-series ordering, window functions and joins to a date/calendar dimension.
business_questions: What were sales on 2024-01-05 for each store?
Show monthly net sales trend starting from 2024-01-01.
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as DATE. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT or equivalent to parse 'YYYY-MM-DD' into DATE. Normalize to DATE during ingestion or in query: e.g. TRY_CAST(date AS DATE) or PARSE(date AS DATE). If parsing fails for some rows, filter with ISDATE or regex. Index on parsed date column improves range filters.

store_id.description: Identifier for the POS store (e.g. STORE_AHD_001). Low cardinality (3 stores in dataset).
store_id.analysis_use: Grouping, partitioning, filtering, and joining to store metadata tables (pos_stores). Useful for per-store KPIs and comparisons.
business_questions: Which store generated the most net sales last month?
How many bills did STORE_VDR_003 process in the last 7 days?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Keep as a CHAR/VARCHAR of appropriate length (trimmed). Use as join key to pos_stores. Ensure consistent casing/trim during joins (use exact match). Consider indexing store_id and store_id + date composite for common queries.

total_bills.description: Count of bills/transactions for the store on that date. Stored as nvarchar but values represent small integers (max length 3).
total_bills.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) to compute total transactions over periods, average bills per day, and used to compute average ticket values (gross/net per bill).
business_questions: What is the total number of bills for each store this month?
What is the average number of bills per day across all stores?
total_bills.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INTEGER: TRY_CAST(total_bills AS INT). Handle non-numeric or null values defensively (COALESCE/TRY_CAST). For NL2SQL, recognize 'bills'/'transactions' synonyms and map to this column. If total_bills is missing or zero, ensure division guards when computing averages.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the store on that date. Stored as nvarchar; high-cardinality decimals present.
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) for total weight sold, average weight per bill (total_qty_kg / total_bills), trend analysis and sales per kg ratios.
business_questions: How many kgs were sold across all stores last week?
What is the average kg sold per bill for STORE_SUR_002?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g. DECIMAL(18,4) or appropriate scale) via TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT. Be aware of formatting (decimal points). Use NULL-safe casts and trim whitespace. For queries combining with product-level qty, ensure units align (kg vs grams).

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount (before discounts/tax?) for the day and store; stored as nvarchar with many distinct decimal values.
gross_sales.analysis_use: Key aggregation measure (SUM, AVG). Used to calculate discount %, tax %, and reconciled against net_sales. Important for leaderboards and revenue trend reports.
business_questions: What were the gross sales per store last fiscal quarter?
Which days had the top 5 highest gross sales across all stores?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL (e.g. DECIMAL(18,2)). Use TRY_CAST and TRIM. Confirm whether gross_sales includes tax or not; document this mapping. When computing aggregations ensure consistent currency formatting and rounding. Avoid string comparisons in WHERE clauses; convert first.

discount_amount.description: Total discount amount applied for the store on that date. Stored as nvarchar; high-cardinality decimals.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation and calculation of discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), trending discounts over time, and identifying promotion impact.
business_questions: What is the total discount given this month across all stores?
Which days had unusually high discount percentages vs gross sales?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL (TRY_CAST). When computing discount rate, protect against divide-by-zero (CASE WHEN gross_sales<>0). Consider negative values representation. Map synonyms like 'discount', 'promo' in NL2SQL parsing to this column.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the store on that date. Stored as nvarchar with decimal-like values.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation for tax reporting, tax rate calculation (tax_amount / net or gross depending on definition), and reconciliation with tax remittance records.
business_questions: How much tax was collected by each store last month?
What is the average tax per bill across stores?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC. Clarify whether tax_amount is included in net_sales or excluded. Use safe casting and null handling. NL2SQL should map 'tax', 'vat', 'gst' phrases to this column if appropriate.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for the day and store (presumably gross - discounts + tax or another definition). Stored as nvarchar and high-cardinality decimals.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for reporting and KPI computations; aggregation, time-series, contribution analysis, and per-bill average calculations.
business_questions: What is total net sales by store for the year-to-date?
Show top 10 days by net sales and which store they belong to.
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL. Ensure understanding of net definition (after discounts, taxes?). When NL2SQL users ask for 'revenue' or 'sales', prefer net_sales but confirm business semantics. Consider precomputing derived columns like margin or net per bill for faster queries.

created_at.description: Ingestion timestamp for the row. Stored as nvarchar(48) and appears constant (single distinct value present).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit purposes: to know when the data snapshot was created. Not useful for business time-series analysis (use date column instead).
business_questions: When was this dataset last ingested?
Was the data loaded after a given timestamp (for ETL troubleshooting)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME if needed for audit queries. Note that it's constant across dataset; consider dropping or moving to metadata if redundant. NL2SQL should not confuse created_at with the reporting date; prefer 'date' for time filters.

Unnamed: 9.description: Completely empty column (100% null). Likely an artifact from source CSV/ingestion.
Unnamed: 9.analysis_use: None — safe to drop. No analytical value.
Unnamed: 9.nl2sql_hints: Exclude from NL2SQL schema mapping. If present in natural language, ignore. Remove column from transformed schema to simplify queries.

Unnamed: 10.description: Completely empty column (100% null). Ingestion artifact.
Unnamed: 10.nl2sql_hints: Drop or ignore. Do not surface to NL2SQL vocabulary.

Unnamed: 11.description: Sparsely populated short text column (4 distinct values: 'da','gs','ns','ta') with ~99.8% nulls. Possibly a code or legacy tag indicating column origin or metric type.
Unnamed: 11.analysis_use: Limited; may be a category/tag for certain aggregate rows. Investigate source to determine meaning before using in analysis.
business_questions: What do the codes 'da','gs','ns','ta' represent and how many rows are tagged?
Are these codes indicators of aggregated totals or special rows to exclude?
Unnamed: 11.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string with many nulls. Do not assume it's a reliable key. If used, map codes to human-readable labels in NL2SQL synonyms. Prefer to consult data owner; otherwise exclude from core metrics until meaning is known.

Unnamed: 12.description: Sparsely populated numeric-looking field (4 distinct large decimal values) with ~99.8% nulls. Likely a summary/total value or foreign metric accidentally included.
Unnamed: 12.analysis_use: Investigate source: could be an overall total or checksum. Not safe for use in standard per-day/per-store queries without confirmation.
business_questions: Are these values dataset-level totals or identifiers from the source system?
Should these values be treated as diagnostic (ingestion totals) or merged into per-row metrics?
Unnamed: 12.nl2sql_hints: If numeric, cast to DECIMAL after validating. Do not join on this column. Exclude from NL2SQL mappings unless its purpose is clarified. If it represents a global total, store in metadata table rather than per-row field.

year.description: Reporting year for the monthly aggregate (stored as nvarchar). In this dataset all values are '2024'.
year.analysis_use: Used as a high-level filter/grouping key for year-over-year comparisons and partitioning. Typically used in WHERE or GROUP BY clauses.
business_questions: Filter the monthly metrics to a specific year (e.g., show all months for 2024).
Compare same-month performance across different years (when multiple years exist).
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical/time partition key. Since it's stored as text, be explicit when comparing numeric year values (e.g., WHERE year = '2024'). If date math is required, convert to integer using CAST(year AS INT). Expect low cardinality.

month.description: Reporting month as a string (1-12). Represents the month of the aggregate row.
month.analysis_use: Primary temporal grain within a year. Used for ordering time series, filtering on months, grouping to compute monthly aggregates and month-over-month changes.
business_questions: Show net sales by month for a given store.
Identify the top 3 months by gross sales for the year.
month.nl2sql_hints: Month is stored as text numeric ('1'..'12'). Convert to integer for correct ordering: CAST(month AS INT) or use CASE mapping for month names. When users provide month names ('January'), map them to numeric values in NL2SQL generation. Beware of leading zeros if present in other datasets.

store_id.description: Identifier for the store where sales occurred (e.g., STORE_AHD_01).
store_id.analysis_use: Used to slice and dice aggregates by store, join to store dimension for metadata (region, manager), and group for comparative analyses.
business_questions: Which store generated the highest net sales for March 2024?
Compare discount percentage across stores for each month.
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as a join key to pos_stores or other store-level dimensions. It is textual—match exact values. Consider normalization (consistent casing/trimming) before joins. Low cardinality; suitable for GROUP BY without high cost.

total_bills.description: Total number of bills (transactions) in that store-month (stored as nvarchar integer-like).
total_bills.analysis_use: Aggregated/count metric for transaction volume. Useful for rate calculations (average bill value = net_sales/total_bills) and performance metrics per transaction.
business_questions: What is the average net sale per bill for each store this month?
Which month had the highest transaction volume for a store?
total_bills.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—must CAST to INTEGER/DECIMAL before arithmetic. Handle NULLs or non-numeric entries defensively (TRY_CAST where supported). Use SUM(CAST(total_bills AS INT)) only if duplicates per grain are possible; usually one row per store-month so direct CAST is fine.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the store-month (stored as nvarchar, decimal-like).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Measure of volume sold; useful for unit-price calculations (gross_sales / total_qty_kg), inventory planning, and trend analysis of physical volume vs revenue.
business_questions: How does quantity sold (kg) trend month-over-month for each store?
What is the average price per kg (net_sales / total_qty_kg) by month?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric (DECIMAL/DOUBLE) before aggregations. Watch for precision and locale decimal formatting. Use NULL-safe casts and guard against division by zero when computing per-kg metrics.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales amount (before discounts/taxes) for the store-month (stored as nvarchar decimal-like string).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for top-line analysis, used in sums, trend analyses, and to calculate discount/tax ratios. Aggregated and compared across stores and months.
business_questions: What are the monthly gross sales for each store?
What fraction of gross sales is lost to discounts each month?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before SUM/AVG. Use consistent scale (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)). When generating SQL, prefer safe casts (TRY_CAST) or explicit REPLACE for thousands separators. Ensure to compute derived ratios as (SUM(cast(gross_sales AS DECIMAL)) - SUM(cast(discount_amount AS DECIMAL))) etc.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied for the store-month (stored as nvarchar decimal-like).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to measure promotional/markdown impact, compute discount percent (discount_amount / gross_sales), and see how discounts affect net sales.
business_questions: How much discount (absolute and percent) was applied each month at each store?
Which store/month had the largest discount rate relative to gross sales?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—CAST to DECIMAL before aggregations and ratio calculations. When computing percentages, guard against division by zero (gross_sales = 0). Consider grouping and summing casts to avoid per-row cast issues when merging with other datasets.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the store-month (stored as nvarchar decimal-like).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute tax rates (tax_amount / gross_sales or tax_amount / net_sales), analyze tax impacts on net revenue, and reconcile tax-ledger figures.
business_questions: What is the effective tax collected per month by store?
Is tax as a percent of gross sales stable across months and stores?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL for arithmetic. Consider that tax rules may change across periods—treat as a measure, not a constant rate. Use safe casting and aggregate on casted values.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for the store-month (after discounts and taxes or as defined by the business) stored as nvarchar decimal-like.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for performance reporting, profitability trending, comparisons, and KPIs like ARPT (average revenue per transaction) and revenue per kg.
business_questions: What were the net sales per month and per store?
Which store contributed the most to net sales year-to-date?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL for SUM/AVG. Confirm business definition of net_sales (whether tax is added or subtracted) before combining with gross/discount/tax. Use aggregated casts and be explicit when computing derived metrics (e.g., SUM(cast(net_sales AS DECIMAL)) / SUM(cast(total_bills AS INT))).

created_at.description: Ingestion or snapshot timestamp (stored as nvarchar). In this dataset the value is constant across rows.
created_at.analysis_use: Used for ETL auditing, determining freshness of the snapshot, or filtering by ingestion batch. Not useful for time series of sales (use year/month instead).
business_questions: When was this monthly summary snapshot loaded into the warehouse?
Filter rows by the latest ingestion timestamp to ensure using the newest snapshot.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—convert to proper DATETIME/TIMESTAMP with CAST/CONVERT if needed for time comparisons. Since it is constant in this snapshot, avoid grouping by it in analytical queries unless auditing. Use TRY_CAST to prevent parsing errors.

year.description: Calendar year for the aggregated metrics (stored as nvarchar). Represents the year these rollups belong to (e.g., '2024').
year.analysis_use: Used for grouping and filtering (annual reports, year-over-year comparisons). Must be converted/cast to integer when numeric comparisons or ranges are needed.
business_questions: Which stores' performance in year = 2024 exceeded targets?
How do metrics change year-over-year (requires multiple years in the table)?
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string in raw queries but prefer to CAST(year AS INT) for numeric filters or comparisons. Watch for non-standard values (NULL, 'FY24', '2024-2025'); normalize in preprocessing. Use equality or IN for specific years; range operators require numeric cast.

store_id.description: Identifier for the physical store where sales were made (string like 'STORE_AHD_01').
store_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/aggregation key in this table for per-store metrics. Useful as a join key to pos_stores and other store-level tables to enrich with location, region, or manager.
business_questions: Which store (store_id) generated the highest gross_sales in the year?
How do regional aggregates look after joining store_id to store master?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Use exact string matching for filters (e.g., WHERE store_id = 'STORE_AHD_01') and JOIN on store_id to pos_stores. Be aware of casing/whitespace; TRIM() and UPPER()/LOWER() may be necessary. No surrogate key in table; treat store_id as de-facto key for joins.

total_bills.description: Total number of bills/transactions for the store during the year, stored as nvarchar (integer-like).
total_bills.analysis_use: Aggregate as SUM or AVG across stores; compute average bill value by dividing gross_sales by total_bills (requires numeric cast). Useful for transaction volume analysis and traffic proxies.
business_questions: What is the total transaction volume for each store this year?
What is the average gross value per bill for a store?
total_bills.nl2sql_hints: Cast to an integer (e.g., CAST(total_bills AS INT)) before aggregations to avoid string concatenation. Guard against NULLs and non-numeric strings using TRY_CAST or CASE WHEN ISNUMERIC(...) depending on SQL dialect. Use SAFE division to avoid divide-by-zero when computing averages.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the store during the year (numeric-like string, may contain decimals).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate as SUM to analyze volume sold; compute unit price per kg (gross_sales/total_qty_kg). Useful for inventory planning and weight-based sales analysis.
business_questions: How many kilograms did each store sell this year?
What is the average revenue per kg per store?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to a decimal/float (e.g., CAST(total_qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Handle commas or localized formats if present. Protect against zero or NULL before dividing. Use ROUND after calculation for reporting.

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount (pre-discount, pre-tax) for the store for the year, stored as a numeric string with decimals.
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary metric for revenue analysis; SUM, AVG, and ranking operations. Used to compute discount and tax rates and contribute to company-level revenue metrics.
business_questions: Which stores contributed the most to gross sales in the year?
How does gross_sales compare to net_sales and discounts at the store level?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before aggregation (e.g., CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Beware of precision: choose scale to capture cents. Use COALESCE/TRY_CAST to handle malformed strings. Do not rely on string MAX/MIN for numeric comparisons.

discount_amount.description: Total discount amount applied to sales for the store during the year (string containing decimal).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Sum to understand total discounting; compute discount rate = discount_amount / gross_sales. Use to analyze promotional impact and margin erosion.
business_questions: What percentage of gross sales was given as discounts at each store?
Which stores had the highest absolute discount amounts?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for math operations. When computing rates, ensure gross_sales is non-zero. Consider negative/positive sign conventions (ensure discount is positive amount representing reduction).

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected/applied for the store during the year (string with decimal).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to get total tax liability/collection. Useful for tax reporting and to compute effective tax rate relative to gross or net sales.
business_questions: What is the effective tax rate per store (tax_amount / gross_sales)?
Which stores collected the most tax this year?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before aggregation. Verify whether tax_amount is inclusive/exclusive of discounts and whether values are net of returns. Normalize sign conventions if necessary.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount after discounts and tax adjustments for the store for the year (string numeric).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for top-level reporting. Use SUM and comparison with gross_sales to analyze net/gross gap. Compute per-bill averages and year-over-year changes when multiple years exist.
business_questions: What are the net sales by store for the year?
What is the net-to-gross ratio per store?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before numeric operations. Check whether net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount (validate rollup consistency). Use COALESCE/TRY_CAST and consider rounding issues when comparing derived vs stored net_sales.

created_at.description: Ingestion or record creation timestamp for the row (stored as nvarchar). In sample data it appears constant and likely represents when the rollup was generated.
created_at.analysis_use: Used to determine freshness of the rollup, filter by ingestion/creation time, or audit when the aggregation was produced. Not typically used for business aggregation other than recency checks.
business_questions: When was this yearly rollup generated?
Have there been updated rollups after the reported created_at timestamp?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Convert to proper DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)) when filtering by time. Expect a single value per export; if multiple, use max(created_at) to get latest. Be careful with timezone or format inconsistencies; parse using appropriate format specifiers if needed.

shift_id.description: Unique identifier for a closed shift (string).
shift_id.analysis_use: Grouping and identifying individual shifts; de-duplication; primary identifier for joins to transaction-level data if available.
business_questions: How many unique shifts were closed in a given date range?
Which shift(s) show the largest discrepancy between expected and counted cash?
shift_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. Expect uniqueness but validate (COUNT(*) vs COUNT(DISTINCT)). Use in WHERE/ GROUP BY/ JOIN clauses. Avoid numeric casts.

terminal_id.description: Identifier for the POS terminal where the shift ran (string).
terminal_id.analysis_use: Filter/group by terminal to compute per-terminal totals, frequency, and discrepancy rates. Good candidate for JOIN to pos_terminals to enrich with location/ store metadata.
business_questions: Which terminal processes the highest average total_sales per shift?
Which terminal has the highest rate of cash-count discrepancies?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality (4 values). Use as equality filter or GROUP BY. When joining, ensure matching data types with pos_terminals (likely nvarchar). Consider indexing in production for frequent filtering.

user_id.description: Identifier of the user/cashier who closed the shift (string).
user_id.analysis_use: Group/aggregate by user to measure performance, total sales, refund exposure, and reconciliation success. Join to user metadata (pos_users) for names/roles.
business_questions: Which cashier has the highest cancellations/refunds total?
Which cashier had the largest cumulative discrepancy over the month?
user_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality (3 values). Use in GROUP BY and JOINs to pos_users. Cast not required. Watch for multiple aliases or different user_id formats across tables.

shift_start_ts.description: Shift start timestamp recorded as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
shift_start_ts.analysis_use: Filter shifts by start date/time, compute shift durations (with shift_end_ts), aggregate metrics by date/day/hour, detect overnight shifts. Must CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP to perform time arithmetic or date truncation.
business_questions: How many shifts started during peak hours (e.g., 12:00-15:00)?
What is total sales for shifts starting on a specific date?
shift_start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text: convert to datetime in NL2SQL (e.g., CAST(shift_start_ts AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP depending on dialect). Verify format consistency and handle parsing errors/nulls. Use explicit time zone handling if needed.

shift_end_ts.description: Shift end timestamp recorded as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
shift_end_ts.analysis_use: Calculate shift durations, filter by end time, compute late-night shifts, and reconcile transactions that post after shift end. Must convert to timestamp/datetime for arithmetic.
business_questions: What is the average shift duration per user or terminal?
Which shifts ended after midnight?
shift_end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Same parsing considerations as shift_start_ts. Use safe casts and handle potential null/invalid strings. For duration compute: TIMESTAMPDIFF(second/minute, CAST(shift_start_ts AS TIMESTAMP), CAST(shift_end_ts AS TIMESTAMP)).

total_sales.description: Total sales amount for the shift stored as a numeric value in nvarchar.
total_sales.analysis_use: Primary metric for revenue aggregation by shift/user/terminal/date. Sum/avg/min/max and trend analysis. Must CAST/TO_NUMBER for numeric aggregations.
business_questions: What are total sales per day/week/month?
Which shifts are top N by total_sales?
total_sales.nl2sql_hints: Contains decimal strings. Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., CAST(total_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Handle commas/currency symbols (none observed) and invalid values. Use NULL-safe conversion in NL2SQL.

cash_collected.description: Amount of cash collected during the shift stored as nvarchar.
cash_collected.analysis_use: Aggregate cash inflows, compute expected vs counted differences, analyze cash handling by user/terminal. Requires numeric cast for sums and comparisons.
business_questions: How much cash was collected per store/terminal over a period?
Which shifts show large variances between expected_cash and counted_cash?
cash_collected.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before arithmetic. Watch for rounding precision; prefer DECIMAL(18,2). Use COALESCE after cast for missing values.

card_collected.description: Amount collected by card for the shift (string).
card_collected.analysis_use: Separate payment channel analysis (card vs cash) and reconciliation with card settlement tables. Aggregate by user/terminal/date after numeric casting.
business_questions: What proportion of total_sales was collected by card vs cash?
Which terminals have higher card share?
card_collected.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric for ratio and sum calculations. When computing percentages, guard against division by zero.

refunds_amt.description: Total refunds processed during the shift stored as a string numeric.
refunds_amt.analysis_use: Track refund exposure per shift/user/terminal, subtract from gross sales for net revenue calculations. Use aggregations and thresholds to flag outliers. Cast to numeric.
business_questions: Which shifts had refunds greater than X and who processed them?
What is the total refunds amount per cashier this month?
refunds_amt.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for arithmetic. Consider negative vs positive representation of refunds (values appear positive here). Confirm sign convention before netting with sales.

expected_cash.description: System-calculated expected cash amount at shift close (string).
expected_cash.analysis_use: Compare with counted_cash to compute discrepancies; aggregate expected cash by user/terminal. Cast to numeric for comparisons and aggregations.
business_questions: How often does expected_cash differ from counted_cash and by how much?
What is the total expected cash across all shifts in a period?
expected_cash.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Derive discrepancy = CAST(counted_cash AS NUMERIC)-CAST(expected_cash AS NUMERIC). Use ABS() and sign awareness to detect over/short.

counted_cash.description: Physically counted cash at shift close as recorded by cashier (string).
counted_cash.analysis_use: Primary field for reconciliation against expected_cash; used to compute over/short amounts and to detect theft/errors. Cast to numeric for calculations.
business_questions: Which shifts were over or short by more than a set threshold?
Which cashier has the highest cumulative shortages?
counted_cash.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL and handle nulls. Use comparisons with expected_cash. When used in GROUP BY, cast first to avoid grouping on string representations with inconsistent formatting.

discrepancies_note.description: Free-text note for discrepancies (nullable). Currently 100% NULL in sample.
discrepancies_note.analysis_use: Qualitative explanations for cash variances; useful when present for auditing and root-cause. Low value for automated numeric analysis unless populated.
business_questions: What reasons are recorded for cash discrepancies and which are most common?
Can notes be used to explain persistent shortages for a specific user?
discrepancies_note.nl2sql_hints: Text field; expect many NULLs. Use LENGTH/IS NOT NULL filters to find annotated rows. Beware of inconsistent casing/spelling if used for categorization; consider normalizing or mapping notes to categories.

store_id.description: A textual identifier for each POS store (e.g., 'STORE_AHD_001'). Intended to be the canonical key for a store.
store_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to link store metadata to transactions, sales summaries, shifts, inventory, terminals and other POS tables. Useful for grouping, counting distinct stores, filtering to specific stores or store patterns (e.g., by prefix).
business_questions: Which transactions or sales belong to a specific store_id?
How many distinct stores are active in a specific city or region?
Which stores have not been online since a cutoff date?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string join key. Use equality or prefix matching (LIKE 'STORE_%') for filters. Normalize whitespace/case when matching. Consider CAST/CONVERT only if IDs stored inconsistently. For performance define an index/primary key on store_id in production. Expect exact-match joins to pos_transactions.store_id and other POS tables.

city.description: City where the store is located (human-readable name like 'Ahmedabad').
city.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation dimension for geospatial reporting (counts, rollups). Filter dimension to scope analyses to particular areas. Useful in dashboards and region-level rollups when joined with transactional metrics.
business_questions: How many stores are there in each city?
What is the list of store_ids located in a given city?
Which cities contain stores that haven't been online recently?
city.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a short text categorical field. Use equality or IN for city filters (e.g., WHERE city = 'Ahmedabad'). Beware of inconsistent capitalization/typos — consider using UPPER(TRIM(city)). When building NL2SQL intents, map natural-language city names directly to this column. Avoid substring matches unless needed.

region.description: Higher-level geographic label (region) for the store. In the sample data region values match city values, but region can represent broader area or division.
region.analysis_use: Roll-up dimension for regional reporting, grouping, and filtering. Useful to aggregate store counts or sales at the region level and to join with regional targets or manager assignments.
business_questions: How many stores exist per region?
Which stores belong to a specific region and what are their last_online timestamps?
Are there region entries that conflict with city (data quality checks)?
region.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical text. Use equality/IN and normalize input (TRIM/UPPER) when matching. If region is hierarchical, be explicit in NL2SQL prompts whether region means state/district/area. Consider joining to a reference table of regions if available.

first_active_date.description: Timestamp when the store first became active/registered. Currently stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
first_active_date.analysis_use: Used to compute store tenure (current_date - first_active_date), filter stores by activation window, cohort analyses by activation month/week. Must be cast to datetime for date arithmetic and ordering.
business_questions: Which stores were activated between two dates?
What is the average tenure of stores (time since first_active_date)?
How many stores were activated in each month?
first_active_date.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — always CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type before comparisons or date functions (e.g., CAST(first_active_date AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE depending on RDBMS). Validate date format and handle NULLs or malformed strings (use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT where available). In NL2SQL, map phrases like 'activated in October 2025' to a range on this column. Clarify timezone if needed.

last_online.description: Timestamp when the store last connected or was observed online. Stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
last_online.analysis_use: Recency and availability checks: compute last_seen recency, filter stores offline longer than threshold, order stores by last_online. Combine with transactional timestamps to detect staleness.
business_questions: Which stores were last online within the past 7 days?
Which stores have not been online since a given cutoff date (possibly inactive or offline)?
What is the most recent online time for each region or city?
last_online.nl2sql_hints: Like first_active_date, convert to a datetime type for comparisons (CAST/TRY_CAST). Use date_diff/time functions to compute recency (e.g., DATEDIFF(day, CAST(last_online AS DATETIME), GETDATE())). Handle NULLs and malformed strings; treat absent last_online as unknown/offline. When NL2SQL asks for 'recently online' map to a sensible default window or ask follow-up (e.g., last X days).

sync_id.description: Identifier for the sync attempt or batch. Appears unique per row in sample (50 distinct).
sync_id.analysis_use: Use for deduplication, identify/retrieve a specific sync record, count distinct sync attempts. Can group or filter on specific sync_id when troubleshooting.
business_questions: How many unique sync attempts occurred in a given period?
Retrieve full details for a specific sync_id when investigating a reported failure.
sync_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key; expect high cardinality. For exact-match queries use equality. If joining to other tables, ensure other table stores the same string format. Avoid converting unless necessary; if deduplication required, use DISTINCT or GROUP BY sync_id.

terminal_id.description: Identifier of the POS terminal that initiated the sync (e.g., 'TERM-001-2'). Low cardinality with skew toward a primary terminal.
terminal_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering key for per-terminal metrics (counts, success rates, volumes). Useful for joining to pos_terminals or pos_stores for enrichment.
business_questions: Which terminals have the most FAILED syncs in the last 7 days?
What is the average records_sent per terminal per day?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; comparisons should be string-based. When used in WHERE or GROUP BY, consider normalizing case/format if other tables vary. Indexing this column will speed terminal-level aggregations. Join on terminal_id to pos_terminals or pos_stores if present.

sync_ts.description: Timestamp of the sync event (ISO-like string). Represents when the sync occurred or was reported from the terminal.
sync_ts.analysis_use: Critical for time-series analysis: filter by date ranges, aggregate by day/hour, detect spikes. Can compute intervals relative to created_at or last_success_ts to measure delays.
business_questions: How many syncs occurred per hour/day over the last 30 days?
At what times of day do sync failures spike?
sync_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; for accurate range queries and ordering cast/convert to a DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., TRY_CONVERT(datetime, sync_ts) in SQL Server). Use consistent timezone assumptions. For NL2SQL, explicit mention of date format and conversion is helpful (e.g., 'convert sync_ts to datetime').

sync_status.description: Outcome of the sync attempt (e.g., SUCCESS or FAILED). Low cardinality (2 values in sample).
sync_status.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate to compute success/failure counts and rates, group by status to surface error patterns, combine with retry_count and error_message to understand failure resolution.
business_questions: What percentage of syncs are successful vs failed in the last month?
How many failed syncs were retried more than twice?
sync_status.nl2sql_hints: Normalized categorical values (uppercase). Use equality or IN predicates for filtering. Be aware of potential other statuses in production; consider mapping synonyms or nulls to UNKNOWN. For aggregated metrics, GROUP BY sync_status is useful.

records_sent.description: Number of records the terminal reported it sent to the central system for this sync (stored as string).
records_sent.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) after casting to integer. Useful to measure data throughput per sync/terminal and identify missing or truncated batches.
business_questions: What is the total number of records sent by each terminal per day?
Are failed syncs sending fewer or more records on average than successful syncs?
records_sent.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; convert to INTEGER using TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to avoid errors from malformed values. Handle NULLs and non-numeric entries. When writing NL2SQL, request explicit CAST: CAST(records_sent AS INT) or safe parse with TRY_CAST. Use SUM(CAST(records_sent AS INT)) for totals.

records_received.description: Number of records the central system acknowledged or returned to the terminal (stored as string).
records_received.analysis_use: Compare with records_sent to detect discrepancies. Aggregate per terminal or time window to measure inbound processing throughput and potential data loss.
business_questions: Which syncs had discrepancies between records_sent and records_received?
What is the average records_received per successful sync?
records_received.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar; cast to integer for arithmetic and comparisons. In NL2SQL, include safe conversion and NULL handling (e.g., COALESCE(TRY_CAST(records_received AS INT),0)). Use difference expressions: CAST(records_sent AS INT) - CAST(records_received AS INT).

error_message.description: Text describing the failure reason when sync_status is FAILED. Largely NULL in sample; single non-null value 'Network error'.
error_message.analysis_use: Categorize and count error types, filter failed syncs by error message, and identify repeating issues for prioritization. Text may need normalization (trim, consistent casing).
business_questions: What are the top error messages causing sync failures?
How many failures are due to network errors vs other causes?
error_message.nl2sql_hints: May contain NULLs; use IS NOT NULL to find errors. For grouping, normalize (UPPER/LTRIM/RTRIM) and consider mapping long texts to short categories. Beware of punctuation/typos—apply fuzzy grouping if needed. Limit text length in responses.

retry_count.description: Number of retry attempts for this sync (stored as string). Low cardinality values (0-3) in sample.
retry_count.analysis_use: Analyze distribution of retries, correlate retries with eventual success/failure, identify terminals with frequent retries indicating flakiness.
business_questions: What proportion of syncs required more than 2 retries and what were their outcomes?
Which terminals have the highest average retry_count?
retry_count.nl2sql_hints: Convert to SMALLINT/INT for numeric aggregations (AVG, MAX). Stored as nvarchar so use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to guard against malformed values. When used in predicates (e.g., retry_count > 2), cast first.

last_success_ts.description: Timestamp of the last successful sync for the terminal or for this sync context (ISO-like string). Useful to know the last healthy state.
last_success_ts.analysis_use: Compute recency per terminal (time since last success), filter terminals that have not succeeded within SLA window, trend successful sync frequency. Compare with sync_ts to detect long gaps between successes.
business_questions: Which terminals have not had a successful sync in the last 24 hours?
How long (in hours) since each terminal's last successful sync?
last_success_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; must be converted to datetime for comparisons and intervals. Use TRY_CONVERT and explicit timezone handling. When computing 'time since last_success_ts' in NL2SQL, request conversion and use DATEDIFF or equivalent.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this log row was created in the database or ingestion time (ISO-like string).
created_at.analysis_use: Use for data ingestion monitoring, to detect delayed ingestion (created_at vs sync_ts), filter by ingestion window, and for retention/archival policies.
business_questions: How long after sync_ts are logs created on average (ingestion latency)?
Show syncs that were recorded (created_at) outside the expected window (e.g., more than 1 hour after sync_ts).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar; convert to datetime for latency and ordering calculations. When using for time-based filters in NL2SQL, explicitly request conversion (e.g., WHERE TRY_CONVERT(datetime, created_at) BETWEEN ...). If timezone is unspecified, clarify assumptions.

terminal_id.description: Logical identifier for the POS terminal (tenant-specific id like TERM-001-2).
terminal_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for grouping, deduplication and linking to terminal-level activity. Useful for counting unique terminals, latest-last_online per terminal, time-series of terminal events when joined to event/transaction tables.
business_questions: How many unique terminals are active in the fleet?
What is the last online timestamp per terminal (identify stale terminals)?
Which terminal_ids correspond to terminals generating the most transactions? (when joined)
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. When joining, prefer matching on terminal_id if other tables store it; otherwise use terminal_code or device_serial. Use GROUP BY terminal_id for per-terminal aggregates. Beware of inconsistent casing/whitespace; use TRIM()/LOWER() if necessary.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store where the terminal is located (e.g., STORE_AHD_001).
store_id.analysis_use: Common filter and GROUP BY key for per-store summaries (terminal counts, software coverage, last-online health). Primary join key to pos_stores to enrich with store name, region, timezone, etc.
business_questions: How many terminals are installed in each store?
Which stores have terminals that haven't checked in recently?
What stores still run older software versions?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—use exact string equality for joins. Join to zuna_seed_raw.pos_stores or pos_stores in schema: ON pos_terminals.store_id = pos_stores.store_id. Consider normalizing whitespace and case for robust joins.

terminal_code.description: Human-friendly code for the terminal within a store (e.g., STORE_AH-T1).
terminal_code.analysis_use: Useful for UI-level reporting, mapping terminals to cashier-facing identifiers, and linking to transaction-level tables if they use code. Can be grouped for intra-store terminal comparisons.
business_questions: Which terminal codes at STORE_AHD_001 are producing the most transactions?
Which terminal_code is assigned to which user currently?
terminal_code.nl2sql_hints: May duplicate terminal_id; check which key other tables reference (terminal_code vs terminal_id). Treat as string; use it in filters for user-facing queries. Consider TRIM() if codes have trailing spaces.

device_serial.description: Hardware serial number of terminal device (vendor-provided SN).
device_serial.analysis_use: Useful to identify physical units across repairs, replacements, and vendor warranty lookups. Join key to hardware inventory or vendor partner tables if available.
business_questions: Which device serials have required maintenance or replacements?
Are there multiple terminal records mapped to the same device_serial (device reassignments)?
device_serial.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; may be unique. Use for joins to any hardware inventory or vendor tables. Watch for different serial formatting; canonicalize before joins if necessary.

pos_software_version.description: POS application version installed on the terminal (text).
pos_software_version.analysis_use: Used for rollout tracking, compliance checks, and filtering devices needing upgrade. Low cardinality typically; used in GROUP BY to compute coverage percentages.
business_questions: What proportion of terminals run version v3.2.1?
Which stores have terminals not yet upgraded to the target version?
pos_software_version.nl2sql_hints: String column; compare literal versions. Because sample shows single value, watch for sparse variation. For semantic version comparisons, parse version string into components (major/minor/patch) for numeric comparisons.

assigned_user_id.description: Identifier of the user/operator assigned to the terminal (e.g., PU1001).
assigned_user_id.analysis_use: Join key to pos_users for operator details, used for accountability reports and to find terminals per operator. Filter/group to see user assignments or reassignments over time when combined with history.
business_questions: Which terminals is PU1001 assigned to?
How many terminals are currently unassigned vs assigned per user?
assigned_user_id.nl2sql_hints: String ID used to join to pos_users: ON pos_terminals.assigned_user_id = pos_users.user_id (or equivalent). Expect NULLs for unassigned; include IS NULL handling. Use exact equality for filters and consider normalizing casing.

last_online_ts.description: Timestamp (as string) when the terminal last checked in or was seen online (format: 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MI:SS').
last_online_ts.analysis_use: Primary signal for availability/health — used for recency filters, SLA/uptime calculations, identifying offline devices, and computing time-since-last-contact aggregations.
business_questions: Which terminals have not been online in the last 24/48/72 hours?
What is the distribution of time-since-last-online across terminals?
last_online_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—convert to datetime for comparisons. In SQL Server use TRY_CONVERT(datetime, last_online_ts, 120) or CONVERT(datetime, last_online_ts, 120); in other DBs use CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP with correct format. Handle invalid/NULL values with TRY_/NULLIF patterns. When writing NL2SQL, prefer explicit casting and ISO format to avoid locale issues.

status.description: Operational status of the terminal (e.g., ACTIVE, INACTIVE).
status.analysis_use: Filter and segment devices by operational state for summaries and alerts. Used to exclude decommissioned devices from active counts.
business_questions: How many terminals are ACTIVE vs INACTIVE?
Which ACTIVE terminals have not reported in the last 24 hours?
status.nl2sql_hints: String values (case-sensitive depending on DB). In this sample it's constant 'ACTIVE' — expect more states in production. Use WHERE status = 'ACTIVE' or IN (...) for multi-state filters. Normalize case if source is inconsistent.

location_lat.description: Latitude of terminal location stored as text (decimal string).
location_lat.analysis_use: Map terminals spatially, cluster by geography, compute distances between terminals and stores, and create heatmaps. Cast to numeric for calculations and geospatial indexing if supported.
business_questions: What are the geographic coordinates of terminals in STORE_SUR_001?
Which terminals are within X km of a reference point (e.g., distribution center)?
location_lat.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric: TRY_CAST(location_lat AS decimal(10,7)) or similar. Handle trailing/extra precision (examples show long decimal strings). Use both location_lat and location_long together for geospatial queries; NULLs will break distance calculations so filter them out.

location_long.description: Longitude of terminal location stored as text (decimal string).
location_long.analysis_use: Paired with location_lat for mapping and spatial calculations; useful for region-based filtering and visualizations.
business_questions: Show terminal locations on a map for a selected city/region.
Which terminals are located within a delivery area polygon?
location_long.nl2sql_hints: Same as location_lat—cast to decimal/float before numeric operations: TRY_CAST(location_long AS decimal(11,7)). Combine with location_lat for geospatial calculations. Consider normalizing extreme precision (rounding) for grouping.

created_at.description: Creation/provision timestamp of the terminal record (as string).
created_at.analysis_use: Onboarding and lifecycle analysis: compute how long a terminal has been in service, cohort analysis by provisioning date, filter terminals added during a rollout period.
business_questions: How many terminals were provisioned in the last quarter?
What is the average age (in days) of terminals in the fleet?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—convert to datetime (TRY_CONVERT/CONVERT or CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP depending on engine). Use date functions to compute age or cohorts. Watch for NULLs and invalid strings; use TRY_/safe parsing to avoid query failures.

pos_line_id.description: Identifier for the POS transaction line; appears unique per row and represents the line-level id on a receipt.
pos_line_id.analysis_use: Use as a row-level unique identifier (de-duplication). Useful for counting distinct lines, tracing specific line items, and joining if other tables reference this exact id.
business_questions: How many line items were recorded in total?
Can we identify or de-duplicate duplicate line records by pos_line_id?
pos_line_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string/varchar. Because there is no declared primary key, do not assume referential integrity. Use pos_line_id for selection or exact-match filters. If performing joins, confirm target table uses the same string encoding/trim and consistent casing.

pos_txn_id.description: Identifier of the parent POS transaction/receipt containing this line. Multiple lines map to the same transaction.
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Group by pos_txn_id to compute lines per transaction, transaction-level totals (SUM(line_total)), and join to pos_transactions to get transaction metadata (store, terminal, cashier, timestamp).
business_questions: How many lines does each POS transaction contain?
What is the total value per transaction (sum of line_total) and how does it compare with transaction-level records?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string. When joining to pos_transactions, ensure matching data types and trimming. Aggregations across transactions should use GROUP BY pos_txn_id. Be careful: POS transaction id cardinality is low vs lines (multiple lines per txn).

sku.description: Stock keeping unit code for the product sold on this line. Low cardinality with a few SKUs dominating.
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping dimension for sales analytics: units sold (SUM(qty)), revenue (SUM(line_total)), average price. Use to join with product master tables for product name/category/brand.
business_questions: Which SKUs are top-sellers by units and revenue?
What is the price and revenue distribution per SKU?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string and can be joined to products or pos_products. Normalize case and whitespace before joining. Expect low cardinality—use exact equality or IN lists for filters. If matching to product_variants, prefer left join in NL2SQL when product master may lack rows.

qty.description: Quantity sold on the line. Stored as nvarchar but appears integer-like (small ints).
qty.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation: SUM(qty) for units sold, AVG(qty) for typical line quantity. Use in revenue per unit computations (line_total / qty) after casting to numeric.
business_questions: How many total units of a SKU were sold (sum of qty)?
What is the typical quantity per line (average qty) and distribution (counts by qty)?
qty.nl2sql_hints: Must cast to integer (e.g., CAST(qty AS INT)) or numeric; guard for null/empty strings. Use TRY_CAST/SAFE_CAST to avoid errors if input is malformed. When used in WHERE clauses, compare after casting or ensure numeric literals are compared as strings with caution.

unit_price.description: Unit price applied for the line. Stored as string but numeric-like (several repeated values).
unit_price.analysis_use: Compute revenue consistency checks, detect price variations, average unit price per SKU, and compare with product price lists (joins). Use in derived calculations: expected_line_total = qty * unit_price.
business_questions: What is the average unit price for a SKU over the dataset?
Are there price discrepancies between unit_price * qty and recorded line_total?
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/decimal before arithmetic (e.g., CAST(unit_price AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Be mindful of currency and scaling. Use numeric casts in WHERE and GROUP BY for accurate grouping by price bands.

line_total.description: Total amount for the line (qty * unit_price minus discounts plus taxes if included). Stored as a string; many distinct values.
line_total.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure: SUM(CAST(line_total AS DECIMAL)) for total revenue, per-SKU revenue, per-transaction totals, and reconciliation with transaction totals. Use to compute per-unit realized price (line_total/qty).
business_questions: What is the total revenue generated by these POS lines?
Which lines or SKUs contribute most to revenue and how does line_total compare to expected amounts?
line_total.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal/float for accurate sums. Watch out for floating-point textual artifacts in tax_amt and line_total; prefer DECIMAL for financial sums. Use ROUND when presenting results. When reconciling, allow small tolerances (e.g., absolute difference) due to rounding.

discount_amt.description: Discount amount applied on the line. In this dataset it is consistently '0' for all rows.
discount_amt.analysis_use: Normally used to compute net revenue after discounts (line_total - discount_amt). Here it provides no variation; include but expect zero effect. Useful to flag if discounts appear in the future.
business_questions: How much discount was given per line and in aggregate?
Which SKUs or transactions received discounts?
discount_amt.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; cast to numeric if used. Since values are all '0', queries filtering for discount_amt != 0 will return empty in current data — handle as potential historical/streaming field. Use COALESCE/NULLIF to treat empty strings as zero.

tax_amt.description: Tax amount applied to the line. Stored as string with decimal values and occasional floating-string artifacts (e.g., '4.7999999999999998').
tax_amt.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liability (SUM(tax_amt)), tax per SKU/transaction. Validate tax computation relative to line_total and tax rules. Identify rounding issues.
business_questions: What is the total tax collected for the recorded lines?
Which SKUs incur the most tax or highest average tax per unit?
tax_amt.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL with appropriate precision (e.g., DECIMAL(12,2)) to normalize floating-string artifacts. Use rounding to 2 decimals for presentation. When validating arithmetic (tax as percentage of line_total), guard against division by zero and small floating differences.

variant_info.description: Holds variant-level metadata about the product (e.g., size, color). In this dataset the column is 100% null.
variant_info.analysis_use: When populated, this is valuable for variant-level sales analysis (size/color). Currently unusable until data appears; keep schema mapping for future enrichment.
business_questions: Which size/color variants of a product sell best?
Do variant-specific promotions impact sales?
variant_info.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nullable string. When present, trim and normalize values before grouping. Use left joins to product_variants if needed. For NL2SQL, avoid assuming non-null—always include null-safe comparisons.

expiry_date.description: Expiry date associated with this line's product batch (if applicable). Currently 100% null in this dataset.
expiry_date.analysis_use: When populated, supports expiry-based sell-through analysis and inventory risk (near-expiry promotions). Currently not usable without data.
business_questions: How many sold items were near expiry at the time of sale?
Which batches are expiring soon and how many units sold from them?
expiry_date.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; when used cast to DATE or DATETIME. Handle multiple possible formats and nulls. NL2SQL should include parsing logic or rely on normalized expiry_date in joined inventory tables.

batch_id.description: Identifier for the inventory/manufacturing batch associated with the sold item. Almost unique per line (124 distinct), indicating strong batch-level traceability.
batch_id.analysis_use: Group/slice by batch_id for recall/traceability, compute units sold per batch, and join to inventory_batches or product_inventory for batch metadata (manufacture date, expiry).
business_questions: Which batches generated how many sales and what revenue?
Can we trace which customers/transactions received items from a specific batch?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string and prefer exact-match joins. Because batch_id is nearly unique, aggregations by batch will often be per-row. Use joins to inventory tables to get batch metadata; handle missing foreign key constraints and potential casing/whitespace mismatches.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the line record was created (transaction time). Stored as nvarchar strings in a datetime format.
created_at.analysis_use: Time-series analysis: filtering by date ranges, grouping by day/week/month for sales trends, and aligning with business calendar. Use to compute time-based KPIs and to join with date dimension after casting.
business_questions: What are daily/weekly/monthly sales trends from these POS lines?
Which hours of day have the highest unit sales or revenue?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., TRY_CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)) before temporal grouping/filters. Expect ISO-like format but validate parsing and timezone context. Use date_trunc or equivalent after casting for grouping. In NL2SQL, recommend explicit date parsing to avoid string comparisons.
