pos_txn_id.description: Unique identifier for the POS transaction (transaction header ID).
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/lookup key for transaction-level aggregations, joins to line-level tables, deduplication and reconciliation.
business_questions: How many transactions occurred in a period (count of pos_txn_id)?
Are there duplicate transaction IDs or reprocessed transactions?
Which transactions failed reconciliation with external references?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique identifier (string). Prefer equality filters and joins using pos_txn_id. If inferring PK, cast as varchar/char of appropriate length. For existence checks use EXISTS or JOIN to pos_transaction_lines. Avoid casting unless required by target join types.

terminal_id.description: Identifier of the POS terminal where the transaction was processed.
terminal_id.analysis_use: Grouping and filter dimension for operational metrics (sales by terminal), join key to terminal metadata for location/ownership attributes.
business_questions: Which terminals generate the highest sales?
Are there terminal-specific anomalies in average txn_total?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality string. Use direct equality or IN filters. When joining to pos_terminals, ensure consistent casing and trimming. Consider grouping by terminal_id for aggregates; avoid full-text functions.

pos_user_id.description: Identifier of the POS operator/cashier who processed the transaction.
pos_user_id.analysis_use: Dimension for staff performance (sales per cashier), pivoting and attribution of transactions to users.
business_questions: What is each cashier's total sales and transaction count over a period?
Which pos_user_id has the highest average transaction value?
pos_user_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality string. Use for GROUP BY and JOIN to pos_users to enrich with names. Be aware of aliasing or multiple user_id formats; trim/normalize before joins.

txn_ts.description: Timestamp of the transaction event (string in ISO-like format).
txn_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-series analyses, windowing, time bucket aggregation (hour/day/week), latency and trend analysis.
business_questions: How many transactions occurred by hour of day or day of week?
What are peak transaction hours for each terminal or store?
txn_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP in SQL (e.g., CAST(txn_ts AS datetime) or PARSE). Expect ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'. For NL2SQL, interpret temporal phrases (yesterday, last 7 days) and translate to appropriate ranges on cast(txn_ts). Support time-grain semantics: day, week, month, hour.

txn_type.description: Category of the transaction (e.g., SALE, REFUND).
txn_type.analysis_use: Filter dimension to include/exclude transaction types; segmentation of totals by type.
business_questions: What proportion of transactions are sales vs refunds?
How much value is returned in refunds compared to sales?
txn_type.nl2sql_hints: Single-valued in this sample ('SALE'). Treat as low-cardinality categorical string. NL2SQL should allow synonyms (refund, return) and map them to txn_type values. Use equality filters.

txn_total.description: Total monetary amount for the transaction (stored as string, includes subtotal, taxes, discounts).
txn_total.analysis_use: Primary numeric measure for sums, averages, medians, and revenue calculations; used in KPI calculations like AOV (average order value).
business_questions: What is total revenue in a date range (SUM(txn_total))?
What is the average transaction value by payment mode or shift?
txn_total.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar with floating formatting. Cast to numeric/decimal in queries (e.g., TRY_CAST(txn_total AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Be mindful of locale/formatting and trailing floating artifacts. Use SUM/AVG on cast values. Handle NULLs and bad parses with COALESCE/TRY_CAST.

tax_total.description: Monetary tax amount charged for the transaction (stored as string).
tax_total.analysis_use: Aggregation to compute total tax collected, tax rate checks, and tax reporting per period.
business_questions: How much tax was collected over a period or by terminal?
What is the average tax per transaction or effective tax rate (tax_total/txn_total)?
tax_total.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric (decimal) before aggregation. Use safe casting (TRY_CAST) and filter out nulls. When computing rates, avoid division by zero and consider rounding. NL2SQL should allow expressions like 'tax collected last month' -> SUM(CAST(tax_total AS DECIMAL)).

discount_total.description: Total discount applied to the transaction (stored as string). In sample it's constant '0'.
discount_total.analysis_use: Measure for promotional impact; aggregated to compute total discounts and discount rate per transaction.
business_questions: How much discount value was given in a period?
What percentage of transactions received discounts?
discount_total.nl2sql_hints: Currently constant 0 in sample; still cast to numeric for safety. Expect low variability. NL2SQL should handle zero/NULL and treat this as numeric for SUM/COUNT filtering (e.g., discount_total > 0).

payment_mode.description: Mode of payment used (e.g., CASH, UPI, CARD).
payment_mode.analysis_use: Categorical segmentation for revenue by payment type, payment mix analysis, and operational reconciliation.
business_questions: What share of revenue comes from each payment mode?
Are there differences in average transaction size by payment_mode?
payment_mode.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality text; use GROUP BY or pivot. Map common synonyms (UPI -> UPI, CREDIT_CARD/CARD) and handle case/whitespace. When NL2SQL interprets user language like 'card vs cash', map to these literal values.

external_txn_ref.description: External payment or gateway reference associated with the transaction. Useful for reconciliation with external systems.
external_txn_ref.analysis_use: Join/reconcile with external payment logs, deduplicate external references, and detect mismatches between internal and external ids.
business_questions: Which transactions have missing or duplicate external references?
Which external references failed to reconcile with payment gateway reports?
external_txn_ref.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string, often unique. When instructing NL2SQL to find unmatched external refs, plan LEFT JOINs to external payment tables. Watch for partial matches or different prefixes; consider normalization or SUBSTRING patterns.

shift_id.description: Identifier for the work shift during which the transaction occurred.
shift_id.analysis_use: Group and filter by operational shifts for staffing and shift-level KPI calculations; join to shift metadata to get start/end times and managers.
business_questions: Which shifts produce the most sales and transactions?
Is there a shift-level variance in average transaction value or tax collection?
shift_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality string (e.g., SHIFT1..SHIFT5). Use in GROUP BY. For time-bounded queries, joining to pos_shift_closures (if present) gives actual shift windows; NL2SQL should allow mapping phrases like 'during morning shift' to shift_id values or join to shift metadata.

cashier_note.description: Free-text note entered by cashier; in this sample it is entirely NULL.
cashier_note.analysis_use: Qualitative information for exceptions, refunds and special instructions; rarely used in aggregates but useful for investigations and text search.
business_questions: Which transactions have cashier notes indicating adjustments or issues?
Are there recurring notes that indicate a systemic problem at a terminal?
cashier_note.nl2sql_hints: Nullable free-text. Use LIKE or full-text search (if available) for NL2SQL intent like 'show transactions with note refund'. Handle nulls explicitly. If always null, avoid referencing in typical aggregates.

status.description: Operational status of the transaction (e.g., COMPLETED, PENDING, CANCELLED).
status.analysis_use: Filter to include only relevant statuses (completed) for revenue, or to surface failed/incomplete transactions for investigation.
business_questions: How many transactions are completed vs cancelled?
What is the count/value of non-completed transactions requiring follow-up?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string. In this sample it's always COMPLETED; still use equality filters. NL2SQL should allow synonyms (completed/finished) and include status filters by default for revenue queries (e.g., WHERE status = 'COMPLETED').

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the system (string). May mirror txn_ts if ingested immediately.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/time of ingestion dimension; can be used to measure ingestion latency (created_at vs txn_ts) and for incremental ingestion filters.
business_questions: What is the latency between txn_ts and created_at (ingest delay)?
Which transactions were created in a certain ingestion window?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP. Use for temporal filters when txn_ts is missing or to compute delays (CAST(created_at AS datetime) - CAST(txn_ts AS datetime)). For incremental ETL, use created_at > last_run_time filters.

synced_flag.description: Flag indicating whether the transaction was synced to downstream systems (string '1' for synced in sample).
synced_flag.analysis_use: Filter to find unsynced transactions for retry/monitoring; KPI for data pipeline health.
business_questions: How many transactions are not yet synced to downstream systems?
What is the total value of unsynced transactions needing reconciliation?
synced_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string (e.g., '1'/'0'). Cast or compare as string depending on DB. NL2SQL should interpret boolean-like phrases (unsynced, pending) to WHERE synced_flag <> '1' OR synced_flag IS NULL. If always '1', queries should still allow the condition but expect zero results.

pos_user_id.description: Unique identifier for the POS user (employee/staff) within the POS system.
pos_user_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for joins to transactions, shifts and audit logs; grouping and counting users; deduplication of user records.
business_questions: How many unique POS users are assigned to each store?
Which user performed a particular transaction (when joined to transactions)?
pos_user_id.nl2sql_hints: Although nvarchar, treat as a key/identifier. Use equality joins (pos_user_id = ...). Consider enforcing uniqueness/PK in upstream schema. Avoid casting; match exact string. For suggestions to the user, recommend indexing this column and declaring it primary key for faster joins.

name.description: Full human-readable name of the POS user.
name.analysis_use: Used for reporting, display, and grouping when presenting human-readable results. Useful in SELECT lists and simple groupings (e.g., top performing users after join).
business_questions: What are the names of users assigned to STORE_AHD_001?
List users and their contact details for a given role.
name.nl2sql_hints: Free-text nvarchar. When matching by name consider case sensitivity depending on DB collation. Avoid using name as join key; prefer pos_user_id. Use LIKE for partial name searches (e.g., name LIKE '%Kishan%').

role.description: User role or job function at the POS (e.g., Cashier, Supervisor).
role.analysis_use: Low-cardinality attribute for grouping and filtering (counts by role, role-based access reports). Useful for role-based aggregates like count of cashiers per store.
business_questions: How many cashiers vs supervisors are there across stores?
Which supervisors have not logged in within the last 30 days?
role.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical; use equality or IN for filtering (role = 'Cashier'). Consider normalizing to a roles table if role values grow. Watch for synonyms/typos; provide standardized role list for NL2SQL mapping.

phone.description: Contact phone number for the POS user, stored as a string (includes country code).
phone.analysis_use: Used for contact lists, deduplication and quick lookups; not useful for numeric aggregation. Can be used to detect duplicates or validate contact coverage of staff.
business_questions: Provide phone numbers for all active users in a store.
Which users do not have a phone number recorded?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar with formatting (+91 ...). Use exact match or normalized pattern matching (remove spaces/characters) for comparisons. Do not cast to numeric. Consider normalization when building NL2SQL intents that search by phone fragments.

email.description: User's email address used for notifications and identity contact.
email.analysis_use: Used to build contact lists and to deduplicate accounts; can join to other systems that reference email as an identifier.
business_questions: List emails of supervisors for outreach.
Which users have corporate vs personal email addresses (domain-based segmentation)?
email.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; support LIKE '%@example.com' queries. Be cautious exposing emails in automated responses (PII). If NL2SQL intent requests PII, ensure policy compliance. Use lower() when comparing domains to avoid case issues.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store the POS user is assigned to (likely links to pos_stores table).
store_id.analysis_use: Key for grouping and filtering by store. Crucial for join to store metadata (store name, location) and for calculating per-store headcount or per-store activity when joined with transactional tables.
business_questions: How many users are assigned to each store?
Show active users for STORE_RJT_001.
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Although nvarchar, use as join key to pos_stores. Matches should be exact (store_id = 'STORE_AHD_001') or IN list. Consider indexing store_id; if store lookup is required return store name via join. Watch for NULLs if unassigned.

active_flag.description: Flag indicating whether the user is active (sample uses '1'); stored as a string.
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter active vs inactive users (headcount of active staff). Useful for workforce management and security access controls.
business_questions: How many active POS users are there across all stores?
List inactive users who still have transactions (requires join).
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar (values like '1'/'0' or 'Y'/'N' may appear). NL2SQL should map semantic terms: 'active' -> active_flag = '1' or similar. Recommend normalizing to boolean or tinyint upstream. Always handle possible values and NULLs (e.g., active_flag IS NULL as inactive).

created_at.description: Datetime string when the POS user record was created (format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS in sample).
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal analysis: user creation trends, on-boarding velocity, cohorts. Use for filtering by date ranges and calculating tenure.
business_questions: How many users were created in the last month?
Show users created between two dates.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; convert/cast to datetime for comparisons (e.g., CAST(created_at AS datetime) or TRY_PARSE depending on DB). NL2SQL parsers should detect natural language date ranges and generate proper CASTs. Validate format and handle invalid or NULL values.

last_login.description: Datetime string of the user's most recent login to the POS system (format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS in sample).
last_login.analysis_use: User activity and recency metrics: identify inactive users, compute days since last login, filter by last active date, correlate activity with transactions.
business_questions: Which users have not logged in for over 30 days?
When did each user last log in?
last_login.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar; cast to datetime for date arithmetic. Use functions like DATEDIFF or current_date - CAST(last_login AS datetime) depending on SQL dialect. Handle NULLs (never-logged-in) explicitly. NL2SQL should translate terms like 'inactive for 30 days' into appropriate date arithmetic comparisons.

pin_hash.description: Hashed representation of the user's PIN; sensitive credential data (hash).
pin_hash.analysis_use: Not used for analytics; presence can be checked (e.g., users missing PIN hashes) but actual values should not be exposed. Useful for security audits (who has PIN set).
business_questions: Which users do not have a PIN set (missing pin_hash)?
How many users have a PIN hash present vs missing?
pin_hash.nl2sql_hints: Treat as sensitive PII/credential. Avoid returning full values in automated NL2SQL responses; prefer existence checks (pin_hash IS NOT NULL). Do not attempt to reverse or compare hashes as plaintext. Ensure role-based access controls when querying this column.

vendor_id.description: Alphanumeric identifier for the vendor (system-level vendor code).
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup/join key for vendor-level enrichment; grouping and filtering by vendor; identifying unique vendors. Should be used for exact-match joins to transactional tables.
business_questions: What transactions are associated with vendor_id = 'POSV1001'?
How many unique vendors do we have (count distinct vendor_id)?
Which vendors have had activity in a given period after joining with transactions?
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string for equality joins and filters. Use exact matches for joins (vendor_id = '...'). Avoid LIKE for matching unless vendor_id patterns are used. Consider trimming whitespace and normalizing case when matching across sources. There is no declared PK so guard against duplicates by using COUNT(DISTINCT vendor_id).

vendor_name.analysis_use: Used for display, grouping, fuzzy matching and de-duplication. Useful in text searches and reporting (top vendors by name).
business_questions: List vendors whose name contains 'Bobal' or 'Choudhary'.
Which vendor names are similar and might indicate duplicates?
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: Perform case-insensitive searches using LOWER() or ILIKE depending on SQL dialect. For substring matches use LIKE '%term%'. When joining to external data, prefer vendor_id or gst_no over vendor_name because names can vary/contain punctuation.

phone.description: Vendor contact phone number, stored as text (includes country code/formatting).
phone.analysis_use: Contactability checks, filtering vendors with/without phone, grouping by area code extraction, and match keys for deduplication.
business_questions: Which vendors do not have a phone number recorded?
Show all vendor phones in a particular area code or country prefix (+91).
phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone is textual and may include spaces and punctuation. Normalize by removing non-digit characters when comparing (e.g., REGEXP_REPLACE(phone, '\D','') or translate). Use IS NULL or phone = '' to find missing values. Avoid numeric casts because of leading zeros/country codes.

email.analysis_use: Contact lists, validation of available electronic contact, deduplication and identity resolution, and filtering vendors for notifications.
business_questions: Which vendors have email addresses on the vendor.in domain?
How many vendors are missing email addresses?
email.nl2sql_hints: Case-insensitive comparisons (LOWER(email)). Validate format with simple pattern checks (LIKE '%@%._%') or regular expressions depending on SQL dialect. Watch for commas inside stored email strings (observed value 'buch,bansalandtalwar@vendor.in') — data cleaning may be required before strict validation or joining.

address.description: Full mailing/street address for the vendor (one free-form text field).
address.analysis_use: Geographic enrichment, standardization, and display. Useful for extracting city/postal code if not separately stored. Can be used for fuzzy matching and proximity-based reporting if normalized.
business_questions: What are the full addresses for vendors in Anand?
Which vendors have incomplete addresses or appear to share the same address?
address.nl2sql_hints: Address is free-form text. Use substring (LIKE) queries for partial matches, and regular expressions or string functions to extract components (postal code). Avoid relying on consistent formatting; consider creating derived columns (postal_code, street) after cleaning.

city.description: City where the vendor is located (categorical, low cardinality).
city.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation (counts by city), filtering vendors by location, and joining to location-level analytics (regional spend). Good candidate for indexing or dimension table for lookups.
business_questions: How many vendors are in Gandhinagar vs Vadodara?
Which cities contribute the most vendors for Equipment Repair business_type?
city.nl2sql_hints: City is short categorical text — use equality or IN(...) clauses for filters. Be mindful of variations/spelling (use normalized mapping or a city dimension for robust NL2SQL). Consider indexing if used frequently in WHERE clauses (though table is small).

gst_no.description: Vendor GST (tax) number, stored as text.
gst_no.analysis_use: Unique legal identifier for vendors; useful for reconciliation with invoices, compliance, and duplicate detection. Good join key to external accounting or tax systems.
business_questions: Which vendors have missing or malformed GST numbers?
Are there multiple vendor_ids associated with the same GST number (potential duplicates)?
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string and use exact matches for reconciliation. Strip non-alphanumeric characters when comparing across sources. Validate length/format with regex. Prefer gst_no joins when vendor_id is not available in external datasets.

business_type.description: Categorical description of the vendor's business (e.g., Equipment Repair, Hardware).
business_type.analysis_use: Segmentation, grouping and filter for reporting (counts by supplier type), and joining to category-level KPIs. Good for pivot and breakdowns.
business_questions: How many vendors are in 'Equipment Repair' and what proportion of the vendor base is that?
List all hardware suppliers and their contact details.
business_type.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical text; equality and IN filters will be common. Normalize category values (trim/case) in preprocessing to avoid mismatches. Use business_type in GROUP BY for aggregated NL2SQL queries.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the vendor record was created, stored as nvarchar rather than datetime. Observed to be identical for all rows in this snapshot.
created_at.analysis_use: Auditing and change tracking; in this snapshot it's not useful for trend/time analyses because it's constant. Should be converted to a proper datetime column to support time-based filtering in the future.
business_questions: When was the vendor master loaded/created?
Filter vendors created after a specific date (after casting this column to datetime).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Column is stored as text. Cast to a datetime type before performing comparisons (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP depending on SQL dialect). Guard against parsing errors if formats vary. Because current value is identical across rows, time-based filters on this snapshot will return either all or none unless data is updated.

inventory_id.description: Unique identifier for an inventory record (combination of sku and centre/lot).
inventory_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for row-level inventory records; use for deduplication, identifying specific lots; join key if inventory_id exists in inventory transactions/adjustments tables.
business_questions: What is the inventory record for inventory_id = 'INV-APP009-AHMEDABAD'?
Which inventory_id rows correspond to a given SKU and centre?
inventory_id.nl2sql_hints: Treated as string. Often unique in this dataset (40 distinct). Use equality filters or IN lists. If joining to audit/adjustment tables, ensure they reference the exact same inventory_id string; consider trimming/case-normalization.

sku.description: Product SKU code representing the product/variant.
sku.analysis_use: Group/aggregate inventory by product; join to product catalog and sales tables to enrich with product attributes and demand history.
business_questions: What is total available_stock for SKU 'APP009' across all centres?
Which SKUs have the highest reserved_stock?
sku.nl2sql_hints: String column representing an identifier; join key to products/product_variants tables. Expect short codes—use exact match or IN. Normalize case if needed when joining to other sources.

centre_id.description: Human-readable name of the centre/warehouse/collection point holding the inventory.
centre_id.analysis_use: Filter/group by location for centre-level stock reports, temperature compliance, and replenishment routing.
business_questions: Which centre has the most available_stock for SKU 'BRN007'?
List SKUs at 'Rajkot Packaging Unit' below reorder point.
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: String column with small cardinality (4). Use in GROUP BY and WHERE. If joining to location metadata, match on exact name or map to a canonical centre_id in the location table.

qty_units.description: Quantity expressed in discrete units (stored as nvarchar).
qty_units.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) to compute unit-level inventory by SKU/centre; ratio calculations (units per order); conversion when comparing to kg-based metrics.
business_questions: How many units of each SKU are currently in stock across centres?
Which SKUs have low unit counts that may cause stockouts?
qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—cast to INTEGER/NUMERIC for SUM, AVG, comparisons. Watch out for non-numeric or null values; apply TRY_CAST or ISNUMERIC checks. Use COALESCE(TRY_CAST(qty_units AS INT),0) patterns.

qty_kg.description: Quantity expressed in kilograms (stored as nvarchar).
qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate weight-based inventory, important for perishables and storage constraints; compute weight-based safety stock coverage and freight calculations.
business_questions: What is the total kg on-hand for each SKU across all centres?
Which centres have the largest weight (kg) of perishable inventory?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before aggregation. Be careful with localized decimal separators. Use TRY_CAST or equivalent to avoid query failures on bad data.

reserved_stock.description: Quantity reserved (units or kg depending on schema context) and not available for allocation (stored as nvarchar).
reserved_stock.analysis_use: Subtract from qty to calculate free/available inventory; used in allocation, backorder, and planning calculations.
business_questions: How much stock is reserved for pending orders per SKU and centre?
Are reservations causing shortages for urgent orders?
reserved_stock.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—cast to numeric. Clarify unit semantics (units or kg) before combining with qty_units/qty_kg. Use numeric casting with defaults for NULL/non-numeric.

available_stock.description: Quantity immediately available for new allocations (stored as nvarchar).
available_stock.analysis_use: Primary field for allocation, seller-facing available-to-promise (ATP) metrics, and for computing days of cover vs forecast/sales.
business_questions: Which SKUs are currently available for immediate fulfillment at each centre?
List SKUs with available_stock below safety_stock_kg.
available_stock.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to numeric. Confirm whether available_stock is in units or kg; match datatype when comparing to safety_stock_kg or reorder_point. Use COALESCE/TRY_CAST to avoid exceptions.

reorder_point.description: Configured threshold below which a reorder should be triggered (stored as nvarchar).
reorder_point.analysis_use: Compare available_stock against reorder_point to identify replenishment needs; used in automated alerting and ordering logic.
business_questions: Which inventory rows are below their reorder point right now?
How many SKUs require immediate reorder at each centre?
reorder_point.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—cast to numeric for comparisons. Clarify unit semantics (units or kg). When building NL2SQL, translate linguistic terms like 'below reorder point' to WHERE CAST(available_stock AS NUMERIC) < CAST(reorder_point AS NUMERIC).

lead_time_days.description: Supplier/fulfilment expected lead time in days (stored as nvarchar).
lead_time_days.analysis_use: Used to compute expected replenishment arrival, safety stock planning and reorder timing (reorder point often = demand*lead_time + safety_stock). Low cardinality helps segmentation.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the longest lead_time_days and require larger safety stocks?
Show inventory rows where lead_time_days >= 3 and available_stock < reorder_point.
lead_time_days.nl2sql_hints: String field representing small set of integer values. Cast to INT for date arithmetic. Use in date-add logic: expected_arrival = CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL CAST(lead_time_days AS INT) DAY (DB-specific syntax).

safety_stock_kg.description: Configured safety stock level (in kg), used to absorb demand/lead-time variability (stored as nvarchar).
safety_stock_kg.analysis_use: Compare against available_stock to assess risk of stockout; feed into reorder calculations and service-level analyses.
business_questions: Which SKUs have available_stock below safety_stock_kg?
How many kgs of stock are held as safety stock across warehouses?
safety_stock_kg.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—cast to numeric (DECIMAL). Confirm that safety_stock is indeed in kg; avoid mixing units. Use COALESCE/TRY_CAST when computing differences like CAST(available_stock AS NUMERIC) - CAST(safety_stock_kg AS NUMERIC).

expiry_ts.description: Expiry timestamp for the inventory lot (stored as nvarchar datetime string).
expiry_ts.analysis_use: Compute days-to-expiry, filter for expired or near-expiry inventory, prioritize allocations, and schedule promotions or disposals.
business_questions: Which inventory items will expire in the next 7 days?
List all expired inventory as of today.
expiry_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—convert to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for date operations. Be careful with format (appears ISO-like: 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'). Use TRY_CAST or safe parsing. When filtering for 'expiring soon', use WHERE CAST(expiry_ts AS DATE) <= DATE_ADD(CURRENT_DATE, INTERVAL X DAY). Consider timezone implications if present.

storage_temp_c.description: Required or recorded storage temperature in degrees Celsius (stored as nvarchar).
storage_temp_c.analysis_use: Group products by storage class (cold vs ambient), enforce compliance checks, determine transport requirements and cold-chain capacity planning.
business_questions: Which SKUs require cold storage (storage_temp_c = 4) and are located in centres without cold storage?
How much kg of cold-chain inventory is stored across centres?
storage_temp_c.nl2sql_hints: Small cardinality numeric-like string (values '10' and '4'). Cast to INT for numeric comparisons. Use when filtering for temperature-sensitive items. If joining to centre capability data, map numeric temperature to storage class (e.g., cold <=4).

price_id.description: Unique identifier for the pricing record / price version. Appears to be distinct per row (e.g., 'PR-APP009-1').
price_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, lineage and auditing. Useful for selecting or referencing a specific price record when reconciling changes or linking to external logs.
business_questions: Which price_id corresponds to the price that was active for SKU X on DATE?
For a given price_id, when was it created and what validity window did it cover?
price_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. Use equality or IN filters when referencing specific versions. Consider enforcing uniqueness (or using price_id in WHERE/ORDER BY when resolving ties). Avoid numeric or temporal operations on this column.

sku.description: Product stock keeping unit — the key used to associate a price record with a product.
sku.analysis_use: Main grouping/joining column for per-product aggregations (price history, averages, counts) and joins to product/catalog tables. Use in WHERE to filter to specific products.
business_questions: What is the price history for SKU APP009?
Which SKUs have had the most price updates in the last quarter?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use sku to join to products/product_variants tables. Values are short strings; equality/group-by operations are common. Ensure joins use the same SKU normalization (case, padding) as other tables.

start_ts.description: Start of the validity window for the price record. Stored as nvarchar but contains timestamp-like values (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS).
start_ts.analysis_use: Filter and determine active price windows (start <= query_time and end > query_time). Useful for ordering price versions and time-based aggregations. Must be cast to a DATE/TIMESTAMP type for correct comparisons and range queries.
business_questions: When did this price version for SKU X become effective?
Which prices became effective within a specified period (e.g., last month)?
start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to proper TIMESTAMP/DATETIME before comparisons (e.g., CAST(start_ts AS datetime) or to_timestamp). When building NL2SQL, interpret common phrases like 'on', 'after', 'from' to compare against start_ts. Watch for inclusive/exclusive semantics (start often inclusive).

end_ts.description: End of the validity window for the price record. Stored as nvarchar but contains timestamp-like values.
end_ts.analysis_use: Used with start_ts to determine active periods and detect overlaps or gaps. Must be cast to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME for accurate range logic. Useful for filtering records that expire within a period.
business_questions: Which pricing records expire within the next N days?
Do any price validity windows overlap for the same SKU?
end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME. Typical NL2SQL patterns: 'active on DATE' -> start_ts <= DATE AND end_ts >= DATE (or > depending on business rule). Be careful with open/closed interval semantics and null/empty end_ts if present in other datasets.

price_per_unit.description: Unit price for the record. Stored as nvarchar but contains decimal numeric values (e.g., '156.65').
price_per_unit.analysis_use: Numeric analyses: min/max/avg price, price deltas between consecutive versions, percentile pricing, and revenue-impact estimations when joined with sales data. Must be cast to numeric/decimal for arithmetic.
business_questions: What was the average unit price for SKU X over the last quarter?
How much did the price change between the two latest versions for a SKU?
price_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/FLOAT before aggregation or arithmetic (e.g., CAST(price_per_unit AS decimal(18,4))). Beware of string formatting/locale issues. Use numeric casting when constructing expressions like price differences or weighted averages.

currency.description: Currency code for the price value (ISO code like 'INR'). Here it's constant (INR).
currency.analysis_use: Validate and convert prices across currencies if present. Useful for multi-currency reports and for detecting inconsistent currency entries.
business_questions: Are there any pricing records in non-INR currencies?
If multiple currencies exist, what is the price in a common reporting currency after conversion?
currency.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a short string. If constant, it can be ignored in single-currency queries. For NL2SQL, consider currency conversion joins if the user asks for prices in a different currency.

min_order_qty.description: Minimum order quantity required for this price record (stored as string but small integer-like values: 1,5,10).
min_order_qty.analysis_use: Segment prices by order quantity tiers, analyze pricing strategies by min_order_qty, and filter applicable prices for orders. Cast to integer for numeric aggregations or comparisons.
business_questions: What are the price levels for orders with min_order_qty = 10?
How many active price records require a min_order_qty of 5?
min_order_qty.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INT for comparisons and grouping. When users ask for 'prices for bulk orders' map terms like 'bulk' to explicit thresholds or values from this column. Watch for small domain values and use equality filters where appropriate.

created_by.description: Identifier of the user/system that created the pricing record (here 'pricing_admin' for all rows).
created_by.analysis_use: Audit and accountability (who created/updated prices), filter changes by actor, and measure activity by user. If multiple actors appear in other datasets, used for change tracking.
business_questions: How many price records were created by pricing_admin in the last month?
Which users have made price updates for SKU X?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string. Use in WHERE clauses for audits. If only a single value exists, user might not need to specify it; NL2SQL should still allow filtering if the natural language mentions actor names.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the price record was created (stored as nvarchar but contains timestamp-like strings).
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal analytics on rate of price updates, ordering records by creation time to pick latest changes, and filtering changes within windows. Must be cast to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME for accurate time-based queries.
business_questions: How many price records were created between DATE1 and DATE2?
What is the most recent price update time for SKU X?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME for comparisons and ORDER BY. When resolving 'latest' use created_at DESC (after casting) and consider ties (use price_id as tie-breaker). NL2SQL should normalize date expressions and support relative date phrases (e.g., 'last month').

sku.description: Product stock-keeping unit identifier. nvarchar(22) with actual max length 6 in this sample; 10 distinct values in 10 rows.
sku.analysis_use: Used for grouping, filtering, joining to product master tables and for per-SKU aggregations. Ideal for rollups and SKU-level comparisons.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the top 5 gross sales?
Are there SKUs present here that are missing in the canonical product master?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical/join key. Use exact equality joins (products.sku). Watch for whitespace/case differences: normalize by TRIM() and UPPER()/LOWER() before joining. Avoid numeric casts. If ambiguous, match against product tables using LIKE or mapping tables. Example hint for SQL: SELECT ... FROM zuna_seed_raw.product_sales ps JOIN products p ON TRIM(ps.sku)=TRIM(p.sku)

Gross Sales.description: Reported gross sales amount for the SKU, stored as text (nvarchar). Values have decimals and large magnitudes.
Gross Sales.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue analysis: summing, averaging, sorting, computing growth and shares. Needs casting to numeric (DECIMAL/NUMERIC) for arithmetic and aggregation.
business_questions: What is total Gross Sales across all SKUs or by SKU?
Which SKU contributed the largest share of Gross Sales?
Gross Sales.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/TRY_CONVERT/TRY_CAST to a numeric type before aggregation. Handle empty strings or non-numeric values with TRY_CAST or CASE WHEN ISNUMERIC(...) (SQL Server) to avoid runtime errors. Example: TRY_CAST([Gross Sales] AS DECIMAL(18,2)) AS gross_sales_num. Quoted column name (with space) must be bracketed: [Gross Sales]. Consider validating decimal punctuation (commas) before cast.

Discount.description: Total discount applied to the SKU, stored as text (nvarchar). Contains decimal values.
Discount.analysis_use: Used to compute discount amounts and rates (e.g., discount as % of gross), aggregate total discounts, and analyze impact of promotions. Requires casting to numeric for computations.
business_questions: What is the total discount given per SKU and as a percentage of gross sales?
Which SKUs have the highest absolute or relative discount amounts?
Discount.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to numeric (DECIMAL) before using in arithmetic. Use defensive parsing to treat missing/NULL/empty as zero where appropriate. When asking NL2SQL to compute discount %: ensure it generates safe SQL like CASE WHEN TRY_CAST([Gross Sales] AS DECIMAL(18,2))>0 THEN TRY_CAST([Discount] AS DECIMAL(18,2))/TRY_CAST([Gross Sales] AS DECIMAL(18,2)) END. Bracket column name: [Discount].

Delivery Fee.description: Delivery fee amount attributed to the SKU, stored as text (nvarchar).
Delivery Fee.analysis_use: Can be aggregated to compute total delivery charges per SKU, used to compute net contribution, and to separate product revenue from fulfillment-related fees. Requires casting to numeric.
business_questions: How much delivery fee is collected per SKU or as a percentage of gross sales?
Are delivery fees materially affecting net sales for specific SKUs?
Delivery Fee.nl2sql_hints: Column name includes a space; use brackets [Delivery Fee] in SQL. CAST to decimal via TRY_CAST to avoid errors. Decide whether delivery fee should be treated as part of revenue or as a separate fee in calculations — specify formula explicitly in NL2SQL prompts (e.g., net = gross - discount - tax + delivery_fee or supply the correct business rule).

Tax.description: Tax amount applied to the SKU, stored as text (nvarchar).
Tax.analysis_use: Used to analyze tax burden per SKU, to compute tax rates and to reconcile tax collected. Must be cast to numeric before arithmetic or aggregation.
business_questions: What is total tax collected per SKU and effective tax rate relative to gross sales?
Are there SKUs with unexpectedly high or low tax amounts (data errors or exemptions)?
Tax.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to numeric. Watch for differing tax conventions; clarify whether tax is included in gross or added on top. Use explicit SQL like TRY_CAST([Tax] AS DECIMAL(18,2)) and handle NULL/'' as 0. Bracketed column name required: [Tax].

Net Sales.description: Reported net sales amount for the SKU, stored as text (nvarchar). Expected to be a derivation of the other monetary fields but exact formula is not documented here.
Net Sales.analysis_use: Used as the target value for validation (compare computed net vs reported net), for reporting net revenue, and for downstream KPIs. Must be cast to numeric for comparisons and aggregations.
business_questions: Does reported Net Sales equal the expected computed value from Gross Sales, Discount, Delivery Fee and Tax?
What are total Net Sales by SKU and which SKUs have the highest net revenue?
Net Sales.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to numeric before comparisons/aggregations. NL2SQL prompts should instruct to validate formula explicitly (e.g., computed_net = TRY_CAST([Gross Sales] AS DECIMAL(18,2)) - TRY_CAST([Discount] AS DECIMAL(18,2)) + TRY_CAST([Delivery Fee] AS DECIMAL(18,2)) - TRY_CAST([Tax] AS DECIMAL(18,2)) — or the business-specific formula). Because column name contains a space, use [Net Sales]. Use safe null-handling to avoid misleading mismatch results.

variant_id.description: Unique identifier for the variant (string). Appears to uniquely identify each row in sample.
variant_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup key for variant-level reporting and joins; grouping for variant-specific aggregations; deduplication and lineage checks.
business_questions: What are the details for variant VAR3001?
How many sales/stock movements map to a given variant_id?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as identifier string. Use exact equality in WHERE clauses (e.g., variant_id = 'VAR3001'). Ensure joins to other tables use the same case/trim semantics; consider creating an index or declaring a primary key if used frequently for joins.

sku.description: Product-level SKU that groups multiple variants (short product code).
sku.analysis_use: Group variants by product (GROUP BY sku) to compute per-product metrics; join key to product master or sales tables that store SKU; filter for product-centric queries.
business_questions: What variants exist for SKU APP009?
What is the average retail price of variants for each SKU?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Often used as join key to products and sales tables. Values are strings—use equality or IN lists for filtering. Be mindful of whitespace and casing; normalize (TRIM/UPPER) if other tables differ.

variant_name.description: Human-readable variant label (e.g., 'Loose', 'Packed 500g'). Low cardinality in sample.
variant_name.analysis_use: Categorical grouping (e.g., loose vs packed) for segmentation, filtering or pivoting; used in user-facing queries and labels.
business_questions: How many 'Loose' vs 'Packed' variants exist?
What is the price difference between 'Loose' and 'Packed' variants?
variant_name.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string—use equality or IN. Watch for synonyms/spacing (e.g., 'Loose' vs 'loose'). Useful for natural language queries like 'show me packed variants'.

variant_sku.description: Compound SKU including product SKU and variant descriptor (e.g., APP009-LOOSE). Unique per variant.
variant_sku.analysis_use: Exact variant-level identifier for joins and display; often used in exports and for matching to external systems that reference full variant SKU.
business_questions: Return the full variant SKU and price for each variant of APP009.
Which variant_sku values have been sold in the last month (when joined with orders)?
variant_sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as stable string identifier for joins. Supports pattern searches (LIKE '%-PACKED%') to filter variant types. Consider normalization if separators vary.

uom.description: Unit of measure for the variant (e.g., 'kg', '250g', '500g').
uom.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering to standardize measures; used to convert/normalize weights for aggregation and inventory calculations.
business_questions: How many variants use 'kg' as UOM vs gram-level UOMs?
What is the distribution of UOMs among active variants?
uom.nl2sql_hints: Values are strings and may be inconsistent (e.g., 'kg' vs 'KG' vs 'Kg'). Normalize the field before numeric conversions. When converting to base units, combine with weight_kg or units_per_pack.

units_per_pack.description: Number of units per pack (string representation of numeric, e.g., '0.5', '1.0').
units_per_pack.analysis_use: Convert to numeric to compute units in inventory/shipments, aggregate packs to unit counts, calculate price per unit.
business_questions: What is the price per unit for variants with units_per_pack = 0.5?
Which pack sizes (units_per_pack) sell best when joined with sales data?
units_per_pack.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—use TRY_CAST/CAST to DECIMAL or FLOAT for arithmetic (e.g., TRY_CAST(units_per_pack AS DECIMAL(10,4))). Handle NULLs and malformed strings. Use COALESCE or CASE to provide defaults.

weight_kg.description: Weight of the variant in kilograms (stored as string, e.g., '0.5', '1.0').
weight_kg.analysis_use: Normalize to numeric weight for inventory weight calculations, shipping cost estimates, and weight-based pricing analyses.
business_questions: Which variants exceed 1.0 kg in weight?
What is the total shipping weight of variants in a given order when joined to order_lines?
weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric (TRY_CAST(weight_kg AS DECIMAL(10,4))). If uom uses grams, ensure consistent unit conversion. Watch out for empty strings or inconsistent formats.

barcode.description: Machine-readable barcode assigned to the variant (string).
barcode.analysis_use: Join/lookup key for scan-level inventory, shipments and returns; quality checks for duplicates/missing barcodes.
business_questions: Which variants are missing a barcode?
Are there barcode duplicates across variants?
barcode.nl2sql_hints: Use equality and GROUP BY to find duplicates. Trim/normalize formatting; some external systems may store leading zeros—ensure consistent string comparisons. Joins to inventories/shipments often use barcode.

price_retail.description: Retail price for the variant stored as nvarchar (contains numeric-like strings with floating artifacts).
price_retail.analysis_use: Convert to numeric for pricing analytics: averages, aggregations, price distribution, price changes over time (if tracked), and margin calculations when joined with wholesale cost or sales revenue.
business_questions: What is the average retail price by SKU or variant_name?
Which variants have retail price above X or below a threshold?
price_retail.nl2sql_hints: Must cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT) before arithmetic (e.g., TRY_CAST(price_retail AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Handle malformed values and floating point string artifacts. Consider storing canonical numeric column to speed up numeric queries.

price_wholesale.description: Wholesale price for the variant (nvarchar storing numeric-like values).
price_wholesale.analysis_use: Used with price_retail to compute margins, markups and pricing strategies; aggregate wholesale price by sku or supplier when joined.
business_questions: What is the markup percentage ((retail-wholesale)/wholesale) per variant?
Which variants have wholesale price above a given threshold?
price_wholesale.nl2sql_hints: Same as price_retail: cast to numeric before calculations. Beware of NULLs and string formatting. Use TRY_CAST to avoid query failures on bad data.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the variant record was created, stored as nvarchar (e.g., '2023-11-17 01:23:29').
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal filtering (new variants), cohort analyses, audit and change tracking when combined with other temporal datasets.
business_questions: Which variants were added in Q4 2023?
How many variants were created each month?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—use TRY_CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or database-specific parsing to convert. Watch timezone assumptions. Use ISDATE / TRY_CONVERT checks to handle malformed dates. Indexing on parsed datetime may require a persisted computed column.

active_flag.description: Indicator whether the variant is active (stored as string; sample shows '1' for active).
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter active/inactive variants in queries; compute counts of active inventory; ensure only active variants appear in consumer-facing lists.
business_questions: How many active vs inactive variants exist?
Show only active variants for display or export.
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: String field representing boolean—treat '1'/'0' (or 'Y'/'N') consistently. Normalize to boolean in NL2SQL (e.g., WHERE active_flag = '1' or WHERE active_flag = '0'). Consider migrating to a BIT/BOOLEAN column for efficiency.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit identifier for the product (alphanumeric code). Appears unique per row in this sample.
sku.analysis_use: Primary join key to link product attributes to transactions, pricing and inventory. Useful for grouping and counting distinct products. Frequent filter in NL queries (e.g., WHERE sku = ...).
business_questions: What are the sales and returns for a given SKU over time?
Which SKUs currently lack images or HS codes?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Prefer exact match filters and equality joins. Trim and normalize case if data from other tables differs in case/whitespace. Use as the primary join to tables like product_sales, product_pricing, product_inventory, order_items.

product_name.analysis_use: Display label in query results, text search and fuzzy matching for catalog lookups. Can be grouped for reporting where SKU-level aggregation is not necessary.
business_questions: Show product names for top-selling SKUs.
Find products whose names contain keywords (e.g., 'Apple') for promotions.
product_name.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; support LIKE / ILIKE or full-text search when available. Be careful with parentheses and unit suffixes — consider normalizing units out of names to avoid duplication.

product_type.description: High-level type of product (e.g., 'Vegetable', 'Fruit'). Low cardinality.
product_type.analysis_use: Filter or GROUP BY for high-level category analysis and channel reporting. Good for segmentation and pivoting.
business_questions: What proportion of sales comes from Vegetables vs Fruits?
How many SKUs per product_type are active in the catalog?
product_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string. Use equality checks or IN lists. Consider mapping/standardizing synonyms. Cast not required but watch for trailing spaces or inconsistent capitalization.

category.description: Broad category label (in this snapshot always 'Fresh Produce').
category.analysis_use: Used to filter or group multi-category catalogs. In this dataset it's constant so not useful for discrimination but keep for completeness.
business_questions: How many SKUs exist within each category?
Are there categories missing HS codes or images?
category.nl2sql_hints: String with very low cardinality. Verify upstream whether category will vary across full dataset; treat as categorical attribute in GROUP BY queries.

subcategory.description: More granular classification of the product (e.g., 'Apple', 'Bitter Gourd').
subcategory.analysis_use: Key grouping dimension for product-level reports, assortment analysis and shelf planning.
business_questions: Which subcategories contribute most to revenue/volume?
How many SKUs exist in each subcategory?
subcategory.nl2sql_hints: String. Use for grouping and filtering. Normalize values (trailing unit text sometimes present) to avoid splitting the same logical subcategory into multiple labels.

brand.description: Brand name for the product (here 'Daily Greens').
brand.analysis_use: Brand-level reporting, vendor analysis, and promotions. In this snapshot single-brand so low discrimination.
business_questions: What are sales by brand?
Which brands have the most SKUs missing images or HS codes?
brand.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string — watch for variations/casing. If joining to vendor or brand master tables, ensure consistent normalization of brand keys.

uom.description: Unit of measure for the SKU (e.g., 'kg', '250g', '500g').
uom.analysis_use: Important for converting quantities, aggregating volumes and normalizing sales/returns. Use to standardize weight/volume calculations.
business_questions: What is total quantity sold in kg after normalizing UOMs?
Which UOMs are most common among best-selling SKUs?
uom.nl2sql_hints: String representing unit. When aggregating quantity across SKUs, convert or normalize units (e.g., 250g -> 0.25 kg). Consider mapping table for unit conversion in NL2SQL translation.

gross_weight_kg.description: Gross weight of the packaged product in kg (stored as nvarchar).
gross_weight_kg.analysis_use: Used for shipping cost estimation, cartonization and logistics weight calculations. Aggregate to compute total shipped weight.
business_questions: What is the total gross weight shipped per order or SKU?
Which SKUs contribute most to shipping weight/cost?
gross_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; cast to numeric (decimal/float) before arithmetic. Handle nulls and inconsistent formats (e.g., '1.0', '1'). Trim spaces. Validate and fallback for non-numeric values.

net_weight_kg.description: Net product weight (without packaging) in kg (stored as nvarchar).
net_weight_kg.analysis_use: Used for inventory by net product weight, nutrition labeling, and price-per-kg calculations.
business_questions: What is price per kg for each SKU?
What is total net weight sold for a given period?
net_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast from string to numeric before mathematical operations. If both gross and net present, handle comparisons and missing values carefully.

pack_size.description: Count of units per pack (string, constant '1' in sample).
pack_size.analysis_use: Useful to compute units sold from pack-level sales and for packaging/inventory conversions.
business_questions: How many units are in each pack for SKU X?
Convert pack-level sales to unit-level sales using pack_size.
pack_size.nl2sql_hints: Numeric semantics but stored as string; cast when used in calculations. Check for default/constant values; if always 1 consider omitting in queries unless pack sizes vary.

hs_code.description: Harmonized System (HS) code for customs classification. 100% NULL in this snapshot.
hs_code.analysis_use: Used for cross-border trade reporting, customs classification, and tariff calculations. Also for regulatory reporting if populated.
business_questions: Which SKUs are missing HS codes?
What HS codes map to our products for international shipments?
hs_code.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string; expect many nulls in raw feed. When filtering for exports/imports, guard against NULL. If joining to tariff tables use trimmed string and consistent formatting.

shelf_life_days.description: Shelf life in days (stored as nvarchar, values like '3','5','7','10').
shelf_life_days.analysis_use: Critical for perishable inventory planning, FIFO/FEFO picking rules, waste/shrinkage forecasting and promotions for near-expiry items.
business_questions: Which products have a shelf life under X days and need expedited turnover?
What is the distribution of shelf life across perishable SKUs?
shelf_life_days.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for numeric comparisons. Beware of text values like 'NA' or missing; handle nulls. When generating FEFO queries, join to inventory lots and compare production/expiry dates.

storage_temp_c.description: Recommended storage temperature in degrees Celsius (stored as nvarchar).
storage_temp_c.analysis_use: Used for cold-chain logistics segmentation, fridge/freezer assignment and route planning. Group items by required temperature for consolidation.
business_questions: Which SKUs require refrigerated transport (<10°C)?
How many SKUs require a specific temperature range for storage?
storage_temp_c.nl2sql_hints: String with numeric semantics; CAST to numeric for range filters. Normalize values and handle units if sometimes recorded with '°C' or ranges. Use in conjunction with inventory/location temperature capability for feasibility checks.

is_perishable_flag.description: Flag indicating if product is perishable (here stored as '1' for all rows).
is_perishable_flag.analysis_use: Quick filter to separate perishables vs non-perishables for reporting, logistics and promotions.
business_questions: List all perishable SKUs that require FEFO picking.
How much of total SKU count is perishable?
is_perishable_flag.nl2sql_hints: Binary flag stored as string ('1','0' or other). Normalize values into boolean semantics in NL2SQL (e.g., is_perishable_flag = '1' -> TRUE). Expect limited variance in this sample; verify across full dataset.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the product row was created or ingested (stored as nvarchar, format e.g., '2023-10-21 07:04:58').
created_at.analysis_use: Use to track ingestion time, versioning of product metadata, and to filter by when product attributes were added or changed. Useful for incremental ETL and auditing.
business_questions: Which SKUs were added to the catalog in the last 30 days?
Show product metadata created between two dates.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; convert to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for range queries and ordering. Be cautious of inconsistent formats/timezones. Prefer parsing with explicit format in NL2SQL translation.

default_image_url.description: URL to the product's default image. 100% NULL in this sample.
default_image_url.analysis_use: Used to audit and identify products missing imagery for catalog completeness and UI display. Join or map to CDN/asset tables if available.
business_questions: Which SKUs are missing a default image?
Count SKUs with images vs without images by subcategory.
default_image_url.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string; test for NULL or empty string. When present, may need URL validation. Consider left joins to an assets table instead of relying solely on this column.

return_id.description: Unique identifier for the return request (string).
return_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for de-duplication, counting distinct returns, and linking to external return-specific systems. Use in filters or as grouping key when listing returns.
business_questions: How many unique returns exist in a given period?
Show full details for return RET10001.
return_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical/string key. No casting required. Use equality or IN filters (e.g., WHERE return_id = 'RET10001'). It is effectively unique in this dataset but not declared primary key—avoid relying on DB-level constraints.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the return.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders tables to attribute returns to customers, order date, items and revenue. Useful for counting returns per order and computing per-order return rates.
business_questions: Which orders have more than one return?
List returns and corresponding customer/order data for order EORD2089.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Cast/join as string to b2b_orders or ecom_orders on order_id. Ensure fully qualified table names when multiple orders tables exist (e.g., b2b_orders vs ecom_orders). When computing return rate, join with orders table and count distinct order_id.

request_ts.description: Timestamp when the return was requested (stored as nvarchar in ISO-like format).
request_ts.analysis_use: Primary temporal attribute for time-based analyses (daily/weekly/monthly trends), filtering by date range, and calculating latencies relative to other timestamps.
business_questions: How many returns were requested each day?
What is the trend of return requests over the last 30 days?
request_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to proper datetime (e.g., CAST(request_ts AS datetime) or TO_TIMESTAMP) before date arithmetic or grouping. Use explicit format parsing if DB requires it. Support WHERE request_ts BETWEEN '2025-11-01' AND '2025-11-30' only after casting or normalizing string format.

reason_code.description: Categorical reason for the return (e.g., QUALITY, WRONG_ITEM, NOT_AS_DESCRIBED, DAMAGED).
reason_code.analysis_use: Group and filter to understand return drivers, calculate reason-specific rates and refunds, and prioritize quality issues.
business_questions: What percentage of returns are due to QUALITY issues?
Which reason_code is associated with the highest average refund_amount?
reason_code.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; ideal for GROUP BY. Be mindful of casing/whitespace—normalize (UPPER/TRIM) to avoid duplicates. When building NL2SQL, allow synonyms (e.g., quality vs QUALITY) and map user phrases to these codes.

requested_items_json.description: JSON field that should list items requested for return; in this sample it contains '[]' for all rows.
requested_items_json.analysis_use: When populated, parse to extract returned SKUs, quantities and unit-level reasons for item-level analysis. Currently offers no item-level detail because it's empty.
business_questions: Which SKUs are being returned most frequently (requires non-empty JSON)?
What is the breakdown of quantities returned per return?
requested_items_json.nl2sql_hints: Use JSON functions (JSON_VALUE, JSON_QUERY or equivalent) to extract item arrays. If empty array, queries should handle empty/NULL gracefully. NL2SQL should detect when user asks for SKU-level answers and warn/auto-join to a dedicated return_items table if this field is empty or insufficient.

status.description: Lifecycle status of the return (REQUESTED, APPROVED, REJECTED, COMPLETED).
status.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate to show open vs closed returns, compute approval and completion rates, and SLA monitoring.
business_questions: How many returns are currently REQUESTED vs COMPLETED?
What is the completion rate of approved returns?
status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical. Use exact string matches but also support synonyms (e.g., closed includes REJECTED and COMPLETED). When users request 'open returns', translate to status IN ('REQUESTED','APPROVED') depending on business rules.

approved_by.description: Identifier of the approver (ops_manager, quality_head) or NULL if not approved.
approved_by.analysis_use: Performance and workload analysis per approver, approval rate and average refund by approver. Filter to find returns pending approval (NULL).
business_questions: Which approver approved the highest number of returns?
What is the average refund_amount for returns approved by ops_manager?
approved_by.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string with many NULLs. Normalize values. When grouping, treat NULL as 'UNAPPROVED' if business semantics require. Be explicit in NL2SQL when asking about approvers to include/exclude NULLs.

pickup_scheduled_ts.description: Timestamp when the pickup for the return was scheduled (string).
pickup_scheduled_ts.analysis_use: Calculate scheduling latency (time from request to scheduled pickup), measure SLA adherence, and correlate with completed returns.
business_questions: What is the average time from request to pickup scheduled?
How many pickups were scheduled on a particular day?
pickup_scheduled_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for arithmetic. Handle NULLs for returns without scheduled pickup. Use COALESCE or conditional logic when computing average latencies to avoid skew from NULLs.

received_at_centre_ts.description: Timestamp when returned items were received at the central processing centre (string).
received_at_centre_ts.analysis_use: Compute transit time from pickup to reception, end-to-end return cycle time, and identify delays in logistics.
business_questions: What is the average time from pickup_scheduled_ts to received_at_centre_ts?
List returns that took longer than X days to be received at centre.
received_at_centre_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime and handle timezone or format differences. Use WHERE received_at_centre_ts IS NOT NULL for completed receipt-based metrics. Consider negative or inconsistent values—validate chronology (request_ts <= pickup_scheduled_ts <= received_at_centre_ts).

refund_amount.description: Monetary amount refunded for the return, stored as a string representing a decimal number.
refund_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute total refunds, average refund per return, and refunds by reason or status. Reconcile with credit notes via credit_note_id.
business_questions: What is the total refund amount issued in November 2025?
Which reason_code results in the highest total refunds?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/decimal before SUM/AVG (e.g., CAST(refund_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Handle malformed values and nulls. Apply proper rounding/precision in results. When generating NL2SQL, prefer numeric casting in SELECT and WHERE (e.g., WHERE CAST(refund_amount AS DECIMAL) > 100).

credit_note_id.description: Identifier of the credit note generated for the refund (string), often links to a credit note/invoice table.
credit_note_id.analysis_use: Join to credit notes table for reconciliation and tracing of refund documentation. Check for returns lacking credit notes and reconcile amounts.
business_questions: Which returns do not have an associated credit_note_id?
Match credit note CN1656 to its return and refund_amount for reconciliation.
credit_note_id.nl2sql_hints: Join key to b2b_credit_notes or analogous tables. Treat as string; watch for inconsistent prefixes. Nulls indicate missing credit note. Use LEFT JOIN when listing returns with or without credit notes.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the raw table (string). May be close to request_ts for ingest.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time analyses, identify late-arriving records, and compare created_at vs request_ts for data latency monitoring.
business_questions: How long after request_ts are returns recorded in the raw table on average?
Show returns created after a given ingestion date/time.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for comparisons. Be careful: created_at may reflect ingestion time rather than business event time—prefer request_ts for business timelines. Use created_at to filter incremental ingestion windows in NL2SQL-generated ETL queries.

resolution_notes.description: Free-text resolution notes; in this dataset the column is fully NULL.
resolution_notes.analysis_use: When populated, supports qualitative analysis, issue categorization and escalation tracking. Could be used for keyword search (e.g., 'customer refused', 'missing accessory').
business_questions: List returns where resolution_notes mention 'replacement' or 'refund only'.
How many returns include manual notes about investigation outcomes?
resolution_notes.nl2sql_hints: Text column often null—handle NULLs in filters (e.g., WHERE resolution_notes IS NOT NULL). For keyword searches use LIKE or full-text search if available. NL2SQL should allow free-text queries mapping to LIKE '%keyword%'. If analytics need structured resolution reasons, recommend normalizing into a separate coded column.

shipment_id.description: Unique identifier for the shipment record (string).
shipment_id.analysis_use: Primary label for a shipment row; used for deduplication, counting distinct shipments, and as an identifier when joining to external shipment systems. Useful in GROUP BY when reporting at shipment granularity.
business_questions: How many distinct shipments were created in a given period?
List shipment details for shipment_id X for investigation.
shipment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. No PK constraint in DDL — queries should use DISTINCT if deduplication is required. Exact-match filters on shipment_id are efficient; avoid range predicates.

order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders tables to enrich shipment records with order-level fields (customer, order value, items). Also used for grouping shipments per order (e.g., multi-parcel orders).
business_questions: Which orders have more than one shipment?
Show shipments and status for order_id X.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Although string, this is the main foreign-key-like column. NL2SQL should preferentially map to b2b_orders or ecom_orders using exact string equality. When joining, ensure trimmed/canonical forms if upstream order_id formats vary. Consider indexing when used frequently in joins or filters.

carrier_id.analysis_use: Categorical filter or GROUP BY dimension for performance and cost comparisons (carrier-level KPIs like delivery time, cost per shipment, success rates).
business_questions: Which carrier has the fastest average delivery time?
Which carrier accounts for the highest shipping spend?
carrier_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality (3 values). Use equality or GROUP BY. When NL2SQL maps to a textual filter, match exact values; consider synonyms (e.g., 'Local Courier' vs 'LocalCourier'). If joining to a carriers master, use exact equality on carrier_id.

tracking_no.description: Carrier tracking number assigned to the physical shipment.
tracking_no.analysis_use: Join key to shipment-tracking-event tables, used for searching a shipment's event history, troubleshooting, and external carrier reconciliation.
business_questions: Show tracking events for tracking_no X.
Which shipments have no tracking number recorded?
tracking_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as exact-match textual key. When NL2SQL receives natural language asking about tracking events, map to equality filters on tracking_no and join to tracking/event tables. Beware of leading/trailing spaces or formatting differences.

shipment_date.description: Timestamp when the shipment was dispatched (stored as text in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
shipment_date.analysis_use: Used for time-based filters, bucketed aggregations (by day/week/month), and calculating lead times (when paired with order creation). Must be cast to proper datetime type for arithmetic and range queries.
business_questions: How many shipments were dispatched each day last week?
Show shipments dispatched between date A and date B.
shipment_date.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, values follow an ISO-like format — cast to datetime (e.g., CAST(shipment_date AS datetime) or equivalent) before range filtering, grouping by date, or date arithmetic. NL2SQL should generate casts when users ask for timeframe filters. Validate null/empty strings.

expected_delivery_date.analysis_use: Reference for SLA/on-time calculations. Used to compute delivery delay (actual_delivery_date - expected_delivery_date) and to identify overdue shipments.
business_questions: How many shipments were expected to deliver today but are not delivered?
What percentage of shipments met the expected delivery date?
expected_delivery_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime before comparison. When computing delay, ensure both expected and actual are non-null. Handle same-timezone assumptions; NL2SQL should include CASTs and null checks.

actual_delivery_date.description: Timestamp when the shipment was actually delivered (text). Contains many nulls for undelivered or in-transit shipments.
actual_delivery_date.analysis_use: Critical for computing delivery time, delivery delays, on-time rates and throughput. Use in filters to find undelivered shipments (IS NULL) and in aggregations only after casting to datetime.
business_questions: What is the average delivery time (hours/days) from shipment to actual delivery?
List shipments without an actual_delivery_date (possible exceptions).
actual_delivery_date.nl2sql_hints: Handle null prevalence (~41%). CAST to datetime for arithmetic. When generating NL2SQL, include WHERE actual_delivery_date IS NOT NULL for delay aggregations. Consider timezone and format validation if data is inconsistent.

shipment_status.description: Current lifecycle state of the shipment (OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, IN_TRANSIT, PICKED, DELIVERED).
shipment_status.analysis_use: Categorical slicer for operational dashboards; used for counts, proportions, and filters to focus on pending/delivered shipments and to drive alerts (e.g., many OUT_FOR_DELIVERY that are overdue).
business_questions: How many shipments are currently OUT_FOR_DELIVERY by pickup centre?
What is the trend of shipments in IN_TRANSIT vs DELIVERED over time?
shipment_status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field; NL2SQL should map status names verbatim. When users specify statuses in natural language (e.g., 'active shipments'), translate to the correct set of statuses. Use equality or IN(...) clauses. Consider canonicalization (uppercase) if needed.

pickup_centre_id.description: Identifier/name of the pickup/dispatch centre handling the shipment (e.g., 'Surat Distribution Centre').
pickup_centre_id.analysis_use: Group and compare operational metrics across pickup centres: shipment volume, average dispatch-to-delivery time, average shipping charge, and exception rates.
business_questions: Which pickup centre handles the most shipments?
Which pickup centre has the highest late-delivery rate?
pickup_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality (2 values). Use as GROUP BY or filter. When mapping NL2SQL queries, match the full centre name or support semantic synonyms ('Surat DC' => 'Surat Distribution Centre').

package_weight_kg.description: Package weight recorded in kilograms, stored as text (numeric-like values).
package_weight_kg.analysis_use: Convert to numeric to compute totals, averages, weight buckets (e.g., shipments > 5kg) and cost per kg. Useful for weight-based pricing analysis and carrier selection optimization.
business_questions: What is the total weight shipped per day and per carrier?
List shipments heavier than 10 kg.
package_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast/converted to numeric (e.g., DECIMAL/DOUBLE) before numeric comparisons or aggregations. Handle malformed or empty strings; include safe-cast or TRY_CAST where supported. NL2SQL should add casts when user asks for numeric thresholds or aggregations.

package_dimensions_json.description: JSON field intended to hold package dimensions but currently contains '{}' for every row.
package_dimensions_json.analysis_use: Currently no analytical value (empty JSON). If populated in the future, would be parsed for dimension-based volumetric weight calculations or packaging analytics.
business_questions: If populated: what is the volumetric weight distribution?
Which shipments exceed the allowed package dimension thresholds?
package_dimensions_json.nl2sql_hints: Currently constant '{}' — queries can ignore this column. If future rows contain JSON, use JSON_EXTRACT/->> depending on DB to parse keys (length/width/height). NL2SQL should detect constant column and avoid unnecessary parsing.

shipping_charge.description: Monetary shipping charge for the shipment stored as text (numeric-like).
shipping_charge.analysis_use: Convert to numeric for sums, averages, cost-per-order, carrier cost comparisons and revenue/expense breakdowns. Useful for cost optimization and margin calculations when joined to order value.
business_questions: What is total shipping spend by carrier this month?
What is the average shipping charge per kg?
shipping_charge.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before SUM/AVG or comparisons. Watch currency conventions; currently appears numeric only. Use TRY_CAST/SAFE_CAST or filter out non-numeric rows. When NL2SQL sees queries about totals or averages, include the numeric cast.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the shipment record was created in the system (text).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time analysis, event sequencing and recent activity filtering (e.g., shipments created in last 24 hours). Cast to datetime for time-window queries.
business_questions: Show shipments created in the last 7 days.
How many shipments are created per hour during peak times?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Textual timestamp — CAST to datetime for range filters and bucketing. Use this field for record-level recency filters; if both created_at and shipment_date exist, clarifying which the user means is important in NL2SQL prompts.

last_update.description: Timestamp of the last update to the shipment record (text).
last_update.analysis_use: Used for freshness checks, detecting status changes (e.g., a status updated recently), and incremental sync logic. Cast to datetime for time-based analysis.
business_questions: Which shipments were updated in the last hour?
List shipments whose status changed today (using last_update as proxy).
last_update.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — CAST to datetime before comparisons. For change-detection queries, consider combining last_update with shipment_status or join to event tables. NL2SQL should confirm whether 'updated' refers to record timestamp or tracking events.
